question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer by using the codes given below the lists
List I | List II
I. Ernesto Zedillo | (A) Uruguay
II. Alberto Fujimori | (B) Brazil
III. Julio Maria Sanguinetti | (C) Mexico
IV. Fernando H. Cardoso | (D) Bolivia
| (E) Peru
subject: International Relations

prelims.trap_factor: Names have misleading phonetics — 'Cardoso' sounds Central American, 'Zedillo' sounds generically Spanish. Without specific knowledge of 1990s Latin American politics, all options look plausible.
prelims.elimination_hint: Anchor on Fujimori = Peru (famous Japanese-Peruvian, unique identifier). From the code that maps II to E (Peru), check which option has II→E and work outward.
factual_hooks: Alberto Fujimori = Peru (Japanese-Peruvian president 1990–2000)
Ernesto Zedillo = Mexico (PRI party, last PRI president)
Fernando H. Cardoso = Brazil (sociologist-economist president)
Julio Maria Sanguinetti = Uruguay (twice president)

contrast_with: Cardoso (Brazil) vs Zedillo (Mexico) — both technocrat-economists from same era, easily swapped

question:
A, B, C, D, E and F, not necessarily in that order are sitting on six chairs regularly placed around a round table. It is observed that A is between D and F, C is opposite D, and D and E are not on neighbouring chairs.
Which one of the following pairs must be sitting on neighbouring chairs ?
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Circular seating is counter-intuitive — 'opposite' means 3 seats apart in a 6-seat circle. Students also confuse 'A is between D and F' in circular context with linear meaning.
prelims.elimination_hint: Draw the 6-seat circle. Fix C opposite D (seats 1 & 4). Place A between D and F. D and E are non-adjacent, so E must be placed away from D. Verify each option against all constraints.
factual_hooks: In 6-chair circle, 'opposite' = 3 seats apart
'Between' in circle = one on each side
Each person has exactly 2 neighbors in a 6-seat circle

prerequisite_concepts: Opposite seat concept in even-number circles
contrast_with: Circular arrangement (no absolute ends) vs linear arrangement (fixed left/right endpoints)

question: Persons below the poverty line in India are classified as such based on whether
option_a: they are entitled to a minimum prescribed food basket
option_b: they get work for a prescribed minimum number of days in a year
option_c: they belong to agricultural labourer household and the scheduled caste/tribe social group
option_d: their daily wages fall below the prescribed minimum wages
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Option D ('wages below minimum wages') sounds logical since low wages = poverty. Option C ('agricultural labourers + SC/ST') conflates social identity with poverty criterion. India's official poverty line is purely consumption-based.
prelims.elimination_hint: India's poverty line = minimum food basket/caloric intake (Tendulkar committee). Eliminate B (employment-based), C (caste/occupation-based), D (wage-based). Only A (food basket) is the official criterion.
factual_hooks: India poverty line = minimum caloric consumption (2100 kcal urban, 2400 kcal rural)
Tendulkar Committee (2009) revised poverty estimation
Rangarajan Committee (2014) further revised — higher poverty count
BPL cards linked to food entitlements under NFSA

mains.contemporary_relevance: India now also uses Multidimensional Poverty Index (MPI) alongside consumption-based poverty line; significant MPI reduction reported post-2015.

contrast_with: Absolute poverty (caloric/consumption based — India's method) vs Relative poverty (% below median income — used in Europe)

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the codes given below the Lists
List I | List II
I. Cotton | (A) Rainfall 1000-1500 mm; Temperature 40° - 60°C
II. Flax | (B) Rainfall 1500-2000 mm; Temperature 25° - 35°C
III. Sugarbeet | (C) Rainfall 600 - 800 mm; Temperature 5° - 18°C
IV. Jute | (D) Rainfall 500 - 1000 mm; Temperature 18° - 22°C
| (E) Rainfall 500 - 600 mm; Temperature 18°- 22°C
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Jute needs HIGH rainfall (1500–2000mm) which students confuse with Cotton. Sugarbeet is a TEMPERATE crop (5–18°C) which is counter-intuitive since India is tropical.
prelims.elimination_hint: Anchor on Jute = Bengal delta = high rainfall (1500–2000mm). Sugarbeet = European/temperate crop = low temperature (5–18°C). Cotton = semi-arid = moderate rainfall. Match remaining.
factual_hooks: Jute = high rainfall 1500–2000mm (Bengal/Bangladesh)
Sugarbeet = temperate crop, low temp 5–18°C
Cotton = semi-arid, 500–1000mm (Deccan plateau)
Flax = cool, moderate rainfall 600–800mm

prerequisite_concepts: Major cash crops and their agroclimatic requirements
Rabi vs Kharif vs Zaid classification
contrast_with: Jute (high rainfall, tropical) vs Cotton (low rainfall, semi-arid) — both cash crops but opposite water needs

question:
Consider the volumes of the following:
1. A parallelepiped of length 5 cm, breadth 3 cm and height 4 cm
2. A cube of each side 4 cm
3. A cylinder of radius 3 cm and length 3 cm
4. A sphere of radius 3 cm The volumes of these in the decreasing order is
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Students misremember sphere volume as (4/3)πr² instead of (4/3)πr³. Also confusion between cylinder r×h and r²×h.
prelims.elimination_hint: Calculate: Parallelepiped=5×3×4=60; Cube=4³=64; Cylinder=π×9×3≈84.8; Sphere=(4/3)×π×27≈113.1. Decreasing: Sphere>Cylinder>Cube>Parallelepiped = IV,III,II,I = D.
factual_hooks: Sphere volume = (4/3)πr³
Cylinder volume = πr²h
Cube volume = a³
Parallelepiped = l×b×h

question: Consider the following statements about Sikh Gurus: 1. Banda Bahadur was appointed as the military leader of the Sikhs by Guru Tegh Bahadur. 2. Guru Arjan Dev became the Sikh Guru after Guru Ram Das. 3. Guru Arjan Dev gave to Sikhs their own script—Gurumukhi. Which one of the statements given above is/are correct?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Statement 1 tricky — both Guru Tegh Bahadur (9th) and Guru Gobind Singh (10th) are prominent; students confuse who sent Banda Bahadur. Statement 3 about Gurumukhi plausible since Arjan Dev compiled the Adi Granth in Gurumukhi script.
prelims.elimination_hint: Gurumukhi = Guru Angad Dev (2nd Guru), NOT Arjan Dev — eliminate S3. Banda Bahadur commissioned by Guru Gobind Singh (10th), not Guru Tegh Bahadur (9th). Guru Arjan Dev DID succeed Guru Ram Das — S2 correct.
factual_hooks: Guru Arjan Dev = 5th Guru, son/successor of Guru Ram Das (4th)
Gurumukhi = Guru Angad Dev (2nd Guru, not Arjan Dev)
Banda Bahadur sent to Punjab by Guru Gobind Singh (10th, not 9th)
Guru Arjan Dev compiled Adi Granth and built Harmandir Sahib

contrast_with: Guru Arjan Dev (5th, Adi Granth) vs Guru Angad Dev (2nd, Gurumukhi) — commonly confused
Guru Tegh Bahadur (9th, martyrdom) vs Guru Gobind Singh (10th, Khalsa, Banda Bahadur)

question:
Assertion (A)
: In our houses, the current in AC electricity line changes direction 60 times per second.
Reason (R)
: The frequency of alternating voltage supplied is 60 hertz.
option_a: Both A and R are individually true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_b: Both A and R are individually true but R is not the correct explanation of A
option_c: A is true but R is false
option_d: A is false but R is true
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Students who know India uses 50 Hz may mark Assertion as false. Trap: the question tests logical consistency between A and R. The Reason incorrectly states India's frequency as 60 Hz — so R is factually wrong for India.
prelims.elimination_hint: India's AC frequency = 50 Hz, NOT 60 Hz (60 Hz = USA/Canada). So the Reason is factually WRONG. At 50 Hz, current changes direction 100 times/sec (not 60). Answer C: the assertion may be stated as true (in 60 Hz context) but the Reason is false for India.
factual_hooks: India uses 50 Hz AC (like Europe, Australia)
USA/Canada use 60 Hz AC
At frequency f Hz, current changes direction 2f times per second
AC direction changes = twice per complete cycle

contrast_with: 50 Hz systems (India, Europe) vs 60 Hz systems (Americas) — incompatible appliances without converters

question: What was the purpose of the Operation Sukoon launched by the Government of India?
option_a: Helping Indonesia in its efforts to rehabilitate the victims of earthquake in that country
option_b: Evacuating the Indian Nationals from Lebanon during the conflict in the Middle East
option_c: Assisting United Nations in its efforts to help the civil war victims in the Darfur region of North Africa
option_d: Providing a relief package to farmers after a spate of suicides by other farmers in Andhra Pradesh
subject: International Relations

prelims.trap_factor: Options A (Indonesia earthquake) and C (Darfur/UN) sound like plausible humanitarian missions. 'Sukoon' name exploited: sounds like it could relate to any peace operation.
prelims.elimination_hint: 'Sukoon' = peace/relief in Urdu/Hindi → fits evacuation from conflict zone. 2006 = Israel-Hezbollah war in Lebanon → Indian nationals evacuated. INS Mumbai deployed. Eliminate A (earthquake relief, not evacuation) and C (UN Darfur).
factual_hooks: Operation Sukoon = 2006, Lebanon evacuation during Israel-Hezbollah war
~2,000 Indian nationals evacuated by INS Mumbai and INS Brahmaputra
'Sukoon' = comfort/peace in Urdu

mains.contemporary_relevance: India's NEO capability demonstrated in Yemen (2015) and Ukraine (2022); key component of diaspora security policy.

contrast_with: Operation Sukoon (Lebanon 2006) vs Operation Raahat (Yemen 2015) vs Operation Ganga (Ukraine 2022) — all Indian evacuations from conflict zones

question:
With reference to Union Government, consider the following statements :
1. The number of Ministries at the Centre on 15th August 1947 was 18.
2. The number of Ministries at the Centre at present is 36.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct ?
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Both statements require specific numerical recall. Statement 2 about '36 at present' (2009) seems plausible — India had more than 36 ministries by 2009 making S2 wrong.
prelims.elimination_hint: S1 (18 ministries on Aug 15, 1947) = known historical fact about Nehru's lean cabinet. S2 ('36 at present in 2009') = wrong, India had more ministries by 2009. With S1 correct and S2 wrong, answer = A.
factual_hooks: Nehru's first cabinet: 18 Ministries on Independence Day 1947
Ministry count grew significantly over decades
91st Amendment 2003: CoM capped at 15% of Lok Sabha strength

contrast_with: Council of Ministers (political, changes with government) vs IAS/Civil Services (permanent administration)

question: Two numbers X and Y are respectively 20% ad 28% less than a third number Z. By what percentage is the number Y less than the number X?
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Most common error: subtract 28%−20%=8% directly, picking option A. Wrong because percentages must be compared relative to BASE (X), not to Z.
factual_hooks: Set Z=100 to simplify
Percentage difference formula: (X−Y)/X × 100
Never subtract percentages referenced to different bases

prerequisite_concepts: Percentage change vs percentage point difference

question:
In the context of Indian economy, consider the following statements:

1. The growth rate of GDP has steadily increased in the last five years.
2. The growth rate in per capita income has steadily increased in the last five years.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Students assume India's economy was on smooth upward trajectory in 2000s. The 2008 global financial crisis caused a dip — growth was NOT monotonically increasing. Both statements say 'steadily increased' which was false.
prelims.elimination_hint: India's GDP growth: peaked ~9.8% in 2006-07, fell to ~6.7% in 2008-09. 'Steadily increased' = false. Per capita also did not steadily increase. Both statements wrong = D (Neither).
factual_hooks: India GDP growth 2006-07 ≈ 9.8% (peak)
2008-09 global crisis: India's growth dipped to ~6.7%
'Steadily increasing' ≠ growth that dipped
Per capita = GDP growth minus population growth (~1.4%/yr)

mains.contemporary_relevance: India's 7%+ growth in recent years marks recovery; V-shaped recovery post-COVID frequently referenced.

prerequisite_concepts: Impact of 2008 global crisis on India
Per capita income = National Income ÷ Population
contrast_with: GDP growth rate (can dip) vs cumulative GDP (always grows nominally)

question: When the Reserve Bank of India reduces the Statutory Liquidity Ratio by 50 basis points, which of the following is likely to happen?
option_a: India's GDP growth rate increases drastically
option_b: Foreign Institutional Investors may bring more capital into our country
option_c: Scheduled Commercial Banks may cut their lending rates
option_d: It may drastically reduce the liquidity to the banking system
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Option B (FII capital inflow) tempting if SLR confused with interest rate cuts. Option D says 'drastically reduce liquidity' which is the exact OPPOSITE of SLR reduction's effect.
prelims.elimination_hint: SLR reduction = banks hold LESS in liquid assets = MORE funds for lending = expanded credit supply = competitive pressure to cut rates = C. SLR reduction INCREASES liquidity (eliminate D). Doesn't directly affect FII flows (eliminate B).
factual_hooks: SLR = % deposits kept in liquid assets (govt securities, gold, cash)
Reducing SLR frees more funds for lending
SLR = expansionary monetary policy tool
Current SLR ~18%, down from historical high of 38.5%

mains.contemporary_relevance: RBI regularly adjusts SLR for monetary transmission; SLR reduction now less frequent as RBI focuses on repo rate-based policy.

contrast_with: SLR (liquid assets including govt securities) vs CRR (cash held with RBI) — both reserve requirements but SLR broader

question: Which one of the following dances involves solo performance ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Kathak and Odissi are also solo forms, so students may second-guess Bharatanatyam. Key: Kuchipudi ORIGINALLY was a GROUP performance by male Brahmin community — it was NOT historically solo unlike the others.
prelims.elimination_hint: Kuchipudi = originated as group dramatic performance by male Brahmin community of Kuchipudi village (AP), NOT traditionally solo. Bharatanatyam = Devadasi solo tradition (Tamil Nadu). Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Bharatanatyam = Tamil Nadu, Devadasi tradition, always solo female
Kuchipudi = Andhra Pradesh, originally group male Brahmin performance
Kathak = North Indian courts, solo
Odissi = Odisha temple tradition, solo

contrast_with: Kuchipudi (originally group/male, AP) vs Bharatanatyam (solo/female, Tamil Nadu)

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists
List I | List II
I. Theory of Mutation | (A) Beadk and Tatum
II. Theory of Evolution | (B) Jacob and Monod
III. One gene one enzyme hypothesis | (C) Darwin
IV. Operon concept | (D) De Vries
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Beadle & Tatum (one gene-one enzyme) vs Jacob & Monod (operon) easily mixed up — both 20th-century molecular biology Nobel laureates.
prelims.elimination_hint: Anchor: Darwin = Evolution. De Vries = Mutation (1901). Beadle & Tatum = One gene-one enzyme (1941, Nobel 1958). Jacob & Monod = Operon/lac operon (1961, Nobel 1965). Match: I-D, II-C, III-A, IV-B = B.
factual_hooks: Hugo De Vries = Mutation Theory (1901)
Charles Darwin = Theory of Evolution
Beadle & Tatum = One gene-one enzyme hypothesis (1941)
Jacob & Monod = Operon concept (1961)

contrast_with: De Vries (abrupt heritable change = mutation) vs Darwin (gradual accumulation = evolution)

question: The Minimum Alternative Tax (MAT) was introduced in the Budget of the Government of India for the year
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Students associate MAT with 1991 LPG reforms, marking 1991-92. Actually introduced in 1996-97 budget — 5 years after LPG reforms.
prelims.elimination_hint: MAT introduced in Budget 1996-97 by Finance Minister P. Chidambaram. Purpose: ensure companies with book profits but zero taxable income pay minimum tax. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: MAT introduced Budget 1996-97
Finance Minister P. Chidambaram
MAT ensures zero-tax companies pay minimum on book profits
MAT credit can be carried forward 15 years

mains.contemporary_relevance: 2019 corporate tax cuts reduced base rates; MAT continues as floor. MAT vs normal tax is key corporate tax planning issue.

contrast_with: MAT (companies) vs AMT (Alternate Minimum Tax, non-corporate entities)

question:
Consider the following States :
I. Gujarat
II. Karnataka
III. Maharashtra
IV. Tamil Nadu.
The descending order of these States with reference to their level of Per Capita Net State Domestic Product is
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Students rank by total GSDP (absolute size) rather than per capita NSDP. Maharashtra highest in both, but Tamil Nadu vs Karnataka ordering is non-obvious.
prelims.elimination_hint: Maharashtra = highest per capita NSDP (Mumbai = financial capital). Gujarat = 2nd (Surat/Ahmedabad industrial wealth). Tamil Nadu slightly above Karnataka in 2001. Order: Maharashtra>Gujarat>Tamil Nadu>Karnataka = III,I,IV,II = B.
factual_hooks: Maharashtra = historically India's highest per capita NSDP
Gujarat = 2nd (strong trade/industry)
NSDP = Net State Domestic Product = state-level NDP
Per capita = NSDP ÷ state population

contrast_with: NSDP (after depreciation) vs GSDP (gross) — NSDP more accurate welfare indicator

question: A big rectangular plot of area 4320 m^2 is divided in to 3 square-shaped smaller plots by fencing parallel to the smaller side of the plot. However some area of land was still left as a square could not be formed. So, 3 more square-shaped plots were formed by fencing parallel to the longer side of the original plot such that no area of the plot was left surplus. What was the dimensions of the original plot?
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Students confuse which direction squares are formed first (parallel to shorter vs longer side). The two-step fencing process is counter-intuitive.
prelims.elimination_hint: Option C (120×36): Shorter side=36. 3 squares of 36×36 use 108m, leaving 12×36=432. 3 squares of 12×12=432 (36/12=3 ✓). Total=4320 ✓. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Area 4320=120×36 satisfies both conditions
First phase: 3 squares of 36m side
Second phase: 3 squares of 12m side
Key: remainder (12) must divide breadth (36) exactly into 3

question:
Consider the following statements regarding the political parties in India:
I. The Representation of the People Act, 1951 provides for the registration of political parties.
II. Registration of political parties is carried out by the Election Commission.
III. A national level political party is one which is recognised in four or more States.
IV. During the 1999 general elections, there were six National and 48 State level parties recognised by the Election Commission.
Which of these statements are correct ?
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Statement IV mentions specific numbers (6 national, 48 state parties 1999). Students unsure of exact counts may eliminate D despite I,II,III being clearly correct.
prelims.elimination_hint: Statements I (RPA 1951 provides registration), II (EC registers parties), III (national party = 4+ states) are all textbook facts. Statement IV is historically correct for 1999. If I,II,III all correct and 'all four' option exists, choose D.
factual_hooks: RPA 1951 = key electoral law
Election Commission = sole authority for party recognition
National party = recognized in at least 4 states
1999: 6 national parties (INC, BJP, NCP, CPI, CPM, BSP)

mains.contemporary_relevance: Party recognition rules debated; 2024 elections saw realignment of national vs state status. Electoral bond controversy.

contrast_with: National party (4+ states, national symbol) vs State party (specific state, state symbol)

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the codes given below the Lists
(Institute) | (Location)
I. Central Institute of Medicinal and Aromatic Plants | (A) Chandigarh
II. Centre for DNA Finger Printing and Diagnostics | (B) Hyderabad
III. Institute of Microbial Technology | (C) New Delhi
IV. National Institute of Immunology | (D) Lucknow
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: CDFD=Hyderabad well-known. But CIMAP=Lucknow (not Delhi) and IMTech=Chandigarh create confusion. NII in New Delhi (not Hyderabad despite biotech hub) is counter-intuitive.
prelims.elimination_hint: CDFD=Hyderabad (biotech hub, B). NII=New Delhi (National=Capital, C). IMTech=Chandigarh (CSIR Microbial Technology, Punjab-region, A). CIMAP=Lucknow (CSIR medicinal plants = UP, D). I-D,II-B,III-A,IV-C = option B.
factual_hooks: CIMAP = Lucknow (CSIR, medicinal & aromatic plants)
CDFD = Hyderabad (DNA fingerprinting, DBT)
IMTech = Chandigarh (CSIR, microbial technology)
NII = New Delhi (DBT, immunology)

prerequisite_concepts: CSIR vs DBT labs in India
contrast_with: CSIR labs (industrial R&D) vs DBT institutions (biotechnology/life sciences)

question: In which one of the following areas did the Indira Gandhi Centre for Atomic Research make significant progress in the year 2005?
option_a: Reprocessing the uranium-plutonium mixed carbide fuel of the Fast Breeder Test Reactor.
option_b: New applications of radioisotopes in metallurgy.
option_c: A new technology for production of heavy water.
option_d: A new technology for high level nuclear waste management.
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Options B (radioisotopes/metallurgy) and C (heavy water) sound more broadly significant. Students unfamiliar with fast breeder milestones may choose them.
prelims.elimination_hint: IGCAR = Kalpakkam = SPECIFICALLY for fast breeder reactor. FBTR = Fast Breeder Test Reactor = uses mixed carbide fuel. 2005 milestone = reprocessing this unique carbide fuel. Anchor on IGCAR=FBR=Kalpakkam.
factual_hooks: IGCAR = Kalpakkam, Tamil Nadu (fast breeder research)
FBTR uses mixed uranium-plutonium carbide fuel
India's 3-stage nuclear: Stage 1 (PHWR) → Stage 2 (FBR) → Stage 3 (AHWR/Thorium)
Carbide fuel reprocessing = closing fast breeder fuel cycle

mains.contemporary_relevance: India's fast breeder program key to energy security using thorium; PFBR commissioning awaited.

contrast_with: FBTR (test reactor, 40MW, since 1985) vs PFBR (500MW commercial prototype, also Kalpakkam)

question:
Match List-I (Distinguished Person) with List-II (Area of Work) and select the correct answer using the code given below the list
List I | List II
A. B. V. Rao | 1. Automobile Manufacture
B. C. K. Prahalad | 2. Fisheries Economy
C. John Kurien | 3. Information Technology and Software
D. Kiran Karnik | 4. Poultry Farming
| 5. Management Science
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: C.K. Prahalad famous for 'Fortune at Bottom of Pyramid' — students may associate with development/social work rather than 'Management Science'. Kiran Karnik=NASSCOM=IT is most guessable.
prelims.elimination_hint: Kiran Karnik=NASSCOM=IT(D→3). C.K. Prahalad=Wharton=Management Science(B→5). John Kurien=Kerala fisheries economist(C→2). B.V. Rao=Venkateshwara Hatcheries=Poultry(A→4). Answer: D.
factual_hooks: B.V. Rao = 'Father of poultry revolution' (Venkateshwara Hatcheries)
C.K. Prahalad = 'Fortune at Bottom of Pyramid'
John Kurien = fisheries economist, Thiruvananthapuram
Kiran Karnik = NASSCOM president

question:
The range of
Agni-II
missile is around
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Agni ranges commonly confused: Agni-I≈700km, Agni-II≈2000km, Agni-III≈3500km. Option C (3500km) = Agni-III's range — classic mix-up.
prelims.elimination_hint: Memorize: Agni-I=700km, Agni-II=2000km, Agni-III=3500km, Agni-IV=4000km, Agni-V=5000+km. Answer = B (2000km).
factual_hooks: Agni-II = ~2000km range = MRBM
DRDO developed, first tested 1999
Can reach major Chinese cities from central India

mains.contemporary_relevance: Agni-V with MIRV capability is India's strategic deterrent against China; Agni-Prime is new composite missile.

prerequisite_concepts: ICBM vs IRBM vs MRBM by range
contrast_with: Agni (ballistic, strategic) vs BrahMos (supersonic cruise, tactical)

question: What is IndoNext which was launched in Jan. 2005?
option_a: A new scheme to promote Indian tourism.
option_b: A new scheme to promote export of Indian handicrafts.
option_c: An association of the Non-Resident Indians to organize Pravasi Bharatiya Divas every year in India.
option_d: An alternative trading platform being promoted by the Bombay Stock Exchange and Regional Stock Exchanges.
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Name 'IndoNext' sounds NRI-related or tourism ('India's next generation'). Options A (tourism) and C (NRI organization) designed to exploit this name ambiguity.
prelims.elimination_hint: 'BSE and Regional Stock Exchanges' in option D = financial/capital markets. IndoNext = BSE + 19 Regional Stock Exchanges joint venture for alternative trading platform for smaller companies.
factual_hooks: IndoNext = BSE + Regional Stock Exchanges joint venture
Launched January 2005
Alternative trading platform for companies not qualifying for main BSE
Targeted SMEs and regional companies

mains.contemporary_relevance: IndoNext evolved into BSE SME exchange; SEBI's SME platform enables small companies to access public capital.

contrast_with: BSE Main Board (large companies) vs IndoNext/SME platform (smaller companies, relaxed norms)

question: Which one of the following is the correct statement? Service Tax is a/an
option_a: Direct Tax levied by the Central Government
option_b: Indirect Tax levied by the Central Government
option_c: Direct Tax levied by the State Government
option_d: Indirect Tax levied by the Statement Government
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Students confuse who levies it — service tax is Central Government (not State). Also confuse direct vs indirect: indirect tax = paid through intermediary (service provider) not directly by consumer to government.
prelims.elimination_hint: Indirect tax = paid by intermediary and passed on to consumer (GST, service tax, excise). Direct tax = paid directly by taxpayer (income tax, corporate tax). Central Government levied service tax before GST. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Service tax = indirect tax (consumer pays through service provider)
Levied by Central Government
Introduced 1994 by Finance Minister Manmohan Singh
Replaced by GST in July 2017
Standard rate 12.36% (with education cess)

mains.contemporary_relevance: Understanding service tax prerequisite to GST structure; input tax credit and Centre-State revenue sharing disputes ongoing.

contrast_with: Service tax (Central, indirect) vs Entertainment tax (State, indirect) — both indirect but different government levels; both subsumed into GST

question:
Which of the following pairs in respect of current power generation in India is/are correctly matched? (Rounded Figure)
1. Installed electricity generation capacity : 100000 MW
2. Electricity generation : 660 billion kWh
Select the correct answer using the code given below: Code:
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Both 100,000 MW (installed capacity) and 660 billion kWh (generation) seem plausible for 2008. India's actual installed capacity was ~140,000-150,000 MW in 2008, making S1 wrong.
prelims.elimination_hint: India's installed electricity capacity 2008 ≈ 140,000-150,000 MW (NOT 100,000 MW). S1 wrong. 660 billion kWh generation was approximately correct for 2007-08. Answer: B (S2 only).
factual_hooks: India installed capacity 2008 ≈ 140,000-150,000 MW
660 billion kWh generation approximately correct 2007-08
India crossed 100,000 MW installed capacity around 2006
India = world's 3rd largest electricity producer

mains.contemporary_relevance: India's installed capacity crossed 900,000 MW (900 GW) by 2024 with major renewable expansion.

prerequisite_concepts: MW vs kWh: capacity vs generation
contrast_with: Installed capacity (MW, maximum potential) vs Actual generation (kWh, actually produced)

question: How do most insects respire?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Students near water may associate insects with gills. 'Simple' looking insects suggest skin respiration. Tracheal system is specific to arthropods and counter-intuitive — no visible breathing organ.
prelims.elimination_hint: Insects = Arthropoda = tracheal system. Spiracles on thorax/abdomen connect to tracheal tubes running through body. No blood carries oxygen. Fish=gills, mammals=lungs, earthworms/frogs=skin. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Insects breathe via tracheal system (tracheae + tracheoles)
Spiracles = external openings on thorax/abdomen
No respiratory pigment in insect blood — oxygen via air tubes directly
Tracheal system limits insect body size

mains.contemporary_relevance: Insect tracheal system studied for bioinspired micro-robotics and pesticide delivery mechanisms.

prerequisite_concepts: Comparative: gills vs lungs vs trachea vs skin
contrast_with: Trachea in insects (entire tube network) vs trachea in humans (single windpipe) — same word, different structures

question:
Consider the following statements : Glass can be etched or scratched by
I.
diamond.
II.
hydrofluoric acid.
III.
aquaregia.
IV.
cone, sulphuric acid.
Which of these statements are correct ?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Aqua regia famous for dissolving gold — students assume it dissolves everything. Conc. H₂SO₄ is powerful, suggesting it attacks glass. Neither attacks glass = SiO₂.
prelims.elimination_hint: Glass = SiO₂. Only HF reacts with Si-O bonds: SiO₂ + 4HF → SiF₄ + 2H₂O. Diamond scratches physically (Mohs 10 vs glass 5-6). Aqua regia attacks noble metals, not silicon. Conc. H₂SO₄ doesn't react with SiO₂. Answer: C (I and II).
factual_hooks: Glass = SiO₂ (amorphous silicon dioxide)
HF unique in attacking silicon compounds
SiO₂ + 4HF → SiF₄ + 2H₂O
Diamond = Mohs 10, glass = 5-6
Aqua regia = 3:1 HCl:HNO₃, dissolves Au and Pt only

mains.contemporary_relevance: HF glass etching fundamental to semiconductor chip manufacturing and decorative glass.

contrast_with: HF (attacks silicon/glass chemically) vs aqua regia (attacks noble metals) — different reactive targets

question: Which one of the following is not a member of the Shanghai Cooperation Organization?
subject: International Relations

prelims.trap_factor: All options are post-Soviet states — hard to distinguish without specific knowledge. Russia, Kazakhstan, Uzbekistan are original SCO members. Ukraine's European orientation is the key differentiator.
prelims.elimination_hint: SCO = China + Russia + Central Asian states. Original members (2001): China, Russia, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Uzbekistan. Ukraine oriented toward Europe/NATO, never joined SCO. Answer: C (Ukraine).
factual_hooks: SCO founded June 2001
Original 6: China, Russia, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Uzbekistan
India + Pakistan joined 2017 (Astana Summit)
Ukraine = EU/NATO orientation, never SCO member
Iran joined 2023; Belarus 2024

mains.contemporary_relevance: SCO expansion significant for India's Central Asia connectivity; India's SCO chairmanship 2023 was diplomatically important.

contrast_with: SCO (China-Russia led, Eurasian security/trade) vs NATO (US-led, transatlantic defence) — Ukraine's choice reflects geopolitical orientation

question:
With reference to the steel industry in India in the recent times, consider the following statements:
1. Vizag Steel Plant (RINL) has been declared Mini Ratna.
2. Merger of IISCO with SAIL has been completed.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Students unsure of PSU classification tiers. RINL being 'Mini Ratna' (not Navratna) for a large steel plant seems counter-intuitive. IISCO-SAIL merger is specific corporate fact.
prelims.elimination_hint: RINL = Rashtriya Ispat Nigam Limited = Vizag Steel = declared Mini Ratna. IISCO = Indian Iron & Steel Co., Burnpur (WB) formally merged into SAIL in 2006. Both statements correct. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: RINL = Vizag Steel Plant (only integrated coastal steel plant)
Mini Ratna = PSU with some operational autonomy
IISCO = Burnpur (WB), merged with SAIL 2006
SAIL = Steel Authority of India (Maharatna PSU)

mains.contemporary_relevance: RINL privatization attempted 2021-22 but stalled; debate about strategic PSU disinvestment in steel.

prerequisite_concepts: PSU categories: Mini Ratna, Navratna, Maharatna
contrast_with: SAIL (integrated, multiple plants, Maharatna) vs RINL (single coastal plant, Mini Ratna)

question:
With reference to the international meetings held in the year 2006, which of the following pairs is/are correctly matched?
1. NAM Summit : Havana
2. APEC Meeting : Bangkok
3. EU-India Summit : Helsinki
4. UN Climate : Geneva Change Conference
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
subject: International Relations

prelims.trap_factor: APEC 2006 location (Hanoi, Vietnam) confused with Bangkok (Thailand). UN Climate 2006 = Nairobi, Kenya (COP12) NOT Geneva. Only NAM=Havana and EU-India=Helsinki correct.
prelims.elimination_hint: NAM 2006 = Havana, Cuba (14th NAM Summit) ✓. EU-India 2006 = Helsinki (Finnish EU Presidency) ✓. APEC 2006 = Hanoi, Vietnam ✗. UNFCCC COP12 = Nairobi ✗ (not Geneva). Answer: B (pairs 1 and 3).
factual_hooks: NAM Summit 2006 = Havana, Cuba (14th Summit)
EU-India Summit 2006 = Helsinki (Finnish EU Presidency H2 2006)
APEC 2006 = Hanoi, Vietnam
UNFCCC COP12 2006 = Nairobi (first African COP)

contrast_with: APEC (Asia-Pacific, includes USA, economic focus) vs SCO (Eurasian security, excludes USA)

question: A rectangular water tank measures 15 m x 6 m at top and is 10 m deep. It is full of water. If water is drawn out lowering the level by 1 metre, how much of water has been drawn out ?
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Test-takers often compute 15×6×10 (full tank volume) and then divide by 10, giving 45,000 litres — forgetting that only 1m depth is drawn, not the full 10m.
prelims.elimination_hint: Volume drawn = 15 × 6 × 1 = 90 m³. Convert: 1 m³ = 1000 litres → 90,000 litres. Eliminate options that use full depth or wrong unit conversion.
factual_hooks: 1 cubic metre = 1000 litres
volume = length × breadth × depth

contrast_with: Pipes & Cisterns — time-based vs volume-based framing

question:
Solve the given equations : x^2 + y^2 = 34
x^4 - y^4 = 544 The values of x and y are
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: x⁴−y⁴ can be factored as (x²+y²)(x²−y²); skipping this step leads to brute-force substitution errors. Also, negative values like ±3,±5 get confused with ±5,±3.
prelims.elimination_hint: Factor: (x²+y²)(x²−y²)=544 → 34(x²−y²)=544 → x²−y²=16. Combine with x²+y²=34 → x²=25, y²=9 → x=±5, y=±3.
factual_hooks: Difference of squares: a²−b²=(a+b)(a−b)
System of equations with even powers

contrast_with: Linear equations — direct substitution vs polynomial factoring

question: A worker reaches his factory 3 minutes late if his speed from his house to the factory is 5 km/hr. If he walks at a speed of 6 km/hr, then he reaches the factory 7 minutes early. The distance of the factory from his house is
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Combining 3 minutes late and 7 minutes early as a difference of 10 minutes is correct, but many students set up the equation as d/5 − d/6 = 3/60 (only using 3 min) giving wrong answer.
prelims.elimination_hint: Total time difference = 3+7 = 10 min = 1/6 hr. Equation: d/5 − d/6 = 1/6 → d(1/30) = 1/6 → d = 5 km. Verify: at 5 km/h takes 60 min; at 6 km/h takes 50 min — difference = 10 min ✓
factual_hooks: d/s = time formula
When late+early, add both times for total difference

contrast_with: Relative speed problems — involve two moving objects, not one with two speeds

question:
Match List I (Ethnic Community) with List II (Country ) and select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists
(Ethnic community) | (Country)
A. Apatani | 1. China
B. Dayak | 2. India
C. Dinka | 3. Indonesia
D. Uighur | 4. Sudan
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Dayak people live in Borneo which spans both Indonesia and Malaysia; Uighur-China link is well-known but some confuse them with Central Asian countries. Dinka-Sudan link is confused with Ethiopia.
prelims.elimination_hint: Lock Uighur=China (highly newsworthy) and Dinka=Sudan/South Sudan first. Apatani are from Arunachal Pradesh (India). Dayak are from Borneo=Indonesia. Use elimination once 2-3 are confirmed.
factual_hooks: Apatani: Arunachal Pradesh, India — known for facial tattoos
Dayak: Borneo/Indonesia — sea-faring indigenous
Dinka: South Sudan — largest ethnic group there
Uighur: Xinjiang, China — Turkic Muslim minority

mains.contemporary_relevance: Uighur issue remains a major human rights and geopolitical concern involving China and western nations.

contrast_with: Maasai — East Africa (Kenya/Tanzania) vs Dinka — South Sudan

question: A conveyer belt delivers baggage at the rate of 3 tons in 5 minutes, and a second conveyer belt delivers baggage at the rate of 1 ton in 2 minutes. How much time will it take to get 33 tons of baggage delivered using both the conveyer belts ?
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Students add rates incorrectly as 3+1=4 tons per 5+2=7 minutes. Must convert to common unit (per minute) before adding.
prelims.elimination_hint: Rate1 = 3/5 tons/min = 0.6; Rate2 = 1/2 tons/min = 0.5; Combined = 1.1 tons/min. Time = 33/1.1 = 30 minutes exactly.
factual_hooks: Work rate addition: R_total = R1 + R2
Always convert to same unit before adding rates

contrast_with: Pipes & Cisterns — one fills, one empties; here both add

question: Water is filled in a container in such a manner that its volume doubles after every five minutes. If takes 30 minutes for the container to be full, in how much time will it be one-fourth full ?
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Students work forward (1/4 full at 7.5 or 10 min) instead of backward from full. If full at 30 min, half-full at 25 min, quarter-full at 20 min — work backward.
prelims.elimination_hint: Think backward: full at t=30; halved each step backward means half-full at t=25, quarter-full at t=20. The answer is 20 minutes.
factual_hooks: Doubling time: reverse means halving each step back
1/4 = two halvings backward from full

contrast_with: Arithmetic growth — linear vs geometric/exponential growth pattern

question: A city has a population of 3,00,000 out of which 1,80,000 are males. 50% of the population is literate. If 70% of the males are literate, the number of literate females is
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Some students compute 50% of 120,000 females directly = 60,000 (treating female literacy rate as 50%) instead of using total literate minus literate males.
factual_hooks: Total literate = overall rate × total population
Literate females = Total literate − Literate males

contrast_with: Directly applying female literacy rate — incorrect since female rate isn't given

question: In a survey, it was found that 80% of those surveyed owned a car while 60% of those surveyed owned a mobile phone. If 55% owned both a car and a mobile phone, what per cent of those surveyed owned a car or a mobile phone or both ?
subject: CSAT
aspect: HOW – Set Theory and Venn Diagrams

prelims.trap_factor: Many pick 80% thinking 'car owners' is already the maximum; they forget to add phone-only owners and subtract double-counted 'both' group.
prelims.elimination_hint: P(A∪B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A∩B) = 80 + 60 − 55 = 85%. Standard Venn diagram formula.
factual_hooks: P(A∪B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A∩B)
Always subtract the intersection to avoid double counting

contrast_with: Mutually exclusive events — P(A∩B)=0, so union = simple addition

question: In the figure below, ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral, AB = BC and angle BAC = 70°, then angle ADC is
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Students often confuse angle in the same segment theorem with the opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral. AB=BC creates an isosceles triangle trap where BAC≠BCA is assumed.
prelims.elimination_hint: AB=BC → △ABC isosceles → ∠BAC=∠BCA=70°. So ∠ABC=180−140=40°. Opposite angles in cyclic quad sum to 180°: ∠ADC=180−40=140°.
factual_hooks: Opposite angles of cyclic quadrilateral sum to 180°
Isosceles triangle: base angles equal

contrast_with: Inscribed angle theorem — angle at centre vs angle at circumference

question: Among the following cities, which one is at the highest altitude above mean sea level?
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Delhi is India's capital and seems prominent; Jodhpur feels elevated due to desert/hill imagery. Nagpur is geographic centre of India. Bangalore on Deccan Plateau (~920m) is easily overlooked.
prelims.elimination_hint: Deccan Plateau = elevated (600-900m). Bangalore (~920m) >> Nagpur (~310m) >> Jodhpur (~231m) >> Delhi (~216m). Remember Bangalore sits on the highest part of the Deccan Plateau.
factual_hooks: Bangalore: ~920m above sea level on Deccan Plateau
Delhi: ~216m, the lowest among options
Deccan Plateau avg elevation: 600-900m

mains.contemporary_relevance: Bangalore's elevation contributes to its pleasant climate, making it a tech hub — relevant to urban geography discussions.

contrast_with: Shimla/Manali in Himalayas — much higher but not among options

question:
With reference to Visible Light Communication (VLC) technology, which of the following statements are correct?

1. VLC uses electromagnetic spectrum wavelengths 375 to 780 nm.
2. VLC is known as long-range optical wireless communication.
3. VLC can transmit large amounts of data faster than Bluetooth.
4. VLC has no electromagnetic interference.

Select the correct answer using the code given below:
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Statement 2 says VLC is 'long-range' which sounds plausible for modern tech, but VLC is explicitly SHORT-range (limited by line-of-sight and light intensity). This is the key trap.
prelims.elimination_hint: VLC uses visible light (375-780nm) ✓ and has no EM interference ✓. But VLC is short-range (line of sight, blocked by walls), not long-range. Eliminate options containing statement 2 (A,B,D). Answer: C (1,3,4).
factual_hooks: VLC uses 375-780nm (visible light spectrum)
VLC is short-range, line-of-sight technology
No electromagnetic interference — uses light not radio waves
Faster data transfer than Bluetooth possible

mains.contemporary_relevance: VLC/Li-Fi being explored as alternative to Wi-Fi in secure environments like hospitals and aircraft.

contrast_with: Radio waves — long range, penetrate walls vs VLC — short range, blocked by walls

question: Who among the following was never the Lok Sabha Speaker?
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: G.S. Dhillon, Baliram Bhagat, Hukum Singh are all real Lok Sabha Speakers. K.V.K. Sundaram is a real IAS officer and Chief Election Commissioner, never Lok Sabha Speaker — his name sounds like a parliamentary figure.
prelims.elimination_hint: Know actual speakers list: Mavalankar, Ananthasayanam, Hukum Singh, Dhillon, Bhagat... K.V.K. Sundaram was Chief Election Commissioner (1958-67), not Lok Sabha Speaker.
factual_hooks: K.V.K. Sundaram: Chief Election Commissioner, NOT Lok Sabha Speaker
Hukum Singh: Speaker 1962-67
G.S. Dhillon: Speaker 1969-75
Baliram Bhagat: Speaker 1976-77

mains.contemporary_relevance: Role of Lok Sabha Speaker remains contested in anti-defection disputes and parliamentary adjournments.

prerequisite_concepts: Constitutional position of Lok Sabha Speaker
contrast_with: Rajya Sabha Chairman — is Vice President ex-officio vs Lok Sabha Speaker elected by members

question: The original home of the gypsies was
subject: Society

prelims.trap_factor: The word 'gypsy' derives from 'Egyptian' — a common medieval European belief — making Egypt seem correct. Russia is plausible due to large Romani population in Eastern Europe.
prelims.elimination_hint: Linguistic and genetic studies conclusively place Romani origins in Northwest India (Punjab/Sindh). The Egyptian etymology is a misnomer. Eliminate Egypt (etymology trap) and Russia (settlement, not origin).
factual_hooks: Romani/Gypsies: originated from NW India (Punjab-Sindh region)
Word 'gypsy' = misnomer from 'Egyptian'
Romani language related to Sanskrit/Hindi
Genetic studies confirm South Asian origin

mains.contemporary_relevance: Romani rights remain a significant human rights issue in Europe with debates on inclusion and discrimination.

contrast_with: Sinti (German Romani) vs Roma (Balkan Romani) — same origin, different regional names

question:
Which of the following are correctly matched ?
I. Hamas—Palestinian extremist group
II. Sinn Fein—IRA’s political wing
III. True Path Party—A major constituent of the ruling coalition in Turkey
Choose the correct answer from the codes given below : Codes :
subject: International Relations

prelims.trap_factor: Sinn Féin–IRA link is often questioned (some say Sinn Féin is separate); True Path Party (DYP) of Turkey is obscure — students may confuse it with AKP or MHP.
prelims.elimination_hint: Hamas as Palestinian group ✓. Sinn Féin as IRA's political wing ✓ (historically correct pre-Good Friday). True Path Party was indeed in Turkish coalition (Çiller govt 1990s) ✓. All three correct → C.
factual_hooks: Hamas: Islamist Palestinian movement, founded 1987
Sinn Féin: political wing of IRA (pre-Good Friday Agreement 1998)
True Path Party (DYP): centre-right Turkish party, PM Tansu Çiller's coalition in 1990s

mains.contemporary_relevance: Hamas–Israel conflict remains a defining global geopolitical issue; Good Friday Agreement remains a model for conflict resolution.

contrast_with: Hamas vs Fatah — Islamist vs secular Palestinian factions

question: The normal temperature of human body on the Kelvin scale is
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Students confuse the conversion: some add 273 to Fahrenheit (98.6°F), others mistakenly use 37+260=297 or 37+270=307, arriving at 300K.
prelims.elimination_hint: K = °C + 273. Normal body temperature = 37°C. 37 + 273 = 310K. Eliminate 300K (uses 27°C=room temp), 280K and 290K (too cold).
factual_hooks: Normal body temperature = 37°C = 310K = 98.6°F
Room temperature ≈ 27°C = 300K

contrast_with: Fahrenheit scale — 98.6°F (not 37°F) for body temperature

question: A simple machine helps a person doing
option_b: the same amount of work with lesser force.
option_d: the same amount of work much faster.
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Option A ('less work') is tempting because machines seem to 'save' effort. Option D ('much faster') is tempting for modern context. The key principle: machines don't reduce work, they redistribute force over distance.
prelims.elimination_hint: Work = Force × Distance. Simple machines trade force for distance while keeping work constant. Eliminates A (less work), C (same work without context), D (not faster necessarily). Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Simple machines: lever, pulley, inclined plane, wheel, wedge, screw
No machine increases total work — work input ≥ work output
Mechanical advantage: less force over greater distance

contrast_with: Motor/engine — converts energy form vs simple machine — redirects force

question: About 50% of the world population is concentrated between the latitudes of
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: 5°N–20°N seems correct as it includes tropical India/SE Asia/Africa; 40°N–60°N seems correct as it includes all of Europe. The actual answer 20°N–40°N covers China, India's most populated belt, USA, and Europe.
prelims.elimination_hint: 20°N–40°N covers: North China, Indo-Gangetic plain, Bangladesh, Pakistan, North Africa coast, USA, Southern Europe — together these hold >50% of world population. Eliminate equatorial belt (too sparse in Amazon/Congo) and high northern latitudes.
factual_hooks: 20°N–40°N: densest human habitation belt
Includes: China's heartland, India's north, USA, Mediterranean Europe
World population concentration follows monsoon + temperate zones

mains.contemporary_relevance: Demographic shifts — China and India together hold ~35% of world population within this belt.

contrast_with: 5°N–20°N — tropical belt, less densely settled due to heat/humidity in parts

question: After consolidating his power, Balban assumed the grand title of
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Din-i-Ilahi (Akbar's syncretic religion) and Zil-i-Ilahi both sound similar and divine. Tute-i-Hind ('Parrot of India') is Amir Khusrau's title. Students confuse these between Balban and Akbar.
prelims.elimination_hint: Balban's title: 'Zil-i-Ilahi' = Shadow of God on Earth — reflects his divine kingship theory. Tute-i-Hind = Amir Khusrau (poet). Din-i-Ilahi = Akbar (religion). Kaisr-i-Hind = British medal (Mahatma Gandhi returned it).
factual_hooks: Zil-i-Ilahi = Shadow of God on Earth — Balban's claim to divine authority
Balban's theory: king is god's representative, established court protocol (sijda, paibos)
Tute-i-Hind = Amir Khusrau

contrast_with: Akbar's Din-i-Ilahi — attempted religious synthesis vs Balban's divine kingship — political authority claim

question: The average rate of domestic savings (gross) for the Indian economy is currently estimated to be in the range of
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: This is a time-specific factual question (1997 data). The 'currently estimated' in 1997 = 20-25%; by 2007 it had risen to ~32-34%. Modern students applying current data pick option D.
prelims.elimination_hint: This question is dated (1997); the answer B (20-25%) reflected India's savings rate in the late 1990s. Modern savings rate ~30-35% (option D). Flag as historically-contextual — note year before answering.
factual_hooks: India's gross domestic savings rate 1997: ~20-24% of GDP
India's savings rate peaked ~36% in 2008, now ~30%
High savings rate supports investment and growth

mains.contemporary_relevance: India's savings rate decline post-2012 is a concern for sustaining investment-led growth.

contrast_with: Household savings vs government savings vs corporate savings — components of national savings

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer by using the codes given below the lists
List I | List II
I. Butler Committee Report | (A) Jallianwala Bagh Massacre
II. Hurtog Committee Report | (B) Relationship between the Indian States and Paramount Power
III. Hunter Inquiry Committee Report | (C) Working of Dyarchy as laid down in the Montague-Chelmsford Reforms
IV. Muddiman Committee Report | (D) The growth of education in British India and potentialities of its further progress
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Butler Committee (Indian States) vs Muddiman Committee (Dyarchy) gets swapped. Hunter Committee for Jallianwala Bagh vs Hunter Education Commission (earlier, 1882) creates confusion — two different Hunter committees.
prelims.elimination_hint: Lock Hunter=Jallianwala Bagh (1919-20) first — most memorable. Then Butler=Indian States & Paramount Power (1927). Hartog=Education (1929). Muddiman=Dyarchy review. Answer: I-B, II-D, III-A, IV-C.
factual_hooks: Hunter Inquiry Committee (1919): investigated Jallianwala Bagh massacre
Butler Committee (1927): relationship between Indian States and Paramount Power
Hartog Committee (1929): growth of education in British India
Muddiman Committee (1924): working of Dyarchy under Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms

contrast_with: Simon Commission (1927) — constitutional review vs Butler (1927) — princely states specifically

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer by using the codes given below the lists
List I (Crops) | List II (Geographical conditions)
I. Barley | (A) Hot and dry climate with poor soil
II. Rice | (B) Cool climate with poorer soil
III. Millets | (C) Warm and moist climate with high altitude
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Rice=moist climate is correct but 'high altitude' element confuses — students expect rice in plains/deltas. Barley thriving in 'cool with poorer soil' surprises those who associate barley with cold Himalayan regions only.
prelims.elimination_hint: Barley = tolerates cool, poor, dry soils (B). Rice = warm, moist, can grow on higher altitude terraces too (D). Millets = dry/arid with poor soil (A). Match these → Answer A: I-B, II-D, III-A.
factual_hooks: Barley: hardy crop, cool climate, poor soils, used in beer/roti
Rice: requires standing water, warm-humid, flooded paddies
Millets (bajra, jowar): drought-resistant, poor soils, hot climate

mains.contemporary_relevance: Climate-resilient millets (UPSC millet year 2023) being promoted as sustainable alternative to water-intensive rice and wheat.

contrast_with: Wheat vs Barley — both rabi, but wheat needs better soil and more moisture than barley

question:
In a town 25% families own a phone and 15% own a car. 65% families own neither a phone nor a car. 2000 families own both a car and a phone. Consider the following statements in this regard:
I.
10% families own both a car and a phone.
II.
35% families own either a car or a phone.
III.
40,000 families live in the town.
Which of the above statements are correct ?
subject: CSAT
aspect: HOW – Set Theory and Venn Diagrams

prelims.trap_factor: Statement I says 10% own both — but the correct 'both' is 5% (25+15−35=5%). Statement III says 40,000 total — this is correct (5%=2000 → total=40,000). Statement I is the deliberate wrong statement.
prelims.elimination_hint: P(phone∪car)=100−65=35%. P(both)=25+15−35=5%. If 5%=2000 families, total=40,000 ✓ (III). 35% own either ✓ (II). But 10% own both is wrong (I). So II and III only = C.
factual_hooks: P(neither)=65% → P(union)=35%
If 5%=2000, total population=40,000

contrast_with: P(union) ≠ P(A)+P(B) unless mutually exclusive

question:
Match List I (Country) with List II (President) and select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists
(Country) | (President)
A. Congo | 1. Levy Mwanawasa
B. Nigeria | 2. Joseph Kabila
C. Uganda | 3. Olusegum Obasanjo
D. Zambia | 4. Yoweri Museveni
subject: International Relations

prelims.trap_factor: All four leaders are real but obscure — test-takers confuse Nigeria's Obasanjo (military background turned civilian president) with other military rulers. Zambia's Mwanawasa is least known.
prelims.elimination_hint: Lock Congo=Joseph Kabila (came to power 2001 after father's assassination — high profile news) and Uganda=Yoweri Museveni (long-serving, well-known). Nigeria=Obasanjo (prominent). Zambia=Mwanawasa by elimination.
factual_hooks: Joseph Kabila: became DRC president in 2001 after Laurent Kabila's assassination
Olusegun Obasanjo: Nigerian president 1999-2007 (previously military ruler 1976-79)
Yoweri Museveni: Uganda president since 1986
Levy Mwanawasa: Zambia president 2002-2008

mains.contemporary_relevance: DRC continues to face instability; current leadership context different but regional dynamics persist.

contrast_with: Congo-Brazzaville vs DRC (Congo-Kinshasa) — two different 'Congo' countries

question: Who among the following was selected for the 2001 Dada Saheb Phalke Award?
subject: Governance

prelims.trap_factor: Lata Mangeshkar is the most famous Indian cultural icon and was highly prominent in 2001 (received Bharat Ratna that year), making her a strong distractor for this award in the same year.
prelims.elimination_hint: Lata Mangeshkar received Bharat Ratna in 2001, NOT Phalke Award (she received Phalke in 1989). Dev Anand received Phalke in 2002. 2001 Phalke Award = Yash Chopra. Year-Award-Person mapping required.
factual_hooks: Yash Chopra: Dadasaheb Phalke Award 2001 (49th National Film Awards)
Lata Mangeshkar: Dadasaheb Phalke 1989, Bharat Ratna 2001
Dadasaheb Phalke: India's highest film honour, given annually

contrast_with: National Award for Best Film vs Phalke Award — one is annual best-film; other is lifetime achievement

question:
Match List I and List II and select the correct answer using the code given below the lists
(Famous Person) | (Well-Known as)
A. Anna Hazare | 1. Lawyer
B. Deepak Parekh | 2. Banker
C. GVK Reddy | 3. Gandhian and Social activist
D. Harish Salve | 4. Industrialist
subject: Governance

prelims.trap_factor: Deepak Parekh (HDFC) and GVK Reddy (industrialist) are often confused — both are business figures. Harish Salve is commonly mistaken for a politician; Anna Hazare's correct description as 'Gandhian' not 'activist/politician' matters.
prelims.elimination_hint: Lock Anna Hazare=Gandhian (3) and Harish Salve=Lawyer (1) first. Then Deepak Parekh=Banker (HDFC Chairman) (2). GVK Reddy=Industrialist (GVK Group) (4). Answer C: A-3, B-2, C-4, D-1.
factual_hooks: Anna Hazare: Gandhian social activist, led 2011 anti-corruption movement
Deepak Parekh: Chairman HDFC Bank, prominent banker
GVK Reddy: Chairman GVK Group (infrastructure, airports, energy)
Harish Salve: Senior Advocate, appeared in Kulbhushan Jadhav ICJ case

mains.contemporary_relevance: Harish Salve's role in Kulbhushan Jadhav case (ICJ) remains relevant to India's international legal strategy.

contrast_with: Harish Salve (lawyer) vs P. Chidambaram (lawyer-turned-politician) — different career arcs

question: P, Q, R' and S are four men. P is the oldest but not the poorest. R is the richest but not the oldest. Q is older than S but not than P or R. P is richer than Q but not than S. The four men can be ordered (descending) in respect of age and richness, respectively, as
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Two separate orderings (age and richness) on four people creates cognitive overload. Students often mix clues across the two orderings or forget P is oldest (not R, who is richest).
prelims.elimination_hint: AGE: P oldest → P first. R not oldest but Q<R in age? Clue: Q not older than R → R>Q. So P>R>Q>S = PRQS. RICHNESS: R richest → R first. P richer than Q but not S → S>P>Q. R,S,P,Q = RSPQ. Answer B.
factual_hooks: Two-parameter ranking: solve each dimension separately
Read each clue carefully — 'not older than' = equal or younger

contrast_with: Seating arrangement — circular vs linear ordering problems

question: The member of Shivaji’s Astha Pradhana who looked after foreign affairs was
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Peshwa (PM) is the most remembered Ashta Pradhan minister; students guess Peshwa for foreign affairs. Sachiv (secretary) sounds administrative. Sumant/Dabir is the least-known minister name.
prelims.elimination_hint: Peshwa = Prime Minister. Sachiv = correspondence/letters. Pandit Rao = religious affairs. Sumant = foreign affairs/protocol (also called Dabir). Senapati = military. Lock Peshwa=PM first, then Sumant=Foreign by elimination.
factual_hooks: Sumant (Dabir): Shivaji's foreign affairs minister in Ashta Pradhan
Peshwa: PM of Maratha council
Pandit Rao: head of religious/charity affairs
Senapati: commander-in-chief

contrast_with: Mughal mansabdari system — rank-based vs Maratha Ashta Pradhan — functional ministries

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer by using the codes given below the lists
List I (Special Characteristic) | List II (Name of Planet)
I. Farthest planet from the sun | (A) Mercury
II. Largest planet of the solar system | (B) Venus
III. Planet second from the Sun in the solar system | (C) Jupiter
IV. Planet nearest to the Sun | (D) Pluto
| (E) Saturn
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Pluto was a planet in 1998 (question year) but was reclassified as dwarf planet in 2006 — students with modern knowledge may skip Pluto as farthest. Also Jupiter vs Saturn as 'largest' confuses some.
prelims.elimination_hint: In 1998 context: Farthest=Pluto (D), Largest=Jupiter (C), Second from Sun=Venus (B), Nearest=Mercury (A). Match: I-D, II-C, III-B, IV-A. Note: Neptune is now considered farthest after Pluto's reclassification.
factual_hooks: Mercury: closest to Sun; Venus: 2nd planet
Jupiter: largest planet (318× Earth's mass)
Pluto: farthest (as of 1998); reclassified as dwarf planet 2006
Saturn: 2nd largest, known for rings

mains.contemporary_relevance: India's Aditya-L1 solar mission and Mars/Moon missions make astronomy highly relevant to UPSC.

contrast_with: Jupiter vs Saturn — both gas giants, but Jupiter is larger; Saturn has prominent rings

question: An air bubble in water will act like a
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Intuition says a bubble (curved like a lens) should behave like a convex lens. But it's filled with less-dense air inside more-dense water — a rarer medium inside denser medium acts as diverging (concave) lens.
prelims.elimination_hint: Refractive index: water > air. A bubble is air (low n) surrounded by water (high n). Light entering a less-dense centre from denser medium diverges — same as concave lens. Contrast with glass (high n) in air = convex behaviour.
factual_hooks: Air bubble in water: rarer medium inside denser → acts as concave lens (diverging)
Glass lens in air: denser medium inside rarer → acts as convex lens (converging)
Refractive index of water (~1.33) > air (~1.0)

contrast_with: Glass lens in water — both have similar refractive index so lensing effect reduced

question: Prem Vatika, poems on the life of Krishna, was composed by
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Surdas wrote extensively on Krishna (Sur Sagar) making him the obvious choice. Raskhan — a Muslim devotee of Krishna — is the correct answer but is lesser-known among UPSC aspirants.
prelims.elimination_hint: Prem Vatika = Raskhan (Muslim poet, devotee of Krishna, wrote 52 verses). Sur Sagar = Surdas. Bihari Satsai = Bihari. Kabir = Dohas (monotheist, no Krishna-specific devotion). Raskhan's uniqueness: Muslim writing bhakti poetry on Krishna.
factual_hooks: Raskhan: Muslim Bhakti poet, devoted to Krishna, wrote Prem Vatika and Sujanranjana
Sur Sagar: Surdas (blind poet, disciple of Vallabhacharya)
Raskhan: Said 'Maanush hau to wohi Raskhan basio braj gokul gaon ke gwaran'

contrast_with: Nirguna Bhakti (Kabir, Nanak) — formless God vs Saguna Bhakti (Raskhan, Surdas) — God with form

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer by using the codes given below the lists.
List I | List II
I. European transcontinental railway | (A) Paris to Istanbul
II. Trans-Andean railway | (B) Leningrad to Vladivostok
III. Trans-Siberian railway | (C) Leningrad to Volgograd
IV. Orient Express | (D) Buenos Aires to Valparaiso
| (E) Paris to Warsaw
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Trans-Siberian and Orient Express are commonly confused; students may link Orient Express to Eastern Europe rather than Paris-Istanbul. European Transcontinental Railway is rarely studied and easily mixed with Orient Express.
prelims.elimination_hint: Anchor on certainties: Trans-Siberian is Leningrad to Vladivostok (B), Orient Express is Paris to Istanbul (A). Buenos Aires to Valparaiso (D) is Trans-Andean. Then assign the European Transcontinental to E.
factual_hooks: Trans-Siberian: Leningrad (St. Petersburg) to Vladivostok
Orient Express: Paris to Istanbul
Trans-Andean: Buenos Aires to Valparaiso

prerequisite_concepts: Geography of Eurasia and South America
contrast_with: Trans-Siberian vs Orient Express — both cross Eurasia but different endpoints

question: Which one of the following is the largest mutual fund organisation in India?
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: SBI Mutual Fund is a large bank-sponsored fund and may seem the obvious answer; the question is dated (1995) and UTI's dominance has since ended, making this a historical fact.
prelims.elimination_hint: UTI (Unit Trust of India) was the first and historically largest mutual fund in India until its restructuring post-2003. Eliminate bank-sponsored funds for the pre-2003 era.
factual_hooks: UTI was India's first and largest mutual fund until restructured in 2003
UTI Act 1963 established UTI under RBI supervision

contrast_with: UTI vs SEBI-regulated AMCs — UTI was statutory, others are corporate

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the codes given below the Lists
Characteristic) | (Particle)
I. Zero mass | (A) Positron
II. Fractional charge | (B) Neutrino
III. Fractional spin | (C) Quark
IV. Integral spin | (D) Phonon
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Phonon (D) is a quasi-particle with integral spin, often confused with photon; Positron (A) has spin 1/2 (fractional), not zero mass — students may confuse it with neutrino.
prelims.elimination_hint: Neutrino has near-zero (effectively zero) mass and spin 1/2; Quark has fractional charge (e.g. +2/3, -1/3); Phonon has integral spin (boson); Positron has fractional spin 1/2 but non-zero mass.
factual_hooks: Neutrino: near-zero mass, spin 1/2
Quark: fractional electric charge (+2/3 or -1/3)
Phonon: integral spin (boson, spin 1)

mains.contemporary_relevance: Neutrino research (INO project) is a current S&T topic in UPSC context.

contrast_with: Positron vs Neutrino — both light particles but differ in mass and charge

question:
Match List-I (National Park/Sanctuary) with List-II (state) and select the correct answer using the codes given below the Lists
(National Park Sanctuary) | (State)
A. Kanger Ghati National Park | 1. Chhattisgarh
B. Nagerhole National Park | 2. Haryana
C. Kugti Wildlife Sanctuary | 3. Himachal Pradesh
D. Sultanpur Bird Sanctuary | 4. Karnataka
subject: Environment

prelims.trap_factor: Sultanpur Bird Sanctuary is in Haryana (not Karnataka); Kugti Wildlife Sanctuary is in Himachal Pradesh (not Chhattisgarh). State-park matching is a classic UPSC trap.
prelims.elimination_hint: Kanger Ghati is exclusively in Chhattisgarh (anchor point). Nagarhole is in Karnataka (4). Sultanpur is in Haryana (2). Kugti is in Himachal Pradesh (3). Answer: A-1, B-4, C-3, D-2 = option B.
factual_hooks: Kanger Ghati NP: Chhattisgarh (only NP there)
Nagarhole NP: Karnataka
Sultanpur Bird Sanctuary: Haryana

mains.contemporary_relevance: Conservation of biodiversity hotspots remains a recurring UPSC theme.

contrast_with: National Park vs Wildlife Sanctuary — NP has stricter protection

question:
Consider the following statements:
1. Regarding the procurement of food grains, Government of India follows a procurement target rather than an open-ended procurement policy.
2. Government of India announces minimum support prices only for cereals.
3. For distribution under Targeted Public Distribution System (TPDS), wheat and rice are issued by the Government of India at uniform Central issue prices to the States/ Union Territories.
Which of the statements given above is/ are correct?
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Statement 1 is false — India follows open-ended procurement (not target-based). Statement 2 is false — MSP covers many crops beyond cereals. Students who confuse MSP scope often mark 1 and 2 as correct.
prelims.elimination_hint: Statement 3 is the sure-correct one: uniform Central Issue Price for wheat and rice under TPDS is a well-known fact. Eliminate options without 3 (A, B). Between C and D, statement 1 is false (open-ended procurement), so answer is D.
factual_hooks: India follows open-ended procurement, not target-based
MSP covers 23 crops, not just cereals
Central Issue Price for TPDS is uniform across states

mains.contemporary_relevance: MSP and food security debates remain highly relevant in current policy discourse.

contrast_with: Open-ended procurement vs procurement target — India uses former

question: In the well-known Lawn Tennis doubles team, Max Mirnyi—the partner of Mahesh Bhupati, comes from which of the following countries?
subject: Governance

prelims.trap_factor: This is a highly specific current-affairs question from 2004 about a sports doubles team. Belarus is easily confused with Baltic states (Estonia, Latvia) or Russia.
prelims.elimination_hint: Max Mirnyi is a well-known Belarusian tennis player. Eliminate Italy, Sweden, Croatia by process of elimination if Belarus is known as a former Soviet republic producing tennis talent.
factual_hooks: Max Mirnyi is Belarusian
Mahesh Bhupathi is a prominent Indian doubles tennis player

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the code given below the Lists
(Person) | (Position/Organisation)
A. Nancy Pelosi | 1. WTO
B. Margaret Chan | 2. Speaker, U.S. House of Representatives
C. Steve Ballmer | 3. WHO
| 4. Microsoft
subject: International Relations

prelims.trap_factor: Margaret Chan is associated with WHO, not WTO — a common confusion given the similar acronyms. Nancy Pelosi's role as Speaker is often confused with Senate leadership.
prelims.elimination_hint: Anchor: Nancy Pelosi = Speaker, US House of Representatives (2); Steve Ballmer = Microsoft (4); Margaret Chan = WHO (3). Then assign remaining: answer is A-2, B-3, C-4.
factual_hooks: Nancy Pelosi: Speaker, US House of Representatives (2007)
Margaret Chan: WHO Director-General
Steve Ballmer: CEO of Microsoft

contrast_with: WHO vs WTO — health vs trade organizations

question: Which one of the following is the largest (areawise) Lok Sabha constituency?
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Kachchh is large geographically but is not a Lok Sabha constituency in the same sense as Ladakh. Students confuse district/state area with constituency area.
prelims.elimination_hint: Ladakh (J&K) is the largest Lok Sabha constituency by area — it covers the entire Ladakh region. This is a well-known fact used as an anchor to eliminate others.
factual_hooks: Ladakh is the largest Lok Sabha constituency by area
Malkajgiri (Telangana) is among the largest by voter population

mains.contemporary_relevance: Ladakh's UT status (2019) makes this even more relevant.

contrast_with: Largest by area (Ladakh) vs largest by electorate (Malkajgiri)

question:
In a carrom board game competition, m boys and n girls (m > n > 1) of a school participate in which every student has to play exactly one game with every other student. Out of the total games played, it was found that in 221 games one player was a boy and the other-player was a girl. Consider the following statements :
1. The total number of students that participated in the competition is 30.
2. The number of games in which both players were girls is 78.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct ?
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Students may set up mn = 221 directly but 221 = 13×17, so m=17, n=13. Statement 2 requires nC2 = 13×12/2 = 78 — students often forget to verify both statements.
prelims.elimination_hint: m×n = 221 = 13×17 (since m>n>1). Total students = 13+17 = 30 ✓. Girls-only games = C(13,2) = 78 ✓. Both statements correct, answer C.
factual_hooks: nC2 for 13 girls = 78 games
Total players = 30

question:
A cuboid has six sides of different colours. The red side is opposite to black. The blue side is adjacent to white. The brown side is adjacent to blue. The red side is face down.
Which one of the following would be the opposite to brown?
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Students often confuse 'adjacent' with 'opposite'. The cuboid has 3 pairs of opposite faces; given red-black and the positional clues, deriving brown's opposite requires careful spatial reasoning.
prelims.elimination_hint: Red (down) opposite Black (up). Blue adjacent to white; brown adjacent to blue. The face opposite brown must be white (since blue-brown-white form one axis and red-black form another). Answer: C (White).
factual_hooks: On a cuboid, each face has exactly one opposite face
Adjacent means sharing an edge, not opposing

contrast_with: Adjacent vs Opposite faces on a cuboid

question:
With reference to the history of ancient India, which of the following statements is/are correct?

1. Mitakshara was the civil law for upper castes and Dayabhaga was the civil law for lower castes.
2. In the Mitakshara system, the sons can claim right to the property during the lifetime of the father, whereas in the Dayabhaga system, it is only after the death of the father that the sons can claim right to the property.
3. The Mitakshara system deals with the matters related to the property held by male members only of a family, whereas the Dayabhaga system deals with the matters related to the property held by both male and female members of a family.

Select the correct answer using the code given below.
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Statement 1 is false — Mitakshara and Dayabhaga are NOT divided by caste; they are regional schools (Mitakshara: most of India; Dayabhaga: Bengal/Assam). Statement 3 is also false — Dayabhaga does not specifically cover female members.
prelims.elimination_hint: Only Statement 2 is correct: under Mitakshara, sons get coparcenary rights by birth (during father's lifetime); under Dayabhaga, rights arise only after father's death. Answer: B (2 only).
factual_hooks: Mitakshara: sons acquire rights by birth (coparcenary)
Dayabhaga: rights arise only on father's death
Mitakshara applies in most of India; Dayabhaga in Bengal/Assam

mains.contemporary_relevance: Hindu succession law reforms remain relevant to gender justice debates.

contrast_with: Mitakshara vs Dayabhaga — birth-based vs death-based inheritance

question: Who among the following is the president of the FICCI?
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: This is a highly dated current-affairs question (2005) about the FICCI president — such questions are not recurring and the answer changes every year.
prelims.elimination_hint: This is a memorization-based current affairs question from 2005. No elimination strategy works; only direct recall helps. Skip if unsure.
factual_hooks: FICCI president in 2005 was Onkar S. Kunwar
FICCI is Federation of Indian Chambers of Commerce & Industry

question: One local and another express train were proceeding in the same direction on parallel tracks at 29 km/hour and 65 km/hour respectively. The driver of the former noticed that it took exactly 16 seconds for the faster train to pass by him. What is the length of the faster train?
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Students forget to take relative speed (65-29=36 km/h) for same-direction motion and instead use total speed. Converting km/h to m/s is another error point.
prelims.elimination_hint: Relative speed = 65-29 = 36 km/h = 10 m/s. Length = speed × time = 10 × 16 = 160 m. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Relative speed (same direction) = difference of speeds
Convert km/h to m/s: divide by 3.6

contrast_with: Same direction vs opposite direction relative speed

question: In a family, a couple has a son and a daughter. The age of the father is three times that of his daughter and the age of the son is half of his mother. The wife is nine years younger to her husband and the brother is seven years older than his sister. What is the age of the mother ?
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Multiple variable relationships make students lose track — father's age vs daughter's age and mother's age via son's age. Setting up equations carefully is essential.
prelims.elimination_hint: Let daughter=d, father=3d, son=s, mother=2s, wife=husband-9 → 2s=3d-9, and s=d+7. Solve: s=d+7, 2(d+7)=3d-9 → d=23, s=30, mother=60. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Set up simultaneous equations for age problems
Mother's age = 2 × son's age

question: In the given map, which one of the following pairs of ocean currents is shown?
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: This question references a map that is not available in text form, making it impossible to answer without the original visual. Among the options, Benguela and Guinea are both Atlantic currents near Africa, making option D plausible.
prelims.elimination_hint: Without the map, anchor on geographic logic: Benguela (cold, South Atlantic off SW Africa) and Guinea (warm, tropical Atlantic off W Africa) are both near the African coast — likely shown together.
factual_hooks: Benguela: cold current off SW Africa (South Atlantic)
Guinea Current: warm current off W Africa
Falkland: cold current off E South America

contrast_with: Benguela (cold) vs Guinea (warm) — both near Africa but different temperatures

question: The name by which Asoka is generally referred to in his inscriptions is
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Chakravarti means 'universal ruler' and sounds like a regal title Asoka might use; Dharmadeva sounds plausible given his Buddhist leanings. But inscriptions consistently use 'Priyadarsi' (beloved of the gods).
prelims.elimination_hint: Asoka used 'Priyadarsi' (Devanampiya Piyadassi) in his inscriptions — this is a fundamental Mauryan history fact. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Asoka called himself 'Priyadarsi' (Devanampiya) in inscriptions
The name 'Asoka' rarely appears in inscriptions themselves

contrast_with: Priyadarsi (Asoka's self-reference) vs Chakravarti (a generic royal title)

question:
Who among the following were famous jurists of medieval India ?
I. Vijnanesvara
II. Hemadri
III. Rajasekhara
IV. Jimutavahana
Choose the correct answer from the codes given below : Codes :
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Rajasekhara was a poet/playwright, NOT a jurist. Students may confuse literary figures with legal scholars. Jimutavahana (Dayabhaga) and Vijnanesvara (Mitakshara) are the two most famous jurists.
prelims.elimination_hint: Vijnanesvara (Mitakshara) and Jimutavahana (Dayabhaga) are definitive jurists. Hemadri wrote Chaturvarga Chintamani — a legal/dharmashastra text. Rajasekhara is a poet, not a jurist. Answer: C (I, II, IV).
factual_hooks: Vijnanesvara wrote Mitakshara (commentary on Yajnavalkya Smriti)
Jimutavahana wrote Dayabhaga (Bengal school)
Hemadri wrote Chaturvarga Chintamani

contrast_with: Jurists vs poets/playwrights in medieval India

question: Of the four forms of political protests mentioned below, which one is derived from the name of the person who used it as a political weapon for the first time ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Students may think 'Hartal' or 'Bandh' have eponymous origins. 'Boycott' is derived from Captain Charles Boycott, a land agent in Ireland — this is the only eponymous political protest term.
prelims.elimination_hint: The word 'Boycott' comes from Captain Charles Boycott (1880, Ireland) who was socially ostracized by Irish Land League. The other terms (Gherao, Bandh, Hartal) are Indian in origin and not named after persons. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Boycott derives from Captain Charles Boycott (1880)
'Hartal' is from Sanskrit/Gujarati meaning trade stoppage
Gherao is an Indian labour protest tactic

contrast_with: Boycott (eponymous, Irish origin) vs Hartal/Bandh (Indian origin)

question: Who among the following was a prominent leader of the Congress Socialist Party ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: M.N. Roy is often associated with socialist thought but he left the Congress and founded the Communist Party of India — not the Congress Socialist Party. Acharya Narendra Dev is the correct answer.
prelims.elimination_hint: Congress Socialist Party (1934) was founded by Jayaprakash Narayan and Acharya Narendra Dev within the INC framework. M.N. Roy was a communist (outside INC). Eliminate accordingly. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Congress Socialist Party founded 1934 within INC
Acharya Narendra Dev was its prominent leader along with JP
M.N. Roy founded CPI, not CSP

contrast_with: Congress Socialist Party vs Communist Party of India — both left but different paths

question: Consider the following landmarks in Indian education : (i) Hindu College, Calcutta (ii) University of Calcutta (iii) Adam’s Report (iv) Wood’s Despatch The correct chronological order of these landmarks is
subject: History
