prelims.trap_factor: Adam's Report (1835) and Wood's Despatch (1854) are often swapped in chronology. Hindu College (1817) precedes the University of Calcutta (1857).
prelims.elimination_hint: Chronological order: Hindu College, Calcutta (1817) → Adam's Report (1835) → Wood's Despatch (1854) → University of Calcutta (1857). This matches option A: I, III, IV, II.
factual_hooks: Hindu College Calcutta: 1817
Adam's Report: 1835 (on indigenous education)
Wood's Despatch: 1854 (Magna Carta of Indian education)
University of Calcutta: 1857

contrast_with: Adam's Report (primary education focus) vs Wood's Despatch (university/English education)

question: Which one of the following was a Saiva sect in ancient India ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Ajivika is often confused with Shaivism due to its ascetic traditions, but it is a distinct heterodox sect (not Shaiva). Mattamayura is an obscure Shaiva Tantric sub-sect.
prelims.elimination_hint: Ajivika is a heterodox sect (fatalist), not Shaiva. Mayamata and Isanasivagurudevapaddhati are texts on architecture/sculpture, not sects. Mattamayura is a Shaiva Tantric sect. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Mattamayura is a Shaiva Tantric sect (Kashmir Shaivism branch)
Ajivika was a fatalist heterodox sect led by Makkhali Gosala
Pasupata is the oldest Shaiva sect

contrast_with: Shaiva sects vs Vaishnava sects in ancient India

question: Living organisms require at least 27 elements of which 15 are metals. Among these, those required in major quantities include
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Manganese and Molybdenum are required in trace (micro) quantities, not major quantities. Students confuse macro-minerals with micronutrients.
prelims.elimination_hint: Macro-minerals (required in large quantities): Potassium (K), Sodium (Na), Magnesium (Mg), Calcium (Ca). Eliminate options with Mn, Mo, or Cu as they are micronutrients. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Macro-minerals: K, Na, Mg, Ca (required in gram quantities)
Micronutrients/trace minerals: Mn, Mo, Cu, Fe (required in milligrams)

contrast_with: Macronutrients (K, Na, Ca, Mg) vs micronutrients (Mn, Mo, Cu)

question: The following maps shows four of the sixteen mahajanapadas that existed in ancient India : The places marked A, B, C and D respectively are
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: This question requires a map (not available in text) to identify positions A, B, C, D. Without the map, one must recall the approximate geographic positions of the 16 Mahajanapadas.
prelims.elimination_hint: Key geographic anchors: Matsya (Rajasthan/Jaipur area), Avanti (Malwa/Ujjain), Vatsa (Allahabad/Kaushambi), Anga (Bengal/Bhagalpur). Answer C: Matsya, Avanti, Vatsa, Anga.
factual_hooks: 16 Mahajanapadas of 6th century BCE
Matsya: near Jaipur (Rajasthan)
Magadha: Bihar (south of Ganga)
Avanti: Malwa (MP)

contrast_with: Matsya vs Magadha — both important but in different regions

question:
What is the correct chronological sequence of the following ?
I. Wood’s Education Despatch
II. Macaulay’s Minute on Education
III. The Sargeant Education Report
IV. Indian Education (Hunter Commission)
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Macaulay's Minute (1835) and Wood's Despatch (1854) are frequently swapped. The Hunter Commission (1882) is sometimes placed before Wood's Despatch by confused students.
prelims.elimination_hint: Chronological order: Macaulay's Minute (1835) → Wood's Despatch (1854) → Hunter Commission (1882) → Sargeant Report (1944). Matches option A: II, I, IV, III.
factual_hooks: Macaulay's Minute: 1835 (English medium advocacy)
Wood's Despatch: 1854 (Magna Carta of Indian education)
Hunter Commission: 1882
Sargeant Report: 1944

contrast_with: Macaulay's anglicist position vs Orientalists on education

question: In the series POQ, SRT, VUW, ? The blank space refers to
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Pattern identification: each triplet has a middle letter that is between the outer two in alphabetical sequence but the order is mixed. Students may not notice the consistent middle-last-first pattern within each triplet.
prelims.elimination_hint: POQ: P(16),O(15),Q(17) — P+2=Q, O is middle. SRT: S(19),R(18),T(20) — same pattern. VUW: V(23),U(21? no,22),W(24) — same. Next: Y(25),X(24? no),Z(26) → YXZ. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Pattern: each group = (n+1), n, (n+2) in letters
POQ→SRT→VUW→YXZ skips by +3 each time

question: The meeting of G-15 countries held in Malayasia in 1997 was attended by
subject: International Relations

prelims.trap_factor: G-15 is named for its original 15 members but has expanded. The 1997 Malaysia meeting having 16 members (one joined by then) is highly specific current-affairs knowledge from that era.
prelims.elimination_hint: G-15 was established with 15 members in 1989 but expanded; by the 1997 Malaysia summit, it had 16 members (India among them). The name remained G-15 despite expansion — similar to G-7/G-8 dynamics.
factual_hooks: G-15 established 1989 at NAM summit in Belgrade
G-15 had 17 members by early 2000s though named G-15
India is a member of G-15

contrast_with: G-15 (developing nations forum) vs G-7 (advanced economies)

question: The minimum land area recommended for forest cover to maintain proper ecological balance in India is
subject: Environment

prelims.trap_factor: 25% is the common wrong choice as it sounds like a quarter. The National Forest Policy 1988 specifically recommends 33% for plains and 67% for hills — students must remember the plains figure.
prelims.elimination_hint: National Forest Policy 1988 recommends 33% forest cover for plains, 67% for hilly/tribal areas, and overall 33% nationally. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: National Forest Policy 1988: 33% of land under forest cover recommended
India's actual forest cover is ~21-22% (as per FSI reports)

mains.contemporary_relevance: India's forest cover debate is perennial in environment discussions.

contrast_with: Current forest cover (~21%) vs recommended (33%)

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists
List I | List II
I. Fruit | (A) Ovule
II. Seed | (B) Leaf
III. Wood | (C) Stem
IV. Starch | (D) Ovary
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Students may confuse fruit-ovary with seed-stem. The plant biology match: Fruit←Ovary, Seed←Ovule, Wood←Stem, Starch←Leaf (photosynthesis) is a standard biology fact.
prelims.elimination_hint: Fruit develops from Ovary (D), Seed from Ovule (A), Wood from Stem (C), Starch is produced in Leaf (B). Answer: B (I-D, II-A, III-C, IV-B).
factual_hooks: Fruit = developed ovary
Seed = developed ovule
Starch synthesized in leaves via photosynthesis

contrast_with: Ovary vs Ovule — fruit vs seed development

question: The most short-lived of all of Britain’s constitutional experiments in India was the
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: The Indian Councils Act of 1909 (Morley-Minto Reforms) introduced separate electorates for Muslims — revolutionary in scope but quickly superseded. Students may confuse 'short-lived' with 'unpopular'.
prelims.elimination_hint: The 1909 Act was replaced by the 1919 Act (Montagu-Chelmsford) within a decade — making it the most short-lived major constitutional reform. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Indian Councils Act 1909 (Morley-Minto): introduced separate electorates
Replaced by Government of India Act 1919 within ~10 years
1861 Act lasted until 1892 — much longer

contrast_with: 1909 Act (Morley-Minto) vs 1919 Act (Montagu-Chelmsford) — scope and duration

question: The language spoken by the largest number of people in the world is
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: English is frequently cited as the world's most spoken language due to its global spread, but Mandarin (Putonghua) has more native speakers. The question specifies 'largest number of people' — native speakers.
prelims.elimination_hint: Mandarin has over 1 billion native speakers, far exceeding English (~400 million native). Hindi (~600 million) is second. Answer: C (Mandarin).
factual_hooks: Mandarin: ~1+ billion native speakers (largest)
English: widest geographic spread but fewer native speakers
Hindi: growing rapidly in total speakers

prerequisite_concepts: Difference between native and total speakers
contrast_with: Native speakers (Mandarin) vs total speakers including second language (English)

question: Canola refers to special type of oil seed mustard varieties bred for human consumption. The main characteristic of these varieties is that the
option_a: seeds have very high oil content
option_b: oil is rich in unsaturated fatty acids
option_d: oil has very low Erucic acid content
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Students may think Canola is about high oil content or unsaturated fats; the defining characteristic is low erucic acid content (erucic acid is harmful to heart). High oil content is a feature of many oilseeds.
prelims.elimination_hint: Canola = 'Canadian Oil, Low Acid' — bred specifically to have very low erucic acid (<2%), making it safe for human consumption. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Canola name = Canadian Oil, Low Acid
Low erucic acid (<2%) is the defining trait
Regular mustard oil has high erucic acid (~40-50%)
paired_with: PUFA and MUFA in cooking oils

mains.contemporary_relevance: GM mustard HT-1 debate in India involves similar food safety considerations.

related_concepts: PUFA and MUFA in cooking oils
contrast_with: Canola (low erucic acid) vs regular mustard (high erucic acid)

question: Which one of the following duties is NOT performed by the Comptroller and Auditor General of India ?
option_a: To audit and report on all expenditure from the Consolidated Fund of India
option_b: To audit and report on all expenditure from the Contingency Funds and Public Accounts
option_c: To audit and report on all trading, manufacturing, profit and loss accounts
option_d: To control the receipt and issue of public money, and to ensure that the public revenue is lodged in the exchequer
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Students often think CAG 'controls' public money (like a treasurer), but it only audits — it does not control receipt/issue of public money. That is the function of the Controller General of Accounts, not CAG.
prelims.elimination_hint: CAG (Article 148) audits expenditure but does NOT control public money receipts/issue — that role belongs to the Ministry of Finance/CGA. The question asks for what CAG does NOT do. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: CAG audits Consolidated Fund, Contingency Funds, and Public Accounts
CAG does NOT control the flow of public money — that is the executive's role
CAG reports go to PAC (Public Accounts Committee)

mains.contemporary_relevance: CAG reports on PM CARES, MGNREGA, and other schemes are current affairs topics.

contrast_with: CAG (audit) vs CGA (accounts management) — common confusion

question: Which one of the following pairs is correctly matched ?
option_b: The Kushans ... Gandhara School of Art
option_d: The Marathas ... Pahari School of Painting
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Ajanta Paintings are Gupta-era (not Mughal); Painted Grey Ware is Iron Age (not Harappan); Pahari School belongs to Rajput hill kingdoms (not Marathas). Kushans and Gandhara art is the correct match.
prelims.elimination_hint: Gandhara School of Art (Greco-Buddhist) flourished under Kushans in Gandhara (NW India/Afghanistan) — the most distinctive art-patron relationship in ancient India. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Gandhara art: Kushan period, Greco-Buddhist style, depicted Buddha in human form
Ajanta Paintings: Gupta/Vakataka era, not Mughal
Painted Grey Ware: associated with later Vedic/Iron Age, not Harappa

prerequisite_concepts: Indian art schools and their patrons
contrast_with: Gandhara (Hellenistic influence, Kushans) vs Mathura (indigenous, Kushans also)

question:
Consider the following statements: The Draft Electricity Bill, 2001 seeks to replace
1. Indian Electricity Act, 1910
2. Electricity (Supply) Act, 1948
3. Electricity Regulatory Commission Act, 1998
4. State Electricity Boards
Which of these statements are correct?
subject: Governance

prelims.trap_factor: The Draft Electricity Bill 2001 sought to replace three existing Acts (1910, 1948, 1998) but NOT the State Electricity Boards per se (they were to be restructured/reformed). Statement 4 is thus debatable.
prelims.elimination_hint: Statements 1, 2, and 3 are clearly about repealing existing Acts. Statement 4 (SEBs) was about restructuring not a direct replacement via the Bill. Answer A (1,2 and 3) is correct.
factual_hooks: Electricity Act 2003 replaced the 1910, 1948, and 1998 electricity acts
SEBs were unbundled as a result, not directly replaced by the Bill

mains.contemporary_relevance: Power sector reforms under DISCOMS/RDSS are ongoing government priorities.

question: The members of the Constituent Assembly which drafted the Constitution of India were
option_c: elected by the Legislative Assemblies of various provinces
option_d: elected by the Indian National Congress and Muslim League
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Students may confuse the method of election (indirect by provincial assemblies) with nomination by British Parliament or Governor-General. The Constituent Assembly was elected by provincial Legislative Assemblies under Cabinet Mission Plan.
prelims.elimination_hint: Under the Cabinet Mission Plan (1946), members of Constituent Assembly were elected by provincial Legislative Assemblies using proportional representation. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Constituent Assembly members elected by Provincial Legislative Assemblies
Elections held July-August 1946 under Cabinet Mission Plan
Total strength: 389 members (296 from British India + 93 princely states)
paired_with: B.R. Ambedkar as Chairman of Drafting Committee

related_concepts: B.R. Ambedkar as Chairman of Drafting Committee
contrast_with: Elected Constituent Assembly vs nominated colonial councils

question: The main reason for unrest and violence in Zimbabwe in recent years is due to
option_a: conflict between different native ethnic groups
option_c: economic crisis due to continuous drought and famine
option_d: power struggle between political groups of white European settlers and native black community
subject: International Relations

prelims.trap_factor: Zimbabwe's crisis is multifaceted; students may mark option A (ethnic conflict) or C (drought). The primary cause was Mugabe's forced land redistribution from white farmers to Black Zimbabweans.
prelims.elimination_hint: Zimbabwe's crisis from late 1990s was overwhelmingly about forced land reforms under Mugabe — land redistribution from white commercial farmers collapsed agricultural productivity. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Mugabe's fast-track land reform (2000) drove Zimbabwe's economic collapse
Hyperinflation followed collapse of commercial farming
White Zimbabwean farmers displaced to neighboring countries

contrast_with: Land reform (Zimbabwe) vs post-apartheid land policy (South Africa)

question: Which among the following National Highway routes is the longest?
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Agra-Mumbai (NH3) or Chennai-Thane seem long, but Kolkata-Hajira (NH6) used to be one of the longest NHs in India. Note: NH numbering was revised in 2010 and again in 2015.
prelims.elimination_hint: NH6 (old numbering) from Kolkata to Hajira (near Surat, Gujarat) was one of India's longest highways. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Old NH6: Kolkata to Hazira (Gujarat) — ~1,945 km (one of longest)
NH numbering was completely revised post-2010

prerequisite_concepts: National Highway network of India (old and new numbering)
contrast_with: Old NH numbering vs new NH numbering post-2010

question: Which one among the following countries has the lowest GDP per capita?
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: This is a dated question (2003) — India's per capita GDP ranking among these countries has changed significantly since then. Sri Lanka actually had higher per capita GDP than India even then.
prelims.elimination_hint: As of 2003, India had the lowest GDP per capita among these four nations. Sri Lanka and Indonesia were ahead of India. China was also ahead. Answer: B (India).
factual_hooks: India historically had lower per capita income than Sri Lanka and Indonesia
By 2020s, India has surpassed Sri Lanka but China is far ahead

contrast_with: India vs Sri Lanka per capita — Sri Lanka was higher until 2022 crisis

question:
Consider the following statements:
1. In the last five years, Indian software exports have increased at a compound annual growth rate of about 60%
2. The software and service industry in India registered an overall growth of about 28% in rupee terms during the year 2001-200
2.
Which of these statements is/are correct?
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Both statements about Indian IT/software sector are likely false for the specific figures cited. 60% CAGR and 28% growth are specific numbers that may not match actual data — common UPSC trap with precise statistics.
prelims.elimination_hint: Both statements contain specific percentage claims that are unlikely to be accurate simultaneously. The safest answer is D (Neither 1 nor 2) — a common pattern when both statistics seem inflated or unverifiable. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Indian IT exports grew ~50-55% CAGR in late 1990s, not exactly 60%
Software sector data varies by source and measurement

question: During the colonial period in India, what was the purpose of the Whitley Commission?
option_a: To review the fitness of India for further political reforms
option_b: To report on the existing conditions of labour and to make recommendations
option_c: To draw up a plan for financial reforms for India
option_d: To develop a comprehensive scheme for Civil Services in India
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: The Whitley Commission sounds like a political/financial reforms body. Students may confuse it with the Simon Commission (political reforms) or Holt-Thomas Commission. Whitley was specifically about labour conditions.
prelims.elimination_hint: The Royal Commission on Labour (Whitley Commission, 1929-31) was set up to study labour conditions in India and recommend improvements. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Whitley Commission (1929-31): Royal Commission on Labour in India
Recommended improvements in working conditions, wages, and labour organisation

contrast_with: Whitley (labour) vs Simon (political reforms) vs Hunter (education) Commissions

question: Octopus is
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Octopus is sometimes confused with crustaceans (arthropods) due to its marine environment. Squid and cuttlefish (also molluscs) are in the same class (Cephalopoda).
prelims.elimination_hint: Octopus belongs to Phylum Mollusca, Class Cephalopoda. It is not an arthropod (insects/crustaceans), echinoderm (starfish/sea urchin), or hemichordate. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Octopus: Phylum Mollusca, Class Cephalopoda
Other cephalopods: Squid, Nautilus, Cuttlefish
Molluscs have mantle and often shells

contrast_with: Mollusca vs Arthropoda — soft body vs exoskeleton

question:
Consider the following companies:
1. Voltas
2. Titan Industries
3. Rallis India
4. Indian Hotels
Which of the above companies are in the Tata Group of Industries?
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: This is a dated corporate knowledge question (2004). Students may not know all Tata Group companies. Voltas, Titan, Rallis, and Indian Hotels are all Tata companies.
prelims.elimination_hint: All four — Voltas, Titan Industries, Rallis India, and Indian Hotels (Taj) — are indeed Tata Group companies. Answer: D (all four).
factual_hooks: Tata Group includes: Voltas, Titan, Rallis India, Indian Hotels (Taj), TCS, Tata Steel
Taj Hotels = Indian Hotels Company Limited (IHCL)

question: Which of the following pairs is not correctly matched?
subject: Governance

prelims.trap_factor: Reinhold Messner is a famous mountaineer (first to climb all 14 eight-thousanders), NOT a computer technologist. The question asks for the 'not correctly matched' pair.
prelims.elimination_hint: Reinhold Messner is a renowned mountaineer, not related to computer technology. All other pairs (Shapley-Astronomy, Mendel-Heredity, Hounsfield-CT Scan) are correct. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Reinhold Messner: first to summit all 14 eight-thousanders solo without oxygen
Godfrey Hounsfield: inventor of CT scan (Nobel Prize 1979)
Gregor Mendel: father of genetics

contrast_with: Mountaineering achievements vs scientific/technology contributions

question:
Match List-I (State / Province / Overseas Territory), with List-II (Country) and select the correct answer using the codes given below the Lists
State/Province/ Overseas Territory) | (Country)
A. British Colombia | 1. USA
B. Bavaria | 2. UK
C. Gibraltar | 3. Canada
D. Rhode Island | 4. Germany
| 5. Denmark
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Gibraltar is often confused as belonging to Spain; it is a British Overseas Territory on the Iberian Peninsula. Bavaria is clearly Germany, Rhode Island is USA.
prelims.elimination_hint: British Columbia (Canada-3), Bavaria (Germany-4), Gibraltar (UK-2), Rhode Island (USA-1). Answer: B (A-3, B-4, C-2, D-1).
factual_hooks: Gibraltar: British Overseas Territory (not Spain)
British Columbia: westernmost province of Canada
Rhode Island: smallest US state

contrast_with: Gibraltar (British) vs Spain — ongoing sovereignty dispute

question:
Consider the following statements:
1. Femur is the longest bone in the human body.
2. Cholera is a disease caused by bacteria.
3. ‘Athlete’s foot’ is a disease caused by virus.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Statement 3 is false: Athlete's foot is caused by a fungus (Tinea pedis), NOT a virus. Students may confuse fungal skin infections with viral ones.
prelims.elimination_hint: Statement 1: Femur is longest bone — TRUE. Statement 2: Cholera is bacterial (Vibrio cholerae) — TRUE. Statement 3: Athlete's foot is fungal, NOT viral — FALSE. Answer: A (1 and 2 only).
factual_hooks: Femur (thigh bone) is the longest bone in human body
Cholera: Vibrio cholerae (bacterium)
Athlete's foot (Tinea pedis): caused by dermatophyte fungi, NOT virus
paired_with: Bacterial vs viral vs fungal diseases

related_concepts: Bacterial vs viral vs fungal diseases
contrast_with: Fungal diseases (athlete's foot, ringworm) vs viral diseases (influenza, HIV)

question:
Consider the following statements:
1. Anhydrous sodium carbonate is commonly known as baking soda.
2. Baking soda is used in fire extinguishers.
3. Bleaching powder is manufactured in Hasenclever plant.
Which of the statements is/are correct?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Statement 1 is false: Baking soda is sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), NOT anhydrous sodium carbonate (Na2CO3, which is washing soda). This is the classic baking soda vs washing soda confusion.
prelims.elimination_hint: Baking soda = NaHCO3 (sodium bicarbonate), NOT Na2CO3 (washing soda/anhydrous sodium carbonate). Statement 1 is wrong. Statements 2 and 3 are correct. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Baking soda = Sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3)
Washing soda = Sodium carbonate (Na2CO3)
Baking soda used in fire extinguishers (releases CO2)
Hasenclever plant: bleaching powder manufacturing

contrast_with: Baking soda (NaHCO3) vs Washing soda (Na2CO3) — classic confusion

question: For which one of the following countries, is Spanish not an official language?
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Republic of Congo (Congo-Brazzaville) uses French as official language, not Spanish. Students may confuse Spanish-speaking Latin America with similarly named African nations.
prelims.elimination_hint: Republic of Congo (Congo-Brazzaville) is a former French colony — official language is French, not Spanish. Chile, Colombia, and Cuba are all Spanish-speaking. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Republic of Congo (Brazzaville): official language is French
Democratic Republic of Congo also uses French
Cuba, Colombia, Chile: all Spanish-speaking

contrast_with: Republic of Congo (French) vs Equatorial Guinea (Spanish in Africa)

question:
Match List I (Centre of Handicrafts) with List II (State) and select the correct answer using the codes given below the Lists
(Centre of Handicrafts) | (State)
I. Mon | (A) Arunachal Pradesh
II. Nalbari | (B) Assam
III. Pasighat | (C) Meghalaya
IV. Tura | (D) Nagaland
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: This is a very specific match about handicraft centres in NE India — Mon (Nagaland), Nalbari (Assam), Pasighat (Arunachal Pradesh), Tura (Meghalaya). Students often mix up NE state geography.
prelims.elimination_hint: Mon is the district headquarters in Nagaland; Nalbari is in Assam; Pasighat is in Arunachal Pradesh (East Siang); Tura is in Meghalaya (West Garo Hills). Answer: A (I-D, II-B, III-A, IV-C).
factual_hooks: Mon: Nagaland (Konyak tribe region)
Pasighat: Arunachal Pradesh (oldest town in state)
Tura: Meghalaya (Garo Hills district HQ)

contrast_with: Assam vs Meghalaya vs Nagaland districts — all NE but distinct

question: Which one of the following pairs is not correctly matched? Railway Zone Headquarters
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: South Eastern Railway HQ is in Kolkata (Garden Reach), not Bhubaneshwar. Students may confuse 'South Eastern' with a coastal/Odisha connection.
prelims.elimination_hint: South Eastern Railway headquarters is in Kolkata (not Bhubaneshwar). Bhubaneshwar is in the jurisdiction of East Coast Railway (now a separate zone). Answer: B (not correctly matched).
factual_hooks: South Eastern Railway HQ: Kolkata (Garden Reach)
North Eastern Railway HQ: Gorakhpur
Eastern Railway HQ: Kolkata (Fairlie Place)

contrast_with: South Eastern Railway (Kolkata HQ) vs East Coast Railway (Bhubaneshwar HQ)

question: Which of the following countries signed the Tshwane Declaration in October, 2006?
subject: International Relations

prelims.trap_factor: The Tshwane Declaration (2006) is an obscure bilateral India-South Africa agreement. Students may confuse it with India-China or India-Brazil agreements from the same period.
prelims.elimination_hint: The Tshwane Declaration was signed during PM Manmohan Singh's visit to South Africa in October 2006 — a bilateral India-South Africa strategic partnership document. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Tshwane Declaration (2006): India-South Africa bilateral strategic partnership
Tshwane is the administrative capital of South Africa (Pretoria)

contrast_with: Tshwane Declaration (bilateral) vs IBSA (trilateral)

question: For which one of the following reforms was a Commission set up under the Championship of Veerappa Moily by the Government of India?
subject: Governance

prelims.trap_factor: Veerappa Moily is more commonly associated with Administrative Reforms Commission — students may confuse it with police reforms or legal reforms given his later roles.
prelims.elimination_hint: M. Veerappa Moily chaired the Second Administrative Reforms Commission (2005-2009), which produced multiple reports on governance reform. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Second ARC (2005-2009) chaired by M. Veerappa Moily
Second ARC produced 15 reports on administrative reforms

mains.contemporary_relevance: Administrative reforms continue to be relevant under DARPG.

contrast_with: First ARC (Morarji Desai) vs Second ARC (Veerappa Moily)

question: Elizabeth Hawley is well-known for her writing relating to which one of the following?
subject: Governance

prelims.trap_factor: Elizabeth Hawley's association with Nepal/Himalayas is not widely known. Students may confuse her with a wildlife writer or Indian cultural writer.
prelims.elimination_hint: Elizabeth Hawley (1923-2018) was an American journalist who chronicled all Himalayan expeditions from Kathmandu for decades — the definitive chronicler of mountaineering in the Himalayas. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Elizabeth Hawley: American journalist, chronicled Himalayan expeditions from Kathmandu since 1960s
She ran the Himalayan Database of all Himalayan climbs

prerequisite_concepts: Notable personalities associated with the Himalayas

question:
Match List-I with List-II and select the correct answer using the code given below the Lists
List-I | List-II
A. Coffee Board | 1. Bengaluru
B. Rubber Board | 2. Guntur
C. Tea Board | 3. Kottayam
D. Tobacco Board | 4. Kolkata
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Tea Board is in Kolkata (not a tea-producing city like Assam or Darjeeling); Rubber Board in Kottayam (not Kochi); Tobacco Board in Guntur (not Andhra state capital Amaravati).
prelims.elimination_hint: Coffee Board: Bengaluru (1); Rubber Board: Kottayam (3); Tea Board: Kolkata (4); Tobacco Board: Guntur (2). Answer: B (A-1, B-3, C-4, D-2).
factual_hooks: Tea Board HQ: Kolkata
Coffee Board HQ: Bengaluru
Rubber Board HQ: Kottayam (Kerala)
Tobacco Board HQ: Guntur (AP)

contrast_with: Tea Board (Kolkata) vs Tea cultivation (Assam/Darjeeling) — HQ not in production state

question: From which one of the following did Kosovo declare its independence ?
subject: International Relations

prelims.trap_factor: Students may confuse Kosovo with Macedonia (FYROM), both former Yugoslav republics. Kosovo declared independence from Serbia, not from a broader Yugoslav entity.
prelims.elimination_hint: Kosovo declared independence from Serbia on 17 February 2008 — one of the most significant post-Cold War sovereignty events. Answer: D (Serbia).
factual_hooks: Kosovo declared independence from Serbia: February 17, 2008
Recognized by ~100 countries; Serbia and Russia do not recognize
Kosovo was formerly a province of Serbia under Yugoslav federation

mains.contemporary_relevance: Kosovo's independence remains unresolved in international law — relevant to IR theory.

contrast_with: Kosovo (declared independence) vs Crimea (annexed by Russia) — both contested separations

question: Who of the following founded the Ahmedabad Textile Labour Association ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Sardar Patel is associated with Kheda Satyagraha (peasant agitation), not the Ahmedabad textile workers. Students confuse Gandhi's textile and agrarian Satyagrahas.
prelims.elimination_hint: Gandhi founded the Ahmedabad Textile Labour Association (Majoor Mahajan Sangh) in 1920 after mediating the 1918 Ahmedabad Mill Strike. Answer: A (Mahatma Gandhi).
factual_hooks: Ahmedabad Textile Labour Association (Majoor Mahajan Sangh): founded by Gandhi in 1920
Gandhi's first industrial dispute mediation was the 1918 Ahmedabad Mill Strike
Anasuya Sarabhai assisted Gandhi in the 1918 strike

contrast_with: Ahmedabad (labour/Gandhi) vs Kheda (peasant/Patel) Satyagrahas

question: In a tournament 14 teams play league matches. If each team plays against every other team once only then how many matches are played ?
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Students may add instead of use combination formula; some compute 14×13=182 (without dividing by 2). Round-robin format means each pair plays once.
prelims.elimination_hint: Total matches = C(14,2) = 14×13/2 = 91. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Round-robin tournament: n(n-1)/2 matches for n teams

question:
With reference to the mineral resources of India, consider the following pairs: Mineral 90% Natural sources in
1. Copper Jharkhand
2. Nickel Orissa
3. Tungsten Kerala
Which of the pairs give above is/are correctly matched?
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Copper is associated with Jharkhand (Singhbhum) but the '90%' claim for Jharkhand is debatable — Rajasthan also has significant copper. Tungsten is NOT from Kerala. Nickel from Odisha (Sukinda Valley) is correct.
prelims.elimination_hint: Only Pair 2 (Nickel-Odisha/Orissa) is correctly matched — Sukinda Valley in Odisha has ~96% of India's nickel reserves. Copper's largest source is Rajasthan (Khetri), not Jharkhand. Tungsten is from Rajasthan/Karnataka. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Nickel: Odisha (Sukinda Valley) — 96% of India's reserves
Copper: Rajasthan (Khetri mines) — largest source, not Jharkhand
Tungsten: Rajasthan/Karnataka, not Kerala

mains.contemporary_relevance: Nickel and other critical minerals are key to EV battery production — highly current.

contrast_with: Copper (Rajasthan) vs Iron (Jharkhand/Odisha) — common mineral-state confusion

question:
Microbial fuel cells are considered a source of sustainable energy. Why?

1. They use living organisms as catalysts to generate electricity from certain substrates.
2. They use a variety of inorganic materials as substrates.
3. They can be installed in waste water treatment plants to cleanse water and produce electricity.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Statement 2 is false — microbial fuel cells use organic (not inorganic) substrates like glucose, acetate, or wastewater. Students may assume 'variety of substrates' includes inorganic materials.
prelims.elimination_hint: Statements 1 and 3 are correct. Statement 2 is wrong — MFCs use organic substrates, not inorganic materials. Answer: C (1 and 3 only).
factual_hooks: Microbial fuel cells: bacteria oxidize organic matter to generate electrons
MFCs can be used in wastewater treatment for dual benefit
MFCs use organic substrates (glucose, acetate, wastewater organics)

mains.contemporary_relevance: Sustainable bioenergy solutions are frequently discussed in environment-energy nexus.

contrast_with: MFC (biological) vs conventional fuel cell (chemical/hydrogen)

question: Which one of the following statements appropriately describes the "fiscal stimulus"?
option_a: It is a massive investment by the Government in manufacturing sector to ensure the supply of goods to meet the demand surge caused by rapid economic growth
option_b: It is an intense affirmative action of the Government to boost economic activity in the country
option_c: It is Government's intensive action on financial institutions to ensure disbursement of loans to agriculture and allied sectors to promote greater food production and contain food inflation
option_d: It is an extreme affirmative action by the Government to pursue its policy of financial inclusion
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Options A, C, and D describe specific sectoral interventions, not the broad macroeconomic definition of fiscal stimulus. Students may select manufacturing-focused option thinking it's stimulus.
prelims.elimination_hint: Fiscal stimulus = government's broad affirmative action to boost aggregate economic activity (through increased spending, tax cuts, or transfers). Option B correctly captures this broadly. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Fiscal stimulus: government spending/tax cuts to boost GDP during slowdown
Distinct from monetary stimulus (central bank cutting rates)
2008 financial crisis triggered major global fiscal stimuli

mains.contemporary_relevance: Post-COVID fiscal stimulus debates are a current affairs staple.

contrast_with: Fiscal stimulus (government) vs monetary stimulus (RBI/central bank)

question:
With reference to the guilds (Shreni) of ancient India that played a very important role in the country's economy, which of the following statements is/are correct?

1. Every guild was registered with the central authority of the State and the king was the chief administrative authority on them.
2. The wages, rules of work, standards and prices were fixed by the guild,
3. The guild had judicial powers over its own members.

Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Statement 1 is false — guilds were NOT directly registered with the central state; they were largely autonomous bodies. The king did not directly control them. Statement 3 is correct — guilds had internal judicial powers.
prelims.elimination_hint: Statement 2 (guilds fixed wages, standards, prices) and Statement 3 (judicial powers over members) are correct. Statement 1 is wrong — guilds were autonomous, not state-registered. Answer: C (2 and 3 only).
factual_hooks: Ancient Indian guilds (Shreni) were autonomous economic organizations
Guilds had own rules (guild laws), could adjudicate member disputes
Guilds fixed wages, working standards, and commodity prices

contrast_with: Ancient guilds (autonomous) vs modern trade unions (regulated by state law)

question: The distribution of powers between the Centre and the States in the Indian Constitution is based on the scheme provided in the
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Students may pick Indian Independence Act 1947 thinking it established the new constitutional framework, or Montagu-Chelmsford 1919 for introducing dyarchy. The 1935 Act's federal scheme is the direct precursor.
prelims.elimination_hint: The Centre-State division of powers (Union, State, Concurrent Lists) in India's Constitution is directly modelled on the Government of India Act 1935, which first introduced this three-list scheme. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Government of India Act 1935: source of Union-State-Concurrent list scheme
The 7th Schedule of Indian Constitution directly drawn from 1935 Act
Federal structure with residuary powers in Centre: both 1935 and Constitution

mains.contemporary_relevance: Centre-State relations and cooperative federalism remain perennial UPSC themes.

contrast_with: 1935 Act (colonial federal scheme) vs 1950 Constitution (sovereign federal scheme)

question:
Which of the following diseases can be transmitted from one person to another through tattooing?

1. Chikungunya
2. Hepatitis B
3. HIV-AIDS

Select the correct answer using the codes given below.
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Chikungunya (1) is transmitted by mosquitoes, NOT through tattooing (blood-to-blood). Students may not know the blood-borne disease transmission routes precisely.
prelims.elimination_hint: Hepatitis B (2) and HIV-AIDS (3) are blood-borne diseases transmitted through contaminated needles/tattooing equipment. Chikungunya is viral but spread by Aedes mosquitoes, not blood contact. Answer: B (2 and 3 only).
factual_hooks: Hepatitis B: blood-borne, transmitted via tattooing/shared needles
HIV-AIDS: blood-borne, transmitted via contaminated tattooing equipment
Chikungunya: mosquito-borne (Aedes aegypti), NOT blood-to-blood

mains.contemporary_relevance: Blood-borne disease awareness is central to India's public health agenda.

contrast_with: Blood-borne diseases (HIV, Hep B, C) vs mosquito-borne (Chikungunya, Dengue, Malaria)

question: 'Broad-based Trade and Investment Agreement (BTIA)' is sometimes seen in the news in the context of negotiations held between India and
option_c: Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development
subject: International Relations

prelims.trap_factor: BTIA sounds like a multilateral forum (WTO-type), but it is specifically a bilateral EU-India trade agreement under negotiation. Students may confuse with RCEP or WTO negotiations.
prelims.elimination_hint: BTIA (Broad-based Trade and Investment Agreement) is a proposed bilateral free trade agreement between India and the European Union, under negotiation since 2007. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: BTIA: India-EU bilateral trade and investment agreement
Negotiations began 2007, relaunched 2022 after a decade-long pause
EU is India's largest trading bloc partner

mains.contemporary_relevance: India-EU BTIA relaunched in 2022 — highly current for IR preparation.

contrast_with: BTIA (India-EU bilateral) vs RCEP (multilateral ASEAN+ deal India exited)

question:
Consider the following statements :

1. Inflation benefits the debtors.
2. Inflation benefits the bond-holders.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Statement 2 is false: inflation hurts bondholders because bonds pay fixed nominal returns, eroded in real terms by inflation. Students may confuse 'benefit' directions for financial assets.
prelims.elimination_hint: Inflation: helps debtors (repay with cheaper money), hurts creditors and bondholders (fixed income loses real value). Statement 1 is correct; Statement 2 is wrong. Answer: A (1 only).
factual_hooks: Inflation benefits debtors: real value of debt reduces over time
Inflation hurts bondholders: fixed coupon payments lose purchasing power
Unanticipated inflation redistributes wealth from creditors to debtors

mains.contemporary_relevance: Inflation debates are permanently relevant in RBI/macroeconomics context.

prerequisite_concepts: Inflation effects on different economic actors
contrast_with: Debtor (benefits from inflation) vs creditor/bondholder (harmed by inflation)

question:
With reference to technologies for solar power production, consider the following statements:

1. 'Photovoltaics' is a technology that generates electricity by direct conversion of light into electricity, while 'Solar Thermal' is a technology that utilizes the Sun's rays to generate heat which is further used in electricity generation process.
2. Photovoltaics generates Alternating Current (AC), while Solar Thermal generates Direct Current (DC).
3. India has manufacturing base for Solar Thermal technology, but not for Photovoltaics.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Statement 2 is false: Photovoltaics produce DC, not AC (inverter converts DC to AC); Solar Thermal generates AC through turbines. Statement 3 is also false — India has PV manufacturing capacity.
prelims.elimination_hint: Only Statement 1 is correct: PV converts light directly to electricity; Solar Thermal generates heat for steam turbines. Statement 2 reverses the AC/DC output. Statement 3 is outdated/wrong. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Photovoltaics (PV): direct conversion of sunlight to DC electricity via semiconductor
Solar Thermal: heat → steam → turbine → AC electricity
India has PV manufacturing (though limited vs China)

mains.contemporary_relevance: Solar energy targets and PV manufacturing (PLI scheme) are highly current.

contrast_with: PV (direct, DC output) vs Solar Thermal (indirect, AC via turbine)

question:
With reference to Urban Cooperative Banks' in India, consider the following statements :

1. They are supervised and regulated by local boards set up by the State Governments.
2. They can issue equity shares and preference shares.
3. They were brought under the purview of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 through an Amendment in 1966.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Statement 1 is false: UCBs are regulated by RBI (not local state boards). Statement 2 is false: UCBs cannot issue equity or preference shares — they are cooperative societies.
prelims.elimination_hint: Only Statement 3 is correct: UCBs were brought under Banking Regulation Act 1949 by amendment in 1966. RBI regulates them, not local/state boards. They cannot issue shares like companies. Answer: B (2 and 3 — wait: 2 is false). Re-check: Answer is B (2 and 3 only) per correct answer field, but statement 2 about equity shares is debatable for multi-state UCBs.
factual_hooks: UCBs brought under Banking Regulation Act 1949 by amendment in 1966
UCBs regulated by RBI (banking functions) and Registrar of Cooperative Societies (non-banking)
UCBs are cooperative societies and cannot issue equity shares like companies

mains.contemporary_relevance: RBI's increased oversight of UCBs after Punjab & Maharashtra Cooperative Bank crisis is highly current.

contrast_with: UCBs (cooperative) vs commercial banks (corporate structure)

question:
With reference to the Union Government, consider the following statements :

1. N. Gopalaswamy Iyengar Committee suggested that a minister and a secretary be designated solely for pursuing the subject of administrative reform and promoting it.
2. In 1970, the Department of Personnel was constituted on the recommendation of the Administrative Reforms Commission, 1966, and this was placed under the Prime Minister's charge.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
subject: Governance

prelims.trap_factor: Statement 1 about N. Gopalaswamy Iyengar Committee is tricky — his recommendations were about reorganising the Cabinet Secretariat and PM's role, but the specific claim about 'minister and secretary for admin reform' may not be accurate.
prelims.elimination_hint: Statement 2 is correct: Department of Personnel was constituted in 1970 based on ARC 1966 recommendations and placed under PM's charge. Statement 1's specific claim is likely incorrect. Answer: B (2 only).
factual_hooks: Department of Personnel: constituted 1970, initially under PM's charge
ARC 1966: First Administrative Reforms Commission under Morarji Desai
N. Gopalaswamy Iyengar Committee: recommended reorganisation of GoI secretariat (1947)

contrast_with: First ARC (1966) vs Second ARC (2005) recommendations

question:
Consider the following phenomena :

1. Size of the sun at dusk
2. Colour of the sun at dawn
3. Moon being visible at dawn
4. Twinkle of stars in the sky
5. Polestar being visible in the sky

Which of the above are optical illusions?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Moon visible at dawn (3) is NOT an optical illusion — the moon can genuinely be visible during daytime. Twinkling of stars (4) IS an optical illusion (atmospheric refraction). Polestar visibility (5) is a real phenomenon.
prelims.elimination_hint: Optical illusions: sun appearing larger at dusk (1, atmospheric refraction), sun's colour at dawn (2, scattering), twinkling of stars (4, atmospheric refraction). Moon visibility at dawn (3) and Polestar visibility (5) are real. Answer: C (1, 2, 4).
factual_hooks: Sun appears larger at horizon: optical illusion (Ponzo illusion + refraction)
Star twinkling: atmospheric refraction (turbulence), an optical illusion
Moon visible at dawn: real phenomenon, not an illusion

contrast_with: Real phenomena (moon visibility) vs apparent phenomena (sun size at horizon)

question:
During a thunderstorm, the thunder in the skies is produced by the

1. meeting of cumulonimbus clouds in the sky
2. lightning that separates the nimbus clouds
3. violent upward movement of air and water particles

Select the correct answer using the codes given below.
option_d: None of the above produces the thunder
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: All three options describe incorrect mechanisms — thunder is NOT caused by cloud collisions, lightning separating clouds, or air/water movement. Thunder is caused by the rapid heating and expansion of air around a lightning channel.
prelims.elimination_hint: Thunder is produced by the rapid expansion of air superheated by the lightning bolt — a sonic shockwave/acoustic effect. None of the three listed causes is correct. Answer: D (None of the above).
factual_hooks: Thunder: caused by rapid expansion of air superheated by lightning (up to 30,000 K)
Not caused by cloud collisions or air/water upward movement
Lightning travels at ~1/3 speed of light; thunder at speed of sound

contrast_with: Thunder (sound from lightning) vs lightning (electrical discharge)

question:
Consider the following languages:

1. Gujarati
2. Kannada
3. Telugu

Which of the above has/have been declared as 'Classical Language/ Languages' by the Government?
subject: Governance

prelims.trap_factor: Gujarati (1) has NOT been declared a classical language — only Tamil, Sanskrit, Telugu, Kannada, Malayalam, and Odia have been recognized as classical languages.
prelims.elimination_hint: Classical languages recognized by GoI: Tamil (2004), Sanskrit (2005), Telugu (2008), Kannada (2008), Malayalam (2013), Odia (2014). Gujarati is NOT among them. Answer: C (Kannada and Telugu only).
factual_hooks: 6 Classical Languages in India: Tamil, Sanskrit, Telugu, Kannada, Malayalam, Odia
Gujarati is NOT a classical language
Criteria: over 1500-2000 years of literature, distinct from other languages

mains.contemporary_relevance: Classical language status demands are a recurring political and cultural issue.

contrast_with: Classical languages (ancient literary tradition) vs Scheduled languages (constitutional recognition)

question:
Consider the following:
The arrival of Babur into India led to the

1. introduction of gunpowder in the subcontinent
2. introduction of the arch and dome in the region's architecture
3. establishment of Timurid dynasty in the region

Select the correct answer using the code given below.
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Gunpowder (1) was NOT introduced by Babur — it was already known in India before him; guns were used in Delhi Sultanate. The arch and dome (2) were introduced by early Sultanate rulers. Only the Timurid dynasty (3) was established by Babur's arrival.
prelims.elimination_hint: Babur established the Timurid (Mughal) dynasty in India (1526). Gunpowder was known before him; arch/dome came with earlier Turkish/Central Asian invaders (Delhi Sultanate). Only Statement 3 is correct. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Babur founded Timurid/Mughal dynasty in India (1526, First Battle of Panipat)
Gunpowder artillery pre-existed Babur in India (used by Delhi Sultans)
Arch and dome introduced by early Turkish conquerors (Qutb-ud-din Aibak era)

prerequisite_concepts: Babur's invasion and First Battle of Panipat 1526
contrast_with: Babur's contributions vs Delhi Sultanate's earlier architectural/military innovations

question:
With reference to the Cabinet Mission, which of the following statements is/are correct?

1. It recommended a federal government.
2. It enlarged the powers of the Indian courts.
3. It provided for more Indians in the ICS.

Select the correct answer using the code given below.
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Students often think Cabinet Mission recommended broader powers for Indian courts (2) or ICS reform (3), but it focused narrowly on the constitutional framework. Only a federal government (1) was recommended.
prelims.elimination_hint: Cabinet Mission Plan (1946) recommended a federal structure with a Union of India. It did NOT enlarge court powers or address ICS composition — those were separate matters. Answer: A (1 only).
factual_hooks: Cabinet Mission 1946: recommended a three-tier federal structure (Union, provinces, princely states)
Did not address ICS reforms or court powers
Led to formation of Constituent Assembly

contrast_with: Cabinet Mission (federal plan) vs Mountbatten Plan (partition plan)

question: Which of the following National Parks is unique in being a swamp with floating vegetation that supports a rich biodiversity?
subject: Environment

prelims.trap_factor: Keoladeo Ghana (Rajasthan) is also a famous wetland park and may confuse students. Sultanpur is a bird sanctuary (Haryana), not a floating swamp. Bhitarkanika is known for mangroves, not floating vegetation.
prelims.elimination_hint: Keibul Lamjao National Park (Manipur) is the world's only floating National Park — its floating biomass (phumdi) supports the endangered Sangai (brow-antlered deer). Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Keibul Lamjao NP (Manipur): world's only floating national park on Loktak Lake
Home to Sangai (brow-antlered deer) — state animal of Manipur
Floating vegetation called 'phumdi'

mains.contemporary_relevance: Loktak Lake's ecological health is under environmental stress — current topic.

contrast_with: Keibul Lamjao (floating swamp) vs Keoladeo Ghana (fixed wetland/bird sanctuary)

question: Which one of the following is the best description of the term ‘ecosystem’?
option_a: A community of organisms interacting with one another
option_b: That part of the Earth which is inhabited by living organisms
option_c: A community of organisms together with the environment in which they live
option_d: The flora and fauna of a geographical area
subject: Environment

prelims.trap_factor: Option A (community of organisms only) misses the abiotic environment. Option B describes the biosphere, not an ecosystem. Option D describes flora and fauna only.
prelims.elimination_hint: Ecosystem = biotic community + abiotic environment in which they live. Option C captures both components correctly. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Ecosystem = biotic (living organisms) + abiotic (physical environment) components
Term coined by A.G. Tansley (1935)
Biosphere = sum of all ecosystems on Earth

mains.contemporary_relevance: Ecosystem services valuation is a key concept in environment policy.

contrast_with: Ecosystem (biotic+abiotic) vs Community (biotic only) vs Biosphere (global)

question:
Consider the following countries:

1. China
2. France
3. India
4. Israel
5. Pakistan

Which among the above are Nuclear Weapons States as recognized by the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons, commonly known as Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty (NPT)?
subject: International Relations

prelims.trap_factor: India (3), Israel (4), and Pakistan (5) are nuclear-armed states but are NOT recognized as Nuclear Weapons States (NWS) under NPT. Only 5 states are officially recognized NWS under NPT.
prelims.elimination_hint: NPT Nuclear Weapons States (NWS): USA, Russia, UK, France, China — the P5 (UN Security Council permanent members). India, Pakistan, Israel are de facto nuclear states but NOT NPT NWS. Answer: A (China and France only from the given list).
factual_hooks: NPT NWS: USA, Russia, UK, France, China (only these 5)
India, Pakistan, Israel: nuclear-armed but not NPT parties/NWS
North Korea withdrew from NPT in 2003

mains.contemporary_relevance: Nuclear non-proliferation and India's NSG bid are recurring UPSC IR topics.

prerequisite_concepts: Nuclear Weapons States vs de facto nuclear states
contrast_with: NPT NWS (P5) vs de facto nuclear states (India, Pakistan, Israel, DPRK)

question:
In the context of mitigating the impending global warming due to anthropogenic emissions of carbon dioxide, which of the following can be the potential sites for carbon sequestration?

1. Abandoned and uneconomic coal seams
2. Depleted oil and gas reservoirs
3. Subterranean deep saline formations

Select the correct answer using the code given below:
subject: Environment

prelims.trap_factor: Students often exclude coal seams thinking only geological formations count; all three are valid geological carbon sinks.
prelims.elimination_hint: All three—coal seams, depleted reservoirs, and deep saline formations—are established CCS sites; eliminate any option that excludes one.
factual_hooks: Carbon sequestration uses geological formations to store CO2
Subterranean saline formations have the largest storage capacity globally
Depleted oil/gas reservoirs have proven sealing capacity

mains.contemporary_relevance: India's net-zero 2070 commitment and National Action Plan on Climate Change make CCS increasingly relevant.

contrast_with: Carbon offsetting vs carbon sequestration — sequestration is physical storage, offsetting is compensatory

question:
With reference to the religious history of India, consider the following statements:

1. Sautrantika and Sammitiya were the sects of Jainism.
2. Sarvastivadin held that the constituents of phenomena were not wholly momentary, but existed forever in a latent form.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Sautrantika and Sammitiya are actually sects of Buddhism, not Jainism—the question exploits confusion between Buddhist sub-schools and Jain sects.
prelims.elimination_hint: Statement 1 is clearly wrong (these are Buddhist sects); Statement 2 about Sarvastivadin's philosophy of eternal dharmas is correct, making B the answer.
factual_hooks: Sautrantika and Sammitiya are Hinayana Buddhist schools, not Jain sects
Sarvastivadin doctrine holds that dharmas exist in past, present, and future

contrast_with: Hinayana sects vs Mahayana schools — different philosophical positions on dharma

question:
Mediterranean Sea is a border of which of the following countries ?

1. Jordan
2. Iraq
3. Lebanon
4. Syria

Select the correct answer using the code given below:
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Jordan and Iraq are often confused as Mediterranean countries; Jordan borders the Red Sea/Gulf of Aqaba and Iraq is landlocked relative to the Mediterranean.
prelims.elimination_hint: Only Lebanon and Syria have Mediterranean coastlines among the four; eliminate Jordan (Red Sea) and Iraq (no Mediterranean coast).
factual_hooks: Lebanon and Syria are the only Mediterranean-bordering countries in this list
Jordan borders the Red Sea/Gulf of Aqaba, not Mediterranean
Iraq has no Mediterranean coastline

mains.contemporary_relevance: Geopolitical importance of Eastern Mediterranean for energy and trade routes.

contrast_with: Mediterranean vs Red Sea vs Arabian Sea coastlines of Middle Eastern nations

question:
It is possible to produce algae based biofuels, but what is/are the likely limitation(s) of developing countries in promoting this industry?

1. Production of algae based biofuels is possible in seas only and not on continents.
2. Setting up and engineering the algae based biofuel production requires high level of expertise/technology until the construction is completed.
3. Economically viable production necessitates the setting up of large scale facilities which may raise ecological and social concerns.

Select the correct answer using the code given below:
subject: Environment

prelims.trap_factor: Statement 1 is a common misconception—algae can be grown in ponds/bioreactors on land, not only in seas; this wrong statement traps those unfamiliar with algae cultivation.
prelims.elimination_hint: Eliminate statement 1 (algae biofuels don't require seas); statements 2 and 3 are genuine limitations, making B correct.
factual_hooks: Algae biofuels can be grown in land-based ponds and closed bioreactors
High capital costs and expertise requirements limit developing country adoption
Large-scale facilities may cause ecological disruption

mains.contemporary_relevance: India's National Biofuel Policy 2018 promotes advanced biofuels including algae-based options.

contrast_with: First-gen (food crop) vs third-gen (algae) biofuels — land-use and ethical concerns differ

question:
Which of the following statements is/are true of the Fundamental Duties of an Indian citizen?

1. A legislative process has been provided to enforce these duties.
2. They are correlative to legal duties.

Select the correct answer using the code given below :
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Many assume Fundamental Duties are legally enforceable like Fundamental Rights, but no specific legislative mechanism exists to enforce them; they are also moral duties, not correlative legal duties.
prelims.elimination_hint: Both statements are false—no enforcement mechanism exists and duties are moral not legal correlatives—so 'Neither 1 nor 2' (D) is correct.
factual_hooks: Fundamental Duties (Article 51A) have no enforcement mechanism in the Constitution
Fundamental Duties are moral obligations, not correlative to legal rights

mains.contemporary_relevance: Debate on making Fundamental Duties enforceable resurfaces periodically in judicial and legislative discourse.

contrast_with: Fundamental Rights (enforceable) vs Fundamental Duties (non-justiciable)

question: If you travel by road from Kohima to Kottayam, what is the minimum number of States within India through which you can travel, including the origin and the destination?
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: The route from Kohima (Nagaland) to Kottayam (Kerala) crosses many states; candidates miscalculate by forgetting Northeast connectivity or counting incorrectly.
prelims.elimination_hint: Nagaland → Assam → West Bengal → Odisha → Andhra Pradesh → Tamil Nadu → Kerala = 7 states including origin and destination.
factual_hooks: Kohima is in Nagaland; Kottayam is in Kerala
Road route must pass through Assam, West Bengal, Odisha, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu

contrast_with: Air vs road distance — road requires transit through multiple states unlike air travel

question:
Consider the following statements :

1. The Fiscal Responsibility and Budget Management (FRBM) Review Committee Report has recommended a debt to GDP ratio of 60% for the general (combined) government by 2023, comprising 40% for the Central Government and 20% for the State Governments.
2. The Central Government has domestic liabilities of 21% of GDP as compared to that of 49% of GDP of the State Governments.
3. As per the Constitution of India, it is mandatory for a State to take the Central Government's consent for raising any loan if the former owes any outstanding liabilities to the latter.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Statement 2 has inverted figures—Centre has ~49% domestic liabilities, States ~21%—which traps candidates who remember numbers but confuse which government has which.
prelims.elimination_hint: Statement 2 is wrong (figures are reversed); Statements 1 and 3 are correct—FRBM recommended 60% (40%+20%) and Article 293 requires state consent from Centre for borrowing.
factual_hooks: FRBM Review Committee: 60% debt-to-GDP (40% Centre + 20% States)
Article 293(3): States need Centre consent to borrow if outstanding dues exist

mains.contemporary_relevance: States' fiscal health and borrowing limits remain contentious post-COVID amid rising debt.

contrast_with: Centre vs State borrowing limits under FRBM framework

question:
Consider the following statements :

1. Coal sector was nationalized by the Government of India under Indira Gandhi.
2. Now, coal blocks are allocated on lottery basis.
3. Till recently, India imported coal to meet the shortages of domestic supply, but now India is self-sufficient in coal production.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Statement 2 (lottery basis) and 3 (self-sufficient) are both wrong—coal is auctioned since SC 2014 ruling and India still imports coking coal—but candidates may accept these plausible-sounding claims.
prelims.elimination_hint: Only Statement 1 is correct (coal nationalized under Indira Gandhi via Coal Mines Nationalization Acts 1971-73); both 2 and 3 are factually incorrect.
factual_hooks: Coal nationalization: 1971 (coking coal) and 1973 (all coal) under Indira Gandhi
Post-2014 SC ruling: coal blocks allocated via competitive auction, not lottery
India still imports metallurgical/coking coal

mains.contemporary_relevance: Commercial coal mining opened to private sector in 2020, a major policy reversal.

contrast_with: Nationalization era vs post-liberalization commercial mining policy

question:
Consider the following statements :

1. Under Ramsar Convention, it is mandatory on the part of the Government of India to protect and conserve all the wetlands in the territory of India.
2. The Wetlands (Conservation and Management) Rules, 2010 were framed by the Government of India based on the recommendations of Ramsar Convention.
3. The Wetlands (Conservation and Management) Rules, 2010 also encompass the drainage area or catchment regions of the wetlands as determined by the authority.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
subject: Environment

prelims.trap_factor: Statements 1 and 2 appear logical but are wrong—Ramsar doesn't mandate conservation of ALL wetlands, and the 2010 Rules were framed under the Environment Protection Act, not Ramsar.
prelims.elimination_hint: Only Statement 3 is correct (Rules cover catchment/drainage areas); Ramsar is non-binding on selection of specific sites, eliminating statements 1 and 2.
factual_hooks: Ramsar Convention is not mandatory for protecting ALL wetlands—nations choose Ramsar sites
Wetlands Rules 2010 based on Environment Protection Act 1986, not Ramsar directly
Rules include drainage/catchment areas of wetlands

mains.contemporary_relevance: India has the highest number of Ramsar sites in Asia as of 2023.

contrast_with: Ramsar sites (internationally designated) vs all wetlands (nationally protected)

question:
With reference to solar water pumps, consider the following statements :

1. Solar power can be used for running surface pumps and not for submersible pumps.
2. Solar power can be used for running centrifugal pumps and not the ones with piston.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Both statements assert false limitations of solar power—solar can run both surface and submersible pumps, and both centrifugal and piston pumps, trapping those with incomplete knowledge.
prelims.elimination_hint: Solar power drives DC/AC motors of any pump type; neither statement is true, so 'Neither 1 nor 2' (D) is the answer.
factual_hooks: Solar pumps can power both surface and submersible pump types
Solar energy can drive centrifugal as well as piston pumps via electric motors

mains.contemporary_relevance: PM-KUSUM scheme targets 3.5 million solar agricultural pumps by 2026.

contrast_with: Diesel pump vs solar pump economics for irrigation

question:
Consider the following statements :

1. Agricultural soils release nitrogen oxides into environment.
2. Cattle release ammonia into environment.
3. Poultry industry releases reactive nitrogen compounds into environment.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
subject: Environment

prelims.trap_factor: Candidates might think only industrial sources emit nitrogen compounds; all three agricultural/livestock sources are genuine emitters of reactive nitrogen.
prelims.elimination_hint: All three statements are scientifically correct—agricultural soils emit N2O, cattle emit NH3, and poultry releases reactive nitrogen compounds; select D.
factual_hooks: Agricultural soils emit nitrous oxide (N2O) through microbial denitrification
Cattle release ammonia through urine and manure decomposition
Poultry industry is a significant source of reactive nitrogen pollution

mains.contemporary_relevance: Reactive nitrogen management is increasingly important for India's air quality and water pollution goals.

contrast_with: Nitrogen fixation (beneficial) vs reactive nitrogen pollution (harmful)

question: Atal Innovation Mission is set up under the
option_d: Ministry of Skill Development and Entrepreneurship
subject: Governance

prelims.trap_factor: The name suggests DST or MSDE as the nodal ministry, but Atal Innovation Mission is under NITI Aayog, not a line ministry.
prelims.elimination_hint: AIM was set up under NITI Aayog in 2016 as India's flagship innovation promotion platform; eliminate all ministry options.
factual_hooks: Atal Innovation Mission (AIM) is set up under NITI Aayog
AIM promotes Atal Tinkering Labs in schools and Atal Incubation Centres

mains.contemporary_relevance: AIM has established 10,000+ Atal Tinkering Labs; relevant to India's science and technology governance.

contrast_with: AIM (NITI Aayog) vs Startup India (DPIIT) — different nodal agencies for innovation

question: On 21st June, the Sun
option_a: does not set below the horizon at the Arctic Circle
option_b: does not set below the horizon at Antarctic Circle
option_c: shines vertically overhead at noon on the Equator
option_d: shines vertically overhead at the Tropic of Capricorn
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Students confuse Arctic and Antarctic effects on June 21—the Sun doesn't set at Arctic Circle (midnight sun) but it does set at Antarctic Circle (polar night).
prelims.elimination_hint: June 21 = Summer Solstice = midnight sun at Arctic Circle; Sun shines overhead at Tropic of Cancer (not Equator or Capricorn); select A.
factual_hooks: June 21 (Summer Solstice): Sun overhead at Tropic of Cancer
Arctic Circle experiences midnight sun (no sunset) on June 21
Antarctic Circle experiences polar night on June 21

contrast_with: June 21 effects at Arctic vs Antarctic Circle—opposite experiences

question:
Consider the following statements :

1. St. Francis Xavier was one of the founding members of the Jesuit Order.
2. St. Francis Xavier died in Goa and a church is dedicated to him there.
3. The Feast of St. Francis Xavier is celebrated in Goa each year.

Which of the statements given above are correct?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Statement 2 says Xavier died in Goa—he actually died on Shangchuan Island (China) in 1552, not Goa; his remains were later brought to Goa.
prelims.elimination_hint: Statement 2 is wrong (Xavier died in China, not Goa); Statements 1 and 3 are correct, making C the answer.
factual_hooks: St. Francis Xavier was a co-founder of the Jesuit Order (1540)
Xavier died on Shangchuan Island near China in 1552; remains enshrined at Bom Jesus Basilica, Goa
Feast of St. Francis Xavier celebrated on December 3 in Goa

contrast_with: Xavier's death in China vs remains preserved in Goa—a common source of confusion

question: The money multiplier in an economy increases with which one of the following?
option_a: Increase in the Cash Reserve Ratio in the banks
option_b: Increase in the Statutory Liquidity Ratio in the banks
option_c: Increase in the banking habit of the people
option_d: Increase in the population of the country
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Increasing CRR/SLR actually reduces money multiplier by keeping more reserves idle; candidates confuse 'more reserves' with 'more money creation'.
prelims.elimination_hint: Money multiplier = 1/CRR; higher banking habit means less cash leakage → more deposits → higher multiplier; select C.
factual_hooks: Money multiplier = 1/reserve ratio; higher banking habit reduces cash leakage
Increasing CRR or SLR decreases the money multiplier
Higher banking penetration means more deposits cycle through the system

mains.contemporary_relevance: UPI and Jan Dhan accounts increasing banking habit, affecting money creation dynamics.

contrast_with: CRR (reduces multiplier) vs banking habit (increases multiplier)—opposite effects

question:
Consider the following statements in respect of the Laureus World Sports Award which was instituted in the year 2000:

1. American golfer Tiger Woods was the first winner of this award.
2. The award was received mostly by 'Formula One' players so far.
3. Roger Federer received this award maximum number of times compared to others.

Which of the above statements are correct?
subject: Governance

prelims.trap_factor: Statement 2 is wrong—Laureus Sportsman of the Year was won mostly by athletes from various sports, not predominantly Formula One; F1's Sebastian Vettel won it a few times.
prelims.elimination_hint: Tiger Woods won the inaugural award (2000); Roger Federer holds the record for most wins; F1 domination claim is false—making C correct.
factual_hooks: Tiger Woods won the first Laureus World Sports Award (2000)
Roger Federer holds the record for most Laureus Sportsman of the Year awards
The award is not dominated by Formula One

question:
Consider the following statements:

1. Other than those made by humans, nanoparticles do not exist in nature.
2. Nanoparticles of some metallic oxides are used in the manufacture of some cosmetics.
3. Nanoparticles of some commercial products which enter the environment are unsafe for humans.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Statement 1 is wrong—nanoparticles occur naturally (sea spray, volcanic ash, forest fires); many candidates assume all nanoparticles are man-made.
prelims.elimination_hint: Statement 1 is false (natural nanoparticles exist); Statements 2 and 3 are correct (cosmetics and environmental safety concerns)—select D.
factual_hooks: Natural nanoparticles exist: sea spray, mineral weathering, volcanic emissions
Zinc oxide and titanium dioxide nanoparticles used in sunscreens and cosmetics
Engineered nanoparticles may pose ecotoxicological risks

mains.contemporary_relevance: India's National Nanotechnology Initiative and regulation of nanomaterials in cosmetics is ongoing.

contrast_with: Engineered nanoparticles vs naturally occurring nanoparticles

question:
Consider the following pairs :
Region often mentioned in the news - Country

1. Anatolia - Turkey
2. Amhara - Ethiopia
3. Cabo Delgado - Spain
4. Catalonia - Italy

How many pairs given above are correctly matched?
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Cabo Delgado is in Mozambique (not Spain) and Catalonia is in Spain (not Italy)—pairs 3 and 4 are swapped, trapping those who know one but not the other.
prelims.elimination_hint: Only pairs 1 (Anatolia-Turkey) and 2 (Amhara-Ethiopia) are correctly matched; Cabo Delgado is in Mozambique and Catalonia is in Spain—select B.
factual_hooks: Anatolia is the Asian part of Turkey (Anatolian Peninsula)
Amhara is a region in Ethiopia—site of conflict in 2020s
Cabo Delgado is in northern Mozambique, not Spain
Catalonia is a region in northeastern Spain, not Italy

mains.contemporary_relevance: Amhara and Cabo Delgado have been conflict zones in 2020-23, frequently appearing in current affairs.

contrast_with: Cabo Delgado (Mozambique) vs Canary Islands (Spain)—different regions in news

question:
Consider the following statements :

1. Statement-I: Carbon markets are likely to be one of the most widespread tools in the fight against climate change.
2. Statement-II: Carbon markets transfer resources from the private sector to the State.

Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
option_a: Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II is the correct explanation for Statement-I
option_b: Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II is not the correct explanation for Statement-I
option_c: Statement-I is correct but Statement-II is incorrect
option_d: Statement-I is incorrect but Statement-II is correct
subject: Environment

prelims.trap_factor: Statement II is wrong—carbon markets transfer resources from polluters (private sector) to emission reducers (often also private), not to the State; candidates confuse carbon tax with carbon markets.
prelims.elimination_hint: Statement I is correct; Statement II is wrong (carbon markets are private-to-private, not private-to-state)—but the question says both are correct, making B the answer if II doesn't explain I.
factual_hooks: Carbon markets are seen as cost-effective tools for climate mitigation via cap-and-trade
Carbon markets transfer money from high-emitters to low-emitters—both typically private entities
Carbon markets are distinct from carbon taxes which do transfer to government

mains.contemporary_relevance: India launched its Carbon Credit Trading Scheme in 2023 under the Energy Conservation Act.

contrast_with: Carbon markets (private transfer) vs carbon tax (public revenue)

question: Which one of the following activities of the Reserve Bank of India is considered to be part of 'sterilization'?
option_b: Oversight of settlement and payment systems
option_c: Debt and cash management for the Central and State Governments
option_d: Regulating the functions of Non-banking Financial Institutions
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Sterilization involves neutralizing foreign exchange inflows to prevent money supply expansion—students often confuse OMOs with routine RBI functions like debt management.
prelims.elimination_hint: Sterilization = absorbing excess liquidity from forex inflows via Open Market Operations (selling government securities); select A.
factual_hooks: Sterilization: RBI sells government securities (OMO) to mop up excess liquidity from forex inflows
OMOs are the primary sterilization tool used by RBI
Sterilization prevents exchange rate appreciation from inflating money supply

mains.contemporary_relevance: RBI regularly uses OMOs for liquidity management and sterilization of forex flows.

contrast_with: Sterilized vs non-sterilized forex intervention—former neutralizes money supply impact

question:
Consider the following information:
| | Waterfall | Region | River |

1. Dhuandhar | Malwa | Narmada |
2. Hundru | Chota Nagpur | Subarnarekha |
3. Gersoppa | Western Ghats| Netravati |

In how many of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Gersoppa (Jog) Falls is on the Sharavathi River, not Netravati; and Dhuandhar is in Jabalpur (Madhya Pradesh), not Malwa plateau—two errors in three rows.
prelims.elimination_hint: Only row 2 (Hundru Falls on Subarnarekha in Chota Nagpur) is correct; Dhuandhar is on Narmada but in Jabalpur/Bhedaghat, not Malwa; Gersoppa is on Sharavathi—select A (only one correct).
factual_hooks: Hundru Falls: Subarnarekha River, Jharkhand (Chota Nagpur)
Dhuandhar Falls: Narmada River near Jabalpur/Bhedaghat, NOT Malwa
Gersoppa/Jog Falls: Sharavathi River, not Netravati

contrast_with: Sharavathi (Gersoppa) vs Netravati—two different Western Ghats rivers

question:
Consider the following :

1. Carabid beetles
2. Centipedes
3. Flies
4. Termites
5. Wasps

Parasitoid species are found in how many of the above kind of organisms ?
subject: Environment

prelims.trap_factor: Parasitoid species are found only among insects with complex life cycles; candidates often include centipedes (not parasitoids) or exclude lesser-known groups like carabid beetles.
prelims.elimination_hint: Parasitoids found in: wasps (most common), flies (tachinid flies), and carabid beetles—three of the five; centipedes and termites are not parasitoids; select B.
factual_hooks: Parasitoid wasps are the most diverse parasitoid group
Tachinid flies are major insect parasitoids
Carabid beetles include some parasitoid species
Centipedes and termites are not parasitoids

mains.contemporary_relevance: Parasitoid wasps increasingly used in IPM (integrated pest management) in agriculture.

contrast_with: Parasitoid (kills host) vs parasite (does not immediately kill host)

question:
Consider the following water bodies :

I. Lake Tanganyika
II. Lake Toné Sap
III. Patos Lagoon Through

how many of them does the equator pass?
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Lake Tanganyika appears to be near the equator but lies south of it; Tonle Sap is in Cambodia far from equator; Patos Lagoon is in Brazil, also south of equator.
prelims.elimination_hint: None of the three water bodies is crossed by the equator; Lake Tanganyika lies ~3-9°S, Tonle Sap ~12°N, Patos Lagoon ~31°S—select D (None).
factual_hooks: Lake Tanganyika lies between 3°S and 9°S—south of equator
Tonle Sap Lake (Cambodia) lies around 12-13°N
Patos Lagoon (Brazil) at approximately 31°S

contrast_with: Lake Victoria (equator crosses it) vs Lake Tanganyika (south of equator)

question:
Consider the following statements about turmeric during the year 2022-23 :

I. India is the largest producer and exporter of turmeric in the world.
Il. More than 30 varieties of turmeric are grown in India.
III. Maharashtra, Telangana, Karnataka and Tamil Nadu are major turmeric producing States in India.

Which of the statements given above are correct?
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Candidates may be uncertain about whether Andhra Pradesh (not listed) or Telangana is correct; the question uses 2022-23 data where Telangana is indeed a major producer.
prelims.elimination_hint: All three statements are correct for 2022-23: India leads global turmeric production/export, 30+ varieties grown, and Maharashtra/Telangana/Karnataka/Tamil Nadu are the major states—select D.
factual_hooks: India produces ~80% of world's turmeric and is the largest exporter
Over 30 varieties of turmeric cultivated in India
Maharashtra, Telangana, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu are top producing states

mains.contemporary_relevance: Turmeric's medicinal properties boosted demand post-COVID; India's turmeric exports reached record highs.

contrast_with: Turmeric (Curcuma longa) vs ginger—both tropical rhizome crops with similar geography

question:
