Consider the following statements :

I. If any question arises as to whether a Member of the House of the People has become subject to disqualification under the 10th Schedule, the President's decision in accordance with the opinion of the Council of Union Ministers shall be final.
II. There is no mention of the word 'political party' in the Constitution of India.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Statement I appears true as the Speaker decides defection cases but actually the Constitution says Speaker's decision is final—not President's; Statement II seems unlikely but is actually true—the Constitution uses 'political party' implicitly but doesn't define it in the body.
prelims.elimination_hint: Statement I is wrong (it's the Speaker/Chairman, not President, who decides per 10th Schedule Para 6); Statement II is also arguably wrong as 'political party' does appear in 10th Schedule—select D.
factual_hooks: 10th Schedule Para 6: Speaker/Chairman (not President) decides disqualification under anti-defection law
The term 'political party' appears in the 10th Schedule though not in the main body of Constitution

mains.contemporary_relevance: Anti-defection law under scrutiny after Maharashtra political crisis (2022) and Supreme Court rulings.

contrast_with: Speaker's quasi-judicial role in defection vs President's role in Governor appointment

question:
Consider the following statements regarding Al Action Summit held in Grand Palais, Paris in February 2025 :

I. Co-chaired with India, the event builds on the advances made at the Bletchley Park Summit held in 2023 and the Seoul Summit held in 2024.
II. Along with other countries, US and UK also signed the declaration on inclusive and sustainable Al.

Which of the statements given above is /are correct?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Statement II is wrong—the US and UK did NOT sign the Paris AI Action Summit's inclusive AI declaration; they declined to sign, citing sovereignty concerns.
prelims.elimination_hint: Statement I is correct (India co-chaired; builds on Bletchley 2023 and Seoul 2024); Statement II is wrong (US and UK did not sign)—select A.
factual_hooks: AI Action Summit held in Paris (Grand Palais), February 2025, co-chaired by France and India
Builds on Bletchley Park (2023) and Seoul (2024) AI Safety Summits
US and UK declined to sign the inclusive AI declaration at Paris 2025

mains.contemporary_relevance: AI governance is a top geopolitical issue; India's co-chairmanship signals its global digital ambitions.

contrast_with: AI safety governance (Bletchley focus) vs inclusive AI (Paris focus)—different emphases

question:
Which of the following are envisaged by the Right against Exploitation in the Constitution of India?

1. Prohibition of traffic in human beings. and forced labour
2. Abolition of untouchability
3. Protection of the interests of minorities
4. Prohibition of employment of children in factories and mines

Select the correct answer using the code given below:
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Abolition of untouchability (Article 17) and minority protection (Article 29-30) are under different fundamental rights categories—not under Right against Exploitation.
prelims.elimination_hint: Right against Exploitation (Articles 23-24) covers only: traffic/forced labour and child labour in factories/mines; select C (1 and 4 only).
factual_hooks: Article 23: Prohibition of traffic in human beings and forced labour
Article 24: Prohibition of child labour in factories, mines, etc.
Untouchability abolition is Article 17 (Right to Equality), not Right against Exploitation

mains.contemporary_relevance: Human trafficking and bonded labour remain current policy concerns covered under Article 23.

contrast_with: Right against Exploitation (Arts 23-24) vs Right to Equality (Arts 14-18)

question: Q2. (a) Carl von Clausewitz once said, "War is a diplomacy by other means." Critically analyse the above statement in the present context of contemporary geo-political conflict. (Answer in 150 words) 10
subject: Ethics

factual_hooks: Clausewitz's dictum links war to political objectives
Contemporary geopolitics involves hybrid warfare, proxy conflicts, and economic coercion

themes: relevance of Clausewitz in modern context

answer_structure.intro_approach: Clausewitz's assertion that war is diplomacy by other means reveals the deeply political nature of armed conflict, a thesis whose validity must be tested against contemporary geopolitical realities.
body_points: Clausewitz argued war is never an end in itself but a rational instrument of state policy
Contemporary validation: Russia-Ukraine war shows military means serving geopolitical objectives (NATO expansion deterrence)
Limitations: Non-state actors, terrorism, and nuclear deterrence complicate rational-actor model
Hybrid warfare blurs the diplomacy-war boundary further
Ethical critique: framing war as 'diplomacy' risks normalizing violence as a legitimate policy tool
answer_structure.conclusion_approach: While Clausewitz's insight remains analytically powerful, ethical governance demands prioritizing dialogue over armed coercion in resolving contemporary geopolitical disputes.

mains.contemporary_relevance: Russia-Ukraine conflict, Indo-Pacific tensions, and India's approach to border disputes all test this Clausewitzian framework.

contrast_with: Hard power (military) vs soft power (diplomacy) as tools of statecraft

question: Consider the map given below : The red line in the map is the
option_c: line of demarcation between India and Pakistan suggested by the Boundary Commission (1947)
option_d: route followed by the Young husband Expedition
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Students may confuse Durand Line (Afghanistan-Pakistan border) with McMahon Line (India-China/Tibet) or the Radcliffe Line (India-Pakistan Partition).
prelims.elimination_hint: Durand Line = Afghanistan-British India border (1893) agreed between Mortimer Durand and Afghan Amir; the map context of Afghan-Pakistan border points to A.
factual_hooks: Durand Line (1893): boundary between British India and Afghanistan, named after Mortimer Durand
McMahon Line: India-Tibet/China boundary (1914 Simla Convention)
Radcliffe Line: India-Pakistan partition boundary (1947)

mains.contemporary_relevance: Pakistan-Afghanistan tensions over Durand Line remain a live geopolitical issue.

contrast_with: Durand Line (Afghanistan border) vs McMahon Line (Tibet/China border)—commonly confused

question: Q2. (b) Keeping the national security in mind, examine the ethical dilemmas related to controversies over environmental clearance of development projects in ecologically sensitive border areas in the country. (Answer in 150 words) 10
subject: Ethics

factual_hooks: Environmental clearances for border road projects involve national security vs ecological trade-offs
EIA processes can be expedited in strategic zones under national security provisions

answer_structure.intro_approach: Border infrastructure development in ecologically sensitive zones presents a fundamental ethical tension between national security imperatives and environmental stewardship.
body_points: Strategic necessity: roads in border areas (Arunachal, Ladakh) are vital for troop mobilization and deterrence
Environmental cost: construction in Himalayas triggers landslides, disrupts biodiversity, and harms water sources
Ethical dilemma: compromising long-term ecological health for short-term security gains
Procedural ethics: EIA exemptions for border projects may bypass community rights
Balancing approach: phased development, green corridors, and technology-based monitoring
answer_structure.conclusion_approach: Ethical governance demands that border infrastructure decisions integrate transparent EIA, community consultation, and ecological safeguards without compromising legitimate national security needs.

mains.contemporary_relevance: China's rapid border infrastructure vs India's delayed projects in Arunachal/Ladakh has intensified this debate.

contrast_with: Development imperative vs conservation ethic in border regions

question: Q1. What do you understand by values' and 'ethics'? In what way is it important to be hical along with being professionally competent? (150 words) 10
subject: Ethics

factual_hooks: Values are deep-seated beliefs about what is important; ethics is the system of moral principles guiding behaviour
Professional competence without ethical grounding can lead to misuse of expertise

answer_structure.intro_approach: Values represent our fundamental beliefs about what matters, while ethics translates those beliefs into principled action—both are indispensable for a civil servant.
body_points: Values: intrinsic beliefs (honesty, justice, compassion) formed through upbringing and experience
Ethics: systematic principles derived from values that guide decisions in specific contexts
Professional competence alone can be dangerous: technically skilled but ethically void officials cause harm (e.g., corruption, discrimination)
Historical examples: Nazi scientists show technical competence without ethical grounding is destructive
For civil servants: ethical competence ensures public trust, procedural fairness, and welfare-oriented outcomes
answer_structure.conclusion_approach: A civil servant must be both professionally competent and ethically grounded; competence without ethics is rudderless, while ethics without competence is ineffective.

mains.contemporary_relevance: ARC II recommendations emphasize ethics training in civil services induction programmes.

contrast_with: Values (what one holds important) vs Ethics (principles guiding action)—related but distinct

question: Bronze icons of Nataraja cast during the Chola period invariably show the deity with
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Candidates may guess six or eight hands associating Shiva with multiple arms; Nataraja specifically in Chola iconography always has four hands.
prelims.elimination_hint: Chola Nataraja bronze is a canonical image with four hands: upper right (damaru), upper left (flame), lower right (abhaya), lower left (pointing to raised foot)—select C.
factual_hooks: Chola Nataraja has four hands: damaru, fire, abhaya mudra, gajahasta
Nataraja represents Shiva as the cosmic dancer
Chola bronzes (9th-13th century) are finest examples of South Indian metal casting

contrast_with: Nataraja (four hands) vs Sadashiva (five faces, ten arms)—different Shaiva iconographic forms

question: Which one of the following is not an instrument of selective credit control in India ?
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Variable cost reserve ratios sounds like it controls volume of credit selectively, but it is actually a quantitative (general) credit control tool, not selective.
prelims.elimination_hint: Selective credit controls target specific sectors; variable cost reserve ratios affect all credit uniformly—making D the odd one out; select D.
factual_hooks: Selective credit controls: margin requirements, rationing of credit, consumer credit regulation
CRR/SLR are quantitative (general) controls, not selective
Selective credit tools target specific assets like shares, commodities

contrast_with: Quantitative (CRR/SLR/Bank Rate) vs Qualitative/Selective (margin requirements, credit rationing)

question: The term Yavanapriya, mentioned in ancient Sanskrit texts, denoted
option_a: a fine variety of Indian muslin
option_c: damsels sent to the Greek court for dance performance
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Yavanapriya literally means 'beloved of Yavanas (Greeks)' suggesting something luxurious; students might guess muslin or ivory, missing that pepper was the main trade commodity.
prelims.elimination_hint: Yavanapriya = pepper, the most prized Indian spice in Roman-Greek trade; muslin was known by other terms; select D.
factual_hooks: Yavanapriya ('beloved of Greeks') = black pepper in ancient Sanskrit texts
Pepper was India's most exported commodity to Rome and Greece
Sangam texts and Sanskrit sources reference Indian Ocean spice trade

contrast_with: Yavanapriya (pepper) vs Sirikol (muslin)—different luxury commodities in ancient trade

question: The Dinesh Goswami Committee was concerned with
option_c: steps to put down insurgency in the North-East
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: The Dinesh Goswami Committee sounds like it could relate to insurgency or ethnic issues; it was specifically about electoral reforms in 1990.
prelims.elimination_hint: Dinesh Goswami Committee (1990) = electoral reforms, including anti-defection, electoral rolls, and election finance; select B.
factual_hooks: Dinesh Goswami Committee (1990): comprehensive recommendations on electoral reforms
Committee recommended depoliticization of election machinery and reform of anti-defection law
Not related to banking, insurgency, or Chakma issue

contrast_with: Dinesh Goswami (electoral reforms) vs Sarkaria Commission (centre-state relations)

question: Which one of the following monuments has a dome which is said to be one of the largest in the world ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Gol Gumbaz in Bijapur has the second largest dome in the world; candidates often choose Jama Masjid or Sher Shah's tomb which are famous but smaller.
prelims.elimination_hint: Gol Gumbaz (Bijapur/Vijayapura) has one of the world's largest domes by diameter; select D.
factual_hooks: Gol Gumbaz, Bijapur: tomb of Muhammad Adil Shah with one of the largest domes globally
Built in 1656 CE
Famous for 'whispering gallery' inside the dome

contrast_with: Gol Gumbaz (Bijapur, largest dome) vs Taj Mahal (Agra, most famous dome)

question: Bank Rate implies the rate of interest
option_a: paid by the Reserve Bank of India on the Deposits of Commercial Banks
option_b: charged by Banks on loans and advances
option_d: at which the Reserve Bank of India discounts the Bills of Exchange
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Students may confuse Bank Rate with repo rate or lending rate; Bank Rate specifically refers to RBI rediscounting commercial paper/bills of exchange.
prelims.elimination_hint: Bank Rate = rate at which RBI rediscounts bills of exchange presented by commercial banks; select D.
factual_hooks: Bank Rate: rate at which RBI discounts bills of exchange and commercial paper
Distinguished from repo rate (collateralized short-term lending)
Bank Rate signals long-term monetary policy stance

contrast_with: Bank Rate (rediscounting, long-term) vs Repo Rate (collateralized, short-term)

question: In which one of the following crops international trade is low in the context of total produce ?
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Wheat and coffee might seem to have high international trade; rice actually has a small tradeable surplus globally despite being the largest food crop.
prelims.elimination_hint: Rice is mostly consumed domestically in producing countries (Asia); only a small fraction (~7%) of total production enters international trade—select A.
factual_hooks: Rice has the lowest international trade relative to production among major food crops
Asia consumes most of its rice domestically
Coffee, rubber, and cocoa have much higher trade-to-production ratios

contrast_with: Rice (subsistence crop, low trade) vs coffee/rubber (plantation crops, high trade share)

question: Ashtapradhan was a Council of Ministers
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Ashtapradhan is associated with Shivaji's Maratha state; candidates may confuse it with Gupta or Vijayanagar administrative systems.
prelims.elimination_hint: Ashtapradhan (council of eight ministers) was Shivaji's Maratha administrative innovation; select D.
factual_hooks: Ashtapradhan: Shivaji Maharaj's eight-minister council for Maratha administration
Peshwa was the most important among the eight ministers
Each minister had specific portfolios (Nyayadhish, Senapati, etc.)

contrast_with: Ashtapradhan (Marathas) vs Ashta Diggajas (Vijayanagar scholars)—similar names, different contexts

question: The Indian subcontinent was originally part of a huge land mass called
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Candidates may choose Jurassic Land Mass or Aryavarta; Gondwanaland is the established scientific term for the southern supercontinent.
prelims.elimination_hint: Gondwana Continent is the correct term for the ancient southern supercontinent that included India; select D.
factual_hooks: Gondwanaland: ancient supercontinent that broke apart ~180 million years ago
Included India, Africa, South America, Antarctica, Australia
India drifted north and collided with Eurasia to form Himalayas

contrast_with: Gondwana (southern) vs Laurasia (northern)—two components of Pangaea

question:
Other than the Fundamental Rights, which of the following parts of the Constitution of India reflect/reflects the principles and provisions of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948)?
1. Preamble
2. Directive Principles of State Policy
3. Fundamental Duties
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Students may limit UDHR reflection only to Fundamental Rights; the question tests knowledge that Preamble, DPSP, and Fundamental Duties all echo UDHR principles.
prelims.elimination_hint: Preamble (equality, dignity), DPSP (economic/social rights), and Fundamental Duties all reflect UDHR—all three are correct; select D.
factual_hooks: UDHR (1948) influenced India's Constitution adopted in 1950
Preamble's justice, equality, dignity echo UDHR Articles 1-2
DPSP Articles 39-51 mirror UDHR's social and economic rights
Fundamental Duties added by 42nd Amendment reflect UDHR's Article 29
paired_with: International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights

mains.contemporary_relevance: India's human rights record under UDHR framework is regularly reviewed at UN Human Rights Council UPR.

related_concepts: International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights
contrast_with: Fundamental Rights (legally enforceable) vs DPSP (non-justiciable) in relation to UDHR

question: Consider the, map given below : The route indicated in the map was followed, during the course of his military exploits, by
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: The map of a southward military route through Deccan could suggest Rajendra Chola or Chandragupta; Malik Kafur's campaigns under Alauddin Khilji followed this exact route.
prelims.elimination_hint: Malik Kafur, general of Alauddin Khilji, conducted campaigns into Deccan and South India (1306-1311); select D.
factual_hooks: Malik Kafur was Alauddin Khilji's general who conquered Deccan and South India
Campaigns reached Madurai, Dvarasamudra, and Warangal
Route from Delhi southward through Deccan corresponds to Malik Kafur's military exploits

contrast_with: Malik Kafur (Delhi Sultanate southward) vs Rajendra Chola (Chola northward expansion)

question: The clouds float in the atmosphere because of their low
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Students may choose 'low pressure' or 'low temperature'; clouds float due to low density relative to surrounding air, not pressure or temperature directly.
prelims.elimination_hint: Clouds are water droplets/ice crystals less dense than surrounding air; buoyancy keeps them aloft—select D (low density).
factual_hooks: Clouds float due to low density—water droplets/ice crystals suspended in air are less dense than surrounding atmosphere
Cloud formation requires cooling of moist air to dew point
Updrafts also help maintain cloud height

contrast_with: Low density (correct cloud float reason) vs low pressure (incorrect)

question: Which one of the following mountain ranges is spread over only one State in India ?
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Aravalli, Satpura, and Sahyadri all extend across multiple states; Ajanta Hills are confined to Maharashtra, making them the odd one out.
prelims.elimination_hint: Ajanta Hills (part of Satpura range system) are within Maharashtra only; Aravalli (Raj+Gujarat), Satpura (MP+Maha), Sahyadri (multiple states)—select C.
factual_hooks: Ajanta Hills are entirely within Maharashtra
Aravalli spreads across Rajasthan, Haryana, Gujarat, Delhi
Satpura Range extends across MP, Maharashtra, Chhattisgarh
Sahyadri (Western Ghats) spans multiple states from Gujarat to Kerala

contrast_with: Ajanta Hills (single state) vs Sahyadri (multi-state)—extent comparison

question: Q4. (b) Corporate social responsibility makes companies more profitable and sustainable. Analyse. (150 words) 10
subject: Ethics

factual_hooks: CSR under Companies Act 2013 mandates 2% profit spending for eligible companies
CSR can build brand reputation, employee morale, and community goodwill

answer_structure.intro_approach: Corporate Social Responsibility, far from being a cost, can function as a strategic investment that enhances both profitability and long-term sustainability.
body_points: CSR builds brand equity and consumer loyalty, translating to revenue gains
Reduces regulatory and reputational risks, lowering long-term costs
Attracts and retains ethical talent, improving productivity
Enhances community relations, reducing conflict risks near operations
However: short-term profitability may be affected; CSR can be misused as 'greenwashing' without genuine commitment
answer_structure.conclusion_approach: When genuinely integrated into business strategy rather than treated as compliance, CSR creates shared value that makes companies more resilient, trusted, and profitable.

mains.contemporary_relevance: India's mandatory CSR under Section 135 of Companies Act has channeled over ₹2 lakh crore since 2014.

contrast_with: Shareholder primacy model (Milton Friedman) vs stakeholder capitalism (CSR model)

question: In the field of space technology, India has demonstrated during 1994, her capability to
option_a: design advance intercontinental ballistic missile warning systems
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: In 1994 India demonstrated capability to launch geostationary satellites for the first time via PSLV; students may confuse satellite building (earlier) with geostationary launch (1994).
prelims.elimination_hint: PSLV-D2 in 1994 demonstrated India's capability to launch geo-stationary satellites; India had built satellites earlier but the launch milestone was 1994—select D.
factual_hooks: PSLV's first successful launch: October 1994 (PSLV-D2)
India demonstrated geostationary satellite launch capability in 1994
INSAT series had previously used foreign launchers

contrast_with: LEO launch capability (earlier) vs geostationary launch (1994 milestone)

question: One of the reasons for India’s occupational structure remaining more or less the same over the years has been that
option_a: investment pattern has been directed towards capital-intensive industries
option_b: productivity in agriculture has been high enough to induce people to stay with agriculture
option_c: ceilings on land holdings have enabled more people to own land and hence their preference to stay with agriculture
option_d: people are largely unaware of the significance of transition from agriculture to industry for economic development
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Students might choose agriculture-related reasons; the correct answer relates to capital-intensive industrial investment pattern that failed to create employment.
prelims.elimination_hint: High capital/output ratio means more capital spent per unit of output with less employment generation; investment in capital-intensive industries kept people in agriculture—select A.
factual_hooks: India's planned industrialization was capital-intensive, limiting job creation
High capital-output ratio means low employment per unit of investment
Agricultural sector absorbed surplus labour due to lack of industrial jobs

contrast_with: Capital-intensive (high K/O ratio, low employment) vs labour-intensive (low K/O, high employment) industries

question: Which one of the following pairs is correctly matched ?
option_a: Battle of Buxar .............. Mir Jafar vs. Clive
option_b: Battle of Wandiwash ............... French vs. East India Company
option_c: Battle of Chilianwala ............... Dalhousie vs. Marathas
option_d: Battle of Kharda ............... Nizam vs. East India Company
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Battle of Buxar involved Mir Qasim (not Mir Jafar); Battle of Chilianwala was against Sikhs (not Marathas); candidates mixing up these famous battles will choose wrong options.
prelims.elimination_hint: Battle of Wandiwash (1760) was a decisive British victory over the French (East India Company vs French); select B.
factual_hooks: Battle of Wandiwash (1760): French defeated by British (EIC) under Sir Eyre Coote
Battle of Buxar (1764): Mir Qasim, Nawab of Awadh + Mughal Emperor vs British
Battle of Chilianwala (1849): Second Anglo-Sikh War, not against Marathas

contrast_with: Battle of Wandiwash (Anglo-French) vs Battle of Plassey (Anglo-Nawab)

question: The word Adivasi was used for the first time to refer to the tribal people by

Founded Bharatiya Adimjati Sevak Sangh for tribal welfare
Worked closely with Gandhi for tribal upliftment

question: The concept of Anuvrata was advocated by
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Anuvrata means 'small vows' and candidates might confuse it with Buddhist vows; Anuvrata is specifically Jain—lesser vows for lay followers vs Mahavrata for monks.
prelims.elimination_hint: Anuvrata = small/minor vows prescribed for Jain lay followers (not monks); contrast with Mahavrata (great vows for monks); select C (Jainism).
factual_hooks: Anuvrata: five minor vows for Jain householders/laity
Mahavrata: five major vows for Jain monks (more strictly observed)
Anuvrata movement by Acharya Tulsi in 20th century revived these principles

question: The Barrah dacoity was the first major venture of the revolutionary terrorist of the freedom movement in

prelims.trap_factor: Barrah Dacoity sounds like it might be from Punjab (known for revolutionary activities); it was actually in East Bengal (now Bangladesh) by the Dhaka Anushilan Samiti.
prelims.elimination_hint: Barrah Dacoity (1908) was conducted by the Dhaka Anushilan Samiti in East Bengal to fund revolutionary activities; select C.
factual_hooks: Barrah Dacoity (1908): first major revolutionary heist in Indian freedom movement
Conducted by Dhaka Anushilan Samiti in East Bengal
Collected funds to finance armed revolutionary activities

question: The main reason for low growth rate in India, in spite of high rate of savings and capital formation is
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: High birth rate and low foreign aid seem intuitive causes; the real issue is structural—high capital-output ratio means investments yield less output and growth.
prelims.elimination_hint: High capital/output ratio means each unit of capital produces less output, resulting in low growth despite high savings; select D.
factual_hooks: High capital-output ratio = inefficient capital utilization = low growth per unit of investment
India's capital-intensive industrialization led to low employment and output growth
Harrod-Domar model: growth rate = savings rate / capital-output ratio

contrast_with: High savings but high ICOR → low growth vs low savings but low ICOR → moderate growth

question:
Consider the following :
I. Industrial Finance Corporation of India

ICICI established 1955

question: In 1930 Mahatma Gandhi started Civil Disobedience Movement from
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Many confuse Sabarmati Ashram (starting point) with Dandi (destination); Gandhi started Civil Disobedience Movement from Sabarmati Ashram, not Dandi.

Gandhi walked 241 miles to Dandi to break salt law on April 6, 1930

question: Which one of the following is a mixed fertilizer ?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Candidates may think urea or ammonium sulphate are mixed fertilizers; a mixed fertilizer contains two or more of N, P, K—NPK is the classic example.
prelims.elimination_hint: NPK fertilizer contains nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium—the three primary macronutrients; it is the definitive mixed fertilizer; select D.
factual_hooks: NPK = Nitrogen, Phosphorus, Potassium — the three primary plant macronutrients
Urea is a single-nutrient (N) fertilizer
Mixed fertilizers contain two or more primary nutrients

contrast_with: Single-nutrient (urea, DAP) vs mixed fertilizers (NPK)

question: Q3. (a) What is meant by conflict of interest ? Illustrate with examples, the difference between the actual and potential conflicts of interest. (150 words) 10
subject: Ethics

factual_hooks: Conflict of interest arises when personal interests could improperly influence professional judgment
Actual conflict: personal interest currently affecting decision; Potential conflict: could affect in future

answer_structure.intro_approach: Conflict of interest occurs when a public servant's private interests could compromise—actually or potentially—their impartial discharge of official duties.
body_points: Actual conflict: personal interest is currently and actively influencing a decision (e.g., official awarding contract to own company)
Potential conflict: interest exists that could influence future decisions (e.g., owning shares in a company one may regulate)
Perceived conflict: others reasonably believe interest may influence, even if it doesn't actually
Examples—actual: judge deciding case involving own investment; potential: minister with family land near proposed highway
Management: disclosure, recusal, divestiture are standard remedies
answer_structure.conclusion_approach: Managing both actual and potential conflicts of interest through transparency and institutional safeguards is essential to maintaining public trust in governance.

mains.contemporary_relevance: Conflict of interest norms for ministers, bureaucrats, and regulators are a recurring governance reform issue.

contrast_with: Actual conflict (present, ongoing) vs potential conflict (future, contingent)

question: Consider the map given below : The divisions along India’s coastal region indicate
option_c: the areas upto which sovereignty extends
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Without seeing the actual map, this question requires elimination based on plausible geographic contexts; underwater relief contours (isobaths) are a standard oceanographic map feature.
prelims.elimination_hint: Lines along India's coastal region on maritime maps typically represent isobaths (underwater depth contours); select D.
factual_hooks: Isobaths/bathymetric contours show underwater relief on coastal maps
Coastal sovereignty extends to 12 nautical miles (territorial sea)
Continental shelf extends to 200nm (EEZ)

contrast_with: Isobaths (depth contours) vs isobars (pressure contours)

question: Which one of the following pairs of materials serves as electrodes in chargeable batteries commonly used in devices such as torchlights, electric shavers, etc?

prelims.trap_factor: Zinc-carbon batteries are used in ordinary torches; nickel-cadmium (NiCd) are used in rechargeable devices like shavers—candidates often confuse primary and secondary batteries.
prelims.elimination_hint: Nickel-cadmium (NiCd) batteries are the rechargeable batteries used in shavers and portable devices; select A.
factual_hooks: Nickel-cadmium (NiCd): rechargeable secondary battery used in torches, shavers, power tools
Zinc-carbon: non-rechargeable primary battery
Lead-acid (lead peroxide + lead): car batteries

contrast_with: Rechargeable (NiCd, Li-ion) vs non-rechargeable (zinc-carbon, alkaline) batteries

question: Cryogenic engines find applications in
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.elimination_hint: Cryogenic engines burn liquid oxygen and liquid hydrogen at extremely low temperatures; used in upper stages of rockets (GSLV); select C.
factual_hooks: Cryogenic engines: burn liquid H2 (−253°C) and liquid O2 (−183°C) as propellants in rockets
India's GSLV uses cryogenic upper stage (CE-20 engine)
Higher specific impulse makes cryogenic engines ideal for geostationary satellite launches

mains.contemporary_relevance: India's cryogenic engine development was critical after technology denial; GSLV Mk III now uses indigenous CE-20.

contrast_with: Cryogenic (high thrust, complex) vs solid fuel (simpler, lower performance) rocket engines

question: Which one of the following fuels causes minimum environmental pollution ?
subject: Environment

prelims.trap_factor: Diesel and kerosene are common fuels; hydrogen produces only water when burned, making it the cleanest—candidates may overlook this due to familiarity with conventional fuels.
prelims.elimination_hint: Hydrogen combustion produces only water vapor—zero carbon emissions, zero particulate matter; select C.
factual_hooks: Hydrogen fuel: H2 + O2 → H2O only; no CO2, no SOx, no particulates
Green hydrogen produced via renewable electrolysis is fully clean
Diesel and kerosene emit CO2, SOx, NOx, PM2.5

mains.contemporary_relevance: India's National Green Hydrogen Mission (2023) targets 5 MT green hydrogen by 2030.

contrast_with: Hydrogen (cleanest) vs CNG (cleaner than diesel but still emits CO2)

question: Zero was invented by
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Aryabhata is commonly credited with zero; but historical evidence attributes the invention of zero (as a number/placeholder) to an unknown Indian mathematician—Aryabhata used it but didn't invent it.
prelims.elimination_hint: The concept of zero was developed by an unknown Indian mathematician; Aryabhata used it but historical records don't conclusively attribute invention to any named individual—select D.
factual_hooks: The inventor of zero is not definitively known—credited to 'an unknown Indian'
Earliest documented use of zero as a number: Bakhshali Manuscript (~3rd-4th century CE)
Aryabhata (5th century CE) used zero in his mathematical work but didn't claim to invent it

contrast_with: Aryabhata (astronomy/math innovator) vs unknown inventor of zero—different contributions

question: Q1. (a) Identify five ethical traits on which one can plot the performance of a civil servant. Justify their inclusion in the matrix. (Answer in 150 words) 10
subject: Ethics

factual_hooks: Civil servant performance matrix should include integrity, empathy, accountability, efficiency, and commitment to constitutional values
ARC II recommended multi-dimensional performance evaluation for civil servants

answer_structure.intro_approach: Evaluating a civil servant's performance requires a matrix that captures not just technical output but the ethical foundation underlying their conduct.
body_points: Integrity: adherence to rules and resistance to corruption—measurable through audit findings and whistleblower complaints
Empathy: sensitivity to public needs, especially marginalized groups—assessed through public feedback
Accountability: willingness to own decisions and be transparent—indicated by RTI responses, audit compliance
Impartiality: non-discriminatory service delivery—complaint ratios across demographic groups
Commitment to constitutional values: respect for rights and democratic norms—peer review and judicial observations
answer_structure.conclusion_approach: A performance matrix combining these five ethical traits with technical outcomes would ensure civil servants are evaluated holistically as public trustees rather than mere bureaucrats.

mains.contemporary_relevance: DARPG's Mission Karmayogi focuses on capacity and competency building including ethical dimensions.

contrast_with: Technical competence (skills) vs ethical traits (values)—both needed for civil service excellence

question: A person with ‘AB’ blood group is sometimes called a universal recipient because of the

option_b: lack of antibodies in his blood
option_c: lack of both antigens and antibodies in his blood

prelims.trap_factor: AB blood group has both A and B antigens but NO antibodies against these; candidates confuse 'lacks antigens' with 'lacks antibodies'.
prelims.elimination_hint: AB blood group has no antibodies in plasma (neither anti-A nor anti-B), so it can receive any blood type without agglutination—select B.
factual_hooks: AB blood group: has A and B antigens on RBCs, but NO antibodies in plasma
Universal recipient because absent antibodies means no rejection of donor blood
O negative is universal donor (no antigens on RBCs)

contrast_with: AB (universal recipient, lacks antibodies) vs O negative (universal donor, lacks antigens)

question: Most of the desert plants bloom during night time because

Described chemical bonding nature and protein alpha-helix structure

question:
Q7. Prabhat was working as Vice President (Marketing) at Sterling Electric Ltd,, a reputed multinational company. But presently the company was passing through the difficult times as the sales were continuously showing downward trend in the last two quarters. His division, which hitherto had been a major revenue contributor to the company's financial health, was now desperately trying to procure some big vernment order for them. But their best efforts did not yield any positive success or breakthrough.

His was a professional company and his local bosses were under pressure from their London-based HO to show some positive results. In the last performance review meeting taken by the Executive Director (India Head), he was reprimanded for his poor performance. He assured them that his division is working on a special contract from the Ministry of Defence for a secret installation near Gwalior and tender is being submitted shortly.

He was under extreme pressure and he was deeply perturbed. What aggravated the situation further was a warning from the top that if the deal is not clinched in favour of the company, his division might have to be closed and he may have to quit his lucrative job.

There was another dimension which was causing him deep mental torture and agony. This pertained to his personal precarious financial health. He was a single earner in the family with two school-college going children and his old ailing mother. The heavy expenditure on education and medical was causing a big strain to his monthly pay packet. Regular EMI for housing loan taken from bank was unavoidable and any default would render him liable for severe legal action.

In the above backdrop, he was hoping for some miracle to happen. There was sudden turn of events. His secretary informed that a gentleman-Subhash Verma wanted to see him as he was interested in the position of Manager which was to be filled in by him in the company. He further brought to his notice that his CV has been received through the office of the Minister of Defence.

During interview of the candidate—Subhash Verma, he found him technically sound, resourceful and experienced marketeer. He seemed to be well-conversant th tendering procedures and having knack of follow-up and liaising in this regard. Prabhat felt that he was better choice than the rest of the candidates who were recently interviewed by him in the last few days.

Subhash Verma also indicated that he was in possession of the copies of the bid documents that the Unique Electronics Ltd. would be submitting the next day to the Defence Ministry for their tender. He offered to hand over those documents subject to his employment in the company on suitable terms and conditions. He made it clear that in the process, the Sterling Electric Ltd. could outbid their rival company and get the bid and hefty Defence Ministry order. He indicated that it will be win-win situation for both-him and the company.

Prabhat was absolutely stunned. It was a mixed feeling of shock and thrill. He was uncomfortable and perspiring. If accepted, all his problems would vanish instantly and he may be rewarded for securing the much awaited tender and thereby boosting company's sales and financial health. He was in a fix as to the future course of action. He was wonder-struck at the guts of Subhash Verma in having surreptitiously removing his own company papers and offering to the rival company for a job. Being an experienced person, he was examining the pros and cons of the proposal/ situation and he asked him to come the next day.

(a) Discuss the ethical issues involved in the case.

(b) Critically examine the options available to Prabhat in the above situation.

(c) Which of the above would be the most appropriate for Prabhat and why? (Answer in 250 words) 20
subject: Ethics

factual_hooks: Corporate espionage involves theft of confidential business information
Whistleblowing vs complicity in unethical acts is a central ethical dilemma

answer_structure.intro_approach: Prabhat's situation presents a classic ethical dilemma where personal financial vulnerability creates pressure to compromise professional integrity and participate in corporate espionage.
body_points: Ethical issues: (a) Corporate espionage—Subhash Verma's offer involves theft of competitor's bid documents, a clear legal and ethical violation; (b) Conflict of interest—Prabhat's personal financial crisis impairs objective judgment; (c) Complicity—accepting the offer makes Prabhat party to unethical and illegal acts; (d) Breach of trust—undermines fair competition and business ethics norms

Options available: (i) Accept offer—resolves financial crisis but involves legal risk, ethical compromise, and possible termination/prosecution; (ii) Reject and report to management—upholds integrity but may not secure his position; (iii) Reject and report to authorities—most ethical but risks personal consequences; (iv) Delay/stall—avoids immediate decision but doesn't resolve the dilemma

Most appropriate: Reject Subhash Verma's offer immediately. Report the attempt to management and if necessary to relevant authorities. Separately address financial issues through legitimate channels (bank negotiation, cost-cutting, alternative employment)
answer_structure.conclusion_approach: An ethical civil servant or professional must refuse to compromise integrity regardless of personal pressures—short-term gains from unethical acts invariably lead to long-term personal and organizational damage.

mains.contemporary_relevance: Corporate espionage and conflict of interest in public procurement are live governance and ethics concerns in India.

contrast_with: Short-term personal gain vs long-term ethical integrity and organizational trust

question: ‘The final Agent Orange raid in Vietnam took place in 1970;... areas have begun to bloom again. But 19 years after the war’s end, it seems plain that Agent Orange is killing and maiming human beings, something it never intended to do. The apparent toxic fall out from those clouds ... is a crop of human miseries including cancers, miscarriages and birth defects—that may persist for decades.” The offensive substance referred to in the above quotation is
option_b: a complex mixture of herbicides and weedicides used to increase agricultural output in the then South Vietnam under the US aid programme
option_c: a complex mixture of DDT and other insecticides used as aerial sprays for protection against malaria and other tropical diseases
subject: Environment

prelims.trap_factor: The passage hints at herbicides used as defoliants; candidates may choose the complex DDT option; but Agent Orange's toxic component was dioxin (TCDD contamination).
prelims.elimination_hint: Agent Orange was a defoliant (herbicide mixture) contaminated with dioxin (TCDD); select D—dioxin used as defoliants.
factual_hooks: Agent Orange: mixture of herbicides (2,4-D and 2,4,5-T) contaminated with dioxin (TCDD)
Used as defoliant in Vietnam War to destroy jungle cover
Dioxin causes cancer, birth defects, and long-term health impacts

contrast_with: Agent Orange (dioxin-contaminated herbicide) vs DDT (insecticide)—different chemical classes

question: “It begins as a single cell and grows into a merciless disease that claims millions of lives year after year. But scientists are steadily unlocking its mysteries, and the fight against it may now have reached a dramatic turning point. New discoveries promise better therapies and hope in the war against ...” The disease referred to in the above quotation is
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: The description 'begins as a single cell' could suggest HIV/AIDS; but 'merciless disease that claims millions of lives' with 'turning point in therapies' fits cancer precisely.
prelims.elimination_hint: The passage describes cancer—begins as a single abnormal cell, claims millions, therapies advancing rapidly; select A.
factual_hooks: Cancer originates from a single mutant cell undergoing uncontrolled division
Leading cause of mortality globally alongside cardiovascular disease
Immunotherapy and targeted therapy represent modern 'turning points' in cancer treatment

contrast_with: Cancer (single cell → tumor) vs AIDS (viral infection)—both kill millions but different pathology

question: In track meets both 100 yards and 100 metres are used as distances. By how many metres is 100 metres longer than 100 yards ?
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Students may confuse yards and metres; 1 yard = 0.9144 metres, so 100 yards = 91.44 m; difference = 100 - 91.44 = 8.56 m.
prelims.elimination_hint: 100 metres − 100 yards (91.44 m) = 8.56 m; select B.
factual_hooks: 1 yard = 0.9144 metres
100 yards = 91.44 metres
Difference: 100 m − 91.44 m = 8.56 m

question: Seven men, A, B, C, D, E, F and G are standing in a queue in that order. Each one is wearing a cap of a different colour like violet, indigo, blue, green, yellow, orange and red. D is able to see in front of him green and blue but not violet. E can see violet and yellow, but not red. G can see caps of all colours other than orange. If E is wearing an indigo coloured cap, then the colour of the cap worn by F is
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: The complex visibility constraints require careful mapping; candidates often misplace cap positions when working backwards from what each person can see.
prelims.elimination_hint: Working backward: G (last) can't see orange → orange is G's cap; E wears indigo → F is between E and G; from visibility clues, F wears red; select C.
factual_hooks: Queue order: A B C D E F G (front to back)
Each sees caps of those ahead; G sees all except own cap's colour
E wears indigo; G can't see orange → orange on G; deduce F wears red

question: A person travelled from one place to another at an average speed of 40 kilometres/hour and back to the original place at an average speed of 50 kilometres/hour. What is his average speed in kilometres/hour during the entire round-trip ?
option_d: Impossible to find out unless the distance between the two places is known
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: The naive answer of 45 (simple average) is extremely tempting. Students forget that average speed = total distance / total time, requiring the harmonic mean formula.
prelims.elimination_hint: Eliminate 45 immediately as it is the arithmetic mean, not harmonic mean. Use 2*40*50/(40+50) = 4000/90 = 400/9.
factual_hooks: Average speed for equal distances = 2ab/(a+b) (harmonic mean)
400/9 ≈ 44.4 km/hr, always less than arithmetic mean

contrast_with: Arithmetic mean of speeds — valid only when time spent is equal, not distance

question: A thief running at 8 km/hr is chased by a policeman whose speed is 10 km/hr. If the thief is 100 metres ahead of the policeman, then the time required for the policeman to catch the thief will be
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Students may try to convert 100 metres to km or confuse relative speed direction. The relative speed approach is straightforward but unit conversion (km/hr to minutes) trips many.
prelims.elimination_hint: Relative speed = 10-8 = 2 km/hr. Gap = 100 m = 0.1 km. Time = 0.1/2 hr = 0.05 hr = 3 minutes. Eliminate all other options.
factual_hooks: Relative speed when chasing = difference of speeds
100 m = 0.1 km; 1 hr = 60 min

contrast_with: Relative speed in opposite direction = sum of speeds

question: A table has three drawers. It is known that one of the drawers contains two silver coins, another contains two gold coins and the third one contains a silver coin and a gold coin. One of the drawers is opened at random and a coin is drawn. It is found to be a silver coin. What is the probability that the other coin in the drawer is a gold coin ?
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Most students answer 1/3 or 1/2 by assuming equal probability among drawers. The correct approach uses conditional probability (Bayes' theorem): given a silver coin is drawn, the probability it came from the mixed drawer is 1/3, not 1/2.
prelims.elimination_hint: There are 3 silver coins total: 2 in SS drawer, 1 in SG drawer. If silver drawn, P(from SG drawer) = (1/3 * 1/2) / (1/3 * 1 + 1/3 * 1/2) = 1/6 / (1/2) = 1/3... recalc: P = 1/4. Answer is 0.25.
factual_hooks: Conditional probability uses Bayes theorem
P(mixed | silver shown) = P(silver|mixed)*P(mixed) / P(silver) = (1/2)(1/3) / (3/6) = 1/3 of 3/4 = 1/4 = 0.25

contrast_with: Simple probability vs conditional probability

question: In the Cartesian plane four points P, Q, R, S have co-ordinates (1, 1), (4, 2), (4, 4) and (1, 4). The area of the quadrilateral PQRS is
option_d: Impossible to find unless the lengths of the diagonals are known
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Option D (impossible without diagonal lengths) is a strong distractor. Students unfamiliar with coordinate geometry area formulas may think more information is needed.
prelims.elimination_hint: Use the Shoelace formula or divide the quadrilateral into triangles. Plot points: P(1,1), Q(4,2), R(4,4), S(1,4). Area = 7.5 sq units. Eliminate D immediately.
factual_hooks: Shoelace formula: Area = ½|Σ(x_i * y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} * y_i)|
Coordinate geometry uniquely determines area of any polygon

contrast_with: Area from diagonals (only for rhombus/kite) vs coordinate method

question: To prevent recurrence of scams in Indian Capital Market, the Government of India has assigned regulatory powers to
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: RBI is a common wrong choice as it is the apex bank; students conflate banking regulation with capital market regulation. SBI (appearing twice in options) is a confusion in the question itself.
prelims.elimination_hint: SEBI (Securities and Exchange Board of India) is the dedicated regulator for capital markets. RBI regulates banks; SBI is a commercial bank.
factual_hooks: SEBI established 1988, statutory body since 1992 under SEBI Act
SEBI regulates capital markets, stock exchanges, mutual funds

mains.contemporary_relevance: SEBI's expanding jurisdiction over crypto assets and alternative investment funds remains relevant.

contrast_with: RBI — regulates banks and monetary policy, not stock markets

question:
Consider the table given below providing some details of the results of the election to the Karnataka State Legislative . In terms of electoral analysis, the voter-seat distortion is to be explained as the result of the adoption of the
Political Party | Percentage of popular votes obtained | Number of seats secured
Janata Dal | 3.6 | 1.16
Congress | 3.1 | 3.5
BJP | 2.0.4 | 4.0
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: The Hare system (proportional representation) seems plausible as it deals with proportional vote-seat ratios; students may confuse it with FPTP.
prelims.elimination_hint: First-Past-The-Post (FPTP) inherently causes vote-seat distortion: a party winning by small margins in many constituencies gets disproportionately more seats. This is the classic critique of FPTP.
factual_hooks: FPTP leads to manufactured majorities — small vote leads swing produces large seat swings
Hare system = STV proportional representation, minimizes distortion

mains.contemporary_relevance: Debate on electoral system reform and proportional representation continues in India.

contrast_with: Hare/STV system — minimizes vote-seat distortion vs FPTP which amplifies it

question: Who among the following was a Brahmavadini who composed some hymns of the Vedas ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Gargi is widely known as a female Vedic scholar and is the most popular wrong answer; she participated in debates but is not credited with composing Vedic hymns. Lopamudra composed hymns in Rigveda.
prelims.elimination_hint: Lopamudra is attributed with composing hymns in the Rigveda (she and Agastya). Gargi was a philosopher in Upanishadic debates, not a hymn composer.
factual_hooks: Lopamudra — wife of sage Agastya, composed Rigveda hymns (Brahmavadini)
Gargi Vachaknavi — Upanishadic philosopher, not hymn composer

contrast_with: Gargi — debater in Upanishads vs Lopamudra — Rigveda hymn composer

question: Most guitarists are bearded males. If A represents all males, B represents bearded males and C represents all male guitarists, then the correct diagram for their relation (shaded portion) is
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Since the premise says 'most' guitarists are bearded males (not 'all'), C must be a proper subset of B (bearded males), with C entirely within A. The overlap is partial, not complete.
prelims.elimination_hint: All guitarists are males (C ⊂ A), most but not all guitarists are bearded (C mostly overlaps B but not fully). The diagram showing C as a subset of A, with C mostly inside B, is correct.
factual_hooks: 'Most' implies partial overlap, not complete inclusion
Venn diagram logic: C⊂A; C ∩ B = most of C

contrast_with: 'All' implies complete subset vs 'Most' implies partial overlap

question:
Which of the following is/are extra-constitutional and extra-legal device(s) for securing cooperation and coordination between the States in India ?
I. The National Development Council
II. The Governors’ Conference
III. Zonal Councils
IV. The Inter-State Council.
Choose the correct answer from the codes given below :
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: The Inter-State Council (IV) is actually a constitutional body under Article 263, so it is NOT extra-constitutional. Students often include it. Zonal Councils are statutory (not constitutional), and NDC and Governors' Conference are extra-constitutional.
prelims.elimination_hint: Inter-State Council has constitutional backing (Art. 263); eliminate options with IV. NDC, Governors' Conference, and Zonal Councils are extra-constitutional/statutory. Answer: I, II and III.
factual_hooks: NDC — no constitutional basis, set up by executive resolution
Zonal Councils — statutory under States Reorganisation Act 1956
Inter-State Council — constitutional under Article 263

mains.contemporary_relevance: Debate on strengthening Inter-State Council as a forum for cooperative federalism.

contrast_with: Constitutional bodies (Art. 263) vs extra-constitutional bodies (NDC, Governors Conference)

question: The Alpine vegetation in the western Himalayas is found only up to a height of 3000 metres, while in the eastern Himalayas it is found up to a height of 4000 metres,. The reason for this variation in the same mountain range is that
option_a: eastern Himalayas are higher than western Himalayas
option_b: eastern Himalayas are nearer to the Equator and sea coast than western Himalayas
option_c: eastern Himalayas get more monsoon rainfall than the western Himalayas
option_d: eastern Himalayan rocks are more fertile than the western Himalayan rocks
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Option A (eastern Himalayas are higher) seems intuitive but is factually wrong — the western Himalayas are generally higher. Option C (more monsoon rainfall) affects density but not the altitude threshold.
prelims.elimination_hint: The key factor is proximity to the Equator → higher temperatures at same altitude → vegetation zones shift upward in eastern Himalayas. Proximity to sea coast also moderates temperature. Correct: B.
factual_hooks: Tree-line altitude increases toward equator due to higher temperatures
Eastern Himalayas: closer to equator, maritime influence → vegetation zones at higher altitudes

mains.contemporary_relevance: Climate change is altering tree-line altitudes across the Himalayas.

contrast_with: Western Himalayas — continental, drier, tree line lower vs Eastern Himalayas — maritime, tree line higher

question: Which one of the following is correct In respect of the commencement of the election process in India ?
option_a: The recommendation for election is made by the government and the notification for election is issued by the Election Commission
option_b: The recommendation for election is made by the Election Commission and the notification for election is issued by the Home Ministry at the Centre and Home Departments in the States
option_c: The recommendation for election is made by the Election Commission and the notification for election is issued by the President and Governors of the States concerned
option_d: Both the exercises of making a recommendation for election and that of issuing a notification in respect of it are done by the Election Commission
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Option D (both done by ECI) seems modern and empowering but is incorrect. Students confuse the role of EC in recommending vs the constitutional role of President/Governors in issuing notifications.
prelims.elimination_hint: Under Articles 324/85/174, the President (for Lok Sabha) and Governors (for State assemblies) issue election notifications on the advice of the Election Commission. Correct: C.
factual_hooks: Election Commission recommends dates; President/Governors issue formal notification
Article 324 — ECI superintendence; Articles 85 & 174 — dissolution and election summons

mains.contemporary_relevance: Debates around ECI autonomy and appointment process of Election Commissioners.

contrast_with: ECI recommends vs President/Governor formally notifies — a constitutional division of roles

question: Q4. (a) Which eminent personality has inspired you the most in the context of ethical conduct in life? Give the gist of his/her teachings. Giving specific examples, describe how you have been able to apply these teachings for your own ethical development. (150 words) 10
subject: Ethics

factual_hooks: Role models in ethics: Gandhi, Lincoln, Ambedkar frequently cited
Ethical development through internalizing teachings — not just knowing them

answer_structure.intro_approach: The foundation of ethical conduct lies in the inspiration drawn from eminent personalities whose lives demonstrate the practical application of moral principles.
body_points: Identify the eminent personality (e.g., Gandhi — truth and non-violence; Ambedkar — social justice; Lincoln — integrity) and briefly describe their ethical teachings
Provide specific instances where their teachings guided personal ethical decisions (e.g., refusing to compromise on honesty under pressure, standing up against injustice)
Reflect on how these teachings were internalized and transformed personal behavior and decision-making over time
answer_structure.conclusion_approach: True ethical development involves not merely admiring role models but actively translating their values into consistent personal conduct in both public and private spheres.

mains.contemporary_relevance: In an era of declining institutional trust, personal ethical conduct inspired by role models remains a cornerstone of public service integrity.

contrast_with: Descriptive ethics vs normative ethics

question:
The standard time of the following countries is ahead or behind Greenwich Mean Time depending on whether they are east or west of the longitude passing through Greenwich :
I. Cuba
II. Greece
III. Iraq
IV. Costa Rica
V. Japan
Which one of the following sequential orders gives the correct arrangement of the countries according to their standard time from ahead to behind GMT?
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Countries must be arranged by longitude (east = ahead of GMT, west = behind). Students often mix up time zones for Cuba and Costa Rica (both west) or confuse Iraq and Greece positions.
prelims.elimination_hint: Japan (GMT+9) is furthest ahead; Iraq (GMT+3), Greece (GMT+2), Cuba (GMT-5), Costa Rica (GMT-6) is furthest behind. Order: Japan > Iraq > Greece > Cuba > Costa Rica = V, III, II, I, IV.
factual_hooks: East of Greenwich = ahead of GMT; West = behind GMT
Japan GMT+9; Iraq GMT+3; Greece GMT+2; Cuba GMT-5; Costa Rica GMT-6

contrast_with: IST (GMT+5:30) vs GMT+5:30 — India does not follow daylight saving

question:
Which of the following are the objectives of the Commission for Agricultural Costs and Prices (CACP) ?
I. To stabilise agricultural prices.
II. To ensure meaningful real income levels to the farmers.
III. To protect the interest of the consumers by providing essential agricultural commodities at reasonable rates through public distribution system.
IV. To ensure maximum price for the farmer.
Select the correct answer from the codes given below :
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Option IV (maximum price) is a trap — CACP recommends Minimum Support Price (MSP), not maximum price. Students often add IV thinking it protects farmers.
prelims.elimination_hint: CACP objectives are: stabilize prices (I), ensure real income to farmers (II), and protect consumers via PDS (III). Maximizing price (IV) is NOT an objective — MSP is a floor, not a ceiling or maximum.
factual_hooks: CACP recommends MSP — Minimum Support Price, not maximum price
CACP set up in 1965; recommends MSP for 23 crops

mains.contemporary_relevance: Debate on legal guarantee for MSP continues to be a key farmer demand in India.

contrast_with: MSP (floor price protecting farmers) vs Maximum Selling Price (ceiling protecting consumers)

question:
Which of the following are matters on which a constitutional amendment is possible only with the ratification of the legislatures of not less than one-half of the States ?
I. Election of the President
II. Representation of States in Parliament
III. Any of the Lists in the 7th Schedule
IV. Abolition of the Legislative Council of a State
Choose the correct answer from the codes given below : Codes :
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Abolition of State Legislative Council (IV) requires only a State resolution and Parliament's simple majority — NOT half-state ratification. Students often include it as a 'special' amendment.
prelims.elimination_hint: Under Article 368, election of President, representation of States in Parliament, and 7th Schedule Lists require half-state ratification. Abolition of Legislative Council only needs State resolution + Parliament ordinary majority.
factual_hooks: Article 368 — special majority + half state ratification for: President election, State representation in Parliament, 7th Schedule
Art. 169 — State LC abolition requires only State resolution + Parliament simple majority

contrast_with: Simple majority amendment vs special majority vs special majority + state ratification

question: In Sanskrit plays written during the Gupta Period women and sudras speak
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Students may confuse Pali (Buddhist texts) or Sauraseni (a Prakrit dialect) with the language of women/Shudras in Sanskrit plays.
prelims.elimination_hint: In Gupta-era Sanskrit plays, women and Shudras spoke Prakrit while upper-caste males spoke Sanskrit — a well-established convention. Eliminate Sanskrit, Pali, and Sauraseni.
factual_hooks: Prakrit — language of common people, women, and Shudras in Sanskrit drama
Sanskrit — language of elite male characters in classical plays

contrast_with: Sanskrit (elite, male) vs Prakrit (women, Shudras, common folk) in classical drama

question: The graph given below shows the mean average monthly temperatures (in °C) and mean monthly rainfall (in cms) of a place : This graph is indicative of which one of the climatic zones of the world ?
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Rainy tropical (Option B) and wet-and-dry tropical look similar; the graph's distinct dry season differentiates them. Without seeing the graph, students need to recall climograph characteristics.
prelims.elimination_hint: Wet and dry tropical (savanna) shows a distinct dry season with high temperatures year-round. Rainy tropical has no dry season. Temperate marine has moderate temperatures. The described graph with seasonal rainfall fits wet-and-dry tropical.
factual_hooks: Wet and dry tropical = savanna climate; distinct dry and wet season
Rainy tropical (equatorial) = rainfall throughout year with two peaks

contrast_with: Rainy tropical (no dry season) vs Wet-and-dry tropical (distinct dry season)

question:
Consider the table given below :
Which one of the following will fit in the place marked ‘X’ ?
option_a: Ministers who are not Members of Parliament but who have to get themselves elected to either House of Parliament within six months after assuming office
option_c: Not more than 20 representatives of Union Territories
option_d: The Attorney-General who has the right to speak and take part in the proceedings of either House of Parliament
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Option B (20 nominated members) is plausible but incorrect; Rajya Sabha has 12 nominated members, not 20. Option D (Attorney-General) is wrong as AG is not a member of Parliament.
prelims.elimination_hint: Rajya Sabha composition: elected by State/UT legislatures + up to 12 nominated by President. Not more than 20 representatives of Union Territories fits the context of the table about House of the People (Lok Sabha).
factual_hooks: Rajya Sabha — 12 nominated members; Lok Sabha — 2 Anglo-Indian nominated (since removed by 104th Amendment)
UT representation in Lok Sabha — not more than 20 seats

prerequisite_concepts: Rajya Sabha vs Lok Sabha membership
contrast_with: Rajya Sabha 12 nominated vs Lok Sabha 2 nominated (now abolished)

question: Who among the following have the right to vote in the elections to both Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha ?
option_a: Elected members of the Lower House of the Parliament
option_b: Elected members of the Upper House of the Parliament
option_c: Elected members of the Upper House of the State Legislature
option_d: Elected members of the Lower House of the State Legislature
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Students confuse who votes for Rajya Sabha vs Lok Sabha. Elected members of the Lower House of STATE legislature vote for Rajya Sabha but not Lok Sabha. General public votes for Lok Sabha.
prelims.elimination_hint: Rajya Sabha is elected by elected members of State Legislative Assemblies (Lower Houses of State Legislatures). Lok Sabha is elected by general public. Only elected MLAs vote for both indirectly through Rajya Sabha elections.
factual_hooks: Rajya Sabha members elected by State Legislative Assembly (Vidhan Sabha) members via STV
Lok Sabha directly elected by voters; Rajya Sabha indirectly elected by MLAs

contrast_with: Direct election (Lok Sabha) vs Indirect election (Rajya Sabha) by MLAs

question: The Nagara, the Dravida and the Vesara are
option_a: the three main racial groups of the Indian subcontinent
option_b: the three main linguistic divisions into which the languages of India can be classified
option_c: the three main styles of Indian temple architecture
option_d: the three main musical gharanas prevalent in India
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: The terms Nagara, Dravida, Vesara sound like they could be racial or linguistic categories given India's diversity context.
prelims.elimination_hint: These three are universally recognized as the three main styles of Indian temple architecture. Nagara (North India), Dravida (South India), Vesara (mixed/Deccan). Eliminate racial, linguistic, and musical options.
factual_hooks: Nagara style — shikhara tower; North Indian temples like Khajuraho
Dravida style — gopuram tower; South Indian temples like Brihadeeswarar
Vesara — hybrid style; Chalukya and Hoysala temples

contrast_with: Nagara (curvilinear shikhara) vs Dravida (pyramidal gopuram)

question: In the interim government formed in 1946, the Vice-President of the Executive Council was
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Students may confuse Nehru's position with that of the President of Congress in 1946; he was Vice-President of the Executive Council (equivalent to PM), not formally President of Congress at that time.
prelims.elimination_hint: Jawaharlal Nehru was the Vice-President of the Interim Government's Executive Council (September 1946), effectively India's first Prime Minister before Independence. Correct: A.
factual_hooks: Interim Government formed September 1946; Nehru as VP of Executive Council
Viceroy Wavell remained as Governor-General; Nehru headed the council

contrast_with: Interim Government 1946 (VP = Nehru) vs First Cabinet after Independence (PM = Nehru)

question:
Which of the following pairs are correctly matched ?
I. Increase in foreign exchange reserves ................. Monetary expansion
II. Low import growth rate in India ................. Recession in Indian Industry
III. Euro-issues ................ Shares held by Indian companies in European countries
IV. Portfolio investment ...................... Foreign institutional investors
Select the correct answer by using the following codes : Codes :
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Statement III is the key trap: Euro-issues are shares of Indian companies listed on European exchanges (GDRs/ADRs), NOT shares held IN European countries. Statement accuracy hinges on this fine distinction.
prelims.elimination_hint: Statement III is incorrect — Euro-issues (GDR/ADR) are Indian company shares listed on foreign exchanges, but not 'shares held by Indian companies in European countries.' I, II, IV are correct. Answer: A (I, II and IV).
factual_hooks: Euro-issues = GDR/ADR — Indian companies raising capital from foreign markets via depositary receipts
Portfolio investment = FII investment in stock/bond markets (not FDI)

mains.contemporary_relevance: India's current account and capital account management remains a key policy area.

contrast_with: FDI (direct control) vs FII/Portfolio investment (financial market investment only)

question:
Article 156 of the Constitution of India provides that a Governor shall hold office for a term of five years from the date on which he enters upon his office.
Which of the following can be deduced from this ?
I. No Governor can be removed from office till completion of his term.
II. No Governor can continue in office beyond a period of five years.
Select the correct answer from the codes given below :
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: The constitutional text says 'five years from the date of entry' but this is qualified — Governor holds office at President's pleasure (Art. 156(1)), so neither deduction I nor II is correct.
prelims.elimination_hint: Art. 156 provides 5-year term BUT Art. 156(1) says Governor holds office 'during the pleasure of the President.' So Governor CAN be removed before 5 years and CAN continue beyond if asked. Neither I nor II can be deduced. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Art. 156(1) — Governor holds office at pleasure of President (removable anytime)
Art. 156(3) — normal term 5 years, but can be extended or cut short

mains.contemporary_relevance: Frequent controversies over Governors' conduct in politically sensitive states highlight importance of understanding tenure provisions.

contrast_with: Fixed term of SC/HC judges vs pleasure-based tenure of Governor

question: Prohibition of discrimination on grounds of religion, etc. (Article 15 of the Constitution of India) is a Fundamental Right classifiable under
option_a: the Right to Freedom of Religion
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Students may confuse Article 15 (discrimination) with Article 25-28 (Freedom of Religion) since the question mentions 'religion.'
prelims.elimination_hint: Article 15 prohibits discrimination on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex, place of birth — this falls squarely under Right to Equality (Articles 14-18), not Freedom of Religion.
factual_hooks: Article 15 — prohibition of discrimination = Right to Equality cluster (Art. 14-18)
Right to Freedom of Religion = Articles 25-28

contrast_with: Art. 15 (discrimination ban under equality) vs Art. 25 (freedom to practice religion)

question: According to the Mimamsa system of philosophy, liberation is possible by means of
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Bhakti (devotion) sounds most associated with liberation in popular understanding. Jnana (knowledge) is also a strong distractor.
prelims.elimination_hint: Mimamsa philosophy (one of the six Darshanas) emphasizes Karma (ritual action/duty) as the path to liberation, not Jnana or Bhakti. It is concerned with correct performance of Vedic rituals.
factual_hooks: Mimamsa — one of six Astika schools; stresses Karma (ritual action) for liberation
Advaita Vedanta stresses Jnana; Vaishnavism stresses Bhakti

prerequisite_concepts: Paths to liberation in Indian philosophy
contrast_with: Mimamsa (Karma/ritual) vs Vedanta (Jnana) vs Bhakti traditions

question: The Nobel Prize for deciphering the language of bees was awarded to
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: H.G. Khurana (Har Gobind Khorana) won Nobel Prize for decoding the genetic code, not bee language — a common confusion. Dorothy Hodgkins won for X-ray crystallography of biochemical substances.
prelims.elimination_hint: Karl von Frisch won the 1973 Nobel Prize in Physiology/Medicine for discovering the 'waggle dance' communication of bees. Eliminate Khorana (genetic code) and Hodgkins (crystallography).
factual_hooks: Karl von Frisch — Nobel 1973 for bee waggle dance/communication
H.G. Khorana — Nobel for genetic code (not bees)

contrast_with: K.V. Frisch (bee language) vs H.G. Khorana (genetic code) — common confusion

question: The palaeomagnetic results obtained from India indicate that in the past, the Indian land mass has moved
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Students unfamiliar with plate tectonics may guess random directions. The Gondwana drift theory clearly establishes northward movement of Indian plate.
prelims.elimination_hint: Palaeomagnetic evidence and continental drift theory both confirm India moved northward from Gondwanaland, eventually colliding with the Eurasian plate to form the Himalayas.
factual_hooks: Indian plate moved northward from Gondwanaland → Himalayan collision with Eurasian plate
Palaeomagnetic data confirms northward drift of Indian subcontinent

contrast_with: Gondwana (southern supercontinent) vs Laurasia (northern) in Pangea

question: Corporation tax
option_a: is levied and appropriated by the States
option_b: is levied by the Union and collected and appropriated by the States
option_c: is levied by the Union and shared by the Union and the States
option_d: is levied by the Union and belongs to it exclusively
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Option D (Union exclusively) sounds plausible since corporation tax appears in the Union List. But the Constitution mandates sharing corporation tax proceeds with states through the Finance Commission.
prelims.elimination_hint: Corporation tax is in Union List (levied by Union) but proceeds are shared between Union and States as per Article 270/271 on divisible pool. Correct: C.
factual_hooks: Corporation tax — Union List item, levied by Centre but shared with states
Articles 270-271 — divisible pool taxes shared between Centre and States

mains.contemporary_relevance: Debate over Centre's share of divisible pool and states' revenue autonomy is perennial.

contrast_with: Taxes assigned exclusively to states (e.g., land revenue) vs shared taxes (corporation tax, income tax)

question: In which one of the following States of India it is legal for a Hindu male and illegal for a Muslim male to have more than one living wife ?
subject: Society

prelims.trap_factor: Nagaland and Mizoram have tribal customs that could suggest different personal laws; students unfamiliar with Goa's civil code may guess tribal states.
prelims.elimination_hint: Goa follows the Goa Civil Code (Portuguese Civil Code), a Uniform Civil Code, under which both Hindus and Muslims are governed by the same marriage law — polygamy is illegal for both. Correct: C (Goa).
factual_hooks: Goa Civil Code — India's only state with a Uniform Civil Code for all communities
Under Goa Civil Code, polygamy is illegal for all including Muslims

mains.contemporary_relevance: The UCC debate intensified after the Supreme Court recommendations and various political promises.

contrast_with: Rest of India (community-specific personal laws) vs Goa (uniform civil code)

question: The radical wing of the Congress Party, with Jawaharlal Nehru as one of its main leaders, founded the ‘Independence for India League’ in opposition to
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: The Nehru Report (1928) could seem like the trigger for Independence League since it proposed dominion status; the league actually opposed the Nehru Report's moderate stance.
prelims.elimination_hint: The Independence for India League was formed in 1928 by Nehru, Subhas Bose and others to oppose the Nehru Report's acceptance of Dominion Status instead of demanding complete independence (Purna Swaraj).
factual_hooks: Independence for India League (1928) — opposed Nehru Report's Dominion Status goal
Lahore Congress 1929 — Purna Swaraj declared after Independence League pressure

contrast_with: Nehru Report (dominion status) vs Independence for India League (complete independence)

question: Agricultural income-tax is assigned to the State Governments by
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Finance Commission appears in the answer as it distributes resources between Centre and States, leading students to think it assigns taxes. But agricultural income tax is a State subject by constitutional provision.
prelims.elimination_hint: Agricultural income is a State List subject (Entry 46, List II of 7th Schedule). The Constitution itself assigns it to states — no Finance Commission or NDC intervention needed.
factual_hooks: Entry 46 — 7th Schedule List II — agricultural income tax assigned to States
Finance Commission deals with sharing of Union taxes, not State List subjects

contrast_with: State List taxes (agricultural income — States keep all) vs Divisible pool taxes (Corporation tax — shared)

question: The meeting of Indian and British political leaders during 1930-32 in London has often been referred to as the First. Second and Third Round Table Conferences. It would be incorrect to refer to them as such because
option_a: the Indian National Congress did not take part in two of them
option_b: Indian parties other than the Indian National Congress participating in the Conference represented sectional interests and not the whole of India
option_c: the British Labour Party had withdrawn from the Conference, thereby making the proceedings of the Conference partisan
option_d: it was an instance of Conference held in three sessions and not that of three separate conferences
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Students often say it was 'three separate conferences' — the question asks why calling them 'three conferences' is incorrect. Option D correctly captures that it was one conference in three sessions.
prelims.elimination_hint: The Round Table Conferences (1930-32) were three sessions of a single conference, not three distinct conferences. Hence calling them 'First, Second, Third' is technically a misnomer. Correct: D.
factual_hooks: Round Table Conferences 1930-32 — three sessions of one conference, not three separate conferences
1st RTC: no Congress; 2nd RTC: Gandhi attended; 3rd RTC: no Congress again

contrast_with: Three sessions of one conference vs three separate conferences — a subtle but important distinction
