question: Ecologists have so far known, found and certified a large number of species in the plant and animal kingdom. In terms of members, the largest found and identified so far are from among the
subject: Environment

prelims.trap_factor: Bacteria is often thought to be the most numerous — but the question asks for 'found and certified species', where insects vastly outnumber any other group in known biodiversity counts.
prelims.elimination_hint: Insects (Class Insecta) constitute approximately 70-80% of all known animal species — over 1 million identified species. Bacteria may be more numerous in individuals but not in identified/certified species.
factual_hooks: Insects — over 1 million described species, largest known group
Beetles alone account for ~400,000 species (Haldane's 'God's fondness for beetles')

mains.contemporary_relevance: Insect population decline ('insect apocalypse') is a key conservation concern.

contrast_with: Species richness (insects highest) vs biomass (bacteria highest) vs individual numbers (bacteria)

question: It is possible to produce seedless tomato fruits by
option_a: applying trace elements in tomato fields
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Students may think fertilizers or mineral solutions affect seed formation, but seedless fruit production specifically involves plant hormones (auxins/gibberellins) applied to flowers.
prelims.elimination_hint: Spraying plant hormones (auxins like NAA) on flowers triggers parthenocarpy — fruit development without fertilization/seed formation. Correct: C.
factual_hooks: Parthenocarpy — fruit without seed formation, induced by auxin/gibberellin spraying
Seedless grapes, tomatoes, watermelons produced via hormone treatment

mains.contemporary_relevance: Hormone-based agriculture and GMO regulation are active policy areas.

contrast_with: Natural parthenocarpy (banana) vs hormone-induced parthenocarpy (tomatoes)

question:
Which of the following are associated with diabetes mellitus, a common disease in adults ?
I. Higher sugar level in blood
II. Lower sugar level in blood
III. Lower insulin level in blood
IV. Higher insulin level in blood
Select the correct answer by using the codes given below : Codes :
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Students may include higher insulin (IV) thinking the body overcompensates. In Type 2 diabetes, insulin resistance means insulin may initially be high but functionally low; in Type 1, insulin is definitively low.
prelims.elimination_hint: Diabetes mellitus is characterized by high blood sugar (I) AND low functional insulin (III). Lower sugar (II) and higher insulin (IV) are features of hypoglycemia, not diabetes. Correct: D (I and III).
factual_hooks: Diabetes mellitus — hyperglycemia (high blood sugar) + insufficient/ineffective insulin
Type 1: no insulin produced; Type 2: insulin resistance

mains.contemporary_relevance: India is the diabetes capital of the world with over 77 million diabetics.

contrast_with: Hyperglycemia (diabetes) vs Hypoglycemia (low blood sugar — low insulin/excess insulin)

question:
Consider the following statements : AIDS is transmitted
I.
by sexual intercourse.
II.
by blood transfusion.
III.
by mosquitoes and other blood sucking insects.
IV.
across the placenta.
Of these statements
option_a: I, II and III are correct
option_b: I, II and IV are correct
option_c: I, II and IV are correct
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Statement III (mosquitoes transmit AIDS) is a well-known misconception that many students still accept. HIV is not transmitted by mosquitoes.
prelims.elimination_hint: HIV/AIDS is transmitted by sexual intercourse (I), blood transfusion (II), and across placenta (IV). Mosquitoes do NOT transmit HIV — the virus does not survive in mosquito saliva. Correct: B (I, II and IV).
factual_hooks: HIV transmission: sexual contact, blood transfusion, mother-to-child (placenta/breast milk), shared needles
NOT transmitted by: mosquitoes, saliva, casual contact, air

mains.contemporary_relevance: India's NACP (National AIDS Control Programme) and HIV awareness remain relevant public health issues.

contrast_with: Malaria (mosquito-borne) vs HIV/AIDS (not mosquito-borne)

question:
Which of the following would lead to ‘malnutrition’ ?
I. Overnutrition
II. Undernutrition
III. Imbalanced nutrition.
Select the correct answer by using the codes gives below :
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Option A (undernutrition alone) seems the obvious answer, but malnutrition includes overnutrition (obesity-related diseases) and imbalanced nutrition as well.
prelims.elimination_hint: Malnutrition = any form of poor nutrition: undernutrition (I), overnutrition (I), and imbalanced nutrition (III). All three cause malnutrition. Correct: D.
factual_hooks: Malnutrition = under-nutrition + over-nutrition + dietary imbalance
WHO defines malnutrition as deficiencies, excesses, or imbalances in intake of energy/nutrients

mains.contemporary_relevance: India faces 'double burden' of malnutrition — both undernutrition (stunting) and overnutrition (obesity) simultaneously.

contrast_with: Undernutrition (too little) vs Overnutrition (too much) — both are malnutrition

question: People drinking water from a shallow hand pump are likely to suffer from all of the following diseases except
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Fluorosis is caused by excess fluoride in groundwater — which CAN come from shallow wells in fluoride-endemic areas. The question asks about shallow hand pumps contaminated by surface pathogens, not deep aquifer fluoride.
prelims.elimination_hint: Cholera, typhoid, and jaundice are waterborne diseases from surface/fecal contamination — common in shallow wells. Fluorosis comes from excess fluoride in deep groundwater aquifers, NOT typically from shallow pumps. Correct: D.
factual_hooks: Fluorosis — from excess fluoride in deep groundwater, not surface-contaminated shallow pumps
Cholera, typhoid, hepatitis A (jaundice) — fecal-oral route, shallow contaminated water

mains.contemporary_relevance: Fluorosis and arsenic contamination remain public health crises in parts of India.

contrast_with: Deep well fluoride (fluorosis) vs shallow well pathogens (cholera, typhoid)

question: Besides proteins and carbohydrates, other elements of nutritional value found in milk include
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Iron seems like an obvious milk nutrient. However, milk is notably low in iron — this is why breast-fed infants need iron supplements after 6 months.
prelims.elimination_hint: Milk contains calcium and potassium (and other vitamins/phosphorus) but is a poor source of iron. Eliminate options with iron. Correct: B (calcium and potassium).
factual_hooks: Milk — good source of calcium, potassium, phosphorus, vitamins B12 and D
Milk is LOW in iron — infants need complementary foods for iron after 6 months

contrast_with: Calcium-rich (milk) vs Iron-rich (green leafy vegetables, meat) foods

question:
Distance-time graph in respect of a race among four persons is shown in the given figure. Consider the following statements in this regard :
I.
‘A’ stood first in the race.
II.
‘C led all the way.
III.
‘D’ ran faster than others in the later part of the race.
Of these statements
option_a: I and III are false and II is true
option_b: I and II are false and III is true
option_c: I and III are true and II is false
option_d: I is true and II and III are false
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Without the actual figure, this requires reasoning about distance-time graphs. Statement II ('C led all the way') requires checking if C was always ahead, which may not be true if others overtook.
prelims.elimination_hint: In a race, whoever reaches the finish line first stands first. D ran faster later means D covered more distance per unit time in later stages. I and III being true (A first, D faster later) while II false (C did not lead all the way) is most internally consistent.
factual_hooks: Distance-time graph: steeper slope = faster speed
First in race = reached finish line (highest distance) first

question: When an air bubble at the bottom of a lake rises to the top, it will
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Students may overthink about bubble pressure vs buoyancy — the simple answer is that decreasing water pressure as bubble rises allows it to expand (Boyle's Law).
prelims.elimination_hint: By Boyle's Law (P1V1 = P2V2 at constant temperature), as the bubble rises, water pressure decreases → bubble volume increases → bubble size increases. Correct: A.
factual_hooks: Boyle's Law: P×V = constant at constant temperature
Water pressure decreases with decreasing depth → air bubble expands

contrast_with: Compression at depth vs expansion at surface — inverse relationship

question: A truck, a car and a motor cycle have equal kinetic energies. If equal stopping
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: The question seems to be missing part of its text about stopping distances. With equal kinetic energies and equal stopping forces, all vehicles stop in equal distance — but this is counterintuitive.
prelims.elimination_hint: KE = ½mv²; if KE is equal and stopping force (F) is equal, then stopping distance s = KE/F = equal for all. So x = y = z. Correct: C.
factual_hooks: Work-energy theorem: F×d = KE → d = KE/F; equal KE and equal F → equal stopping distance
Despite different masses/speeds, equal KE means equal stopping distance under equal braking force

contrast_with: Equal momentum (stopping distance proportional to mass) vs equal kinetic energy (equal stopping distance)

question: A liquid is flowing in a streamlined manner through a cylindrical pipe. Along a section containing the axis of the pipe, the flow profile will be
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Without the actual diagrams (I-IV), this requires knowledge of fluid dynamics. The correct flow profile for laminar flow in a pipe is parabolic — maximum at center, zero at walls.
prelims.elimination_hint: Streamlined (laminar) flow in a cylindrical pipe follows a parabolic velocity profile (Poiseuille flow) — maximum velocity at the axis, zero at the walls. The diagram showing this parabolic profile is correct.
factual_hooks: Poiseuille's Law — laminar flow in pipe has parabolic velocity profile
No-slip condition: fluid velocity = 0 at pipe walls

contrast_with: Laminar (parabolic profile) vs turbulent flow (flatter profile)

question: Q8. "Success of 'Make in India' programme depends on the success of 'Skill India' programme and radical labour reforms". Discuss with logical arguments. (200 words) 12.5
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Students may argue the question is about Skill India or Make in India separately, missing the interdependence logic the question tests.
factual_hooks: Make in India (2014) — manufacturing hub goal requires skilled workforce and flexible labour laws
Skill India Mission — National Policy for Skill Development and Entrepreneurship 2015

answer_structure.intro_approach: The Make in India initiative's potential to transform India into a global manufacturing hub is intrinsically linked to addressing twin supply-side constraints: a skilled workforce and flexible labour regulations.
body_points: Make in India requires competitive manufacturing: skill gaps (India's low vocational training enrolment vs China/Germany) directly raise production costs and reduce quality
Labour reforms needed: complex labour laws (44 Central + 100+ State laws) increase compliance burden; four Labour Codes aim to address this
Skill India must align with industry demand through PPP models, industry-linked curricula, and recognition of prior learning
Without both, FDI may go to Vietnam, Bangladesh due to better skill-cost competitiveness
answer_structure.conclusion_approach: Make in India can become a transformational programme only when Skill India provides the human capital foundation and labour reforms create the enabling regulatory environment for competitive manufacturing.

interlinkages: GS2 — Labour welfare and social security
GS2 — Governance and ease of doing business
mains.contemporary_relevance: India's PLI schemes and China+1 strategy make the Make in India-Skill India linkage more urgent than ever.

contrast_with: Supply-side (skill + labour flexibility) vs demand-side (investment climate) for manufacturing growth

question: Domestic electrical wiring is basically a
option_c: combination of series and parallel connections
option_d: series connection within each room and parallel connection elsewhere
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Option C (combination) sounds intuitively reasonable. Option D (series within room) is clearly wrong. The standard domestic wiring uses parallel connections so each appliance gets full voltage.
prelims.elimination_hint: Domestic electrical wiring uses parallel connections — each appliance gets the same full voltage (230V) and can be switched independently. Series connection would mean switching off one switches all off.
factual_hooks: Parallel wiring — each appliance at same voltage; independent switching
Series wiring — failure of one affects all; used in Christmas lights, not domestic

contrast_with: Series (same current, different voltage) vs Parallel (same voltage, different current)

question:
Consider the following statements : At the present level of technology available in India, solar energy can be conveniently used to
I.
supply hot water to residential buildings.
II.
supply water for minor irrigation projects.
III.
provide street lighting.
IV.
electrify a cluster of villages and small towns.
Of these statements
option_a: I, II, III and IV are correct.
option_d: I, II and III are correct
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Options B and C suggest partial applicability of solar energy, while A (all four) may seem too optimistic. However, by 1996 technology standards, all four were feasible — solar water heating and street lighting were established.
prelims.elimination_hint: All four applications of solar energy (hot water, minor irrigation pumping, street lighting, village electrification) were feasible at the technology level described. Correct: A.
factual_hooks: Solar water heaters — commercially viable since 1970s
Solar photovoltaic for rural electrification — technically feasible even by 1990s

mains.contemporary_relevance: India's National Solar Mission targets 100 GW solar capacity; solar is now cost-competitive.

contrast_with: Solar thermal (water heating) vs Photovoltaic (electricity generation)

question:
Consider the following statements : A person in a spaceship located half way between the earth and the sun will notice that the
I. Sky is jet black.
II. Stars do not twinkle.
III. Temperature outside the spaceship is much higher than that on the surface of the earth.
Of these statements
option_d: I, II and III are correct
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Statement III (much higher temperature outside) seems intuitive near the Sun, but in the vacuum of space, temperature depends on what is measured — the vacuum itself has near absolute zero background, though solar radiation heats objects in direct sunlight to very high temperatures.
prelims.elimination_hint: In space: sky appears black (no atmosphere to scatter light — I correct), stars don't twinkle (no atmospheric refraction — II correct), temperature in direct sunlight would be extremely high (III correct). All three are correct. Correct: D.
factual_hooks: Stars twinkle due to atmospheric refraction — no atmosphere in space = no twinkling
Black sky in space — no Rayleigh scattering in vacuum
Solar radiation heats objects in space to extreme temperatures

contrast_with: Earth atmosphere effects (twinkling, blue sky) vs space vacuum (black sky, no twinkling)

question: ‘Diamond Ring’ is a phenomenon observed
option_a: at the start of a total solar eclipse
option_b: at the end of a total solar eclipse
option_c: only along the peripheral regions of the totality trial
option_d: only in the central regions of the totality trial
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Options A and B (start and end of eclipse) seem logical but are wrong. Diamond Ring appears only at the edges of the totality path, not at start/end for all observers.
prelims.elimination_hint: The Diamond Ring effect is visible only along the peripheral regions of the totality trail where the eclipse is not total but nearly so — the solar corona creates a ring with one bright point. Correct: C.
factual_hooks: Diamond Ring effect — occurs just before/after totality, visible at edges of totality path
Baily's Beads — similar phenomenon caused by light through lunar surface irregularities

contrast_with: Total eclipse (diamond ring visible) vs partial eclipse (not visible)

question: The alpha-particle carries two positive charges. Its mass is very nearly equal to that of
option_c: sum of masses of two positrons and two neutrons
option_d: two positrons as each positron carries a single positive charge
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Option A (two protons) addresses charge but not mass. Option C (two positrons + two neutrons) correctly counts components but positrons are antimatter, not the actual particles. Alpha particle = helium-4 nucleus.
prelims.elimination_hint: Alpha particle = 2 protons + 2 neutrons = helium-4 nucleus. Its mass ≈ mass of helium atom (minus 2 electrons). Correct: B.
factual_hooks: Alpha particle = 2 protons + 2 neutrons = helium-4 nucleus, charge +2
Alpha particles stopped by paper; beta by aluminum; gamma by lead

contrast_with: Alpha (helium nucleus) vs Beta (electron/positron) vs Gamma (EM radiation)

question: Q12. What is the basis of regionalism? Is it that unequal distribution of benefits of development on regional basis eventually promotes regionalism? Substantiate your answer. (200 words) 12.5
subject: Society

factual_hooks: Regionalism rooted in: linguistic identity, historical grievances, uneven development, geographical isolation
Unequal resource distribution and development gaps amplify regional identities

answer_structure.intro_approach: Regionalism in India is a complex socio-political phenomenon rooted in historical, linguistic, and economic factors that differentiates regions and creates distinct identities competing for recognition and resources.
body_points: Bases of regionalism: geographical isolation, linguistic-cultural identity, historical legacy of diverse kingdoms, and administrative convenience
Unequal distribution of development benefits — BIMARU states vs southern states; tribal areas vs plains — creates grievances that fuel regional identities
Economic underdevelopment leads communities to organize along regional lines to demand greater resource allocation, special category status, or statehood
However, uneven development is not the sole cause — cultural and linguistic identity can generate regionalism even in developed areas (e.g., Telangana despite Andhra being relatively developed)
answer_structure.conclusion_approach: While unequal development is a powerful catalyst for regionalism, it intersects with cultural, linguistic, and historical factors, necessitating both equitable development policies and recognition of regional identities within a federal framework.

mains.contemporary_relevance: Demands for new states (e.g., Greater Tipraland) and inter-state water disputes reflect continuing salience of regionalism.

contrast_with: Healthy regionalism (cultural pride) vs separatist regionalism (anti-national)

question: The offending substance in the liquor tragedies leading to blindness, etc.. is
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Ethyl alcohol (ethanol) is safe drinking alcohol — students may confuse it with methanol. Amyl and benzyl alcohols are industrial solvents.
prelims.elimination_hint: Methyl alcohol (methanol/wood alcohol) causes blindness and death when ingested — it metabolizes to formaldehyde and formic acid which damage the optic nerve. Correct: D.
factual_hooks: Methanol — metabolized to formaldehyde → formic acid → optic nerve damage → blindness
Ethanol (safe to drink) vs Methanol (lethal — causes blindness/death)

mains.contemporary_relevance: Periodic hooch tragedies in India (Bihar, Gujarat) remain a public health and law enforcement challenge.

contrast_with: Ethanol (C2H5OH — safe in dilute form) vs Methanol (CH3OH — toxic even in small amounts)

question: Which one of the following elements is alloyed with iron to produce steel which can resist high temperatures and also have high hardness and abrasion resistance ?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Chromium (stainless steel) resists corrosion but not specifically high-temperature hardness and abrasion. Nickel adds toughness. Tungsten (wolfram) specifically provides high-temperature strength and hardness.
prelims.elimination_hint: Tungsten (W) is used in high-speed tool steels for its extreme hardness at high temperatures and abrasion resistance. HSS (High Speed Steel) contains tungsten. Correct: D.
factual_hooks: Tungsten steel — highest melting point metal; used in cutting tools, drill bits
Chromium → stainless steel (corrosion resistance); Nickel → toughness; Tungsten → hardness at high temp

contrast_with: Chromium steel (corrosion-resistant) vs Tungsten steel (high-temperature hardness)

question:
Match List I (Physiological processes) with List II (Cell organelles) and select the correct answer by using the codes given below the lists
List I | List II
I. Photosynthesis | (A) Plasma membrane
II. Mineral uptake | (B) Chloroplast
III. Respiration | (C) Mitochondria
IV. Protein Synthesis | (D) Ribosomes
option_a: I - A, II - B, III - C, IV - D
option_b: I - A, II - B, III - D, IV - C
option_c: I - B, II - A, III - C, IV - D
option_d: I - B, II - A, III - D, IV - C
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Students may confuse the role of plasma membrane in mineral uptake vs active transport, or misplace chloroplast and mitochondria functions.
prelims.elimination_hint: Photosynthesis → Chloroplast (B); Mineral uptake → Plasma membrane (A); Respiration → Mitochondria (C); Protein synthesis → Ribosomes (D). Mapping: I-B, II-A, III-C, IV-D. Correct: C.
factual_hooks: Chloroplast — photosynthesis; Mitochondria — cellular respiration (powerhouse)
Ribosomes — protein synthesis; Plasma membrane — selective permeability, mineral uptake

contrast_with: Chloroplast (energy capture) vs Mitochondria (energy release)

question:
Examine the following statements :
I. George attends Music classes on Monday.
II. He attends Mathematics classes on Wednesday.
III. His Literature classes are not on Friday.
IV. He attends History classes on the day following the day of his Mathematics classes.
V. On Tuesday, he attends his Sports classes. If he attends just one subject in a day and his Sunday is free, then he is also free on
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Students must track each day's assignment systematically. Missing that History is on Thursday (day after Wednesday Math) leads to errors about what day is free.
prelims.elimination_hint: Monday: Music; Tuesday: Sports; Wednesday: Math; Thursday: History (day after Math); Literature: not Friday, so Saturday; Sunday: free. Friday is free! Correct: D.
factual_hooks: Constraint: History = day after Math (Wed) = Thursday; Literature ≠ Friday → Literature = Saturday
Process of elimination: all days assigned except Friday and Sunday (Sunday given free)

question: In an accurate clock, on a period of 2 hours 20 minutes, the minute hand will move over
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Students may calculate only for 20 minutes or fail to convert 2 hours 20 minutes = 140 minutes correctly. The minute hand completes 360° per hour = 6° per minute.
prelims.elimination_hint: 2 hours 20 min = 140 min. Minute hand moves 6°/min. Total = 140 × 6 = 840°. Correct: C.
factual_hooks: Minute hand: 360° per hour = 6° per minute

contrast_with: Minute hand (6°/min) vs Hour hand (0.5°/min)

question:
Two important characteristics of a hypothesis are that it should be testable and that it should be stated in a manner that it can be refuted.
Which one of the following hypotheses, fulfils these characteristics ?
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Options B, C, D are trivially true (birds are animals; some businessmen may be dishonest; all men are mortal) and thus NOT falsifiable. A good scientific hypothesis must be potentially disprovable.
prelims.elimination_hint: A scientific hypothesis must be testable AND falsifiable (refutable). 'Intelligent persons have good memory' can be tested by IQ + memory tests and could potentially be refuted by counter-examples. The others are either definitionally true or impossibly vague.
factual_hooks: Popper's falsifiability criterion — a scientific hypothesis must be capable of being proven false
Trivially true statements (all men are mortal, some birds are animals) are not scientific hypotheses

contrast_with: Falsifiable hypothesis (scientific) vs tautology/trivially true statement (not scientific)

question:
The table given below depicts the composition of India’s exports between 1992-93 and 1994-95 . The changing composition of the export trade is indicative of structural transformation of Indian economy in favour of modernisation. The best indicator of the trend is the
Items | 1992-93 | 1993-94 | 1994-95
Agriculture & allied products | 1.6.9 | 1.8 | 1.5.9
Ores A Minerals | 4. 4 | 3.7
Manufactured floods | 75.5 | 75.6 | 78
Petroleum Products | 2.6 | 1.8 | 1.9
option_a: relative shape of petroleum products in exports
option_b: decline in the share of agricultural products in exports
option_c: constant share of ores and minerals in exports
option_d: increase in the share of manufactured products in exports
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Option B (decline in agricultural share) also shows structural transformation but is a negative indicator. Option D (increase in manufactured goods share) is the best positive indicator of industrial modernization.
prelims.elimination_hint: Structural modernization is best indicated by rising share of manufactured goods in exports (78% in 1994-95) — this shows industrial capacity growth. Decline in agriculture share alone could indicate farm crisis, not modernization.
factual_hooks: Rising manufactured goods share in exports → indicator of industrial structural transformation
From 75.5% to 78% — manufactured goods share increase confirms modernization trend

contrast_with: Agricultural decline (may indicate crisis) vs manufacturing increase (positive modernization signal)

question: When the frequency distribution is normal
option_a: median, mode and mean are all different from one another
option_b: mean, mode and median are identical
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Students who only know that mean > median in right-skewed distributions may think D is always true. In a normal distribution specifically, all three measures of central tendency coincide.
prelims.elimination_hint: In a normal (bell-curve) distribution, mean = median = mode. They are all identical. This is a defining property of the normal distribution. Correct: B.
factual_hooks: Normal distribution — perfectly symmetrical; mean = median = mode
Skewed distributions: right-skewed = mean > median > mode; left-skewed = mean < median < mode

contrast_with: Normal distribution (symmetric — all three equal) vs skewed distributions (three differ)

question: The river most mentioned in early Vedic literature is
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Sarasvati appears prominently in later Vedic religion and Ganga is associated with Indian culture broadly, leading students to pick these over Sindhu.
prelims.elimination_hint: The Rigveda, the earliest Vedic text, most frequently mentions the Sindhu (Indus) river — it gives its name to the civilization. Sarasvati is important but Sindhu is the most mentioned in early Vedic literature.
factual_hooks: Sindhu (Indus) river — most mentioned in Rigveda; gave name to Hindu/India
Sarasvati — mentioned as the greatest river but Sindhu is more frequently referenced overall

contrast_with: Sindhu (most mentioned, early Vedic) vs Ganga (prominent in later Vedic/epics)

question:
Which of the following pairs of states and their important crops are correctly matched ?
I. Kerala : Tapioca
II. Maharashtra : Cotton
III. W. Bengal : Jute
IV. Gujarat : Groundnut
Select the correct answer using the codes given below : Codes :
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Kerala is famous for many crops; tapioca (cassava) is indeed important there. Maharashtra-cotton and W.Bengal-jute are well known. Gujarat-groundnut is correct. The trap: students may think all four are correct.
prelims.elimination_hint: All four pairs (Kerala-Tapioca, Maharashtra-Cotton, W.Bengal-Jute, Gujarat-Groundnut) are correctly matched. The answer should be all four (I, II, III, IV). However, correct option is D (II, III and IV), suggesting Tapioca-Kerala may be disputed. Groundnut is Gujarat's leading cash crop; Cotton-Maharashtra and Jute-W.Bengal are standard.
factual_hooks: Gujarat — largest groundnut producer in India
West Bengal — largest jute producer; Maharashtra — largest cotton producer
Kerala — tapioca (cassava) is grown but not as prominently as coconut/rubber

contrast_with: Kerala's primary crops: coconut, rubber, spices (not tapioca as primary crop)

question:
Consider the following statements about Jawaharlal Nehru :
I. He was the President of the Congress Party in 1947.
II. He presided over the Constituent Assembly.
III. He formed the first Congress Ministry in United Province before India’s independence.
Of these statements
option_a: I, II and III are correct
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Statement I (President of Congress in 1947) — Nehru was President of Congress in 1936 and 1937, NOT in 1947 (Acharya Kripalani was president in 1946-47). Statement II (presided over Constituent Assembly) — Rajendra Prasad was President, not Nehru.
prelims.elimination_hint: Statement I: Wrong — J.B. Kripalani was Congress President in 1947; Statement II: Wrong — Rajendra Prasad presided over Constituent Assembly; Statement III: Correct — Nehru formed first Congress ministry in UP (1937). Answer: D (none is correct based on I and II being wrong).
factual_hooks: Nehru — Congress President 1936, 1937; NOT in 1947
Rajendra Prasad — President of Constituent Assembly (not Nehru)
Nehru formed first Congress ministry in United Provinces (1937 elections)

contrast_with: Nehru (PM/Congress leader) vs Rajendra Prasad (Constituent Assembly President/first President)

question: Which one of the following is not correct about the Cabinet Mission Plan ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Students may think the Cabinet Mission Plan accepted Pakistan since it discussed provincial groupings (A, B, C groups) which could seem like partitioning. The plan explicitly rejected Pakistan.
prelims.elimination_hint: The Cabinet Mission Plan (1946) explicitly rejected Pakistan. It proposed a three-tier federal structure: Union, Groups (A/B/C), and Provinces — without partition. Correct: C (acceptance of Pakistan is NOT part of CMP).
factual_hooks: Cabinet Mission Plan 1946 — rejected Pakistan; proposed loose confederation with three-tier structure
Three groups: A (Hindu-majority), B (Muslim NW), C (Muslim NE + Assam)

contrast_with: Cabinet Mission Plan (rejected Pakistan, proposed federation) vs Mountbatten Plan (accepted partition)

question: Which one of the following is an important historical novel written during the latter half of the nineteenth century ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Rast Goftar (Parsi newspaper) and Nibandhamala (Marathi essays) are reform publications, not novels. Maharatha is fictional/misleading.
prelims.elimination_hint: Durgesh Nandini (1865) by Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay is India's first major historical novel in Bengali. It is the definitive answer for 19th-century Indian historical fiction. Correct: B.
factual_hooks: Durgesh Nandini (1865) — Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay — first major Indian historical novel
Rast Goftar — Parsi newspaper; Nibandhamala — Vishnu Shastri Pandit's Marathi essays

contrast_with: Historical novel (Durgesh Nandini) vs newspaper (Rast Goftar) vs essay collection (Nibandhamala)

question: Which one of the following rivers thrice forks into two streams and reunites a few miles farther on, thus forming the islands of Srirangappattanam, Sivasamudram and Srirangam ?
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Krishna and Godavari have islands but not the specific triple-fork pattern. Students may confuse Tungabhadra (Cauvery tributary) with Cauvery itself.
prelims.elimination_hint: The Cauvery river forks three times creating: Srirangapatna island, Shivanasamudra island (Cauvery Falls), and Srirangam island at Trichy. This is a well-known geographical fact about the Cauvery. Correct: A.
factual_hooks: Cauvery — forks three times: Srirangapatna, Sivasamudram (Cauvery Falls), Srirangam
Srirangam — famous Ranganathaswamy temple on Cauvery island

mains.contemporary_relevance: Cauvery water dispute between Karnataka and Tamil Nadu remains unresolved.

contrast_with: Cauvery (3 forks) vs Ganga-Brahmaputra (large delta formation) — different drainage patterns

question: If the number of seats allocated to a state in the Lok Sabha is 42, then the number of seats reserved for the Scheduled Castes in that state will be
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Students may calculate 1/7 of 42 = 6, or remember the national SC population proportion (~15%) to arrive at different numbers. The actual reservation ratio needs to match population proportion.
prelims.elimination_hint: SC reservation in Lok Sabha is approximately proportional to SC population (~15%). For a 42-seat state, 15% ≈ 6. Correct: D (6). Note: the exact formula is SC seats proportional to SC population of that state.
factual_hooks: SC reservation in Lok Sabha: seats reserved ∝ SC population of state
Nationally SCs ≈ 15% of population; for 42 seats × 15% ≈ 6 seats

mains.contemporary_relevance: 105th Constitutional Amendment (2021) extended SC/ST reservation in Parliament and assemblies by 10 more years.

contrast_with: Population-based reservation (SC/ST) vs no reservation for OBCs in Parliament

question: Q15. How is the Government of India protecting traditional knowledge of medicine from patenting by pharmaceutical companies? (250 words) 15
subject: Science & Technology

factual_hooks: Traditional Knowledge Digital Library (TKDL) — India's database of traditional knowledge to prevent bio-piracy
TKDL linked to patent offices globally (USPTO, EPO) to reject applications based on traditional knowledge

answer_structure.intro_approach: India's rich tradition of medicinal knowledge has been vulnerable to bio-piracy by pharmaceutical companies, prompting the government to develop innovative legal and technological safeguards.
body_points: Traditional Knowledge Digital Library (TKDL): digitized 3.6 lakh formulations from Ayurveda, Unani, Siddha, Yoga in five languages; shared with patent offices worldwide to prevent bio-piracy
Biological Diversity Act 2002: requires prior approval and benefit-sharing for use of biological resources and associated knowledge
TRIPS flexibilities: India uses TRIPS Article 27.3(b) exclusions to deny patents on traditional knowledge
Successful revocations: Turmeric (1997), Neem (2000), Basmati (partially) — landmark cases of preventing/revoking wrongful patents
Geographical Indications Act: protects products like Darjeeling tea, Basmati rice as GI tags
answer_structure.conclusion_approach: India's multi-pronged approach combining TKDL, Biodiversity Act, GI registration, and TRIPS-compliant patent law amendments has significantly strengthened protection of traditional knowledge against pharmaceutical bio-piracy.

mains.contemporary_relevance: Bio-piracy attempts continue; India's TKDL model is being emulated globally by developing countries.

contrast_with: Bio-piracy (patenting traditional knowledge) vs TKDL protection mechanism

question: In the Indian context the term De-notified tribes’ refers to
option_d: tribes which were earlier classified as criminal tribes
subject: Society

prelims.trap_factor: Options A (aboriginals), B (nomadic), and C (shifting cultivation) all describe tribal characteristics. The specific term 'de-notified' relates to the Criminal Tribes Act colonial history.
prelims.elimination_hint: De-notified tribes were those notified under the Criminal Tribes Act 1871 (colonial era) as 'criminal by birth.' After Independence, the Act was repealed in 1952, 'de-notifying' them. Correct: D.
factual_hooks: Criminal Tribes Act 1871 — British branded entire tribes as hereditary criminals
De-notified Tribes — 'de-notified' after repeal of Criminal Tribes Act 1952; still face stigma

mains.contemporary_relevance: De-notified tribes continue to face discrimination; National Commission for De-notified Tribes was set up to address their issues.

contrast_with: De-notified tribes (criminalized by British) vs Scheduled Tribes (constitutionally recognized for affirmative action)

question: Which one of the following temples figured in the news regarding the institution of the Devadasis ?
subject: Society

prelims.trap_factor: Khajuraho temple is famous for erotic sculptures and students associate it with Devadasi; Pasupatinath (Nepal) features in news for different reasons.
prelims.elimination_hint: The Jagannath Temple at Puri (Odisha) is specifically associated with the Devadasi institution (called Maharis). News coverage of Devadasi reform specifically linked to Puri. Correct: A.
factual_hooks: Devadasi system — women dedicated to temple service (originally sacred), later exploited
Jagannath Temple, Puri — Maharis (Devadasis) and their exploitation was subject of reform campaigns

mains.contemporary_relevance: Devadasi system continues in parts of Karnataka and Andhra Pradesh despite legal prohibition.

contrast_with: Devadasi (temple-dedicated) vs other forms of religious prostitution

question: The power of the Supreme Court of India to decide disputes between the Centre and the States falls under its
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Constitutional jurisdiction (Option D) sounds most appropriate for Supreme Court's role in interpreting the Constitution. But 'constitutional jurisdiction' is not a standard legal category; original jurisdiction is the correct term.
prelims.elimination_hint: Under Article 131, the Supreme Court has exclusive original jurisdiction in disputes between Centre and States or between States — this is its Original Jurisdiction. Not appellate (appeals from lower courts) or advisory (President's reference). Correct: C.
factual_hooks: Article 131 — Original jurisdiction of Supreme Court for Centre-State and inter-State disputes
Advisory jurisdiction (Art. 143) — President's reference; Appellate jurisdiction (Arts. 132-136) — appeals

prerequisite_concepts: Original vs appellate vs advisory jurisdiction
contrast_with: Original (Centre-State disputes) vs Appellate (lower court appeals) vs Advisory (Presidential reference)

question: Which one of the following is correct regarding stabilization and structural adjustment as two components of the new economic policy adopted in India ?
option_a: Stabilization is a gradual, multi-step process while structural adjustment is a quick adaptation process
option_b: Structural adjustment is a gradual, multi-step process, while stabilization is a quick adaptation process
option_c: Stabilization and structural adjustment are very similar and complementary policies. It is difficult to separate one from the other
option_d: Stabilization mainly deals with a set of policies which are to be implemented by the Central Government while structural adjustment is to be set in motion by the State Governments
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Option A (stabilization gradual, structural adjustment quick) reverses the correct relationship. Option C (they are inseparable) is partially true but not the best description.
prelims.elimination_hint: Stabilization = short-term macroeconomic adjustment (reducing fiscal/current account deficit quickly). Structural adjustment = long-term reforms of the economy (gradual, multi-step process). Correct: B.
factual_hooks: Stabilization — quick measures: cut deficit, control inflation, devalue currency (IMF prescription)
Structural adjustment — gradual reforms: trade liberalization, privatization, labour reforms

contrast_with: Stabilization (short-term macroeconomic fix) vs Structural adjustment (long-term institutional/economic reform)

question:
Assertion (A)
: An important policy instrument of economic liberalization is reduction in import duties on capital goods.
Reason (R)
: Reduction in import duties would help the local entrepreneurs to improve technology to face the global markets. In the context of the above two statements, which one of the following is correct ?
option_a: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_b: Both A and R are true and R is not a correct explanation of A
option_c: A is true but R is false
option_d: A is false but R is true
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Both A and R are true and R correctly explains A — reduction in import duties on capital goods is indeed an economic liberalization policy instrument, and the reason (improving technology for global competitiveness) correctly explains it.
prelims.elimination_hint: Both statements are independently true AND R is the correct explanation for A — reducing duties on capital goods was a key instrument in 1991 reforms precisely to enable technology upgradation for export competitiveness. Correct: A.
factual_hooks: 1991 reforms — customs duty reduction on capital goods to enable technology import
Reasoning: domestic industry needs modern technology to compete globally after trade liberalization

contrast_with: Import substitution (high tariffs protect domestic industry) vs liberalization (low tariffs improve technology access)

question: Which one of the following is not a part of early Jaina literature ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: All options sound like ancient religious texts. Therigatha belongs to the Pali Buddhist canon — it is Buddhist literature (Theravada), not Jain.
prelims.elimination_hint: Therigatha (Verses of the Elder Nuns) is a Buddhist Pali text. Acarangasutra, Sutrakritanga, and Brihatkalpasutra are all part of the Jain Agamas (canonical texts). Correct: A.
factual_hooks: Therigatha — Buddhist Pali canon; earliest collection of women's spiritual poetry
Jain Agamas include Acarangasutra (oldest Jain text), Sutrakritanga, Brihatkalpasutra

contrast_with: Jain Agamas (Acarangasutra etc.) vs Buddhist Pali Canon (Therigatha, Dhammapada)

question: The Sarabandi (no tax) campaign of 1922 was led by
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: The Sarabandi movement is less known; students may associate it with Gandhi (who led the 1918 Kheda Satyagraha) or confuse it with Non-Cooperation Movement (1920-22).
prelims.elimination_hint: The Bardoli Satyagraha of 1928 (not 1922 Sarabandi) was led by Vallabhbhai Patel. The 1922 Sarabandi (no-tax) campaign in Bardoli, Gujarat was also led by Vallabhbhai Patel. Correct: D.
factual_hooks: Vallabhbhai Patel — led Bardoli Satyagraha/Sarabandi campaign; earned title 'Sardar'
Sarabandi = no-tax campaign; 1928 Bardoli is more famous but 1922 was earlier

contrast_with: Kheda Satyagraha 1918 (Gandhi) vs Bardoli/Sarabandi (Vallabhbhai Patel)

question: Q14. What is Integrated Farming System? How is it helpful to small and marginal farmers in India? (250 words) 15
subject: Economy

factual_hooks: Integrated Farming System (IFS) — combines crops, livestock, fishery, horticulture on same land for circular resource use
IFS reduces input costs by recycling farm waste across components — especially beneficial for marginal farmers with 1-2 hectares

answer_structure.intro_approach: Integrated Farming System (IFS) represents a holistic approach to agriculture that combines multiple farming activities to create synergistic resource flows, offering particular promise for India's small and marginal farmers.
body_points: Definition: IFS integrates crop production with allied activities — animal husbandry, fisheries, horticulture, agroforestry, vermicomposting — creating circular input-output linkages within the farm
Benefits for small/marginal farmers: diversified income streams reduce vulnerability to crop failure; farm waste from one component becomes input for another (e.g., cattle dung → biogas + manure)
Year-round employment and income: unlike mono-cropping (seasonal income), IFS provides continuous cash flow from multiple components
Reduced external input costs: internal recycling of nutrients and pest management reduces dependence on purchased inputs — critical for cash-poor marginal farmers
Examples: fish-paddy system, horticulture-poultry integration, agro-silvi-pastoral systems
answer_structure.conclusion_approach: IFS offers a viable pathway for small and marginal farmers to achieve income security, resilience, and sustainability by transforming subsistence farming into a diversified, resource-efficient enterprise.

interlinkages: GS3 — Agricultural policy and farmer welfare
GS2 — Government schemes for farmer income doubling
mains.contemporary_relevance: Government's goal to double farmers' income by 2022 (extended) highlights the importance of IFS adoption among India's 86% small and marginal landholders.

contrast_with: Monoculture (single crop risk) vs IFS (diversified income, lower risk)

question: One of the important goals of the economic liberalisation policy is to achieve full convertibility of the Indian rupee. This is being advocated because
option_a: convertibility of the rupee will stabilize its exchange value against major currencies of the world
option_b: it will attract more foreign capital inflow in India
option_d: it will help India secure loans from the world financial markets at attractive terms
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Options B, C, D are all benefits of convertibility but are secondary effects. Option A (stabilize exchange value) is actually questionable — full convertibility often leads to exchange rate volatility, not stability. Yet it is the stated correct answer per the question.
prelims.elimination_hint: The stated reason for advocating full convertibility in this context (mid-1990s debate) was that market-determined rates would reflect true value and thus stabilize long-term. Correct per answer key: A. However, note that B (attract foreign capital) is arguably a stronger practical reason.
factual_hooks: Capital Account Convertibility (CAC) recommended by Tarapore Committee (1997)
Full convertibility allows free exchange of rupee for foreign currency at market rates

mains.contemporary_relevance: India has not yet moved to full capital account convertibility; the debate continues amid global financial volatility.

contrast_with: Current account convertibility (already achieved) vs Capital account convertibility (still restricted)

question: Which one of the following first mooted the idea of a constituent assembly to frame a constitution for India ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Congress 1936 is a common wrong pick since Congress championed the CA idea widely, but the Swaraj Party mooted it first in 1934. The 1946 All Parties Conference is confused with the actual CA formation.
prelims.elimination_hint: Remember M.N. Roy's proposal and Swaraj Party's 1934 resolution—both predate Congress's 1936 official demand. Eliminate Muslim League (1942) and All Parties Conference (1946) as both are too late.
factual_hooks: Swaraj Party first mooted CA idea in 1934
Congress formally demanded CA in 1936 after Swaraj Party's initiative

contrast_with: Congress demand 1936 vs Swaraj Party proposal 1934 — Congress endorsed, not originated

question:
Which of the following were common to both Buddhism and Jainism ?
I. Avoidance of extremities of penance and enjoyment.
II. Indifference to the authority of the Vedas
III. Denial of efficacy of rituals
IV. Non-injury to animal life
Select the correct answer using the codes given below :
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Statement I (Middle Path — avoidance of extremities) is actually specific to Buddhism and NOT shared with Jainism, which advocates extreme penance (sallekhana). This is the key trap.
prelims.elimination_hint: Jainism prescribes severe austerities unlike Buddhism's Middle Path, so Statement I is incorrect for Jainism. Statements II, III, IV are common to both — select option B.
factual_hooks: Both reject Vedic authority and rituals
Jainism endorses extreme penance; Buddhism rejects it
Ahimsa (non-injury to animals) is central to both

contrast_with: Buddhism — Middle Path vs Jainism — extreme austerity (Sallekhana)

question: Which one of the following layers of the atmosphere is responsible for the deflection of radio waves ?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Stratosphere is confused because it contains the ozone layer and is often associated with electromagnetic phenomena; mesosphere sounds plausible due to its height.
prelims.elimination_hint: Ionosphere (thermosphere region) contains charged particles (ions) that reflect radio waves — this is the defining property. Eliminate all others by function.
factual_hooks: Ionosphere reflects/deflects AM radio waves back to Earth
Located above mesosphere (~80–600 km altitude)

contrast_with: Troposphere — weather phenomena vs Ionosphere — radio reflection

question: The emerging trading blocs in the world, such as NAFTA, ASEAN and the like, are expected to
option_a: act as constrictions in free trade across the world
option_b: promote free trade on the lines laid down by the WTO
option_c: permit transfer of technology between member countries
option_d: promote trade in agricultural commodities between countries of the North and South
subject: International Relations

prelims.trap_factor: Options B and C seem positive/neutral, making option A (constrictions to free trade) counter-intuitive. Students may confuse preferential trade blocs with universal free trade.
prelims.elimination_hint: Trading blocs like NAFTA create preferential trade among members but raise barriers to outsiders — this is trade diversion, acting as a constraint on global free trade. Select A.
factual_hooks: Regional blocs create trade diversion against non-members
WTO promotes multilateral free trade; regional blocs are partial

mains.contemporary_relevance: Debate on WTO vs FTAs remains central to India's trade policy discussions.

contrast_with: Regional bloc — preferential vs WTO — universal free trade

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer by using the codes given below the lists
List I | List II
I. Governor General of Presidency of Fort William in Bengal (under Regulating Act, 1773) | (A) Archibald Percival Wavell, Viscount and Earl Wavell
II. Governor General of India (under Charter Act, 1833) | (B) James Andrew Broun-Ramsay, Earl and Marquess of Dalhousie
III. Governor General and Viceroy of India (under Indian Councils Act, 1858) | (C) Charles Cornwallis, 2nd Earl and first Marquess of Cornwallis
IV. Governor General and Crown Representative (under Government of India Act. 1935) | (D) Gilbert John Elliot - Murray - Kynynmond, Earl of Minto
| (E) Louis Mountbatten, Earl Mountbatten of Burma
option_a: I - C, II - B, III - D, IV - A
option_b: I - C, II - D, III - A, IV - E
option_c: I - B, II - C, III - D, IV - E
option_d: I - D, II - B, III - C, IV - A
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: The long names and similar-sounding designations (Governor General vs Viceroy) create confusion; Mountbatten is often placed in wrong slot since he is the most famous.
prelims.elimination_hint: Cornwallis was first GoG under Regulating Act 1773; Dalhousie was GoG under Charter Act 1833; Minto was first Viceroy under 1858 Act — this eliminates all wrong pairings.
factual_hooks: Cornwallis — Governor General under Regulating Act 1773
Dalhousie — GoG under Charter Act 1833
Canning (not Minto) was first Viceroy 1858 — but Minto was GoG and Viceroy

contrast_with: GoG of Fort William vs GoG of India vs Viceroy — different constitutional positions

question: Nastaliq was
option_a: a Persian script used in medieval India
option_c: a cess levied by the Mughal rulers
option_d: a manual of code of conduct for the Ulemas
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Nastaliq sounds musical (raga) or administrative (cess), leading students to choose B or C without recognizing it as a calligraphic script style.
prelims.elimination_hint: Nastaliq is a cursive Persian calligraphic script widely used in Mughal-era manuscripts and inscriptions. The '-liq' suffix relates to writing style in Persian tradition.
factual_hooks: Nastaliq is a Persian calligraphic script used in Mughal India
It was the primary script for Persian literary and official Mughal documents

contrast_with: Nastaliq (Persian cursive) vs Naskh (Arabic formal script)

question: Which one of the following regions of India is now regarded as an ‘ecological hot spot’ ?
subject: Environment

prelims.trap_factor: Eastern Himalayas is also a biodiversity hotspot (IUCN recognized), causing confusion. Some may confuse 'ecological hot spot' with high biodiversity hill regions.
prelims.elimination_hint: Western Ghats is one of the 36 recognized global biodiversity hotspots (Norman Myers framework). Eastern Himalayas is also a hotspot but Western Ghats is THE classic answer for India.
factual_hooks: Western Ghats is one of 8 'hottest' biodiversity hotspots globally
Recognized by Conservation International as global hotspot

mains.contemporary_relevance: Western Ghats ecology remains a flashpoint for development vs conservation debates.

contrast_with: Western Ghats — older, higher endemism vs Eastern Himalayas — also hotspot but distinct

question:
Which of the following were the aims behind the setting up of the World Trade Organization (WTO) ?
I. Promotion of free trade and resource flows across countries.
II. Protection of intellectual property rights.
III. Managing balanced trade between different countries.
IV. Promotion of trade between the former East Block countries and the western world.
Select the correct answer by using the codes given below : Codes :
subject: International Relations

prelims.trap_factor: Statements III (balanced trade management) and IV (East-West trade promotion) sound plausible as WTO aims but are not its mandated objectives. WTO does NOT manage balance or promote specific geopolitical trade flows.
prelims.elimination_hint: WTO's core mandate is free trade promotion (I) and IPR protection (II) via TRIPS. Balanced trade management is not a WTO function — eliminate III and IV, select option B.
factual_hooks: WTO administers TRIPS for IPR protection
WTO promotes non-discriminatory free trade — not managed/balanced trade

mains.contemporary_relevance: WTO reform debates continue regarding its dispute settlement mechanism.

contrast_with: WTO vs UNCTAD — WTO regulates trade rules; UNCTAD addresses development

question:
Assertion (A)
: Areas near the equator receive rainfall throughout the year.
Reason (R)
: High temperatures and high humidity cause convectional rain in most afternoons near the equator. In the context of the above two statements, which one of the following is correct ?
option_a: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_b: Both A and R are true but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_c: A is true but R is false
option_d: A is false but R is true
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Students may think R is not the correct explanation because they confuse 'most afternoons' with 'throughout the year' — but convectional rain occurs daily in afternoons, giving year-round rainfall.
prelims.elimination_hint: Both A and R are true: equatorial regions receive year-round rain because high temps + humidity cause convectional uplift almost every afternoon. R directly explains A — select option A.
factual_hooks: Equatorial climate: rainfall > 2000mm, distributed throughout the year
Convectional rain occurs in afternoons due to intense daytime heating

contrast_with: Equatorial — year-round rain vs Monsoon — seasonal rain

question:
Assertion (A)
: The British sovereignty continued to exist in free India.
Reason (R)
: The British sovereign appointed the last Governor-General of free India. In the context of the above two statements, which one of the following is correct ?
option_a: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_b: Both A and R are true but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_c: A is true but R is false
option_d: A is false but R is true
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Statement A sounds plausible because India used British legal/constitutional framework initially. The key is that British sovereignty ceased on August 15, 1947 — the last GoG was appointed by the Indian government, not the Crown.
prelims.elimination_hint: A is false: British sovereignty ended on Aug 15, 1947. R is technically true — C. Rajagopalachari was appointed by the Indian GovernorGeneral-in-Council, not the British sovereign. Select D.
factual_hooks: Mountbatten was last British-appointed GoG; Rajagopalachari was first Indian GoG appointed by India
British sovereignty ended completely on Jan 26, 1950 (Republic)

contrast_with: Dominion status (1947–1950) vs Republic (1950 onwards)

question: Which one of the following sets of conditions is necessary for a good cultivation of wheat ?
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: High temperature with moderate rain (C) sounds plausible. The key is wheat needs a cool growing season and moderate rain — not high temperatures which damage grain formation.
prelims.elimination_hint: Wheat is a rabi crop needing cool winters (moderate temperature) and moderate rainfall (~50–75 cm). Eliminate B (high temp + heavy rain = rice) and D (too cold/dry = impossible).
factual_hooks: Wheat: rabi crop, moderate temp (10–25°C), moderate rain 50–75 cm
Major wheat states: UP, Punjab, Haryana, MP

contrast_with: Wheat — cool climate vs Rice — hot and humid climate

question: What is the system of governance in the Panchayati Raj setup ?
option_a: Single-tier structure of local self-government at the village level
option_b: Two-tier system of local self-government at the village and block levels
option_c: Three-tier structure of local self-government, at the village, block and district levels
option_d: Four-tier system of local self-government at the village, block, district and state levels
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Some states initially implemented a two-tier Panchayati Raj (Rajasthan model), leading to confusion. The 73rd Amendment mandated the three-tier structure for all states above a population threshold.
prelims.elimination_hint: 73rd Constitutional Amendment (1992) mandates three-tier structure: Gram Panchayat (village), Panchayat Samiti (block), and Zila Parishad (district). Select C.
factual_hooks: 73rd Amendment 1992 mandated three-tier Panchayati Raj
Balwant Rai Mehta Committee (1957) first recommended three-tier structure

mains.contemporary_relevance: Devolution of funds, functions, and functionaries to PRIs remains a governance challenge.

contrast_with: Pre-1992 two-tier models vs post-1992 mandatory three-tier

question: Mughal painting reached its zenith under
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Akbar's reign is associated with the establishment of a distinct Mughal painting style, making him a common wrong answer. Shahjahan had fine artistic taste too, causing further confusion.
prelims.elimination_hint: Jahangir was a passionate naturalist and connoisseur — Mughal painting reached its zenith under him with masters like Ustad Mansur and Abu'l Hasan. Akbar established it; Jahangir perfected it.
factual_hooks: Jahangir — zenith of Mughal painting; naturalistic style
Ustad Mansur — court painter under Jahangir, known for flora and fauna

contrast_with: Akbar — established Mughal painting vs Jahangir — perfected/zenith

question:
Consider the following statements :
No one can be compelled to sing the National Anthem since
I. it will be violative of the Right to freedom of speech and expression.
II. it will be violative of the Right to freedom of conscience and practise and propagation of religion.
III. there is no legal provision obliging any one to sing the National Anthem.
Of these statements
option_c: I, II and III are correct
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Students may think that since the National Anthem is a national symbol, there must be a legal compulsion. Also, Statement III (no legal obligation) may be dismissed as 'obviously true' without thinking through its standalone validity.
prelims.elimination_hint: Supreme Court ruled in Bijoe Emmanuel case (1986) that singing the National Anthem cannot be compelled — it violates Articles 19(1)(a) and 25. All three statements I, II, III are valid — select C.
factual_hooks: Bijoe Emmanuel case 1986 — SC held compulsion to sing violates FR
No law makes singing the National Anthem compulsory

mains.contemporary_relevance: National anthem in cinema halls controversy linked to this judgment.

prerequisite_concepts: Prevention of Insults to National Honour Act 1971
contrast_with: Standing during anthem (required) vs singing it (not compulsory)

question: 'Economic Justice' as one of the objectives of the Indian Constitution has been provided in
option_a: the Preamble and the Fundamental Rights
option_b: the Preamble and the Directive Principles of State Policy
option_c: the Fundamental Rights and the Directive Principles of State Policy
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Students may select option C (FR + DPSP) thinking Fundamental Rights also guarantee economic justice through Article 21 (right to livelihood). The Preamble explicitly mentions economic justice; FR doesn't use that terminology.
prelims.elimination_hint: The Preamble mentions 'Justice — social, economic and political'. DPSP (Article 38–51) also mandates economic justice. Fundamental Rights focus on civil-political rights — eliminate options A and C.
factual_hooks: Preamble — 'Justice: social, economic, political'
DPSP Article 39 — equal pay, adequate livelihood, economic justice

contrast_with: Preamble — declaratory vs DPSP — directive but non-justiciable

question:
In India, rural incomes are generally lower than the urban incomes.
Which of the following reasons account for this ?
I. A large number of farmers are illiterate and know little about scientific agriculture.
II. Prices of primary products are lower than those of manufactured products.
III. Investment in agriculture has been low when compared to investment in industry.
Select the correct answer by using the codes given below : Codes :
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Students may exclude Statement II thinking primary product pricing is a market factor rather than a policy cause, or doubt whether all three are simultaneously valid.
prelims.elimination_hint: All three reasons — illiteracy limiting productivity, unfavorable terms of trade for primary goods, and lower agricultural investment — together explain the rural-urban income gap. Select A.
factual_hooks: Terms of trade consistently unfavorable for agriculture vs industry
Public investment in agriculture historically lower than industry in India

mains.contemporary_relevance: Rural-urban income gap remains a policy challenge; PM-KISAN addresses income support.

contrast_with: Agricultural sector — low capital intensity vs Industrial sector — high capital and value addition

question: ‘They are fantastically diverse. They speak hundreds of languages and dialects. They comprise scores of ethnic groups. They include highly industrialised economies and up-and-coming economies. They span half the surface of the earth and are home to two-fifths of the world’s population.” The group of countries referred to here belongs to
subject: International Relations

prelims.trap_factor: The description fits ASEAN on surface (diverse languages, Southeast Asia) but the 'two-fifths of world population' and 'half surface of earth' points unambiguously to APEC which spans Pacific Rim.
prelims.elimination_hint: APEC spans Pacific Rim nations from US to Australia/NZ to Asia — covers half Earth's surface, has highly industrialized (US, Japan) and emerging (China, Vietnam) economies. Select B.
factual_hooks: APEC — 21 member economies, Pacific Rim, ~60% world GDP
APEC includes highly industrialized and developing economies across Pacific

contrast_with: APEC — Pacific-wide vs ASEAN — Southeast Asia only

question: Q11. "Effectiveness of the government system at various levels and people's participation in the governance system are inter-dependent." Discuss their relationship in the context of India.  (200 words) 12.5
subject: Governance

factual_hooks: Multi-level governance requires coordination between centre, state, and local bodies
Citizen participation in governance improves accountability and service delivery

answer_structure.intro_approach: Governance effectiveness and citizen participation are mutually reinforcing pillars of democratic governance in India.
body_points: Effective government systems provide infrastructure for participation (RTI, Gram Sabha, social audits)
Citizen participation improves government accountability and feedback loops
Weak local government reduces meaningful participation — 73rd/74th Amendments tried to bridge this
Digital governance (e-Gram, MyGov) expands participation channels
answer_structure.conclusion_approach: Strengthening both government systems and participatory mechanisms together is essential for India's democratic deepening.

mains.contemporary_relevance: MyGov platform and Direct Benefit Transfer exemplify technology-enabled participatory governance.

contrast_with: Top-down governance vs participatory governance

question:
Assertion (A)
: Though India’s national income has gone up several fold since 1947, there has been no marked improvement in the per capita income level.
Reason (R)
: Sizeable proportion of the population of India is still living below the poverty line. In the context of the above two statements which one of the following is correct ?
option_a: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_b: Both A and R are true but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_c: A is true but R is false
option_d: A is false but R is true
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Option A (R explains A) is tempting because poverty seems to explain low per capita income; but R (poverty) is a symptom of the same phenomenon as A, not the cause — population growth is the missing link.
prelims.elimination_hint: National income rising but per capita stagnating is explained by high population growth rate, NOT by poverty (which is a co-symptom). R doesn't explain A correctly — select B (both true, R not correct explanation).
factual_hooks: India's per capita income growth constrained by population growth rate
Poverty is a result of low per capita income, not its cause

contrast_with: National income growth vs per capita income growth — divergence due to population

question: His ‘principal forte was social and religious reform. He relied Upon legislation to do away with social ills and worked unceasingly for the eradication of child marriage, the purdah system ..... To encourage consideration of social problems on a national scale, he inaugurated the Indian National Social Conference, which for many years met for its annual sessions alongside the Indian National Congress.’ The reference in this passage is to
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar also worked against child marriage, making option A tempting. Ambedkar focused more on caste than the Indian National Social Conference.
prelims.elimination_hint: The key clue is 'Indian National Social Conference' which was founded by M.G. Ranade in 1887 and held alongside INC sessions. This uniquely identifies Ranade — select C.
factual_hooks: M.G. Ranade founded Indian National Social Conference 1887
Ranade worked on child marriage, purdah abolition, widow remarriage

contrast_with: Ranade — social legislation approach vs Tilak — political/cultural approach

question: Who among the following leaders did not believe in the drain theory of Dadabhai Naoroji ?
subject: History
aspect: WHO – Economic Impact of British Rule

prelims.trap_factor: Tilak and Ranade are both associated with critiquing British economic policies, making them distractors. Sir Syed Ahmed Khan's loyalty to British rule is the key differentiator here.
prelims.elimination_hint: Sir Syed Ahmed Khan was pro-British and rejected the economic nationalism implicit in Drain Theory; Tilak, Dutt, and Ranade all accepted/promoted it. Select D.
factual_hooks: Sir Syed Ahmed Khan was pro-British, rejected nationalist economic critique
Dadabhai Naoroji's Drain Theory: India's wealth drained to Britain
R.C. Dutt documented economic drain in 'Economic History of India'

contrast_with: Drain Theory nationalists vs pro-British loyalists like Sir Syed

question: B.R. Ambedkar was elected to the Constituent Assembly from
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Ambedkar is strongly associated with Maharashtra (Bombay Presidency), making option B seem correct. The trick is he was actually elected from West Bengal after losing his Bombay seat.
prelims.elimination_hint: Ambedkar was elected to the Constituent Assembly from West Bengal (with support of Congress) after being defeated from Bombay. Select A — West Bengal.
factual_hooks: Ambedkar elected to CA from West Bengal, not Bombay
He was supported by Congress for this election despite political differences

contrast_with: Ambedkar's home state Maharashtra vs CA election constituency West Bengal

question: “You might see a few curious Danes around, but that is because ..... used to be Danish outpost. This quaint town with its fort and a beautiful church, the New Jerusalem, empty streets and deserted beach front is a quaint gem.” The place referred to in this quotation lies on the
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: The mention of 'Danish outpost' and 'New Jerusalem church' is very specific — students may guess Kerala (Kochi had Dutch/Portuguese presence) or Goa.
prelims.elimination_hint: The Danish colony Tranquebar (Tharangambadi) with its Fort Dansborg and Zion Church is on the Tamil Nadu coast. Select A.
factual_hooks: Tranquebar (Tharangambadi) — Danish outpost on Tamil Nadu coast
Fort Dansborg built by Danes in 1620

contrast_with: Danish Tranquebar (Tamil Nadu) vs Dutch Cochin (Kerala)

question: Which one of the following texts of ancient India allows divorce to a wife deserted by her husband ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Kamasutra sounds like the only text that might deal with marital/sexual relations permissively; Manusmriti (Manavadharmashastra) is associated with rigid rules that actually restricted women.
prelims.elimination_hint: Arthashastra (Kautilya) allows divorce under certain conditions including desertion — it takes a pragmatic legal view. Select D.
factual_hooks: Arthashastra permits nanda-vivaha (divorce) when husband deserts wife
Manusmriti generally restricted women's rights including divorce

prerequisite_concepts: Status of women in ancient India
contrast_with: Arthashastra — pragmatic legal text vs Manusmriti — orthodox dharma text

question:
Assertion (A)
: The word ‘minority’ is not defined in the Constitution of India.
Reason (R)
: The Minorities Commission is not a constitutional body. In the context of the above two statements, which one of the following is correct ?
option_a: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_b: Both A and R are true but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_c: A is true but R is false
option_d: A is false but R is true
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Students may think R is false since the Minorities Commission is not mentioned in Part III; but the trap is that both A and R are independently true facts — the question is whether R explains A.
prelims.elimination_hint: Both statements are true: 'minority' is not defined in the Constitution, and the Minorities Commission is statutory (not constitutional). But R's truth doesn't explain WHY minority is undefined — B is correct.
factual_hooks: 'Minority' not defined in Indian Constitution
National Commission for Minorities is a statutory body (NCM Act 1992), not constitutional

mains.contemporary_relevance: Minority definition remains a contested judicial and policy issue.

contrast_with: Constitutional body (Election Commission) vs Statutory body (NCM)

question: The Palk Bay lies between
option_a: Gulf of Kachchh and Gulf of Khambhat
option_b: Gulf of Mannar and Bay of Bengal
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Gulf of Mannar and Palk Bay/Palk Strait are often confused; Palk Strait separates India and Sri Lanka while Palk Bay is between Tamil Nadu coast and Sri Lanka specifically near Mannar.
prelims.elimination_hint: Palk Bay lies between Gulf of Mannar to the south and Bay of Bengal to the north, bordered by Tamil Nadu coast and Sri Lanka. Select B.
factual_hooks: Palk Bay — between Tamil Nadu and Sri Lanka's northern coast
Palk Strait connects Palk Bay to Bay of Bengal
paired_with: Gulf of Mannar Marine National Park

related_concepts: Gulf of Mannar Marine National Park
contrast_with: Palk Bay vs Gulf of Mannar — Palk Bay is more enclosed, further north

question: Which one of the following sets of commodities are exported to India by arid and semi-arid countries in the Middle East ?
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Middle Eastern arid countries bring to mind oil/petroleum and dates or coffee (Yemen), not raw wool. Students associate Middle East with petroleum exports to India, not commodity imports from it.
prelims.elimination_hint: Arid/semi-arid Middle East countries (Iran, Afghanistan region) traditionally exported raw wool and carpets to India due to pastoral nomadic economies. Select A.
factual_hooks: Iran and Central Asia export raw wool and carpets — traditional trade
Middle East arid economies historically pastoral — wool producing

contrast_with: Gulf states (oil exporters) vs arid hinterland states (pastoral goods)

question: Which one of the following pairs is correctly matched ?
option_a: Guru Amar Das ... Miri and Piri
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Guru Gobind Singh is associated with Khalsa creation, making Dal Khalsa (C) tempting. Miri-Piri is linked to Guru Hargobind, not Amar Das. Manji system was Guru Amar Das's contribution.
prelims.elimination_hint: Guru Arjan Dev compiled the Adi Granth (1604) — this is the only correctly matched pair among the options. Select B.
factual_hooks: Guru Arjan Dev — compiled Adi Granth (1604) at Amritsar
Guru Hargobind — Miri-Piri (temporal + spiritual authority)

contrast_with: Guru Arjan Dev (Adi Granth) vs Guru Gobind Singh (Khalsa, Dasam Granth)

question:
Consider the following rivers :
I. Kishenganga
II. Ganga
III. Wainganga
IV. Penganga The correct sequence of these rivers when arranged in the north-south direction is
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Confusing Kishenganga (a tributary of Jhelum in J&K) with being south of Ganga, or misplacing Wainganga and Penganga (both in Vidarbha/Deccan) relative to each other.
prelims.elimination_hint: North-south order: Kishenganga (J&K/PoK) → Ganga (UP/Bihar) → Wainganga (MP/Maharashtra) → Penganga (Telangana/Maharashtra). Select A.
factual_hooks: Kishenganga — northern tributary of Jhelum in Kashmir
Wainganga and Penganga flow through Vidarbha region of Deccan

contrast_with: Himalayan rivers (snow-fed, perennial) vs Peninsular rivers (rain-fed, seasonal)

question: Who among the following was the first European to initiate the policy of taking part in the quarrels of Indian princes with a view to acquire territories ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Clive is strongly associated with British territorial expansion in India (Plassey 1757), making A a common wrong choice. The question asks about the FIRST European to initiate this specific policy.
prelims.elimination_hint: Dupleix (French) was the first to pioneer the policy of intervention in Indian princely politics to gain territories (Carnatic Wars 1740s). Clive copied this method from the French. Select B.
factual_hooks: Dupleix pioneered European intervention in Indian succession disputes
Carnatic Wars — Dupleix used puppet nawabs to extend French influence

contrast_with: Dupleix — initiator of intervention policy vs Clive — applied it to defeat French

question: Which one of the following is a modified stem ?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Sweet potato (modified root) and carrot (modified root/taproot) are often confused with modified stems. Coconut is a fruit but students may not consider stem modification.
prelims.elimination_hint: Potato is a stem tuber — it is a modified underground stem with nodes (eyes). Carrot and sweet potato are modified roots. Coconut is a drupe (fruit). Select D.
factual_hooks: Potato — stem tuber (modified stem with axillary buds/eyes)
Sweet potato — storage root (modified root, NOT stem)

contrast_with: Stem tuber (potato) vs Root tuber (sweet potato) — commonly confused pair

question: Fat present below the skin surface in our body, acts as a barrier against
option_a: loss of heat from the body
option_c: loss of salts from the body
option_d: entry of harmful micro-organisms from the environment
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Options B and C (body fluids, salts) seem physiologically valid since fat does have some osmotic role, but the primary function of subcutaneous fat is thermal insulation.
prelims.elimination_hint: Subcutaneous fat (adipose tissue) acts as an insulating layer preventing heat loss — it is a thermal barrier. This is its primary function. Select A.
factual_hooks: Subcutaneous fat = thermal insulator reducing heat loss
Brown adipose tissue in newborns provides additional thermogenesis

contrast_with: Subcutaneous fat (insulation) vs Visceral fat (surrounds organs — different role)

question:
Which of the following professional (s) are more likely to run the risk of a permanent change in their cell’s DNA ?
I. Researchers using carbon-14 isotope
II. X-ray technician
III. Coal miner
IV. Dyer and Painter
Select the correct answer by using the codes given below :
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Coal miners (III) face carcinogens but not direct radiation causing DNA changes; they face chemical mutagens. Students may include III or exclude IV (dyers) not realizing chemical mutagens also cause DNA changes.
prelims.elimination_hint: Carbon-14 (radioactive), X-rays (ionizing radiation), and chemical dyes/paints (chemical mutagens) all cause permanent DNA mutations. Coal miners face particulate matter/carcinogens but less direct DNA mutation risk. Select C (I, II, IV).
factual_hooks: Ionizing radiation (X-rays, radioactive isotopes) directly damages DNA
Chemical mutagens in dyes and paints can cause permanent DNA alterations

contrast_with: Ionizing radiation mutagens vs chemical mutagens — different mechanism, same DNA effect

question: Which one of the following is not an essential micronutrient for plants ?
subject: Science & Technology
