prelims.trap_factor: Statement III is the trap — A.O. Hume was a founder of INC but never its President; the first British President was George Yule (1888), not Hume. Statement I is also wrong — Sarojini Naidu was the first Indian woman, not the first woman.
prelims.elimination_hint: Eliminate I (Hume founded but never presided) and III; C.R. Das was indeed in prison (Gaya Congress 1922) and Alfred Webb presided in 1894 — so II and IV are correct. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: C.R. Das (Deshbandhu) presided INC from prison — Gaya session 1922
Alfred Webb — British, INC President 1894
A.O. Hume founded INC (1885) but never served as President
paired_with: George Yule — first British INC President

related_concepts: George Yule — first British INC President
contrast_with: AO Hume — founder but not President vs George Yule — first British President of INC

question: The Raga which is sung early in the morning is
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Bhimpalasi and Bhopali are both early morning-associated ragas in popular perception; Todi is the specifically designated pre-sunrise/early morning classical raga.
prelims.elimination_hint: Indian classical music: Ragas are time-specific. Todi is the morning raga (pre-noon); Darbari is night raga; Bhimpalasi is afternoon. Eliminate by time-association.
factual_hooks: Raga Todi is sung in early morning (before noon)
Indian classical music associates specific ragas with specific times (prahar)

contrast_with: Darbari — night raga vs Todi — morning raga

question: Which one of the following dynasties was ruling over North India at the time of Alexander’s invasion ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Students confuse Maurya with Nanda — Chandragupta Maurya defeated the Nandas after Alexander's invasion; Alexander's invasion (327-325 BCE) predates Mauryan Empire (321 BCE).
prelims.elimination_hint: Alexander invaded around 326 BCE; the Nanda dynasty ruled North India at that time. Chandragupta Maurya overthrew the Nandas only after Alexander departed.
factual_hooks: Nanda dynasty ruled at time of Alexander's invasion (326 BCE)
Chandragupta Maurya overthrew Nandas c.321 BCE to establish Mauryan Empire
Alexander retreated due to his army's refusal to advance further east

contrast_with: Mauryan Empire — post-Alexander vs Nanda Empire — contemporary with Alexander

question: The Swarna Jayanti Shahari Rozgar Yojana which came into operation from Dec. 1, 1997 aims to provide gainful employment to the urban unemployed or underemployed poor but does not include
option_c: Prime Minister’s Integrated Urban Poverty Eradication Programme
subject: Governance

prelims.trap_factor: This is an obscure 1997 scheme detail; PM's Integrated Urban Poverty Eradication Programme (PMIUPEP) was not subsumed — it is the one NOT included in SJSRY.
prelims.elimination_hint: Swarna Jayanti Shahari Rozgar Yojana (SJSRY) merged NRY, UBSP, and PMRY — three schemes. PMIUPEP was kept separate. Eliminate by process of elimination after knowing three included schemes.
factual_hooks: SJSRY (1997) merged: Nehru Rozgar Yojana, Urban Basic Services Programme, and Prime Minister's Rozgar Yojana
PM's Integrated Urban Poverty Eradication Programme was NOT merged

contrast_with: SJSRY — urban vs SGSY — rural livelihood scheme

question:
Consider the following statements regarding the Chakiarkoothu form of dance:
I. It is performed by Chakiar caste.
II. It cannot be traditionally witnessed by the higher caste Hindus.
III. Mizhavu is the accompanying instrument.
IV. Its theatre form is called koothambalam.
Which of these statements are correct ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Statement II is the trap — Chakiarkoothu was traditionally off-limits to higher caste Hindus, not lower caste; it was exclusively for Chakiar caste and low-caste audience separation is reversed.
prelims.elimination_hint: Chakiarkoothu: performed by Chakiars (I correct), Mizhavu is the instrument (III correct), Koothambalam is theatre (IV correct). Statement II is incorrect — higher castes could watch. Answer: I, III, IV = A.
factual_hooks: Chakiarkoothu: Kerala classical performance by Chakiar caste
Mizhavu: copper drum instrument used as accompaniment
Koothambalam: traditional temple theatre for Chakiarkoothu

contrast_with: Koodiyattam — involves multiple castes vs Chakiarkoothu — exclusively Chakiar caste

question: If X = -2, then X^3 - X^2 - X - 1 is equal to
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Sign errors when substituting X=-2 into X³ are common; X³ = (-2)³ = -8, not +8.
prelims.elimination_hint: Substitute X=-2: (-8) - (4) - (-2) - 1 = -8 - 4 + 2 - 1 = -11. Careful with signs at each step.
factual_hooks: Substitution method for polynomial evaluation

question: In the given figure, all line segments of the shaded portion are of the same length and at right angles to each other. The same can be cut out of a board of side 10 cm. What is the area of the shaded portion ?
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Students may calculate total 10×10=100 sq cm and subtract wrongly; the shaded portion needs careful step-pattern area calculation.
prelims.elimination_hint: For a cross/step shape cut from a 10cm square, compute the shaded area by identifying number of unit squares or using the complement method (total - unshaded).
factual_hooks: Area calculation for irregular shapes: use complement or direct counting
Equal-length right-angle segments form a specific pattern

question:
Examine the following relationships among members of a family of six persons A, B, C, D, E and F.
I. The number of males equals that of females.
II. A and E are sons of F.
III. D is the mother of two, one boy and one girl.
IV. B is the son of A. V. There is only one married couple in the family at present.
Which one of the following inferences can be drawn from the above ?
option_a: A, B and C are all females
option_b: A is the husband of D
option_c: E and F are children of D
option_d: D is the grand-daughter of F
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: With 3 males and 3 females and only one married couple, it's easy to misassign genders; the clue that B is son of A and A and E are sons of F helps establish the male line.
prelims.elimination_hint: A, E = sons of F (males). B = son of A (male). D = mother of one boy, one girl = female. One married couple among adults = A married to D. F is likely female (grandmother). Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Blood relation puzzles: draw family tree, establish gender first
One married couple constraint limits possibilities significantly

question: The given diagram shows the number of students who failed in an examination comprising papers in English, Hindi and Mathematics. The total number of students who took the test is 500. What is the percentage of students who failed in at least two subjects ?
subject: CSAT
aspect: HOW – Set Theory and Venn Diagrams

prelims.trap_factor: Students often confuse 'failed in at least two subjects' with 'failed in exactly two subjects'; must include those who failed in all three as well.
prelims.elimination_hint: Sum regions of Venn diagram where two or more circles overlap; divide by 500 and multiply by 100 for percentage. 'At least two' = exactly two + all three.
factual_hooks: Venn diagram: 'at least two' = exactly two + exactly three (all)
Percentage = (count/total) × 100

contrast_with: At least two vs exactly two — critical distinction in Venn problems

question: A rectangular piece of iron sheet measuring 50 cm by 100 cm is rolled into a cylinder of height 50 cm. If the cost of painting the cylinder is Rs. 50 per square metre, then what will be the cost of painting the outer surface of the cylinder ?
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: The unit conversion from cm to metres is critical; painting cost per sq metre but dimensions in cm leads to errors if conversion is skipped.
prelims.elimination_hint: Circumference = 100cm = 1m (width of sheet becomes circumference), height = 50cm = 0.5m. Lateral surface area = 1×0.5 = 0.5 m². Cost = 0.5 × 50 = Rs.25.
factual_hooks: Rolling a rectangle into cylinder: length = circumference, width = height
Lateral surface area of cylinder = 2πr × h = circumference × height

question: In an examination, every candidate took Physics or Mathematics or both. 65.8% took Physics and 59.2% took Mathematics. The total number of candidates was 2000. How many candidates took both Physics and Mathematics ?
subject: CSAT
aspect: HOW – Set Theory and Venn Diagrams

prelims.trap_factor: Students add 65.8 + 59.2 and forget to subtract 100 (since total is 100%, not 125%). The overlap (both subjects) = sum of percentages − 100.
prelims.elimination_hint: Using inclusion-exclusion: Both = Physics% + Maths% − 100% = 65.8 + 59.2 − 100 = 25%. 25% of 2000 = 500.
factual_hooks: Inclusion-exclusion principle: |A∪B| = |A| + |B| − |A∩B|
When all candidates take at least one, |A∪B| = 100%

question: Which one of the following is known as the “Coffee port” of the world ?
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Sao Paulo is Brazil's largest city and often confused as the coffee port; Santos is the actual port city through which coffee is exported.
prelims.elimination_hint: Santos is the port city of Sao Paulo state, Brazil — historically the world's largest coffee export port. Sao Paulo is inland; Santos is on the coast.
factual_hooks: Santos, Brazil = 'Coffee port of the world'
Sao Paulo state is the world's largest coffee producing region
Santos handles ~40% of Brazil's exports

contrast_with: Sao Paulo — city vs Santos — port for coffee export

question:
Match List I (Power generation plant) with List II (Feed material) and select the correct answer using the codes given below the Lists: List I List II
List I | List II
I. M/s Gowthami Solvents Oil Limited, Andhra Pradesh | (A) Rice husk
II. M/s KM. Sugar Mills, Uttar Pradesh | (B) Slaughter-house waste
III. M/s Satia Paper Mills, Punjab | (C) Distillery spent wash
IV. M/s Al Kabeer Exports Limited, Andhra Pradesh | (D) Black liquor
subject: Environment

prelims.trap_factor: Matching industrial units to feed materials requires specific knowledge of which plant uses which biomass; confusing distillery waste with black liquor or slaughter-house waste is common.
prelims.elimination_hint: Key anchors: Paper mills use black liquor (lignin byproduct); Sugar mills generate distillery spent wash; Slaughter-house = animal waste. Rice husk → Gowthami (oil/rice state).
factual_hooks: Black liquor: byproduct of paper/pulp industry used for energy
Distillery spent wash: liquid waste from alcohol distillation used in biogas

contrast_with: Black liquor (paper industry) vs distillery spent wash (sugar industry)

question: The given map refers to the kingdom of
option_a: Akbar at the time of capture of Khandesh in 1601
option_b: Akbar at the time of his death in 1605
option_c: Aurangzeb at the time of capture of Hyderabad
option_d: Aurangzeb at the time of his death in 1707
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Akbar's death in 1605 and capture of Khandesh in 1601 are close dates; the map extent at 1601 (Khandesh capture) vs 1605 (death) differs in the Deccan region.
prelims.elimination_hint: Akbar captured Khandesh (Asirgarh) in 1601; the empire at that point excludes territories added between 1601-1605. Aurangzeb's empire was much larger. Focus on Deccan extent.
factual_hooks: Akbar captured Khandesh/Asirgarh fort in 1601 — last major conquest
Mughal empire's maximum extent under Akbar excludes South Deccan

contrast_with: Akbar's empire (1601) vs Aurangzeb's empire (1707) — South India extent differs

question:
Consider the following events :
I. Reign of Krishna Deva Raya of Vijayanagara.
II. Construction of Qutab Minar.
III. Arrival of Portuguese in India.
IV. Death of Firoz Tughlaq.
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Qutab Minar construction started ~1193 but completed later; students may confuse Firoz Tughlaq's death (1388) with late Sultanate period. Portuguese arrival (1498) is often placed incorrectly.
prelims.elimination_hint: Chronological order: Qutab Minar construction (~1193) → Firoz Tughlaq death (1388) → Portuguese arrival (1498) → Krishna Deva Raya reign (1509-1529). Correct: II, IV, III, I = Answer A.
factual_hooks: Qutab Minar: begun by Qutb-ud-din Aibak c.1193 CE
Firoz Shah Tughlaq died 1388 CE
Portuguese (Vasco da Gama) arrived in India 1498 CE
Krishna Deva Raya reigned 1509-1529 CE

question: The given map shows locations of airports labelled as 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5. What is the correct sequence of the airports in which the hijacked Indian Airlines plane IC-814 landed after its initial take off from Kathmandu in December 1999 ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: This is a map-based question about IC-814 hijacking (1999) requiring knowledge of actual airport sequence: Amritsar → Lahore → Dubai → Kandahar — very event-specific.
prelims.elimination_hint: IC-814 hijacking route: Kathmandu (origin) → Amritsar → Lahore → Dubai → Kandahar (final). Match map labels to these cities in sequence.
factual_hooks: IC-814 hijacking 1999: Kathmandu → Amritsar → Lahore → Dubai → Kandahar
Ended with release of 3 militants including Masood Azhar

question:
Consider the following statements about the ‘Roaring Forties’:
I. They blow uninterrupted in the Northern and Southern Hemispheres.
II. They blow with great strength and constancy.
III. Their direction is generally from North - West to East in the Southern Hemisphere.
IV. Overcast skies, rain and raw weather are generally associated with them.
Which of these statements are correct ?
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Statement I is the trap — Roaring Forties blow only in Southern Hemisphere (uninterrupted by land masses), not in Northern Hemisphere where continents block them.
prelims.elimination_hint: Eliminate I (they blow only in Southern Hemisphere due to absence of land barriers). Statements II, III (NW to East in SH), IV are all correct. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Roaring Forties: westerly winds between 40°-50° South latitude
Blow uninterrupted in Southern Hemisphere — no land to break them
Direction: west to east (NW to E) in Southern Hemisphere

contrast_with: Northern Hemisphere westerlies — interrupted by continents vs Southern Hemisphere Roaring Forties — uninterrupted

question: A college student desires to get elected to the Municipal Council of his city. The validity of his nomination would depend on the important condition, among others, that
option_a: he obtains permission from the Principal of his college
option_b: he is a member of a political party
option_c: his name figures in the Voters’ List
option_d: he files a declaration owing allegiance to the Constitution of India
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Students may think party membership or principal's permission are required; the only legal requirement is being a registered voter (name in voters' list) in the constituency.
prelims.elimination_hint: Electoral law: A candidate must be a registered voter in the constituency (or anywhere for some bodies). No party membership or college permission required. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: To contest elections, name must be in voters' list — basic eligibility
No party membership required to contest local body elections in India

contrast_with: Eligibility to vote vs eligibility to contest — different thresholds

question:
Consider the following provinces of former Yugoslavia :
I. Bosnia
II. Croatia
III. Slovenia
IV. Yugoslavia.
The correct sequence of these provinces from the east to the west is
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Yugoslavia and Bosnia are often assumed to be in the center-east; Slovenia is the westernmost, Croatia is west-center, Bosnia is central, Yugoslavia (Serbia) is east.
prelims.elimination_hint: West to East: Slovenia (westernmost) → Croatia → Bosnia → Yugoslavia/Serbia. So East to West reversed: Yugoslavia, Bosnia, Croatia, Slovenia = IV, I, II, III. Answer B.
factual_hooks: Former Yugoslavia breakup: Slovenia and Croatia declared independence 1991
Geographic sequence E to W: Yugoslavia → Bosnia → Croatia → Slovenia

question: Which one of the following Muslim rulers was hailed as the ‘Jagadguru’ by his Muslim subjects because of his belief in secularism ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Zain-ul-Abidin of Kashmir is also known for religious tolerance; Husain Shah of Bengal is associated with Hindu patronage. Ibrahim Adil Shah II of Bijapur is the one called 'Jagadguru'.
prelims.elimination_hint: Ibrahim Adil Shah II of Bijapur was called 'Jagadguru' for his secular credentials, patronage of music (Kitab-i-Nauras), and tolerance of all religions.
factual_hooks: Ibrahim Adil Shah II: Sultan of Bijapur, titled 'Jagadguru' and 'Abla Baba'
Known for Kitab-i-Nauras — treatise on music and poetry showing Hindu-Muslim cultural synthesis

contrast_with: Aurangzeb — Mughal orthodoxy vs Ibrahim Adil Shah II — Deccan syncretism

question: Which one of the following statements is incorrect ?
option_b: Diu is an island in the Gulf of Khambhat
option_c: Daman and Diu were separated from Goa by the 56th Amendment of the Constitution of India
option_d: Dadra and Nagar Haveli were under French colonial rule till 1954
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Statement D is the trap: Dadra and Nagar Haveli was under Portuguese rule, not French. Pondicherry (not Daman/Diu) was under French rule. Daman and Diu were separated by 56th Amendment — true.
prelims.elimination_hint: Dadra and Nagar Haveli was under Portuguese (not French) colonial rule until 1954. This makes D incorrect. Goa statehood 1987 (A correct), Diu in Gulf of Khambhat (B correct), 56th Amendment (C correct).
factual_hooks: Dadra and Nagar Haveli: Portuguese till 1954, then Indian territory
Pondicherry (Puducherry): French colonial territory till 1954
Goa: Portuguese till 1961, full statehood 1987

contrast_with: Portuguese territories (Goa, Daman, Diu, Dadra & NH) vs French territories (Pondicherry, Karikal, Mahe, Yanam)

question: Which one of the following lakes forms an international boundary between Tanzania and Uganda ?
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Lake Malawi (Nyasa) also borders Tanzania and Mozambique; Lake Chad borders different countries. Victoria is the correct answer for Tanzania-Uganda boundary.
prelims.elimination_hint: Lake Victoria borders Tanzania, Uganda, and Kenya — it forms the Tanzania-Uganda international boundary. Lake Malawi is Tanzania-Mozambique-Malawi. Zambezi is a river, not a lake.
factual_hooks: Lake Victoria: borders Uganda, Tanzania, Kenya — forms Tanzania-Uganda boundary
Second largest freshwater lake in the world by surface area

contrast_with: Lake Victoria (Tanzania-Uganda) vs Lake Malawi/Nyasa (Tanzania-Mozambique-Malawi)

question: The growth rate of per capita income at current prices is higher than that of per capita income at constant prices, because the latter takes into account the rate of
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Students confuse constant prices with population adjustment; constant prices eliminate inflation effect, not population growth. Current vs constant price difference is about inflation, not demographics.
prelims.elimination_hint: Current price includes inflation; constant price removes inflation. So current > constant means price level (inflation) accounts for the difference. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Per capita income at current prices includes inflation effect
Per capita income at constant prices removes inflation (uses base year prices)
Current > Constant because inflation inflates current price figures

contrast_with: Current prices (nominal) vs constant prices (real) — inflation is the difference

question: Which one of the following statements is not true ?
option_a: Ghaggar’s water is utilised in the Indira Gandhi Canal
option_c: Nizam Sagar is situated on the Manjra river
option_d: Penganga is a tributary of the Godavari
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Statement A sounds plausible as Ghaggar flows through Haryana; but the Indira Gandhi Canal uses Ravi and Beas waters via Harike Barrage, not Ghaggar.
prelims.elimination_hint: Indira Gandhi Canal draws water from Sutlej-Beas link at Harike Barrage — not Ghaggar. Ghaggar is an intermittent river (lost river). So statement A is false.
factual_hooks: Indira Gandhi Canal: draws from Ravi and Beas rivers via Harike Barrage
Ghaggar: seasonal river, not a source for major irrigation canals
Nizam Sagar: on Manjra river (tributary of Godavari) — correct

contrast_with: Ghaggar (seasonal, north) vs Sutlej-Beas (perennial, source of IGC)

question: In the rough outline map of a part of Jammu and Kashmir shown in the figure, places marked A, B, C and D represent respectively
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Map-based question on Jammu & Kashmir cities requires precise knowledge of relative positions of Baramula, Srinagar, Anantnag, Kargil — easily confused without map familiarity.
prelims.elimination_hint: Srinagar is central; Baramula is northwest, Anantnag is south, Kargil is northeast. Sequence A (Baramula, Srinagar, Anantnag, Kargil) for ABCD placement matches option C.
factual_hooks: Srinagar: capital of J&K, in Kashmir Valley
Kargil: in Ladakh region, east of Zoji La pass
Baramula: northwest Kashmir, on Jhelum river

question:
Consider the following statements about the megacities of India :
I. Population of each megacity is more than 5 million.
II. All the megacities are important sea ports.
III. Megacities are either national or State capitals.
Which of these statements are correct ?
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Statement II is the trap — not all megacities (e.g., Delhi) are seaports. Statement III is also partially wrong (Hyderabad, Bangalore are not national capitals).
prelims.elimination_hint: Statement I (>5 million) is now outdated but was correct in 2000. Statement II is false (Delhi is not a seaport). Statement III is false (Hyderabad/Bangalore are not national or state capitals at time of question). Answer: D (I and III).
factual_hooks: Indian megacities at time of question: Delhi, Mumbai, Kolkata, Chennai — not all seaports
Delhi is not a seaport — eliminates statement II

contrast_with: Megacity (>10 million now) vs metropolitan city

question: The Parliament can make any law for the whole or any part of India for implementing International treaties
option_a: with the consent of all the States
option_b: with the consent of the majority of States
option_c: with the consent of the States concerned
option_d: without the consent of any State
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Students assume state consent is required for Parliament to enact laws implementing international treaties; Article 253 explicitly overrides this requirement.
prelims.elimination_hint: Article 253 of the Constitution: Parliament can legislate for implementing international treaties without consent of any State — even on State List subjects.
factual_hooks: Article 253: Parliament can make laws for whole/part of India for implementing international treaties without state consent
Overrides federal division of powers for treaty implementation

mains.contemporary_relevance: Relevant to India implementing Paris Agreement, WTO commitments through domestic legislation.

contrast_with: Article 249 — Rajya Sabha resolution needed vs Article 253 — no state/RS consent needed for treaties

question: Which one of the following is not a feature of the Government of India Act of 1935?
option_a: Diarchy at the Centre as well as in the provinces
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Students often think Diarchy was introduced at both Centre and provinces by GoI Act 1935; in fact, Diarchy was abolished at provinces and introduced/retained at Centre under 1935.
prelims.elimination_hint: GoI Act 1935 abolished Diarchy in provinces (replaced by Provincial Autonomy) but introduced Diarchy at the Centre. So 'Diarchy at the Centre as well as in the provinces' (option A) is false — not a feature.
factual_hooks: GoI Act 1935: Provincial Autonomy introduced, Diarchy abolished in provinces
Diarchy at the Centre was a feature of GoI Act 1935
Also introduced: Federal Court, All India Federation (never implemented), bicameral legislatures in some provinces

contrast_with: GoI Act 1919 — Diarchy in provinces vs GoI Act 1935 — Diarchy at Centre only

question: Which one of the following pairs of primitive tribes and places of their inhabitation is not correctly matched ?
subject: Society

prelims.trap_factor: Korba tribe is from Chhattisgarh, not Kodagu (Coorg) in Karnataka. Kodagu (Coorg) has the Kodava community — not Korba.
prelims.elimination_hint: Korba tribe inhabits Chhattisgarh (not Kodagu/Karnataka) — makes option D incorrect pair. Buksa in Pauri-Garhwal (A), Kol in Jabalpur area (B), Munda in Chhotanagpur (C) are all correct.
factual_hooks: Korba tribe: from Chhattisgarh, not Kodagu
Munda tribe: Chhotanagpur plateau (Jharkhand)
Buksa tribe: Uttarakhand (Pauri-Garhwal region)

contrast_with: Korba (Chhattisgarh) vs Kodava/Coorg community (Karnataka)

question: Which one of the following statements about a Money Bill is not correct ?
option_a: A Money Bill can be tabled in either House of Parliament
option_b: The Speaker of Lok Sabha is the final authority to decide whether a Bill is a Money Bill or not
option_c: The Rajya Sabha must return a Money Bill passed by Lok Sabha and send it for consideration within 14 days
option_d: The President cannot return a Money Bill to Lok Sabha for reconsideration
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: The most common trap is option A — students know Money Bills are special but forget they can ONLY be introduced in Lok Sabha, not either house.
prelims.elimination_hint: Article 110: Money Bill can only be introduced in Lok Sabha, NOT Rajya Sabha. This makes option A incorrect.
factual_hooks: Money Bill: introduced only in Lok Sabha (Article 109)
Speaker of Lok Sabha certifies if a bill is a Money Bill — final authority
Rajya Sabha must return within 14 days — can only recommend, not amend
President cannot return Money Bill for reconsideration

contrast_with: Money Bill vs Financial Bill — different constitutional treatment

question: The Indian National Army (INA) came into existence in 1943 in
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: INA was formed in 1942 in Singapore by Mohan Singh, then reorganized by Subhas Chandra Bose in 1943 — students confuse 'came into existence' (1942, Singapore) with Bose's reorganization (1943).
prelims.elimination_hint: INA was first formed in Singapore in 1942 under Mohan Singh after fall of Singapore to Japan; Bose took over and reorganized it in 1943 also in Singapore (Syonan-to).
factual_hooks: INA: first formed in Singapore, 1942 under Mohan Singh
Bose reorganized INA in Singapore 1943 after Japanese transferred it to him
Battle of Imphal-Kohima was INA's major military campaign

contrast_with: INA (Singapore, external) vs Congress (India, internal resistance)

question: A rise in ‘SENSEX’ means
option_a: a rise in prices of shares of all companies registered with Bombay Stock Exchange
option_b: a rise in prices of shares of all companies registered with National Stock Exchange
option_c: an overall rise in prices of shares of group of companies registered with Bombay Stock Exchange
option_d: a rise in prices of shares of all companies belonging to a group of companies registered with Bombay Stock Exchange
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Options A and D confuse SENSEX with all BSE companies; SENSEX tracks 30 selected (blue chip) companies only — not all listed companies.
prelims.elimination_hint: SENSEX = Sensitive Index = weighted index of 30 selected blue-chip companies on BSE. A rise means overall rise in these 30 companies' share prices, not all BSE companies.
factual_hooks: SENSEX: BSE Sensitive Index of 30 blue-chip companies
Rise in SENSEX = weighted average rise in prices of these 30 companies
NIFTY 50 = NSE equivalent of SENSEX

mains.contemporary_relevance: Stock market indices are key economic indicators regularly tracked in budget and monetary policy discussions.

contrast_with: SENSEX (BSE, 30 companies) vs NIFTY (NSE, 50 companies)

question: In how many different ways can six players be arranged in a line such that two of them, Ajit and Mukherjee, are never together?
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Students calculate total arrangements (6! = 720) and forget to subtract properly; the complement method requires subtracting cases where Ajit and Mukherjee ARE together.
prelims.elimination_hint: Total = 6! = 720. Together: treat A+M as one unit → 5! × 2! = 240. Not together = 720 - 240 = 480. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Complement method: arrangements where two are NOT together = total − arrangements where they ARE together
When two people must be together: treat as one unit, multiply by 2! for internal arrangement

contrast_with: 'Never together' vs 'always together' — solved by complement method

question: Which one of the following pairs is not correctly matched ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Giddha is a folk dance form from Punjab; pairing it with Jabbar Patel (who is a Marathi filmmaker) is the incorrect pair — Patel directed Marathi plays, not Giddha.
prelims.elimination_hint: Jabbar Patel is associated with Marathi theatre/film (e.g., Saamna, Simhasan) — not Giddha (Punjabi folk dance). Badal Sarkar-Baki Itihas, Vishnu Das Bhave-Sita Swayamvar, Girish Karnad-Yayati are all correct.
factual_hooks: Jabbar Patel: Marathi playwright/filmmaker — not associated with Giddha
Giddha: traditional Punjabi folk dance form for women
Girish Karnad: playwright known for Yayati, Tughlaq, Hayavadana

contrast_with: Jabbar Patel (Marathi) vs Giddha (Punjabi) — regional mismatch

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the codes given below the Lists
List I (Industrial Unit | List II (Centre)
I. Atlas Cycle Company Ltd. | (A) Bangalore
II. Bharat Earth Movers Ltd. | (B) Bhubaneswar
III. Indian Farmers Fertilisers Cooperative Ltd. | (C) Kalol
IV. National Aluminium Company Ltd. | (D) Sonepat
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Atlas Cycle (Sonepat) and BHEL (Bangalore for BEML) are commonly confused; BEML (Bharat Earth Movers) is in Bangalore, Atlas Cycle is in Sonepat.
prelims.elimination_hint: Atlas Cycle = Sonepat (D), BEML = Bangalore (A), IFFCO = Kalol (C), NALCO = Bhubaneswar (B). Answer: D (I-D, II-A, III-C, IV-B).
factual_hooks: Atlas Cycle Company: Sonepat, Haryana
BEML (Bharat Earth Movers): Bangalore
NALCO (National Aluminium): Bhubaneswar area (Damanjodi/Angul, Odisha)

question: The native State of Tripura became involved in the Freedom Movement early in the 20th century because
option_a: the kings of Tripura were always anti-British
option_b: the Bengal revolutionaries took shelter in Tripura
option_c: the tribes of the State were fiercely freedom loving
option_d: there were already some groups fighting against the kingship and its protector, the British
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Options B and C seem plausible — revolutionaries from Bengal did use Tripura; but option D (anti-kingship groups + British protector) captures the internal dynamic better.
prelims.elimination_hint: Tripura had internal groups fighting against the Manikya kingdom and its British protector — which catalyzed freedom movement engagement, not just external Bengali revolutionary influence.
factual_hooks: Tripura's freedom movement involved local groups resisting both the kingdom and British
Tripura was a princely state under British paramountcy

contrast_with: Direct British provinces vs princely states under paramountcy — different freedom movement dynamics

question:
Match the different ports of Union Territory of Pondicherry labelled as A, B, C and D in the given map with their respective names and select the correct answer using the codes given below the list of ports :
List (Ports of Pondicherry) :
1. Karaikal
2. Mahe
3. Pondicherry
4. Yanam
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Pondicherry's enclaves are spread along east and west coasts — Mahe is on west coast (Kerala), others are on east coast; confusing their north-south positions on map is the main challenge.
prelims.elimination_hint: Mahe is on Kerala (west) coast, Yanam is on Andhra coast (north-east), Karaikal and Pondicherry are on Tamil Nadu coast. A-2 (Mahe), B-1 (Karaikal), C-3 (Pondicherry), D-4 (Yanam) matches option A.
factual_hooks: Pondicherry (Puducherry) has 4 districts: Pondicherry, Karaikal, Mahe, Yanam
Mahe: only district on west (Arabian Sea) coast — in Kerala

contrast_with: Puducherry (east coast) vs Mahe (west coast) — same UT but different coasts

question: After returning from South Africa, Gandhiji launched his first successful Satyagraha in
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Students confuse Champaran (first Satyagraha) with Kheda (second Satyagraha) or Bardoli (later successful campaign). Dandi is the famous Salt March.
prelims.elimination_hint: Champaran Satyagraha (1917) was Gandhi's first successful Satyagraha in India — against indigo planters. This is a fundamental historical fact.
factual_hooks: Champaran Satyagraha 1917: Gandhi's first successful Satyagraha in India
Against indigo plantation system (tinkathia system)
Led to Champaran Agrarian Act and end of tinkathia

contrast_with: Champaran (1917, against planters) vs Kheda (1918, against revenue collection) — both early Gandhian Satyagrahas

question:
Assertion (A)
: The origin of feudal system in ancient India can be traced to military campaigns.
Reason (R)
: There was considerable expansion of the feudal system during the Gupta period.
option_a: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
option_b: Both A and R are true, but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_c: A is true, but R is false
option_d: A is false, but R is true
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: The assertion (military campaigns as origin) and reason (expansion during Gupta period) are both true but independently so — R does not explain why military campaigns led to feudalism.
prelims.elimination_hint: Both A and R are true but R (Gupta expansion) does not explain A (military origins of feudalism). Answer B: both true but R not explanation of A.
factual_hooks: Feudalism in India traced to land grants to military commanders (samantas)
Gupta period: extensive land grants to Brahmins and officials led to feudal relationships
Military samantas became feudal lords

contrast_with: European feudalism (contractual) vs Indian feudalism (patrimonial/ritual)

question:
Assertion (A)
: Ashoka annexed Kalinga to the Mauryan Empire.
Reason (R)
: Kalinga controlled the land and sea routes to South India.
option_a: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
option_b: Both A and R are true, but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_c: A is true, but R is false
option_d: A is false, but R is true
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Both statements are true and well-known facts; the challenge is judging if R correctly explains A. Kalinga's control of trade routes IS the reason Ashoka annexed it.
prelims.elimination_hint: A: Ashoka annexed Kalinga (true). R: Kalinga controlled land and sea routes to South India (true, and this IS the strategic reason for annexation). Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Kalinga War: 261 BCE, most devastating conquest of Ashoka
Kalinga controlled vital trade routes to South India and Southeast Asia
Led to Ashoka's conversion to Buddhism and policy of Dhamma

contrast_with: Pre-Kalinga Ashoka (military expansionist) vs post-Kalinga Ashoka (pacifist)

question:
Assertion (A)
: The basic weakness of the early nationalist movement lay in its narrow social base.
Reason (R)
: It fought for the narrow interests of the social groups which joined it.
option_a: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
option_b: Both A and R are true, but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_c: A is true, but R is false
option_d: A is false, but R is true
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Both A and R seem true at first glance; the distinction is that early nationalists fought for interests BEYOND the narrow groups — they were broadly liberal, not narrow in interest even if narrow in social base.
prelims.elimination_hint: A is true (narrow social base — educated middle class). R is false — they actually fought for broader national interests, not the narrow interests of just their class. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Early INC (moderate phase): dominated by educated middle class, lawyers, and professionals
They championed constitutional methods and Indianization of services — not class interests
Social base was narrow but political demands were broadly national

contrast_with: Narrow social base vs narrow political agenda — early nationalists had the former, not the latter

question:
Q10. You are aspiring to become an IAS officer and you have cleared various stages and now you have been selected for the personal interview. On the day of the interview, on the way to the venue you saw an accident where a mother and child who happen to be your relatives were badly injured. They needed immediate help.

What would you have done in such a situation? Justify your action. (250 words) 20
subject: Ethics

factual_hooks: Conflict between duty to career and duty to family/humanity
Emotional intelligence involves managing competing obligations

answer_structure.intro_approach: This case presents a classic ethical dilemma between professional commitment (IAS interview) and humanitarian duty (injured relatives), requiring balanced decision-making.
body_points: Immediate human life is paramount — first ensure emergency medical help is called (ambulance/108) and someone is arranged to stay with injured relatives
Assess severity: if life-threatening and no other help available, stay and assist — life takes precedence over career opportunity
If manageable help arranged, proceed to interview; inform panel of the circumstances — most will respect humane decision
Document the situation — compassionate ground for re-scheduling may be available
Key principle: A civil servant must demonstrate both competence and compassion — abandoning injured relatives without ensuring care would reflect poor character
answer_structure.conclusion_approach: The ideal response balances humanitarian duty with professional responsibility — ensuring emergency help for relatives first, then attending if possible, demonstrating exactly the character expected of an IAS officer.

interlinkages: GS1: Social bonds and family obligations
mains.contemporary_relevance: Tests whether candidates embody civil service values of compassion, integrity, and sound judgment under pressure.

contrast_with: Professional duty vs humanitarian duty — core ethical dilemma

question: In an open economy, the national income (Y) of the economy is : (C, I, G, X, M stand for Consumption, Investment, Govt. Expenditure, total exports and total imports respectively.)
option_b: Y = C + I + G - X + M
option_d: Y = C + I - G + X - M
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Option A (Y = C+I+G+X) omits imports; option B wrongly adds M and subtracts X. The net exports (X-M) must be added, not M added separately.
prelims.elimination_hint: Open economy GDP = C + I + G + (X - M) where X-M = net exports. Imports are subtracted because they represent foreign production. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: National Income formula: Y = C + I + G + (X - M)
X-M = Net Exports / Trade Balance
(X-M) positive = trade surplus; negative = trade deficit

contrast_with: Closed economy: Y = C + I + G vs Open economy: Y = C + I + G + (X-M)

question: A ‘black hole’ is a body in space which does not allow any radiation to come out. This property is due to its
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Students may select 'very large size' assuming larger mass traps light; it is the very high density (escape velocity exceeding speed of light) not just mass that matters.
prelims.elimination_hint: Black hole property: Escape velocity > speed of light due to extremely high density (matter packed in tiny volume). Size is small, density is enormous. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Black hole: collapse of massive star into extremely dense point (singularity)
Escape velocity exceeds speed of light due to extreme density
Event horizon: boundary beyond which nothing escapes

mains.contemporary_relevance: First image of black hole captured by Event Horizon Telescope (2019) brought renewed interest.

contrast_with: Black hole (density-based) vs neutron star (dense but radiation possible)

question:
Consider the following features of newer models of motor cars :
I. Radial tyres
II. Streamlined body
III. Multipoint fuel injection
IV. Catalytic converter with exhaust
Which of these features make the newer models of motor cars more fuel efficient ?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Catalytic converter (IV) improves emission quality but doesn't improve fuel efficiency directly. Radial tyres reduce rolling resistance improving fuel economy, but question asks specifically about fuel efficiency.
prelims.elimination_hint: Streamlined body reduces aerodynamic drag; multipoint fuel injection optimizes fuel-air mixture. Catalytic converter treats exhaust (not fuel). Radial tyres help but minimally. Answer: B (II and III).
factual_hooks: Streamlined body: reduces drag → less engine work → better fuel economy
Multipoint fuel injection (MPFI): precise fuel delivery → complete combustion → fuel efficiency
Catalytic converter: reduces pollution but not directly fuel efficiency

contrast_with: Fuel efficiency (less fuel used) vs emission control (cleaner exhaust) — catalytic converter does latter

question: Fluorescent tubes are fitted with a choke. The choke coil
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Steps up/steps down voltage are transformer functions; a choke coil is an inductor that limits current by offering inductive reactance — it neither steps up/down voltage.
prelims.elimination_hint: Choke coil = inductor with high inductive reactance to AC → reduces current without wasting energy as heat. It doesn't transform voltage. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Choke coil: inductor used to limit AC current using inductive reactance
Unlike a resistor, choke coil reduces current without significant power loss
Used in fluorescent tube circuits to limit starting current surge

contrast_with: Transformer (steps voltage) vs choke coil (reduces current) — different devices

question: For reproducing sound, a CD (Compact Disc) audio player uses a
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Quartz crystal is used in clocks/oscillators; barium titanate is a piezoelectric ceramic used in microphones. Laser beam is the correct technology for CD audio playback.
prelims.elimination_hint: CD player uses laser beam to read pits and lands on the disc surface; reflected laser intensity variations are converted to digital audio signal. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: CD player: uses semiconductor laser (780nm wavelength) to read disc
Pits and lands on CD surface encode binary data
Reflected light variations converted to audio signal

contrast_with: Vinyl record (needle/stylus) vs CD (laser beam) — different reading mechanisms

question: Q18. The women's questions arose in modern India as a part of the 19th century social reform movement. What were the major issues and debates concerning women in that period? (250 words) 15
subject: History

factual_hooks: 19th century reform movements addressed sati, widow remarriage, child marriage, female education
Reformers: Raja Ram Mohan Roy, Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar, Pandita Ramabai
Debates: tradition vs modernity, religious authority vs social progress

answer_structure.intro_approach: The 19th century social reform movement in India was fundamentally intertwined with the 'women's question' — a debate about the place of women in a modernizing yet tradition-bound society.
body_points: Major issues: Sati (abolished 1829), child marriage, purdah system, widow remarriage prohibition, female infanticide, lack of female education
Sati abolition (1829): Raja Ram Mohan Roy's campaign → Regulation XVII by William Bentinck
Widow Remarriage: Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar's advocacy → Hindu Widow Remarriage Act 1856
Female education: Jyotiba Phule and Savitribai Phule opened first school for girls (1848); Pandita Ramabai championed women's education
Debates: Traditionalists (Dharmasabha) vs reformers on whether reform was anti-Hindu; some nationalists opposed social reform as British interference
Women's agency: Pandita Ramabai, Tarabai Shinde wrote against patriarchal norms — early feminist voices
Inter-linkage with nationalism: Social reform was seen as necessary precondition for political independence by reformers
answer_structure.conclusion_approach: The 19th century women's question was not merely about legal reform but reflected a deeper struggle between colonial modernity and indigenous patriarchy, laying the foundation for modern Indian feminism.

interlinkages: GS1: Art and Culture — changing status of women
mains.contemporary_relevance: Continuity of issues like gender equality and women's safety makes this historically grounded topic perennially relevant.

prerequisite_concepts: Social reform movements in colonial India
Status of women in 19th century India
contrast_with: Reform-led change vs revolutionary change in women's status

question: Which one of the following is not explicitly stated in the Constitution of India but followed as a convention ?
option_a: The Finance Minister is to be a Member of the Lower House
option_b: The Prime Minister has to resign if he loses majority in the Lower House
option_c: All the parts of India are to be represented in the Council of Ministers
option_d: In the event of both the President and the Vice-President demitting office simultaneously before the end of their tenure the Speaker of the Lower House of the Parliament will officiate as the President
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Students confuse constitutional provisions with conventions; many rules about the PM's position (must resign if loses majority) are conventions, not explicit constitutional text.
prelims.elimination_hint: The PM's obligation to resign on losing Lok Sabha majority is a constitutional convention, not written in the Constitution. The other options either have statutory basis or are explicitly provided. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: PM must resign if loses Lok Sabha majority: convention, not written law
Constitutional convention: unwritten but binding political practice
Finance Minister being from Lower House is also a convention

contrast_with: Constitutional convention (unwritten, binding) vs constitutional provision (written in Constitution)

question:
Which of the following benefits are likely to accrue to India from the World Trade Organisation?
I. India’s share in the world trade is to go up from the present 600 million US dollars to 5 billion US dollars by 2000 A.D.
II. It will help boost exports of agricultural commodities from India.
III. India’s share in the world trade is likely to triple by the year 2000 A.D.
Select the correct answer using the codes given below :
subject: International Relations

prelims.trap_factor: Statement I contains specific dollar figures (600 million to 5 billion) — these figures are inaccurate/dated. Statement III claims 'triple by 2000' — also was not realized. Only II (agricultural exports) has some validity.
prelims.elimination_hint: Statement I has specific wrong figures (India's trade share in different units). Statement III (tripling by 2000) was not achieved. Only II (agricultural export boost from WTO) was a stated objective. Answer: D (II and III)... actually II and III based on 1995 context; revisit — in exam answer is D.
factual_hooks: WTO came into existence January 1, 1995 replacing GATT
India's agricultural exports were expected to benefit from reduced global trade barriers
India's actual trade share remained far below early optimistic projections

mains.contemporary_relevance: WTO remains central to India's trade negotiations and agricultural subsidy debates.

contrast_with: GATT (multilateral agreement) vs WTO (international organization with dispute settlement)

question: Comet Shoemaker-Levy 9 hit the planet
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: The dramatic nature of the event (comet hitting a gas giant) is distinctive; Mars is sometimes thought as a target due to proximity, but Jupiter's gravity was the attractor.
prelims.elimination_hint: Comet Shoemaker-Levy 9 famously collided with Jupiter in July 1994 — widely watched astronomical event. Jupiter's strong gravity captured and broke apart the comet.
factual_hooks: Comet Shoemaker-Levy 9 hit Jupiter in July 1994 — first observed extraterrestrial collision
Comet broke into 21 fragments due to Jupiter's tidal forces before impact
Impacts left visible scars on Jupiter's atmosphere

question:
Examine the following statements :
I. All children are inquisitive.
II. Some children are inquisitive.
III. No children are inquisitive.
IV. Some children are not inquisitive.
Among these statements, the two statements which cannot both be true simultaneously but can both be false would be
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Students confuse 'subcontrary' (can't both be false but can both be true) with 'contradictory' (can't both be true or both be false). I (All) and III (No) are contradictories — they can't both be true OR false.
prelims.elimination_hint: In classical logic: 'All' (I) and 'No' (III) are contraries — cannot both be true simultaneously but CAN both be false (if some are inquisitive). Answer: A (I and III).
factual_hooks: Contraries: 'All A are B' and 'No A are B' — cannot both be true, but can both be false
Classical square of opposition: universal affirmative vs universal negative = contrary relationship

contrast_with: Contrary (both can be false) vs Contradictory (exactly one must be true)

question: The signatories to the treaty banning chemical weapons include
option_a: USA, Russia, India, Iran and Israel
option_b: Iraq, Libya, India, Russia and China
option_c: Brazil, Angola, North Korea, Pakistan and USA
option_d: Syria, Sri Lanka, Japan, Singapore and France
subject: International Relations

prelims.trap_factor: This is a 1995-context question about CWC signatories; Israel has signed but not ratified, and Iran has signed and ratified. Syria and North Korea were not signatories in 1995.
prelims.elimination_hint: USA, Russia, India, and Iran signed CWC; Israel signed but hasn't ratified. Syria joined only in 2013. Option A (USA, Russia, India, Iran, Israel) reflects 1995 scenario best.
factual_hooks: Chemical Weapons Convention (CWC) opened for signature 1993, entered into force 1997
India ratified CWC and declared/destroyed chemical weapons
Syria joined CWC in 2013 after chemical weapons use in civil war

mains.contemporary_relevance: OPCW's role in Syria chemical weapons investigations remains contentious.

contrast_with: CWC signatories vs non-signatories — North Korea, Egypt, South Sudan not members

question: Examine the map of Jammu and Kashmir given below : The mountain ranges marked 1, 2, 3 and 4 are respectively
option_a: Ladakh, Zanskar, Karakoram and Pir Panjal
option_b: Karakoram, Ladakh, Zanskar and Pir Panjal
option_c: Karakoram, Zanskar, Pir Panjal and Ladakh
option_d: Ladakh, Pir Panjal, Karakoram and Zanskar
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Zanskar and Ladakh ranges are often transposed; from south to north in J&K: Pir Panjal → Zanskar → Ladakh → Karakoram. Students mix up the middle two.
prelims.elimination_hint: North to south order in J&K: Karakoram (northernmost) → Ladakh → Zanskar → Pir Panjal (southernmost). So 1→4 from north: Karakoram, Ladakh, Zanskar, Pir Panjal. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: J&K mountain ranges N to S: Karakoram > Ladakh > Zanskar > Pir Panjal
Karakoram contains K2 — second highest peak in world
Pir Panjal separates Kashmir Valley from Jammu plains

contrast_with: Greater Himalayas vs Trans-Himalayas (Ladakh, Karakoram ranges)

question: If in an election to a State Legislative Assembly the candidate who is declared elected loses his deposit, it means that
option_b: the election was for a multi-member constituency
option_c: the elected candidate’s victory over his nearest rival was very marginal
option_d: a very large number of candidates contested the election
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: The scenario of 'elected candidate losing deposit' seems contradictory; it's possible only when a very large number of candidates contest, diluting vote shares so even the winner gets less than 1/6th of votes.
prelims.elimination_hint: Deposit is forfeited if a candidate gets less than 1/6th of total valid votes polled. If many candidates split votes, even the winner may get below this threshold. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Deposit forfeited if candidate gets < 1/6th of valid votes polled
Many candidates splitting votes can cause even the winner to lose deposit
RPA 1951 governs election deposits and forfeitures

contrast_with: Low polling percentage vs vote split — both can cause deposit loss

question: Which one of the following pairs is not correctly matched ?
option_a: Jamnalal Bajaj ................... Satyagraha Ashram at Wardha
option_c: Lala Lajpat Rai ................ National School at Lahore
option_d: Bal Gangadhar Tilak ..................... Satya Shodhak Sabha
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Bal Gangadhar Tilak is associated with Ganapati and Shivaji festivals — not Satya Shodhak Sabha. Satya Shodhak Sabha was founded by Jyotiba Phule (not Tilak) in 1873.
prelims.elimination_hint: Satya Shodhak Sabha (Truth-Seeking Society, 1873): founded by Jyotiba Phule for lower caste rights — not associated with Tilak. Tilak founded Deccan Education Society. Answer: D (incorrect pair).
factual_hooks: Satya Shodhak Sabha: founded by Jyotiba Phule (1873) — for lower caste emancipation, not Tilak
Tilak: founded Deccan Education Society, Kesari newspaper, revived Ganapati festival
Jamnalal Bajaj: Satyagraha Ashram Wardha — correct pairing

prerequisite_concepts: Social reform organisations in colonial India
contrast_with: Tilak (orthodox Hindu nationalist) vs Phule (anti-caste reformer) — ideological contrast

question: When a CD (Compact Disc used in audio and video systems) is seen in sunlight, rainbow like colours are seen. This can be explained on the basis of the phenomenon of
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Refraction alone is a common guess (like prism splitting light). A CD doesn't just refract — it uses diffraction grating effect from its tracks plus reflection to produce rainbow colors.
prelims.elimination_hint: CD has microscopic tracks acting as diffraction grating; light reflects off the surface and diffracts into different wavelengths (colors). Answer: A (reflection + diffraction).
factual_hooks: CD tracks act as diffraction grating (1.6 micron spacing)
White light reflects and diffracts into component colors (rainbow)
Combination: reflection from surface + diffraction from track patterns

contrast_with: Prism (refraction only) vs CD (reflection + diffraction)

question: The stones formed in human kidney consist mostly of
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Calcium alone is not the constituent; the compound calcium oxalate is the most common kidney stone — not just calcium, and not magnesium sulphate.
prelims.elimination_hint: Kidney stones (renal calculi): most commonly calcium oxalate crystals. Other types exist (uric acid, struvite) but calcium oxalate is most common. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Kidney stones: ~80% are calcium oxalate
Oxalate from diet + excess calcium → crystal formation in kidney
Prevention: hydration, low oxalate diet

contrast_with: Kidney stones (calcium oxalate) vs gall stones (cholesterol/bilirubin)

question: Most of the explosions in mines occur due to the mixing of
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Hydrogen-oxygen and acetylene-oxygen explosions exist in different contexts; but mine explosions specifically involve methane (firedamp) mixing with air to form explosive mixture.
prelims.elimination_hint: Methane (CH₄) = firedamp in coal mines; when mixed with air in 5-15% concentration, it becomes explosive. This is the cause of most mine explosions. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Methane (firedamp) + air: explosive mixture in coal mines
Methane is released from coal seams during mining
5-15% methane in air = explosive range

contrast_with: Methane (mine explosion) vs CO₂ (suffocation risk) — different mine hazards

question: Which one of the following materials is very hard and very ductile ?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Tungsten is hard but brittle at room temperature; carborundum (silicon carbide) is hard but brittle. Nichrome is an alloy that is both hard AND ductile — unusual combination.
prelims.elimination_hint: Nichrome (nickel-chromium alloy): both very hard and ductile — used in heating elements. Carborundum is hard but brittle; tungsten is hard but less ductile; cast iron is brittle.
factual_hooks: Nichrome: Ni-Cr alloy, high hardness + ductility + high melting point
Used in electric heating elements (toasters, hair dryers)
Carborundum (SiC): very hard but brittle — not ductile

contrast_with: Brittle (cast iron, carborundum) vs ductile (nichrome, copper)

question: A small pouch containing silica gel is often found in bottles of medicine in tablet or powder form because silica gel
option_d: absorbs all gases present inside the bottle
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Option D (absorbs all gases) sounds comprehensive but is wrong — silica gel specifically absorbs water vapor (moisture), not all gases. Option A (kills bacteria) is also tempting.
prelims.elimination_hint: Silica gel (SiO₂·xH₂O) is a desiccant — absorbs moisture/water vapor by adsorption. Prevents drug degradation from humidity. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Silica gel: porous desiccant that adsorbs water vapor
Used to maintain low humidity in drug packaging to prevent degradation
Can be regenerated by heating to remove absorbed moisture

contrast_with: Silica gel (moisture absorber) vs activated carbon (odor/gas absorber)

question: Which one of the following polymers is widely used for making bullet proof material ?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Kevlar (polyamides) is famously used in bulletproof vests, so option B is a strong distractor; however, polycarbonates are used specifically for bulletproof glass and transparent armor.
prelims.elimination_hint: Kevlar is polyamide used in bulletproof fabric/vests; polycarbonates (e.g., Lexan) are used for bulletproof transparent shields/glass. Question asks for 'material' broadly — polycarbonates (D) is correct.
factual_hooks: Polycarbonates: high impact resistance, used in bulletproof glass, shields, visors
Kevlar (polyamide): used in bulletproof vests/fabric — high tensile strength
Polycarbonate (Lexan): 250x stronger than glass

contrast_with: Polycarbonate (transparent bulletproof material) vs Kevlar polyamide (fabric-based armor)

question:
Match List I (Industrial processes) with List II (Industry with which associated) and select the correct answer using the codes given below the Lists
List I | List II
I. Cracking | (A) Rubber
II. Smelting | (B) Petroleum
III. Hydrogenation | (C) Copper
IV. Vulcanization | (D) Edible fats
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Cracking is often associated with rubber (vulcanization is rubber); smelting is associated with metals but students confuse which metal. Hydrogenation of edible fats is less known.
prelims.elimination_hint: Anchor: Vulcanization = Rubber (IV-A); Hydrogenation = Edible fats/vanaspati (III-D); Smelting = Copper/metals (II-C); Cracking = Petroleum (I-B). Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Cracking: breaking heavy petroleum fractions into lighter ones
Smelting: extracting metal (copper) from ore using heat
Hydrogenation: adding H₂ to unsaturated fats → vanaspati ghee
Vulcanization: adding sulfur to rubber for strength and elasticity

contrast_with: Cracking (breaks bonds) vs Hydrogenation (adds hydrogen) — opposite in direction

question:
Assertion (A)
: Large cold storage plants use ammonia as refrigerant while domestic refrigerators use chlorofluorocarbons.
Reason (R)
: Ammonia can be liquified at ambient temperatures at low pressures.
option_a: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
option_b: Both A and R are true, but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_c: A is true, but R is false
option_d: A is false, but R is true
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: The Reason states ammonia can be liquified at ambient temperature at low pressures — this is false; ammonia requires moderate pressure (not low). This makes A false (R is wrong).
prelims.elimination_hint: Assertion A is true (ammonia in large cold storage, CFCs in domestic). Reason R is false — ammonia liquefies at moderate pressure, not low pressure. Answer: C (A true, R false).
factual_hooks: Ammonia: used in large industrial refrigeration — cheaper, efficient, but toxic
CFCs: used in domestic refrigerators — non-toxic, safe for homes (but ozone-depleting)
Ammonia liquefies at ~-33°C at 1 atm, or at ~10 atm at room temp — NOT low pressure

mains.contemporary_relevance: Kigali Amendment to Montreal Protocol phases down HFCs — continuing relevance of refrigerant choice.

contrast_with: Ammonia refrigerant (industrial, toxic) vs CFC (domestic, ozone-depleting)

question: Which one of the following organisms can serve as a biofertiliser for rice crop?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Rhizobium fixes nitrogen in legumes (not rice) and Azotobacter is a free-living nitrogen fixer for upland crops — both are tempting distractors. Blue-green algae (cyanobacteria like Anabaena) are the classic biofertiliser for paddy/rice due to waterlogged conditions.
prelims.elimination_hint: Eliminate Rhizobium (legume-specific), Azotobacter (upland crops), and Mycorrhizal fungi (phosphorus solubiliser, not N-fixer); BGA uniquely thrives in flooded rice fields.
factual_hooks: Blue-green algae (cyanobacteria) fix atmospheric nitrogen in flooded rice fields
Anabaena azollae in Azolla is the classic example used in paddy cultivation

mains.contemporary_relevance: Biofertilisers are central to sustainable agriculture and reducing chemical fertiliser dependence.

contrast_with: Rhizobium — fixes N only in legume root nodules
Azotobacter — free-living but for upland crops

question: Within biological communities, some species are important in determining the ability of a large number of other species to persist in the community. Such species are called
subject: Environment

prelims.trap_factor: Allopatric and sympatric refer to speciation geography, not community roles; threatened species is a conservation status, not an ecological function — these can confuse those who conflate ecological roles with taxonomic/conservation labels.
prelims.elimination_hint: Focus on the phrase 'determining ability of large number of other species to persist' — this is the textbook definition of a keystone species; eliminate others by their unrelated meanings.
factual_hooks: Keystone species have disproportionately large effects on ecosystem relative to their biomass
Sea otters, wolves, and elephants are classic keystone species examples

mains.contemporary_relevance: Keystone species conservation is central to biodiversity hotspot management and rewilding projects.

contrast_with: Umbrella species — large habitat needs protect others
Foundation species — physically structure habitat

question:
A goat is tied to two poles P and Q with ropes that are 15-metre long. P and Q are 20-metre apart as shown in the given diagram:
Which one of the following shaded portions indicates the total area over which the goat can graze ?
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Students may not account for the overlapping area correctly when the two circular arcs (radii 15 m each, poles 20 m apart) intersect; the goat can reach both sides of each pole creating a combined non-circular grazing area.
prelims.elimination_hint: Draw circles of radius 15 from each pole (P and Q, 20 m apart); since 15+15 > 20, circles overlap — the total area is two full circles minus overlap, but the tethering constrains movement, making option II correct.
factual_hooks: When rope length > half the distance between poles, grazing areas overlap
Total grazing area = sum of two circular sectors minus the constraint from rope geometry

contrast_with: Simple single-pole grazing — πr²
Non-overlapping circles — when rope < half pole distance

question: The American multinational company, Monsanto has produced an insect-resistant cotton variety that is undergoing field- trails in India. A toxin gene from which ONE of the following bacteria has been transferred to this transgenic cotton ?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Bacillus subtilis is a commonly known bacterium used in other biotech applications, making it an attractive wrong option; all four options start with 'Bacillus' increasing confusion.
prelims.elimination_hint: Bt cotton is named after Bacillus thuringiensis — the 'Bt' in Bt cotton directly stands for this species; this mnemonic eliminates all other options.
factual_hooks: Bt cotton contains the cry gene from Bacillus thuringiensis that produces insecticidal proteins
Bt toxin is specifically effective against lepidopteran pests like bollworm

mains.contemporary_relevance: Bt cotton remains India's only approved GM crop; Bt brinjal approval debate continues.

contrast_with: Bacillus subtilis — used in probiotics, not transgenic insect resistance

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the codes given below the Lists
(Substance) | (Physiological role)
I. Ptyalin | (A) Converts angioten-sinogen in blood into angiotensin
II. Pepsin | (B) Digests starch
III. Renin | (C) Digests proteins
IV. Oxytocin | (D) Hydrolyses fats
| (E) Induces contraction of smooth muscles
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Renin has two physiological meanings (kidney enzyme vs stomach enzyme in infants), causing confusion; the answer given (I-B correct) but stated correct option is B which matches I-B, II-C, III-A, IV-E — students mixing up renin-kidney vs renin-stomach.
prelims.elimination_hint: Ptyalin (salivary amylase) digests starch (B) — this unique link helps anchor the match; then Oxytocin contracts smooth muscles (E) is well-known, narrowing to option A.
factual_hooks: Ptyalin = salivary amylase → breaks down starch
Renin (kidney) converts angiotensinogen to angiotensin I; different from rennin (stomach)

contrast_with: Rennin (stomach enzyme in infants) vs Renin (kidney enzyme) — different functions

question: In what way does the Indian Parliament exercise control over the administration?
option_a: Through Consultative Committees of various ministries
option_c: By making the administrators send periodic reports
option_d: By compelling the executive to issue writs
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Consultative Committees sound official and may seem like the primary oversight mechanism; the option about compelling writs confuses executive oversight with judicial power.
prelims.elimination_hint: Parliamentary Committees (PAC, Estimates Committee, Standing Committees) are the constitutional instruments of legislative oversight over the executive — this is a well-established polity fact.
factual_hooks: Parliamentary Committees like PAC, Estimates Committee, and Departmental Standing Committees oversee administration
Parliament controls executive through Question Hour, motions, and committees — not direct administrative orders

mains.contemporary_relevance: Parliamentary committee scrutiny of executive is a recurring governance debate.

contrast_with: Writs — issued by courts, not Parliament
Consultative Committees — advisory, informal

question: The chemical used as a ‘fixer’ in photography is
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Sodium sulphate sounds similar to sodium thiosulphate; students unfamiliar with photography chemistry may guess borax (used in other industrial processes).
prelims.elimination_hint: Sodium thiosulphate ('hypo') dissolves unexposed silver halide crystals in photographic film — this specific function is the 'fixing' process; it's a classic chemistry application fact.
factual_hooks: Sodium thiosulphate (hypo) is the fixer in photography — removes unexposed silver salts
Fixing prevents further darkening of the photographic image

contrast_with: Sodium sulphate — unrelated industrial chemical
Ammonium persulphate — oxidising agent, used as developer not fixer

question: Who among the following organised the famous Chittagong armoury raid ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Laxmi Sehgal (INA) and Batukeshwar Datta (Central Assembly bomb) are associated with other famous revolutionary acts, making them plausible distractors.
prelims.elimination_hint: Surya Sen (Masterda) is the definitive organiser of the Chittagong Armoury Raid (1930) — this is a standard fact; J.M. Sengupta was a Congress politician, not a revolutionary raid leader.
factual_hooks: Surya Sen led the Chittagong Armoury Raid on 18 April 1930
The raid aimed to capture arms and inspire an armed uprising against British rule

contrast_with: Batukeshwar Datta — threw bombs in Central Legislative Assembly (1929) alongside Bhagat Singh

question: In which one of the following cities is the Lingaraja Temple located ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Shravanabelagola has famous Jain monuments (Gommateshwara) and Bijapur has Gol Gumbaz — these are famous temple cities that can confuse; Lingaraja is specifically an Odishan Nagara temple.
prelims.elimination_hint: Lingaraja Temple is the most famous temple of Bhubaneswar (City of temples), dedicated to Lord Shiva — this Odisha connection is a standard geography-culture fact.
factual_hooks: Lingaraja Temple in Bhubaneswar is a classic Kalinga/Nagara style temple dedicated to Shiva
Bhubaneswar is called the 'City of Temples' with over 400 temples

contrast_with: Bijapur — Gol Gumbaz (Islamic architecture)
Shravanabelagola — Jain Gommateshwara statue

question: The above map is the Union Territory of
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Without the actual map, this is an image-dependent question; based on the correct answer (Dadra and Nagar Haveli), the enclave nature of this UT surrounded by Gujarat and Maharashtra is the key distinguishing geographic shape.
prelims.elimination_hint: Dadra and Nagar Haveli (before merger) was a landlocked enclave; Daman and Diu had coastal segments; Chandigarh is a city-UT in north India — map shape and location are the only guides.
factual_hooks: Dadra and Nagar Haveli (now merged with Daman & Diu) was a landlocked UT near Gujarat-Maharashtra border
Pondicherry/Puducherry has non-contiguous parts along different coastlines

mains.contemporary_relevance: Dadra & Nagar Haveli merged with Daman & Diu in 2020 to form a single UT.

contrast_with: Daman and Diu — coastal UT with two separate segments on Arabian Sea

question:
Consider the following organisations :
I. Atomic Minerals Directorate for Research and Exploration
II. Heavy Water Board
III. Indian Rare Earths Limited
IV. Uranium Corporation of India
Which of these is/are under the Department of Atomic Energy ?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Students may think only UCIL or AMD are under DAE, overlooking Heavy Water Board and Indian Rare Earths Ltd which are also directly under DAE.
prelims.elimination_hint: All four — AMD, Heavy Water Board, Indian Rare Earths Ltd, and UCIL — are statutory/public sector bodies under the Department of Atomic Energy; when all seem plausible, all-inclusive option D is often correct.
factual_hooks: DAE oversees the entire nuclear fuel cycle: exploration (AMD), heavy water (HWB), rare earths (IREL), and uranium (UCIL)
Indian Rare Earths Ltd processes thorium-bearing minerals critical for India's 3-stage nuclear programme

mains.contemporary_relevance: India's nuclear expansion plans keep DAE bodies strategically important.

contrast_with: NPCIL — operates nuclear power plants, also under DAE but not listed here

question:
The temperature and rainfall of a meteorological station are given below:
which region is represented by the climate ?
J | F | M | A | M | J | J | A | S | O | N | D
Temperature (°C) | 9.4 | 1.0.6 | 1.1.7 | 1.2.2 | 1.3.3 | 1.3.9 | 1.3.9 | 1.4.4 | 1.5.6 | 1.5.0 | 1.3.3 | 1.0.6
Rainfall (cm) | 1.2.2 | 9.1 | 7.9 | 2.5 | 1.8 | 0.3 | - | - | 0.8 | 2.5 | 6.1 | 1.1.7
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: The data shows mild temperatures (9–15°C range) and dry summers (July-Aug near zero rainfall) with winter maximum rainfall — students may confuse with NW European (wetter summers) or Steppe (drier overall).
prelims.elimination_hint: Mediterranean climate signature: mild temperatures year-round, dry hot summers (near-zero rainfall July-Aug), winter maximum rainfall — this pattern precisely matches the data table.
factual_hooks: Mediterranean climate: Cs type — dry summers, wet winters, mild temperatures
Summer drought and winter rain is the defining feature — opposite of monsoon

contrast_with: Monsoon — summer maximum rainfall
Steppe — very low rainfall throughout year

question: Who amongst the following Englishmen, first translated Bhagavad-Gita into English ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: William Jones is famous for founding the Asiatic Society and translating other Sanskrit texts; Alexander Cunningham is associated with archaeology — both are plausible for Orientalist scholarship.
prelims.elimination_hint: Charles Wilkins published the first English translation of the Bhagavad Gita in 1785 under the patronage of Warren Hastings — this is a standard colonial-era fact; Jones translated other texts later.
factual_hooks: Charles Wilkins translated Bhagavad Gita into English in 1785
Warren Hastings wrote the preface to Wilkins' translation — reflecting Company's Orientalist policy

contrast_with: William Jones — translated Shakuntala, founded Asiatic Society (1784)
Alexander Cunningham — founded Archaeological Survey of India

question: The term National Income represents
option_a: Gross National Product at market prices minus depreciation
option_b: Gross National Product at market prices minus depreciation plus net factor income from abroad
option_c: Gross National Product at market prices minus depreciation and indirect taxes plus subsidies
option_d: Gross National Product at market prices minus net factor income from abroad
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: The distinction between NNP at market prices and NNP at factor cost (National Income) trips many students; option A (GNP minus depreciation = NNP at market prices, not National Income) is the classic trap.
prelims.elimination_hint: National Income = NNP at Factor Cost = GNP at market prices − Depreciation − Net Indirect Taxes (indirect taxes minus subsidies); option C correctly captures this formula.
factual_hooks: National Income = GNP at MP − Depreciation − Indirect Taxes + Subsidies = NNP at Factor Cost
NNP at Market Price ≠ National Income; you must also subtract net indirect taxes

mains.contemporary_relevance: National income accounting is fundamental to India's GDP measurement and economic planning.

contrast_with: NNP at market prices = GNP − Depreciation (without adjusting for indirect taxes)

question: The largest number of Buddhists is found in
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Bihar (Bodh Gaya) and Uttar Pradesh (Sarnath, Kushinagar) are strongly associated with Buddhist history, making them attractive wrong answers; Maharashtra has a large Dalit Buddhist population due to B.R. Ambedkar's conversion.
prelims.elimination_hint: Maharashtra has the largest Buddhist population by count (~5.8 million) due to mass conversions by Dalits following Ambedkar's 1956 conversion; Bihar has historical sites but smaller current Buddhist population.
factual_hooks: Maharashtra has India's largest Buddhist population due to neo-Buddhist (Ambedkarite) conversions since 1956
Dr. B.R. Ambedkar converted to Buddhism in Nagpur in 1956 along with ~500,000 followers

contrast_with: Bihar — highest concentration of Buddhist heritage sites but not largest Buddhist population

question:
Consider the following statements regarding environment issues of India:
I. Gulf of Manner is one of the biosphere reserves.
II. The Ganga Action Plan, phase II has been merged with the National River Conservation Plan.
III. The National Museum of Natural History at New Delhi imparts non-formal education in environment and conservation.
IV. Environmental Information System (ENVIS) acts as a decentralised information network for environmental information.
Which of these statements are correct ?
subject: Environment

prelims.trap_factor: Statement I (Gulf of Mannar as biosphere reserve) is true, but the question is from 2001; students may be unsure about specific program mergers (GAP Phase II with NRCP) and ENVIS as decentralised network.
prelims.elimination_hint: All four statements are correct (answer B): Gulf of Mannar is indeed a biosphere reserve; Ganga Action Plan Phase II was merged with NRCP; NMNH provides environmental education; ENVIS is a decentralised environmental information network.
factual_hooks: Gulf of Mannar was India's first Marine Biosphere Reserve (1989)
ENVIS (Environmental Information System) is MoEF's decentralised environmental data network

mains.contemporary_relevance: ENVIS continues to be an important environmental data portal under MoEFCC.

contrast_with: MAB Programme (UNESCO) vs Indian Biosphere Reserve network

question: The technique used to transmit audio signals in television broadcasts is
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Amplitude Modulation (AM radio) is the commonly known audio transmission method; students may confuse TV's FM audio with AM radio audio, or assume PCM for digital TV.
prelims.elimination_hint: Television audio uses FM (Frequency Modulation) while the video signal uses AM — this AM/FM split in TV transmission is a standard electronics fact distinguishing it from radio.
factual_hooks: TV broadcasts: video signal uses AM, audio signal uses FM
FM is preferred for audio due to better noise immunity compared to AM

contrast_with: AM — used for TV video and medium-wave radio
PCM — used in digital audio/CD, not analog TV broadcast

question:
Consider the following statements regarding the Armed Forces :
I. First batch of women pilots was commissioned in Indian Air Force in 1996.
II. Officers’ Training Academy is located in Nagpur.
III. Southern Command of Indian Navy has its Headquarters at Chennai.
IV. One of the Regional Headquarters of Coast Guard is located at Port Blair.
Which of these statements are correct ?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Multiple statements about military geography can be confusing without direct knowledge; Port Blair Regional HQ of Coast Guard (statement IV) is the only correct one, while OTA is in Chennai (not Nagpur) and Southern Naval Command is in Kochi (not Chennai).
prelims.elimination_hint: OTA is in Chennai; Southern Naval Command HQ is in Kochi; women pilots were commissioned in 1994 not 1996 — only statement IV (Port Blair Coast Guard HQ) is correct.
factual_hooks: Officers Training Academy (OTA) is located in Chennai, Tamil Nadu
Southern Naval Command is headquartered at Kochi, Kerala

contrast_with: IMA (Indian Military Academy) — Dehradun; NDA — Pune; OTA — Chennai
