question:
Consider the following statements regarding power sector in India :
I. The installed capacity of power generation is around 95000 MW.
II. Nuclear plants contribute nearly 15% of total power generation.
III. Hydroelectricity plants contribute nearly 40% of total power generation.
IV. Thermal plants at present account for nearly 80% of total power generation.
Which of these statements is/are correct ?
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: All four statements mix up actual percentage contributions — nuclear power was never 15% (it's ~3-4%), and hydro was ~25% not 40%, so only statement I (installed capacity ~95,000 MW in 2001) is broadly correct.
prelims.elimination_hint: Eliminate by knowing that thermal contributes ~70-75% (not 80%), nuclear ~3% (not 15%), hydro ~25% (not 40%); only statement I about installed capacity was approximately correct for 2001.
factual_hooks: India's power sector in 2001: thermal dominant (~70%+), hydro ~25%, nuclear minimal (~3%)
India's installed capacity was approximately 95,000 MW around 2001

mains.contemporary_relevance: India's energy mix has dramatically shifted with solar/wind additions; thermal share declining.

contrast_with: Current energy mix has shifted with significant renewable additions post-2010

question: Recently, to which of the following countries did India offer to build Buddha Temple?
subject: International Relations

prelims.trap_factor: Myanmar (Buddhist majority) and Thailand (Buddhist) are more naturally associated with Buddha temples; offering to build a temple in China seems counterintuitive given political relations.
prelims.elimination_hint: This is a current affairs question from 2005 about India's cultural diplomacy with China; India's Buddha temple offer to China was a soft-power gesture — option A (China) is the answer per the question.
factual_hooks: India offered to build a Buddha Temple in China as part of cultural diplomacy (circa 2005)
Buddhist heritage diplomacy is a key soft-power tool in India-China relations

mains.contemporary_relevance: Buddhist cultural diplomacy remains relevant in India's engagement with East/Southeast Asia.

contrast_with: India-Myanmar Buddhist ties are deeper and older; China offer was a specific diplomatic gesture

question:
The annual agricultural production of a product for the period 1991-92 to 1998-99 is shown in the figure given above
Which one of the following is the product in ?
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Without the actual figure, one must rely on knowledge of production trends; rice production in 1991-99 showed steady growth reaching ~90-95 MT, distinguishing it from wheat (fluctuating), oilseeds (volatile), and pulses (stagnant).
prelims.elimination_hint: This is an image-based question; the correct answer (Rice) is supported by the fact that rice production was the largest and most consistently growing crop in this period at 70-95 MT range.
factual_hooks: Rice is India's largest food crop by production volume; output grew steadily in the 1990s
Pulses showed near-stagnant production in 1991-99; oilseeds showed volatility

contrast_with: Pulses — production stagnant despite high demand, leading to imports

question: Which one of the following animals was NOT represented on the seals and terracotta art of the Harappan culture ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Tiger IS found on Harappan seals (the famous Pashupati seal has tiger); students often confuse 'NOT represented' and select an animal that is represented; the cow is absent while bull/unicorn bull is present.
prelims.elimination_hint: The question asks what was NOT represented; tiger appears on Harappan seals (Pashupati seal); elephant and rhinoceros appear on Harappan terracotta; cow is famously absent while the bull/unicorn is common.
factual_hooks: Harappan seals feature unicorn bull, elephant, rhinoceros, tiger (Pashupati seal), but NOT the domestic cow
The sacred cow absent from Harappan iconography is significant contrast with later Vedic culture

contrast_with: Vedic culture — cow is sacred; Harappan — bull/unicorn is dominant motif, cow absent

question: The approximate age of the Aravallis range is
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: All options are in the hundreds of millions, differing only by a hundred million; without precise knowledge, all seem plausible since Aravallis are known to be among the world's oldest mountain ranges.
prelims.elimination_hint: The Aravallis are considered among the oldest fold mountains in the world at approximately 650-670 million years old (Precambrian/Proterozoic era) — option D (670 million years) is the accepted figure.
factual_hooks: Aravallis are one of the world's oldest fold mountains, approximately 650-670 million years old
They are remnants of ancient Precambrian rocks, predating Himalayan formation by hundreds of millions of years

contrast_with: Himalayas — young fold mountains, ~50-70 million years old; Aravallis — ancient, ~650-670 million years

question:
Consider the following :
I. Market borrowing
II. Treasury bills
III. Special securities issued to RBI
Which of these is/are component(s) of internal debt ?
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Students may think only market borrowings constitute internal debt, overlooking Treasury Bills and Special Securities to RBI which are also debt instruments within the country.
prelims.elimination_hint: Internal debt = all borrowings within the country; market borrowings, T-bills, and special securities to RBI are all internal — option D (all three) is correct.
factual_hooks: Internal debt components: market loans, treasury bills, special securities (to RBI), provident funds
Treasury Bills are short-term government securities — definitely internal debt instruments

mains.contemporary_relevance: India's rising internal debt burden is a fiscal policy concern.

contrast_with: External debt — borrowings from foreign governments, IMF, World Bank

question:
Consider the following statements regarding Reserve Bank of India :
I. It is a banker to the Central Government.
II. It formulates and administers monetary policy.
III. It acts as an agent of the Government in respect of India’s membership of IMF.
IV. It handles the borrowing programme of Government of India.
Which of these statements are correct ?
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Students may underestimate the full scope of RBI's role, selecting only I and II; RBI's role as IMF agent and manager of government borrowing are less-known but established functions.
prelims.elimination_hint: All four functions are standard RBI roles: banker to government, monetary policy, IMF agent, and government borrowing manager — option C (all four) is correct.
factual_hooks: RBI is the banker to both central and state governments, managing their accounts
RBI acts as India's agent at IMF under the RBI Act provisions

mains.contemporary_relevance: RBI's role in monetary policy transmission and financial stability is continuously debated.

contrast_with: SEBI — regulates securities markets, not money supply
Finance Ministry — fiscal policy, not monetary policy

question:
Consider the following statements :
I. Most magmas are a combination of liquid, solid and gas.
II. Water vapour and carbon dioxide are the principal gases dissolved in a magma.
III. Basaltic magma is hotter than the silicic magma.
IV. The magma solidified between sedimentary rocks in a horizontal position is known as dike.
Which of these statements are correct ?
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Statement IV is the classic trap: the horizontal intrusion is a sill (not a dike/dyke); a dike/dyke cuts across rock layers vertically or at an angle — this terminological confusion is frequently tested.
prelims.elimination_hint: Statement IV is false (horizontal intrusion = sill, not dyke); statements I, II, III are correct — magma is liquid+solid+gas mixture, water vapour and CO₂ are principal gases, basaltic magma is hotter than silicic.
factual_hooks: Sill = magma intruded horizontally between rock layers; Dyke = magma intruded vertically cutting across layers
Basaltic (mafic) magma is hotter (~1000-1200°C) than silicic (felsic) magma (~650-800°C)

contrast_with: Sill — concordant intrusion (horizontal); Dyke — discordant intrusion (cuts across layers)

question:
Consider the following statements regarding the earthquakes :
I. The intensity of earthquake is measured on Mercalli scale.
II. The magnitude of an earthquake is a measure of energy released.
III. Earthquake magnitudes are based on direct measurements of the amplitude of seismic waves.
IV. In the Richter scale, each whole number demonstrates a hundredfold increase in the amount of energy released.
Which of these statements are correct ?
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Statement IV is false: each whole number on Richter scale = 10-fold increase in amplitude but ~31.6-fold increase in energy (not 100-fold); this error is commonly believed and is a standard trap.
prelims.elimination_hint: Statements I (Mercalli for intensity), II (magnitude = energy), III (seismic wave amplitude) are correct; statement IV is false (energy increases ~31.6 times, not 100 times, per Richter unit) — answer is A (I, II, III).
factual_hooks: Richter scale: each unit = 10x amplitude but ~31.6x energy increase (not 100x)
Mercalli scale measures intensity (effects felt), Richter measures magnitude (energy released)

mains.contemporary_relevance: Earthquake preparedness and measurement are increasingly relevant with rising seismic activity globally.

contrast_with: Richter magnitude vs Mercalli intensity — objective vs subjective measurement

question: Which Article of the Constitution provides that it shall be the endeavour of every State to provide adequate facility for instruction in the mother tongue at the primary stage of eduction ?
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Articles 349, 350, 351 all deal with language provisions; students confuse the specific article for mother tongue instruction at primary level (350-A) with the general provisions on official language.
prelims.elimination_hint: Article 350-A specifically provides for mother tongue instruction at primary stage; Article 350 deals with representations in mother tongue; Article 351 deals with promotion of Hindi.
factual_hooks: Article 350-A: State shall endeavour to provide instruction in mother tongue at primary stage
Added by 7th Constitutional Amendment 1956, under Directive Principles-related provisions

mains.contemporary_relevance: Mother tongue instruction debate is relevant in NEP 2020 which promotes regional language education.

contrast_with: Article 30 — minority right to establish educational institutions in their language

question: Who among the following Indian rulers established embassies in foreign countries on modern lines ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Haider Ali is associated with diplomatic missions and resistance to the British, making him a plausible answer; Shah Alam II had relations with foreign powers but through weakness, not initiative.
prelims.elimination_hint: Tipu Sultan established embassies in France, Turkey, and Iran — he was the first Indian ruler to seek formal diplomatic representation in foreign countries on modern lines.
factual_hooks: Tipu Sultan sent embassies to France (1787), Turkey, and Iran seeking alliance against British
He was the first Indian ruler to pursue modern-style diplomatic representation abroad

contrast_with: Haider Ali — military strategy focused; Tipu — both military and diplomatic initiatives

question: A great landslide caused by an earthquake killed hundreds of people in January 2001 near
subject: Environment

prelims.trap_factor: The question refers to the January 2001 El Salvador earthquake and landslide — students familiar only with the Gujarat earthquake (also January 2001) may confuse this; San Salvador is in El Salvador.
prelims.elimination_hint: The 2001 El Salvador earthquake caused massive landslides killing hundreds near San Salvador; this is distinct from the January 2001 Gujarat earthquake — both occurred in January 2001.
factual_hooks: January 2001 El Salvador earthquake caused Las Colinas landslide near San Salvador killing ~585 people
This was separate from the January 26, 2001 Gujarat earthquake (Bhuj) that devastated India

contrast_with: Gujarat (Bhuj) earthquake — January 26, 2001 India; El Salvador earthquake — January 13, 2001

question: Which of the following pairs is NOT correctly matched ?
option_a: India’s first Technicolour film ... Jhansi Ki Rani
option_b: India’s first 3-D film ... My Dear Kuttichat
option_d: India’s first actress to win the Bharat Ratna ... Meena Kumari
subject: Governance

prelims.trap_factor: The question asks for the NOT correctly matched pair; students may not know that Meena Kumari never received the Bharat Ratna (she died in 1972 and was never awarded it); Lata Mangeshkar received it in 2001.
prelims.elimination_hint: The first actress to win Bharat Ratna was actually never an actress in classical sense — Bharat Ratna went to M.S. Subbulakshmi (1998), not a film actress; Meena Kumari never received it — option D is incorrectly matched.
factual_hooks: No film actress named 'Meena Kumari' won Bharat Ratna; M.S. Subbulakshmi was the first woman musician to get it
India's first insured film was 'Taal' (1999) — this fact in option C is correct

contrast_with: Bharat Ratna vs Dadasaheb Phalke — different categories of recognition

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the codes given below the Lists
(Bhakti Saint) | (Profession)
I. Namdev | (A) Barber
II. Kabir | (B) Weaver
III. Ravidas | (C) Tailor
IV. Sena | (D) Cobbler
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: The professions of Bhakti saints are frequently confused; Namdev (tailor/calico printer) and Kabir (weaver) are commonly mixed up, and Ravidas (cobbler) vs Sena (barber) is another tricky pair.
prelims.elimination_hint: Key anchors: Kabir = weaver (B), Ravidas = cobbler (D) — these two are most commonly tested; Namdev = tailor/calico printer (C), Sena = barber (A); this matches option C (I-C, II-B, III-D, IV-A).
factual_hooks: Kabir was a weaver, Ravidas/Raidas was a cobbler (chamar), Namdev was a tailor, Sena was a barber
Bhakti saints came from lower castes, challenging caste hierarchy through devotion

contrast_with: Saguna Bhakti saints (Tulsidas, Surdas) — upper caste; Nirguna (Kabir, Ravidas) — lower caste

question: The most appropriate measure of a country’s economic growth is its
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: GDP (gross domestic product) seems the most natural answer for 'economic growth'; NNP at market prices is another trap; the correct measure is per capita real income which accounts for population growth and inflation.
prelims.elimination_hint: GDP doesn't account for population growth; NNP doesn't account for inflation; per capita real income (real GDP/population) is the most appropriate measure of individual welfare and growth — option D.
factual_hooks: Per capita real income = Real GDP ÷ Population — accounts for both inflation and population growth
A country can have rising GDP but falling per capita income if population grows faster

mains.contemporary_relevance: Debate on GDP vs alternate welfare measures like HDI, GNH continues in development economics.

contrast_with: GDP — doesn't account for population; NNP — doesn't adjust for prices; per capita real income — best measure

question: The Supreme Court of India tenders advice to the President on a matter of law or fact
option_b: only if he seeks such advice
option_c: only if the matter relates to the Fundamental Rights of citizens
option_d: only if the issue poses a threat to the unity and integrity of the country
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Students may think the Supreme Court can give advisory opinions on its own initiative (like constitutional bodies in some countries); option C (only on Fundamental Rights) is a common distractor.
prelims.elimination_hint: Article 143 of the Constitution: President may seek Supreme Court's advisory opinion (reference); the SC may or may not give the opinion but it can only do so when the President seeks it — option B is correct.
factual_hooks: Article 143: President can seek advisory opinion from Supreme Court; SC's opinion is not binding
SC does not volunteer opinions on its own; it requires a Presidential reference

mains.contemporary_relevance: Presidential references to SC have been made on sensitive constitutional questions.

contrast_with: Writ jurisdiction — SC can act suo motu in public interest; advisory — only on President's reference

question: Who amongst the following was the first to state that the Earth was spherical?
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Copernicus (heliocentric model) and Ptolemy (geocentric model) are more famous in astronomy; students may confuse 'spherical Earth' with heliocentric theory, picking Copernicus incorrectly.
prelims.elimination_hint: Aristotle (~330 BCE) first argued the Earth is spherical based on the circular shadow during lunar eclipses and stellar observations — predating Copernicus by ~1800 years.
factual_hooks: Aristotle provided empirical evidence for Earth's spherical shape: circular shadow in lunar eclipses, different star positions
Eratosthenes later calculated Earth's circumference accurately (~240 BCE)

contrast_with: Copernicus — heliocentric model (Sun at centre), NOT spherical Earth argument

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the codes given below the Lists
List I (Term) | List II (Explanation)
I. Fiscal deficit | (A) Excess of Total Expenditure over Total Receipts
II. Budget deficit | (B) Excess of Revenue Expenditure over Revenue Receipts
III. Revenue deficit | (C) Excess of Total Expenditure over Total Receipts less borrowings
IV. Primary deficit | (D) Excess of Total Expenditure over Total Receipts less borrowings and Interest Payments
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: The definitions of fiscal deficit, budget deficit, and revenue deficit are frequently confused; primary deficit = fiscal deficit minus interest payments is the most-missed distinction.
prelims.elimination_hint: Key: Fiscal Deficit = Total Expenditure − Total Receipts excluding borrowings (not 'less borrowings and interest'); Primary Deficit = Fiscal Deficit − Interest Payments; Budget Deficit = Total Expenditure − Total Receipts; Revenue Deficit = Revenue Expenditure − Revenue Receipts. Match: I-C (fiscal), II-A (budget), III-B (revenue), IV-D (primary) — answer is A.
factual_hooks: Primary Deficit = Fiscal Deficit − Interest Payments — shows borrowing beyond debt servicing needs
Fiscal Deficit = Total Expenditure − Revenue Receipts − Capital Receipts (excl. borrowings)

mains.contemporary_relevance: India's fiscal deficit management under FRBM is a perennial economic policy issue.

contrast_with: Revenue deficit vs fiscal deficit — revenue covers only current income/expenditure; fiscal is comprehensive

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the codes given below the Lists
(Person) | (Achievement)
I. Deep Sen Gupta | (A) India’s youngest International Master in the Sangli International Chess Tournament, 2000
II. P. Hari krishna | (B) The first Indian ever to win a gold in discuss in the World Athletic Championship in Santiago, 2000
III. Seema Antil | (C) Won the title in the Asian Junior Chess Tournament in Mumbai, 2000
IV. Tejas Bakre | (D) Won the Under-12 title in the World Youth Chess Festival in Oropesa, 2000
subject: Governance

prelims.trap_factor: This is a highly dated current affairs question (2001 sports achievements) with very specific match-list format; without direct knowledge of each person's specific achievement, all options look plausible.
prelims.elimination_hint: Seema Antil won gold in discus at World Athletics Championship 2000 (B); Hari Krishna was Asian Junior Chess champion; Tejas Bakre won Under-12 World Youth Chess; Deep Sen Gupta won Sangli International — answer C (I-D, II-A, III-B, IV-C).
factual_hooks: Seema Antil was India's first World Athletics Championship gold medallist in field events (discus, 2000)
P. Hari Krishna became youngest Indian International Chess Master at Sangli 2000

question: Which one of the countries labelled as 1, 2, 3 and 4 on the given map was grantee membership of G -15 in its VII Summit at Kuala Lumpur ?
subject: International Relations

prelims.trap_factor: Without the actual map, identification of country 4 gaining G-15 membership at Kuala Lumpur VII Summit is impossible to verify; this is a highly specific historical current affairs question.
prelims.elimination_hint: G-15 (Group of Fifteen) is a grouping of developing nations; the VII Summit at Kuala Lumpur (1999) saw new membership being granted; without the map, rely on process of elimination of known G-15 members.
factual_hooks: G-15 is a grouping of developing countries formed in 1989 at the Non-Aligned Movement summit
G-15 membership expanded at various summits to include more developing nations

contrast_with: G-15 (developing nations) vs G-7/G-8 (developed nations)

question: Under the Permanent Settlement, 1793, the zamindars were required to issue pattas to the farmers which were not issued by many of the zamindars. The reason was
option_a: the zamindars were trusted by the farmers
option_b: there was no official check upon the zamindars
option_c: it was the responsibility of the British Government
option_d: the farmers were not interested in getting pattas
subject: History
aspect: WHY – Economic Impact of British Rule

prelims.trap_factor: Options A and D suggest farmer cooperation or disinterest which may seem logical; the real issue was absence of government enforcement mechanism — British created zamindari obligation but no monitoring system.
prelims.elimination_hint: Permanent Settlement had no official check mechanism — zamindars had to pay fixed revenue to British but had total freedom over tenants; option B correctly identifies lack of official monitoring as the reason pattas were not issued.
factual_hooks: Permanent Settlement 1793: zamindars fixed revenue payers to British, leaving tenant relations unregulated
Absence of official check allowed zamindars to exploit tenants without issuing formal pattas

contrast_with: Ryotwari — direct settlement with cultivators, pattas given; Zamindari — intermediary landlord

question:
Consider the following statements :
I. Arya Samaj was founded in 1835.
II. Lala Lajpat Rai opposed the appeal of Arya Samaj to the authority of Vedas in support of its social reform programmes.
III. Under Keshab Chandra Sen, the Brahmo Samaj campaigned for women’s education.
IV. Vinoba Bhave founded the Sarvodaya Samaj to work among rufugees.
Which of these statements are correct ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Arya Samaj was founded in 1875 (not 1835 — statement I is false); students may believe statement II about Lajpat Rai opposing Vedic authority (he was actually an Arya Samaj supporter); statement III about Brahmo Samaj under Keshab Sen is correct.
prelims.elimination_hint: Eliminate on statement I (Arya Samaj founded 1875 not 1835); statement II is also false (Lajpat Rai was a proponent of Arya Samaj); only III (Brahmo Samaj women's education) and IV (Vinoba Bhave's Sarvodaya) are correct — answer D.
factual_hooks: Arya Samaj founded by Dayananda Saraswati in 1875, not 1835
Vinoba Bhave founded Sarvodaya Samaj; Brahmo Samaj under Keshab Chandra Sen promoted women's education

contrast_with: Brahmo Samaj (Bengal focus, modern reform) vs Arya Samaj (return to Vedic tradition)

question: The Mongols under Gengis Khan invaded India during the reign of
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Balban and Muhammad bin Tughlaq are associated with subsequent Mongol invasions; the first major Mongol invasion under Genghis Khan is specifically during Iltutmish's reign.
prelims.elimination_hint: Genghis Khan's Mongols invaded the Punjab region in 1221 during Iltutmish's reign — Iltutmish wisely refused to give asylum to Jalal ud-Din Mangburni to avoid direct Mongol confrontation.
factual_hooks: Mongols under Genghis Khan invaded India in 1221 during Iltutmish's reign (Slave Dynasty)
Iltutmish refused shelter to Khwarezm Shah's son Jalal ud-Din to avoid Mongol wrath

contrast_with: Alauddin Khilji — repulsed multiple Mongol invasions (1297-1305); Iltutmish — faced first invasion

question: The high density of population in Nile Valley and Island of Java is primarily due to
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Industrialisation and urbanisation are common reasons for population concentration in Europe/Americas; Nile Valley and Java are specifically agricultural zones where intensive farming explains density.
prelims.elimination_hint: Nile Valley and Java: fertile alluvial soils support intensive wet rice and irrigated agriculture — option A (intensive agriculture) is the primary factor; industrialisation is not the historic reason for their density.
factual_hooks: Nile Valley: world's most fertile land due to annual Nile floods depositing silt, enabling intensive agriculture
Java (Indonesia): volcanic soils and wet rice cultivation support one of world's highest rural population densities

contrast_with: Ruhr Valley density — due to industrialisation; Nile/Java — due to agriculture

question: A parachutist jumps from a height of 5000 metres. The relationship between his falling speed ‘v’ and the distance fallen through ‘d’ is best represented as
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Without the actual graph images, one must reason conceptually; a parachutist initially accelerates (v increases with d), then terminal velocity reached (v becomes constant) — the correct graph shows initial increase then plateau.
prelims.elimination_hint: A parachutist: initially falls freely (v increases), then air resistance + parachute = terminal velocity (v constant) — the v vs d graph should show increasing curve then horizontal plateau; option D likely shows this shape.
factual_hooks: Terminal velocity occurs when air resistance equals gravitational force; parachute increases air resistance dramatically
v vs d graph for parachutist: initial increase (free fall), then plateau (terminal velocity)

contrast_with: Free fall in vacuum — v increases continuously with d (no terminal velocity)

question: Which among the following ports was called Babul Makka (Gate of Makka) during the Mughal Period ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Calicut and Cambay were also important trading ports; Broach is on the Narmada and was significant in early medieval trade — all four are plausible Gujarat/Kerala ports.
prelims.elimination_hint: Surat was the most important Mughal-era port and the main gateway for Hajj pilgrims, hence called 'Babul Makka' (Gate of Mecca); it was also the seat of the English East India Company's first factory.
factual_hooks: Surat was called 'Babul Makka' as the main port of embarkation for Mecca-bound pilgrims
Surat was the premier port of the Mughal Empire and location of first English factory (1608)
paired_with: English East India Company in Surat

related_concepts: English East India Company in Surat
contrast_with: Calicut — major port for Arab trade in pre-Mughal era; Surat — dominant in Mughal period

question: Volcanic eruptions do not occur in the
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Black Sea and Caspian Sea are landlocked but students may think any sea can have volcanic activity; Caribbean Sea has volcanic islands (Lesser Antilles) making it a plausible distractor.
prelims.elimination_hint: Baltic Sea is a shallow, tectonically stable sea on the ancient Precambrian shield — no volcanic activity; Caribbean has active volcanoes (Lesser Antilles arc); Black and Caspian are also seismically quiet but not completely inert.
factual_hooks: Baltic Sea lies on the ancient Precambrian Fennoscandian Shield — geologically stable, no volcanism
Caribbean Sea borders subduction zones creating volcanic island arcs (Soufrière, Montserrat)

contrast_with: Caribbean — active subduction, volcanic islands; Baltic — stable shield, no volcanism

question: The earlier name of WTO was
subject: International Relations

prelims.trap_factor: UNCTAD and UNIDO are UN bodies dealing with trade and development — students may confuse them with WTO's predecessor; OECD is a developed-country grouping, not a trade organisation.
prelims.elimination_hint: GATT (General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade, 1947) was the predecessor to WTO (1995) — this is a fundamental fact of international trade history.
factual_hooks: WTO replaced GATT on January 1, 1995 — GATT was a treaty, WTO is a formal organisation
GATT (1947) focused on tariff reduction; WTO broadened to services, IP, and investment

mains.contemporary_relevance: WTO's dispute settlement and reform remain key in global trade governance.

contrast_with: UNCTAD — UN body on trade and development; GATT/WTO — actual trade rules organisation

question: Two wires have their lengths, diameters and resistivities, all in the ratio of 1 : 2. If the resistance of the thinner wire is 10 ohms, the resistance of the thicker wire is
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Students may intuitively assume the thicker wire (lower resistance per unit length) gives less resistance; but resistivity also doubles in this problem, which offsets the dimensional effects.
prelims.elimination_hint: Resistance R = ρL/A; all three (L, d, ρ) are in ratio 1:2; A ∝ d², so A ratio is 1:4; R₂/R₁ = (ρ₂L₂/A₂)/(ρ₁L₁/A₁) = (2×2)/(4) × (1/1) = 1; so R₂ = R₁ = 10 ohms — option A.
factual_hooks: R = ρL/A; when L, d (diameter), and ρ all double: R stays same because A increases 4× (d²) while ρL increases 4×
This counterintuitive result tests understanding of all three parameters of resistance simultaneously

contrast_with: If only length doubled, R would double; if only diameter doubled, R would halve

question: Which one among the following was awarded the CSIR Diamond Jubliee Technology Award 2004 in September 2005 by the Prime Minister of India?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Infosys and TCS are India's largest IT companies, making them obvious but wrong choices; Biocon is biotech, not technology — the correct answer Midas Communications Technologies is obscure.
prelims.elimination_hint: This is a highly specific 2005 current affairs question about a relatively unknown company; process of elimination: TCS and Infosys are software, Biocon is biotech — Midas Communications (telecom technology) won CSIR's tech award.
factual_hooks: CSIR Diamond Jubilee Technology Award 2004 went to Midas Communications Technologies for telecom innovations
CSIR awards recognise technology commercialisation and innovation in India

question:
Assertion (A)
: The boiling point of water decreases as the altitude increases.
Reason (R)
: The atmospheric pressure increases with altitude.
option_a: Both A and R are individually true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_b: Both A and R are individually true but R is NOT a correct explanation of A
option_c: A is true but R is false
option_d: A is false but R is true
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: The Assertion is correct (boiling point decreases with altitude) but the Reason is false — atmospheric pressure DECREASES with altitude (not increases), so A is true and R is false — option C.
prelims.elimination_hint: A is true: lower pressure at altitude means less energy needed to vaporise water. R is false: atmospheric pressure DECREASES with altitude (not increases) — option C (A true, R false) is correct.
factual_hooks: Atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude; lower pressure reduces water's boiling point
At Mount Everest (~8850m), water boils at ~70°C instead of 100°C

contrast_with: Pressure cooker — increases pressure, raises boiling point above 100°C

question: Which organelle in the cell, other than nucleus, contains DNA ?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Centriole and Golgi apparatus are other organelles students might consider; lysosome contains digestive enzymes but no DNA — mitochondria is the definitive answer with its own circular DNA.
prelims.elimination_hint: Mitochondria has its own DNA (mtDNA), RNA, and ribosomes — it is semi-autonomous; this is a fundamental cell biology fact frequently tested; only nucleus and mitochondria (+ chloroplasts in plants) contain DNA.
factual_hooks: Mitochondria contains circular DNA (mtDNA) inherited maternally
Endosymbiont theory explains why mitochondria and chloroplasts have their own DNA

mains.contemporary_relevance: Mitochondrial DNA is used in forensics, ancestry tracing, and studying human migration patterns.

contrast_with: Ribosomes have RNA not DNA; Lysosomes have enzymes not DNA; Golgi has no genetic material

question:
Consider the following statements : In a nuclear reactor, self-sustained chain reaction is possible, because
I.
more neutrons are released in each of the fission reactions.
II.
the neutrons immediately take part in the fission process.
III.
the fast neutrons are slowed down by Graphite.
IV.
every neutron realeased in the fission reaction initiates further fission.
Which of these statements are correct ?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Statement II is false — neutrons are NOT immediately used in fission; slow (thermal) neutrons are needed, and graphite moderator slows fast neutrons (statement III is correct); statement IV is false as not every neutron initiates fission.
prelims.elimination_hint: Correct statements: I (more neutrons released per fission — chain reaction possible) and III (graphite slows fast neutrons) — answer B (I and III); II is false (fast neutrons don't immediately cause fission), IV is false (not every neutron initiates fission).
factual_hooks: Each uranium fission releases 2-3 neutrons; if controlled to sustain chain reaction, it's self-sustaining
Graphite (or heavy water) moderates (slows) fast neutrons to thermal neutrons for efficient fission

mains.contemporary_relevance: Nuclear energy is part of India's low-carbon energy transition strategy.

contrast_with: Moderator slows neutrons vs control rods absorb neutrons — different functions in reactor

question:
Consider the following statements : A simple pendulum is set into oscillation. Then
I.
the acceleration is zero when the bob passes through the mean position.
II.
in each cycle the bob attains a given velocity twice.
III.
both acceleration and velocity of the bob are zero when it reaches its extreme position during its oscillation.
IV.
the amplitude of oscillation of the simple pendulum decreases with time.
Which of these statements are correct ?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Statement III is false: at extreme position, velocity is zero but acceleration is maximum (not zero) — this is the classic pendulum misconception. Statement IV (amplitude decreases with time) is true only for damped oscillation.
prelims.elimination_hint: Correct: I (acceleration zero at mean position) and II (bob attains a given velocity twice per cycle — once going each way); III is false (acceleration maximum at extreme); IV is true for real pendulum but ambiguous in ideal context — answer A (I and II).
factual_hooks: Simple pendulum at mean position: max velocity, zero acceleration; at extreme: zero velocity, max acceleration
A given speed is achieved twice per cycle (once in each direction)

contrast_with: Mean position — max KE, min PE; Extreme position — min KE, max PE, max acceleration

question: Cloudy nights are warmer compared to clear cloudless nights, because clouds
option_a: prevent cold waves from the sky from descending on Earth
option_b: reflect back heat given off by Earth
option_c: produce heat and radiate it towards Earth
option_d: absorb heat from the atmosphere and send it towards Earth
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Option A ('cold waves from sky') uses intuitive but scientifically incorrect language; option D (absorb and send heat) reverses the direction — clouds absorb outgoing longwave radiation and re-radiate downward.
prelims.elimination_hint: Clouds act as a blanket — they reflect/absorb Earth's outgoing longwave (infrared) radiation and radiate some back downward; option B (reflect back heat given off by Earth) correctly describes the greenhouse/insulation effect of clouds.
factual_hooks: Clouds absorb and re-emit Earth's outgoing longwave radiation, reducing nighttime heat loss
Clear nights are cooler because heat escapes directly to space without cloud cover

contrast_with: Daytime: clouds reflect incoming shortwave, cooling; Nighttime: clouds trap outgoing longwave, warming

question: Which of the following weather conditions is indicated by a sudden fall in barometer reading ?
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: A steady rise in barometer indicates fair weather; a slow fall indicates rain — students confuse 'sudden fall' with gradual changes; the suddenness is the key distinguishing factor indicating storm.
prelims.elimination_hint: Sudden fall in barometric pressure = rapid drop in air pressure = approaching storm/cyclone; this is a standard meteorological fact — option A (stormy weather).
factual_hooks: Sudden drop in barometer = low pressure approaching = storms or cyclones
Steady high barometer = clear weather; steady low = cloudy/rain; rapidly falling = storms

contrast_with: Rising barometer — indicates improving weather/high pressure; falling barometer — deteriorating/low pressure

question:
Assertion (A)
: A Chemical reaction becomes faster at higher temperatures.
Reason (R)
: At higher temperatures, molecular motion becomes more rapid.
option_a: Both A and R are individually true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_b: Both A and R are individually true but R is NOT a correct explanation of A
option_c: A is true but R is false
option_d: A is fasle but R is true
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: This assertion-reason format tests whether students know WHY reactions are faster — molecular motion (kinetic energy) is the correct reason; some may choose B thinking there's a different explanation.
prelims.elimination_hint: Both A and R are true and R correctly explains A: higher temperature = greater molecular kinetic energy = more frequent and energetic collisions = faster reaction rate (Arrhenius equation basis) — option A.
factual_hooks: Arrhenius equation: rate increases exponentially with temperature due to increased molecular motion
Rule of thumb: reaction rate roughly doubles for every 10°C rise in temperature

contrast_with: Catalyst lowers activation energy without changing temperature; temperature directly increases molecular energy

question:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Periodic Table of chemical elements :
I.
lonisation potential gradually decreases along a period.
II. In a group of elements, electron affinity decreases as the atomic weight increases.
III. In a given period, electronegativity decreases as the atomic number increases.
Which of these statement(s) is/are correct ?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Statement I is false (ionisation potential generally INCREASES across a period, not decreases); statement III is also false (electronegativity generally INCREASES across a period); only statement II about electron affinity decreasing down a group is correct.
prelims.elimination_hint: Across a period: IP increases, electronegativity increases (I and III are false); down a group: electron affinity generally decreases as atomic size increases (II is correct) — answer B (II only).
factual_hooks: Across a period: ionisation potential and electronegativity increase (left to right)
Down a group: electron affinity generally decreases as atomic radius increases

contrast_with: IP increases across period but decreases down group; electron affinity irregular but generally decreases down group

question:
Assertion (A)
: Scientists can cut apart and paste together DNA molecules at will, regardless of the source of the molecules.
Reason (R)
: DNA fragments can be manipulated using restriction endonucleases and DNA ligases.
option_a: Both A and R are individually true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_b: Both A and R are individually true but R is NOT a correct explanation of A
option_c: A is true but R is false
option_d: A is fasle but R is true
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Students may assume DNA manipulation is not universal or think some DNA is incompatible — the Assertion (universal manipulation) and Reason (restriction enzymes + ligases) are both true and causally linked.
prelims.elimination_hint: Both A and R are true: DNA has universal structure (same sugar-phosphate backbone, same base pairing) enabling restriction enzymes to cut at specific sequences regardless of source, and ligases to join them — option A.
factual_hooks: Restriction endonucleases cut DNA at specific palindromic sequences regardless of organism source
DNA ligase joins DNA fragments — together they enable recombinant DNA technology

mains.contemporary_relevance: Recombinant DNA technology underlies vaccines, gene therapy, and GM crops.

contrast_with: Restriction enzymes are site-specific, not species-specific — hence universal applicability

question:
A man whose blood group is not known meets with a serious accident and needs blood transfusion immediately.
Which one of the blood groups mentioned below and readily available in the hospital will be safe for transfusion ?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: O, Rh+ might seem safer as more common, making O, Rh- seem overly restrictive; AB Rh- is the 'universal recipient' which may confuse students into selecting it as a transfusion option.
prelims.elimination_hint: O negative (O, Rh-) is the universal donor: O blood group has no antigens (compatible with A, B, AB, O); Rh- has no Rh antigen (compatible with both Rh+ and Rh- recipients) — option A.
factual_hooks: O, Rh- is universal donor (no A, B, or Rh antigens to trigger immune response)
AB, Rh+ is universal recipient (has all antigens, can accept all blood types)

mains.contemporary_relevance: O negative blood shortage is a perpetual challenge in emergency medicine and blood banks.

contrast_with: Universal donor: O Rh-; Universal recipient: AB Rh+

question: “Metastasis” is the process by which
option_a: cells divide rapidly under the influence of drugs
option_b: cancer cells spread through the blood or laymphatic system to other sites or organs
option_c: the chromosomes in cell nuclei are attached to the spindle before moving to the anaphase poles
option_d: cancer cells are successfully inhibited to divide any further
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Option D ('inhibiting cancer cells') is the opposite of metastasis and may attract students who think the question is about treatment; option C (chromosomal movement) describes mitosis, not metastasis.
prelims.elimination_hint: Metastasis = spread of cancer cells from primary tumour to distant organs via blood or lymph — option B is the textbook definition; it's a standard medical/biology term frequently tested.
factual_hooks: Metastasis: malignant cells break from primary tumour, travel via blood/lymph, colonise new organs
Metastatic cancer is the leading cause of cancer deaths — far more dangerous than primary tumour

mains.contemporary_relevance: Understanding metastasis is central to cancer treatment research.

contrast_with: Benign tumour — doesn't metastasize; malignant/cancer — can metastasize

question: A person travels from X to Y a speed of 40 kmph and returns by increasing his speed by 50%. What is his average speed for both the trips ?
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Students may compute simple average (40+60)/2 = 50 kmph, forgetting that average speed for same distance must use harmonic mean — the simple average overestimates.
prelims.elimination_hint: Return speed = 40 × 1.5 = 60 kmph; average speed = 2 × (40 × 60)/(40 + 60) = 4800/100 = 48 kmph — use harmonic mean formula for equal distances, not arithmetic mean.
factual_hooks: Average speed for equal distances = 2v₁v₂/(v₁+v₂) — harmonic mean, not arithmetic mean
If speeds for equal distances are 40 and 60, average = 48, not 50

contrast_with: Arithmetic mean of speeds — valid only when time is equal, not distance

question:
The given map shows four towns of Central Asian region marked as 1, 2, 3, and 4. Identify these from the following list and select the correct answer using the codes given below :
Towns:
A. Bishkek
B. Ashkhabad
C. Tashkent
D. Dushanbe
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Without the actual map, identification of Central Asian capitals (Bishkek, Ashkhabad/Ashgabat, Tashkent, Dushanbe) by position requires precise geographic knowledge of relative locations.
prelims.elimination_hint: Key positions: Tashkent (Uzbekistan) — most centrally located; Ashkhabad (Turkmenistan) — southernmost near Iran border; Bishkek (Kyrgyzstan) — northeast; Dushanbe (Tajikistan) — southeast; answer A matches A-3, B-1, C-3... per option given.
factual_hooks: Central Asian capitals: Bishkek (Kyrgyzstan), Ashgabat (Turkmenistan), Tashkent (Uzbekistan), Dushanbe (Tajikistan)
Tashkent is the largest city in Central Asia and the most centrally positioned

mains.contemporary_relevance: Central Asia is strategically important for India's connectivity (INSTC) and SCO membership.

contrast_with: Almaty vs Astana — Kazakhstan has two major cities; Tashkent is Uzbekistan's single dominant capital

question: Which one of the following can be used to confirm whether drinking water contains a gamma emitting isotope or not?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Spectrophotometer detects light absorbance (not radiation); lead plate only blocks radiation but can't detect or confirm its presence; microscope is for visible observation — scintillation counter is the radiation-specific detector.
prelims.elimination_hint: Scintillation counter detects gamma radiation by converting it to light flashes; it is the standard instrument for detecting and quantifying gamma-emitting isotopes in liquids — option C.
factual_hooks: Scintillation counter: gamma rays excite scintillating material (NaI crystal) → light pulses → converted to electrical signals
Used in nuclear medicine, environmental monitoring, and water quality testing for radioactive isotopes

mains.contemporary_relevance: Water safety testing for radioactive contamination is increasingly important near nuclear facilities.

contrast_with: GM counter — detects beta/gamma; Scintillation counter — more sensitive, especially for gamma

question:
Consider the following statements: The objectives of the National Renewal Funds set up in February 1992 were
1. to give training and counselling for workers affected by retrenchment or VRS.
2. redeployment of workers
Which of these statements is/are correct?
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Students may think only one of training/counselling OR redeployment was the objective, selecting option C or D; both were explicitly stated objectives of NRF.
prelims.elimination_hint: National Renewal Fund (1992) had dual objectives: worker welfare through training/counselling AND redeployment — both statements are correct, option B.
factual_hooks: National Renewal Fund (Feb 1992) was set up to mitigate job losses from liberalisation/VRS/retrenchment
NRF provided safety net for workers affected by PSU restructuring under LPG reforms

themes: Economic reforms and their social impact

contrast_with: NREGS/MGNREGA — rural employment guarantee; NRF — industrial worker safety net

question:
India’s population growth during the 29th century can be classified into four distinct phases. Match List I (Period) with List II (Phase) and select the correct answer using the using the codes given below the lists
(Period) | (Phase)
A. 1901-21 | 1. Steady growth
B. 1921-51 | 2. Rapid high growth
C. 1951-81 | 3. Stagnant growth
D. 1981-2001 | 4. High growth with definite signs of slowdown
subject: Society

prelims.trap_factor: The phases are easy to mix up; particularly 1901-21 (stagnant, NOT steady) and 1921-51 (steady, NOT stagnant) are frequently confused; the 'Great Divide' year 1921 is the key anchor.
prelims.elimination_hint: 1921 is the 'Great Divide' year of India's population — before 1921: stagnant (3=stagnant); 1921-51: steady growth (1=steady); 1951-81: rapid high growth (2=rapid); 1981-2001: high with slowdown signs (4) — answer C (A-3, B-1, C-2, D-4).
factual_hooks: 1921 is the 'Year of Great Divide' — India's population growth reversed from stagnant to steady
1951-81: Green Revolution, better healthcare = 'population explosion' phase in India
paired_with: Birth rate vs death rate trends

mains.contemporary_relevance: India surpassed China as world's most populous country (2023); demographic dividend window closing.

related_concepts: Birth rate vs death rate trends
contrast_with: Stagnant growth (pre-1921) due to famines, epidemics vs steady growth (post-1921) due to reduced mortality

question:
Consider the following statements: The objectives of the National Renewal Funds set up in February 1992 were
1. to give training and counselling for workers affected by retrenchment or VRS.
2. redeployment of workers
Which of these statements is/are correct?
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Same as idx 62 — duplicate question; both NRF objectives (training/counselling and redeployment) are correct.
prelims.elimination_hint: Identical to idx 62: National Renewal Fund had both objectives — training/counselling and redeployment — answer B (both 1 and 2).
factual_hooks: National Renewal Fund (1992) dual mandate: worker welfare (training) + redeployment support
Created as social cushion for LPG-era industrial restructuring

contrast_with: MGNREGA — rural employment; NRF — industrial/organised sector worker safety

question: The motive behind Shahjahan’s Balkh campaign was to
option_a: secure a friendly ruler in Balkh and Badakshan which bordered Kabul
option_b: conquer Samarqand and Farghana, the Mughal homelands
option_c: fix the Mughal frontier on the ‘scientific line’, the Amu Daria
option_d: expand the Mughal Empire beyond the sub-continent
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Option B (reconquer Mughal homelands Samarqand and Farghana) seems historically plausible given Babur's nostalgia for Central Asia; option C (scientific frontier on Amu Darya) sounds strategic — both are attractive distractors.
prelims.elimination_hint: Shahjahan's Balkh campaign (1646-47) was primarily defensive — to install a friendly buffer ruler in Balkh (near Kabul's northern border) against Uzbek/Persian threats; option A is the strategic rationale.
factual_hooks: Shahjahan's Balkh campaign aimed to install a friendly Mughal ally as buffer against Uzbek Khanate
The campaign was costly and ultimately unsuccessful; Murad Bakhsh and then Aurangzeb led forces

contrast_with: Babur's longing for Samarqand — emotional/dynastic; Shahjahan's Balkh — strategic buffer zone motive

question: The real intention of the British to include the princely states in the Federal Union proposed by the India Act of 1935 was to
option_a: exercise more and direct political and administrative control over the princely states
option_b: involve the princes actively in the administration of the colony
option_c: finally effect the complete political and administrative take-over of all the princely states by the British
option_d: use the princes to counter-balance the anti-imperialist doctrines of the nationalist leaders
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Options A and C suggest direct British control motives, which seem logical from an imperial perspective; the subtler political strategy of using princes against nationalists (option D) requires deeper historical analysis.
prelims.elimination_hint: The real British motive was using conservative princes to dilute the power of elected (nationalist) legislators in the proposed Federal Assembly — princes in Federal Union = counterweight to INC; option D is the correct analytical answer.
factual_hooks: Government of India Act 1935 proposed Federal Union where princes' nominated members would outnumber elected representatives
British calculated that conservative princes would vote against nationalist positions, stalling INC agenda

prerequisite_concepts: Indian National Congress vs British strategy
contrast_with: Actual federal union never implemented as princes refused to join; used as political tool only

question: Sun’s halo is produced by the refraction of light in
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Cirro-Cumulus clouds may seem like a reasonable answer since they produce halo-like optical effects; Stratus clouds are associated with diffuse overcast, not halos — the key is Cirrus ice crystals.
prelims.elimination_hint: Sun's halo (22° halo) is produced by refraction of light through hexagonal ice crystals in Cirrus clouds (high-altitude thin clouds) — option C; Cirro-Cumulus produces 'corona' not halo.
factual_hooks: 22° halo around Sun/Moon = hexagonal ice crystals in high-altitude Cirrus clouds refracting light
Halo vs corona: Halo (large ring, 22°) = ice crystals in Cirrus; Corona (small ring) = water droplets in thin clouds

contrast_with: Cirro-Cumulus — mackerel sky, produces corona not halo; Cirrus — thin wispy, produces 22° halo

question:
Match List I (Acts of Colonial Government of India) with List II (Provisions) and select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists
(Acts of colonial government of India) | (Provisions)
A. Charter Act, 1813 | 1. Set up a Board of Control in Britain to fully regulate the east India Company’s affairs in India
B. Regulating Act | 2. Company’s trade monopoly in India was ended
C. Act of 1858 | 3. The power to govern was transferred from the East India Company to the British Crown
D. Pitt’s India Act | 4. The Company’s directors were asked to present to the British government all correspondence and documents pertaining to the administration of the company
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: The Regulating Act (B) and its provision is often confused with Pitt's India Act; the sequence of Charter Act 1813 (trade monopoly ended) vs Charter Act 1833 (civil service) is another common confusion point.
prelims.elimination_hint: Key anchors: Charter Act 1813 = ended Company's trade monopoly in India (A-2); Act of 1858 = power transferred to Crown (C-3); Pitt's India Act = Board of Control in Britain (D-1); Regulating Act = Company directors present documents (B-4) — answer A (A-2, B-4, C-3, D-1).
factual_hooks: Charter Act 1813: ended EIC's India trade monopoly; Charter Act 1833: ended China trade monopoly
Pitt's India Act 1784: dual control — Board of Control (political) + Court of Directors (commerce)

contrast_with: Regulating Act 1773 — first step of parliamentary control; Pitt's India Act 1784 — strengthened it with Board of Control

question:
Match List I (Mangrove ) with List II (State) and select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists
Mangrove) | (State)
A. Achra Ratnagiri | 1. Karnataka
B. Coondapur | 2. Kerala
C. Pichavaram | 3. Andhra Pradesh
D. Vembanad | 4. Maharashtra
| 5. Tamil Nadu
subject: Environment

prelims.trap_factor: Pichavaram (Tamil Nadu) and Coondapur (Karnataka) are less well-known mangroves; Vembanad (Kerala) is famous as a lagoon/backwater and students may not associate it with mangroves.
prelims.elimination_hint: Key anchors: Achra Ratnagiri (A) = Maharashtra (4); Coondapur (B) = Karnataka (1); Pichavaram (C) = Tamil Nadu (5); Vembanad (D) = Kerala (2) — answer D (A-4, B-1, C-5, D-2).
factual_hooks: Pichavaram (Tamil Nadu) is one of India's largest mangrove forests, near Chidambaram
Vembanad lake (Kerala) has mangrove fringes; Coondapur (Karnataka) has significant mangrove cover

mains.contemporary_relevance: Mangrove conservation is critical for coastal protection against cyclones and sea-level rise.

contrast_with: Sundarbans (West Bengal) — largest mangrove forest in India; Pichavaram — largest in Tamil Nadu

question:
With reference to the human body consider the following statements:
1. The production of somatotropin goes up when a person exercises
2. Men’s testes produce progesterone
3. Women’s adrenal glands secrete testosterone
4. Stress causes the adrenals to release very less amount of cortisol than usual
Which of these statements are correct?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Statement 4 is false (stress INCREASES cortisol, not decreases); Statement 2 is false (testes primarily produce testosterone not progesterone); statement 3 is true (adrenal glands in women produce small amounts of testosterone).
prelims.elimination_hint: Correct statements: 1 (somatotropin/growth hormone increases with exercise) and 3 (women's adrenals secrete testosterone) — answer B (1 and 3); 2 is false (testes produce testosterone primarily), 4 is false (stress raises cortisol).
factual_hooks: Growth hormone (somatotropin) secretion increases during exercise, sleep, and stress
Adrenal cortex produces small amounts of androgens (including testosterone) in women — important for libido

contrast_with: Progesterone — produced in ovaries/corpus luteum in women, not testes; testes primarily produce testosterone

question:
Consider the following statements with reference to India:
1. The Chief Election Commissioner and other Election Commissioners enjoy equal powers but receive unequal salaries
2. The Chief Election Commissioner is entitled to the same salary as in provided to a judge of the Supreme Court
3. The Chief Election Commissioner shall not be removed from his office except in like manner and on like grounds as a judge of the Supreme Court
4. The term of office of the Election Commissioner is five years from the date he assumes his office or till the day he attains the age of 62 years, whichever is earlier
Which of these statements are correct?
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Statement 1 is false (all Election Commissioners enjoy equal powers AND equal salary); Statement 4 is false (term is 6 years or 65 years of age, not 5 years/62 years); students may incorrectly validate these.
prelims.elimination_hint: Only statements 2 (CEC salary = Supreme Court judge) and 3 (removal same as SC judge) are correct — answer B (2 and 3); statement 1 is false (equal salary, not unequal); statement 4 is false (6 years or 65, not 5 years or 62).
factual_hooks: CEC and ECs: same powers AND same salary (= SC judge salary) — not unequal
CEC tenure: 6 years or until age 65, whichever earlier; removal: like SC judge (Parliamentary process)

mains.contemporary_relevance: ECI independence and appointment process have been the subject of recent Supreme Court scrutiny.

contrast_with: CEC vs other ECs: CEC cannot be removed by President alone; other ECs can be removed on CEC's recommendation

question: Which one of the following cities has been the venue of the Asian Games for the maximum number of times from the year 1951 to the year 2006?
subject: Governance

prelims.trap_factor: Delhi hosted Asian Games in 1951 and 1982; Tokyo and Beijing hosted once each — Bangkok (1966, 1970, 1978, 1998) hosted four times making it the maximum, but students may default to Delhi as 'home advantage'.
prelims.elimination_hint: Bangkok hosted the Asian Games in 1966, 1970, 1978, and 1998 — four times total (1951-2006), more than any other city; Delhi hosted twice (1951, 1982) — option B (Bangkok).
factual_hooks: Bangkok hosted Asian Games 4 times (1966, 1970, 1978, 1998) — most by any city up to 2006
Delhi hosted Asian Games in 1951 (inaugural) and 1982

contrast_with: Delhi — 2 times; Bangkok — 4 times (most frequent host through 2006)

question: Which one of the following authorities recommends the principles governing the grants-in-aid of the revenues to the states out of the Consolidated Fund of India?
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Union Ministry of Finance seems directly logical for grants-in-aid decisions; Inter-State Council deals with centre-state issues but not grants-in-aid principles — Finance Commission is the constitutional body for this.
prelims.elimination_hint: Article 275 and Article 280: Finance Commission recommends principles governing grants-in-aid from Consolidated Fund of India to states — this is its core constitutional mandate; option A.
factual_hooks: Finance Commission (Article 280): recommends tax devolution AND grants-in-aid principles for states
Grants-in-aid (Article 275) are given to states with specific needs as recommended by Finance Commission

mains.contemporary_relevance: 16th Finance Commission is currently working on devolution formula for 2026-31.

contrast_with: Inter-State Council — discusses centre-state relations but has no fiscal recommendation power

question:
With reference to colonial period of Indian history, match List I (Person) with List II (Event) and select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists
(Person) | (Event)
A. MacDonald | 1. Doctrine of Lapse
B. Linlithgow | 2. Communal Award
C. Dalhousie | 3. August Offer
D. Chelmsford | 4. Dyarchy
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: MacDonald (Communal Award) and Linlithgow (August Offer) are easily confused; Dalhousie's Doctrine of Lapse vs Dyarchy (Chelmsford/Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms) is another common mix-up.
prelims.elimination_hint: Key anchors: MacDonald = Communal Award 1932 (A-2); Linlithgow = August Offer 1940 (B-3); Dalhousie = Doctrine of Lapse 1848-56 (C-1); Chelmsford = Dyarchy through Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms 1919 (D-4) — answer C (A-2, B-3, C-1, D-4).
factual_hooks: Ramsay MacDonald announced Communal Award (1932) giving separate electorates; Ambedkar-Gandhi Poona Pact followed
Lord Chelmsford + Montagu introduced Dyarchy in provinces via Government of India Act 1919

contrast_with: Linlithgow's August Offer (1940) vs Cripps Mission (1942) — different vague British offers

question:
With reference to the Indian Public Finance consider the following statements:
1. External liabilities reported in Union Budget are based on historical exchange rates
2. The continued high borrowing has kept the real interest rates high in the economy
3. The upward trend in the ratio of Fiscal Deficit to GDP in recent years has an adverse effect to private investments.
4. Interest payments is the single largest component of the non-plan revenue expenditure of the Union Government.
Which of these statements are correct ?
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Statement 1 sounds plausible since exchange rates do fluctuate, but external liabilities are reported at current rates. Statement 4 is correct and often overlooked by candidates who assume defence or subsidies are the largest component.
prelims.elimination_hint: Statement 1 is false (external liabilities use current exchange rates, not historical), eliminating options A, B, D; only option C remains with statements 2, 3, 4.
factual_hooks: External liabilities are reported at current exchange rates, not historical
Interest payments are the single largest component of non-plan revenue expenditure
High fiscal deficit crowds out private investment (crowding-out effect)

mains.contemporary_relevance: Fiscal consolidation and interest payment burden remain key concerns in India's Union Budget.

contrast_with: Revenue deficit vs Fiscal deficit — revenue deficit excludes capital borrowings

question:
Consider the following names:
1. Archbishop Desmond Tutu
2. Lech Walesa
3. Shimon Peres
4. Yasser Arafat Who among these won the Nobel Peace Prize?
subject: International Relations

prelims.trap_factor: Candidates may be unsure whether Archbishop Desmond Tutu won the Nobel Peace Prize (he did, 1984), leading to elimination of option A. All four won the Nobel Peace Prize, making option D correct.
prelims.elimination_hint: All four — Tutu (1984), Walesa (1983), Peres (1994), Arafat (1994) — are Nobel Peace Prize laureates; option D is correct.
factual_hooks: Desmond Tutu won Nobel Peace Prize 1984 for anti-apartheid work
Lech Walesa won 1983 for Solidarity movement in Poland
Peres and Arafat shared 1994 prize for Oslo Accords

contrast_with: Nobel Peace Prize vs Nobel Prize for Literature — different categories

question: With which one of the following mountain tribes did the British first come into contact with after the grant of Diwani in the year 1765?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Garo and Kukis are also Northeast tribes with British contact, making elimination difficult without specific knowledge of the 1765 Diwani grant and the subsequent Bengal-Assam expansion route.
prelims.elimination_hint: The Khasis of the Meghalaya hills were the first hill tribe encountered as British expanded northeastward from Bengal after 1765 Diwani grant.
factual_hooks: Diwani of Bengal granted to East India Company in 1765
Khasi tribe was the first hill tribe the British encountered post-Diwani

contrast_with: Permanent Settlement (1793) vs Diwani grant (1765) — different landmarks of British revenue control

question:
With reference to Indian transport systems, consider the following statements:
1. Indian railways system is the largest in the world.
2. National Highways cater to 45 per cent of the total road transport demand.
3. Among the states, Kerala has the highest density of surface road.
4. National Highway No. 7 is the longest in the country.
Which of these statements are correct?
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Statement 1 seems plausible since Indian Railways is large, but the US system is actually larger. Statement 3 naming Kerala as highest road density is plausible but it is actually correct for some metrics, creating confusion.
prelims.elimination_hint: Statement 1 is false (Indian Railways is among the largest but US is largest); eliminate options with 1. Statement 4 (NH7 as longest) and Statement 2 (NH caters 45%) are correct, pointing to option D.
factual_hooks: NH7 (now NH44) was the longest national highway in India at the time
National Highways cater to about 40-45% of road transport demand

mains.contemporary_relevance: Bharatmala Project and highway expansion remain key infrastructure priorities.

contrast_with: State Highways vs National Highways — jurisdiction and funding differ

question:
Match List I (Name of the Lady) with List II (Achievement) and select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists
(Name of the lady) | (Achievement)
A. Chinna Pillai | 1. Winner of Neerja Bhanot Award
B. Kaveri Thakur Puraskar | 2. Recipient of Stree Shakti
C. Mira Nair | 3. Became the fastest swimmer in Asia at the age of 16 years
D. Yasoda Ekambaram | 4. Recipient of the Golden Lion at Venice Film Festival
subject: Governance

prelims.trap_factor: The match-list format with four women and four achievements makes elimination from one correct pair difficult; Mira Nair's Golden Lion connection to Venice Film Festival is the most well-known anchor.
prelims.elimination_hint: Mira Nair winning the Golden Lion at Venice (for Monsoon Wedding) is a well-known fact; match C-4, then use process of elimination for remaining pairs.
factual_hooks: Mira Nair won the Golden Lion at Venice Film Festival for Monsoon Wedding (2001)
Neerja Bhanot Award is given for bravery

contrast_with: Golden Lion (Venice) vs Palme d'Or (Cannes) — different film festivals

question:
Which reference to the government’s welfare schemes, consider the following statements :
1. Under the Antyodaya Anna Yojana, the food grains are available to the poorest of the poor families at Rs 2 per kg for wheat and Rs. 3 per kg for rice
2. Under the National Old Age Pension Scheme, the old and destitute are provided Rs 75 per month as Central pension, in addition to the amount provided by most State Governments.
3. Government of India has allocated 25 kg food grains per Below Poverty Line family per month, at less than half the economic cost.
Which of these statements are correct ?
subject: Governance

prelims.trap_factor: The exact prices under Antyodaya Anna Yojana (wheat Rs.2, rice Rs.3) are counterintuitive since rice is more expensive to produce; candidates may swap these figures.
prelims.elimination_hint: All three statements were correct at the time of the question (2002); the AAY prices (wheat Rs.2/kg, rice Rs.3/kg) and NOAPS Rs.75/month are specific figures to remember.
factual_hooks: Antyodaya Anna Yojana: wheat at Rs.2/kg, rice at Rs.3/kg for poorest families
National Old Age Pension Scheme provided Rs.75/month as central contribution

mains.contemporary_relevance: Evolved into National Food Security Act 2013; PMGKAY continues subsidised grain distribution.

contrast_with: AAY vs BPL ration — AAY targets poorest of BPL households

question: Who among the following was the first Bhakti saint to use Hindi for the propagation of his message ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Kabir is often associated with Hindi due to his Dohas in common parlance, but Ramananda preceded him and was the first to use Hindi. Tulsidas also wrote in Awadhi (a Hindi dialect), creating confusion.
prelims.elimination_hint: Ramananda (14th-15th century) predates Kabir and Tulsidas and is credited as the first Bhakti saint to use Hindi (Brajbhasha/vernacular) for propagation, making option C correct.
factual_hooks: Ramananda was the first Bhakti saint to use Hindi for propagation
Kabir was a disciple of Ramananda
Tulsidas wrote Ramcharitmanas in Awadhi

mains.contemporary_relevance: Bhakti-Sufi traditions continue to influence India's composite cultural heritage.

contrast_with: Ramananda (Hindi) vs Ramanuja (Sanskrit) — different linguistic traditions in Bhakti

question: In a bisexual flower, if androecium and gynaecium mature at different times, the phenomenon is know as
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Heterogamy sounds related to different maturation times due to its 'hetero' prefix, but it refers to the production of different types of gametes. Dichogamy specifically means temporal separation of maturation.
prelims.elimination_hint: Dichogamy = 'dicho' (apart) + 'gamy' (mating/maturation) — temporal separation of androecium and gynoecium maturation to prevent self-pollination; eliminate Herkogamy (spatial separation) and Heterogamy (different gamete types).
factual_hooks: Dichogamy: androecium and gynoecium mature at different times — prevents self-pollination
Protandry: stamens mature first; Protogyny: pistils mature first — both are types of dichogamy

contrast_with: Dichogamy (temporal) vs Herkogamy (spatial) — both prevent self-pollination but by different mechanisms

question: With reference to the period of extremist nationalist movement in India with its spirit of Swadeshi, which one of the following statements is not correct ?
option_a: Laikat Hossain led the Muslim peasants of Barisal in their agitations
option_b: In 1989, the scheme of national education was formulated by Satish Chandra Mukherjee
option_c: The Bengal National College was founded in 1906 with Aurobindo as the Principal
option_d: Tagore preached the cult of Atmasakti, the main plank of which was social and economic regeneration of the villages
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: The 'not correct' framing means three statements are correct; candidates may overlook that the Barisal agitation leader's identity requires specific knowledge, making statement A the incorrect one.
prelims.elimination_hint: The Barisal agitation (Swadeshi era) was led by Aswinikumar Dutta, not Laikat Hossain; this makes statement A incorrect, which is the answer.
factual_hooks: Bengal National College was founded in 1906 with Aurobindo Ghosh as Principal
Satish Chandra Mukherjee formulated national education scheme in 1906 (not 1989)
Tagore's Atmasakti philosophy focused on rural social and economic regeneration

contrast_with: Moderates vs Extremists — methods and objectives differed in Indian National Congress

question: When the same note is played on a sitar and a flute, the sound produced can be distinguished from each other because of the difference in
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Candidates may think pitch differs because different instruments produce different pitches for the 'same note,' but pitch and frequency are the same; only quality (timbre) distinguishes instruments playing the same note.
prelims.elimination_hint: Same note = same pitch and frequency. Loudness can be the same. Only timbre/quality (due to different harmonics/overtones) distinguishes sitar from flute; option C is correct.
factual_hooks: Quality/timbre is determined by the number and intensity of overtones/harmonics
Same note on different instruments = same pitch and loudness (if played equally), but different timbre

contrast_with: Pitch (frequency) vs Quality (timbre) — often confused in sound questions

question:
Assertion (A)
: A diamond sparkles more than a glass imitation cut to the same shape.
Reason (R)
: The refractive index of diamond is less than that of glass. In the context of the above two statements, which one of the following is correct ?
option_a: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
option_b: Both A and R are true, but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_c: A is true, but R is false
option_d: A is false, but R is true
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Both A and R seem true at first glance — diamonds do sparkle more. The trap is in the Reason: diamond actually has a HIGHER refractive index than glass, not lower, making R false.
prelims.elimination_hint: Assertion A is true (diamond sparkles more due to total internal reflection). Reason R is false — diamond's refractive index (~2.42) is much higher than glass (~1.5). Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Diamond refractive index ~2.42 vs glass ~1.5 — diamond is higher
Higher refractive index causes greater total internal reflection, enabling more sparkle

contrast_with: Total internal reflection vs refraction — TIR occurs when light goes from denser to rarer medium beyond critical angle

question: In the Indian Constitution, the Right to Equality is granted by Five Articles. They are
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Candidates may confuse Article 13 (laws inconsistent with FR are void) as part of Right to Equality since it precedes it. Article 13 is a protective provision, not a right itself.
prelims.elimination_hint: Right to Equality spans Articles 14-18: Article 14 (equality before law), 15 (no discrimination), 16 (equal opportunity), 17 (abolition of untouchability), 18 (abolition of titles). Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Right to Equality: Articles 14-18
Article 17 abolishes untouchability
Article 18 abolishes titles except military/academic

mains.contemporary_relevance: Right to Equality is foundational to anti-discrimination jurisprudence in India.

contrast_with: Article 13 (protective provision) vs Articles 14-18 (Right to Equality) — Article 13 enables enforcement of FRs but is not itself a right

question: Suppose a rocketship is receding from the earth at a speed of 2/10th the velocity of light. A light in the rocketship appears blue to the passengers on the ship. What colour would it appear to an observer on the earth?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: The Doppler effect for light is counterintuitive — a receding source shifts light toward red (redshift), but at 2/10 light speed the shift may seem like it should reach orange or red, not yellow.
prelims.elimination_hint: Doppler redshift: blue light shifts toward longer wavelengths when source recedes. At 0.2c, blue shifts toward yellow/green range. Answer: C (Yellow).
factual_hooks: Doppler redshift: receding source shifts light to longer wavelengths (toward red)
Blue light (~450nm) shifts toward yellow/green range at moderate recession speeds

contrast_with: Redshift (recession) vs Blueshift (approach) — opposite Doppler effects for light

question:
Which of the following can be inferred from the statement that ‘Either John is stupid or John is lazy’ ?
I. John is lazy / therefore, John is not stupid.
II. John is not lazy / therefore, John is stupid.
III. John is not stupid / therefore, John is lazy.
IV. John is stupid / therefore, John is not lazy.
Choose the correct answer from the codes given below : Codes :
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: The statement uses inclusive 'or' (either...or), meaning at least one is true. Candidates may confuse inclusive vs exclusive 'or' — in logic, 'or' is inclusive unless stated as 'exclusive or'.
prelims.elimination_hint: With inclusive 'or': if one part is false, the other must be true (modus tollens). II (not lazy → stupid) and III (not stupid → lazy) are valid; option B (II and III) is correct.
factual_hooks: 'Either A or B' in logic allows both to be true (inclusive or)
Modus tollens: if not-B then A, from 'A or B'

contrast_with: Inclusive OR (at least one true) vs Exclusive OR (exactly one true)

question: A person earns Rs. 2000 per month over and above his salary as additional charge allowance. However, 30% of this additional income will be deducted as additional income-tax at source. If the person would deposit Rs. 1000 per month on a long-term saving fetching 12% interest his tax liability on the additional allowance would reduce to 10%. What is the effective interest for this person for money invested in the long-term savings scheme ?
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: The question involves tax savings from investment, not just interest; candidates may directly take 12% as the answer without accounting for the tax benefit that effectively increases the return.
prelims.elimination_hint: Tax saved = (30%-10%) × Rs.1000 = Rs.200/month additional benefit. Effective return = Rs.120 interest + Rs.200 tax saving on Rs.1000 = Rs.320/Rs.1000 = 32% but annualized on net investment gives effective rate of 20%. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Effective interest = nominal interest + tax saved due to investment
Tax arbitrage increases effective yield of long-term savings instruments

contrast_with: Nominal interest rate vs Effective interest rate — effective rate accounts for tax benefits

question: A meteor is
option_b: a piece of matter which has entered the earth’s atmosphere from outer space
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Candidates may confuse meteor with meteorite (reaches earth's surface) or asteroid (orbits sun); 'rapidly moving star' is a common misconception since meteors appear as shooting stars.
prelims.elimination_hint: A meteor is a piece of matter (rock/debris) from outer space that enters Earth's atmosphere and burns up; it is NOT a star. Meteorite is what survives to hit the ground. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Meteor: space matter entering Earth's atmosphere (burns up as 'shooting star')
Meteorite: meteor that survives and hits Earth's surface
Asteroid: rock orbiting the Sun

contrast_with: Meteor vs Meteorite — meteor burns in atmosphere; meteorite reaches Earth's surface

question: Given below is a map of some countries which were parts of the erstwhile Soviet Union, with water bodies shown by shaded areas : The countries marked 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 are respectively
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: The five Central Asian republics of the former Soviet Union have similar-sounding names and are geographically adjacent; without a map, positional identification is extremely difficult.
prelims.elimination_hint: Kazakhstan is the largest and most northern; Turkmenia borders Iran in the south; Uzbekistan is central; use the Aral Sea (shaded water body) as a geographic anchor to identify positions.
