factual_hooks: Kazakhstan is the largest Central Asian republic, bordering Russia in the north
Turkmenia (Turkmenistan) borders Iran and Afghanistan in the south

contrast_with: Central Asian republics vs Caucasus republics — different geographic regions of former USSR

question:
The time in the wall-clock is
3.25 the acute angle between the hours-hand and the minutes-hand is
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: At 3:25, the minute hand is exactly at '5' (25 minutes = 150°). The hour hand is not at 3 but has moved 25/60 of the way from 3 to 4; candidates forget to account for the hour hand's partial movement.
factual_hooks: Hour hand moves 0.5° per minute; minute hand moves 6° per minute
At 3:25, hour hand is at 102.5°, minute hand at 150°

contrast_with: Clock problems vs direction problems — both involve angles but different setups

question: Recently, several hundred South Africa soldiers were sent to Burundi
option_a: to side with the Tutsi tribe in the country’s civil war
option_b: to side with the Hutu tribe in the ongoing ethnic conflict
option_c: as a part of United Nations special force to keep control on the ethnic groups revolting against the government
option_d: as South Africa’s protection force to help mediate and agreement between warring groups of civil war
subject: International Relations

prelims.trap_factor: Options A and B suggest South Africa sided with one ethnic group, which seems plausible given South Africa's own ethnic history; the correct answer involves a diplomatic/peacekeeping role.
prelims.elimination_hint: South Africa sent troops as a protection force to help mediate between warring groups (Hutu and Tutsi) in Burundi's civil war under Nelson Mandela's mediation initiative; answer D.
factual_hooks: South Africa deployed troops to Burundi in 2001 as part of Mandela's peace mediation initiative
Burundi civil war involved Hutu-Tutsi ethnic conflict similar to Rwanda

contrast_with: South Africa's role in Burundi (mediation) vs in DRC (peacekeeping under AU)

question: “Monoculture of commercially viable trees is destroying the unique natural profile of ............Thoughtless exploitation of timber, deforesting vast tracts for palm cultivation, destruction of mangroves, illegal logging by tribals and poaching only compound the problem. Fresh water pockets are fast drying up due to deforestation and destruction of mangroves.” The place referred to in this quotation is
subject: Environment

prelims.trap_factor: Sunderbans is the most famous mangrove ecosystem in India and may seem the obvious answer; however, the specific mention of palm cultivation, timber exploitation, and freshwater pocket drying uniquely fits Andaman & Nicobar.
prelims.elimination_hint: Clues: 'monoculture of commercially viable trees', 'palm cultivation', 'destruction of mangroves', 'freshwater pockets drying' all point to Andaman & Nicobar Islands, not Sunderbans. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Andaman & Nicobar Islands face threats from palm cultivation and illegal logging
Sunderbans is in West Bengal, not described by 'freshwater pockets drying up'

mains.contemporary_relevance: Andaman & Nicobar Islands remain a biodiversity hotspot under conservation pressure.

contrast_with: Sunderbans (Bengal delta mangrove) vs Andaman mangroves — different ecosystem contexts

question: Coffee cultivation in Sri Lanka was abandoned because of the disease
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Leaf blight and leaf spot are also common plant diseases and may seem plausible; 'leaf rust' is a specific fungal disease (Hemileia vastatrix) that devastated Sri Lanka's coffee in the 1870s.
prelims.elimination_hint: Hemileia vastatrix (coffee leaf rust) destroyed Sri Lanka's coffee plantations in the 1870s, leading to the switch to tea cultivation. Answer: C (leaf rust).
factual_hooks: Coffee leaf rust (Hemileia vastatrix) devastated Sri Lanka's coffee in 1870s
Sri Lanka switched from coffee to tea after the leaf rust epidemic

contrast_with: Leaf rust (fungal) vs leaf blight (bacterial/fungal) — both foliar diseases but different pathogens

question: When a mirror is rotated by an angle of 6, the reflected ray will rotate by
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: The question text appears garbled (options don't match the stated question about mirror rotation and reflected ray); the options about 'increase/decrease in size' suggest a different question about stellar objects may have been merged.
prelims.elimination_hint: Based on the law of reflection: when a mirror rotates by angle θ, the reflected ray rotates by 2θ. The correct answer C (maintain its size) seems to relate to a different question about stellar/celestial objects.
factual_hooks: Law of reflection: when mirror rotates by θ, reflected ray rotates by 2θ
This is a fundamental principle used in optical instruments like galvanometers

contrast_with: Plane mirror (rotates reflected ray by 2θ) vs curved mirror (different reflection geometry)

question: Which one of the following Venn diagrams correctly illustrates the relationship among the classes: Carrot, Food, Vegetable ?
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Candidates may draw Venn diagrams incorrectly by placing Carrot and Vegetable as overlapping circles instead of nested ones. Carrot is a subset of Vegetable which is a subset of Food.
prelims.elimination_hint: Carrot ⊂ Vegetable ⊂ Food — three concentric circles with Food outermost, Vegetable inside it, Carrot inside Vegetable. Select Venn diagram I showing this nested relationship.
factual_hooks: All carrots are vegetables; all vegetables are food — shows set inclusion
Nested Venn diagrams represent subset relationships

contrast_with: Overlapping circles (partial intersection) vs nested circles (full inclusion) in Venn diagrams

question:
Q3. Given below are three quotations of great thinkers. What do each of these quotations convey to you in the present context?
(c) "The strength of a society is not in its laws, but in the morality of its people." - Swami Vivekananda (Answer in 150 words) 10
subject: Ethics

factual_hooks: Vivekananda believed moral strength of individuals underpins societal progress
He emphasized character-building and ethical conduct over legal compliance

answer_structure.intro_approach: Vivekananda's quote highlights that formal laws are secondary to the internalized moral values of citizens and administrators.
body_points: Laws are external constraints; morality is an internal compass — a truly ethical society needs both but morality is foundational
Relevance for civil servants: rule-following without ethical motivation leads to mechanical governance; ethical civil servants go beyond mere compliance
Contemporary context: rising corruption, institutional decay, and governance failures reflect weakening of moral fabric despite strong legal frameworks
Vivekananda's vision: character formation through education and values as the bedrock of national strength
answer_structure.conclusion_approach: Ultimately, sustainable and just governance emerges from ethically grounded individuals, not merely robust legal systems.

mains.contemporary_relevance: Relevant in the context of ethical governance debates and anti-corruption discourse in India.

contrast_with: Vivekananda (morality-based society) vs Bentham (law-based utilitarianism)

question: In the context of ancient Indian society, which one of the following terms does not belong to the category of the other three ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: All four terms sound like ancient Indian social/kinship terms; Kosa (treasury/sheath) is the odd one out as Kula (family), Vamsa (lineage), and Gotra (clan) are all kinship terms.
prelims.elimination_hint: Kula = family group, Vamsa = lineage/dynasty, Gotra = patrilineal clan — all kinship categories. Kosa = treasury/storehouse — belongs to political/economic vocabulary. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Kula, Vamsa, Gotra are kinship/lineage terms in ancient Indian society
Kosa means treasury — part of Saptanga theory of state, not kinship

contrast_with: Kosa (treasury, political concept) vs Gotra (clan, social concept)

question: Chapchar Kut is a festival celebrated in the state of
subject: Society

prelims.trap_factor: Chapchar Kut sounds similar to other Northeast festivals; candidates may confuse it with festivals of Assam or Arunachal Pradesh.
prelims.elimination_hint: Chapchar Kut is the most important festival of Mizoram, celebrated in spring after the jungle is cleared for jhum (shifting) cultivation. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Chapchar Kut is Mizoram's most important spring festival
It celebrates completion of the most arduous part of jhum (shifting) cultivation

contrast_with: Chapchar Kut (Mizoram) vs Bihu (Assam) — both spring festivals but different states and cultures

question: Who among the following is known for his work on medicine during the Gupta period ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Saumilla and Sudraka are known Gupta-era literary figures; candidates may confuse them with medical scholars. Susrutha (Sushruta) is distinctly known for surgery but is associated with earlier or contemporaneous periods.
prelims.elimination_hint: Susrutha (Sushruta) authored Sushruta Samhita — the foundational text of Ayurvedic surgery — and is the correct answer. Shaunaka is a Vedic scholar. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Sushruta authored Sushruta Samhita — foundational text of Ayurvedic surgery
Charaka authored Charaka Samhita — foundational text of Ayurvedic medicine (earlier period)

mains.contemporary_relevance: Sushruta is recognized as a pioneer of plastic surgery globally.

contrast_with: Sushruta (surgery) vs Charaka (medicine/internal) — both Ayurvedic but different specializations

question:
Consider the following statements:
1. NTPD is the largest power utility in India.
2. ONGC accounts for half of the LPG production in India.
3. Indian Oil Corporation operates all the oil refineries in India.
4. The Indian Ordinance Factory is the largest departmentally run industrial undertaking in the country.
Which of these statements is/are correct?
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Statement 1 about NTPC being largest power utility and statement 3 about IOC operating ALL oil refineries are the key traps — IOC does not operate all refineries (HPCL, BPCL, ONGC also operate refineries).
prelims.elimination_hint: Statement 3 is false (IOC doesn't operate all refineries) and Statement 2 is debatable; D (NTPC as largest power utility and Indian Ordnance Factory as largest departmentally run industrial undertaking) is most defensible.
factual_hooks: Indian Ordnance Factory is the largest departmentally-run industrial undertaking
NTPC was the largest power generating utility in India

contrast_with: NTPC (electricity) vs ONGC (oil & gas) — different energy sectors

question:
Match List I (Oxdiation number) with List II (The element) and select the correct answer using the codes given below this lists
(Oxidation number) | (The element)
A. 2 | 1. Oxidation number of Mn in MnO2
B. 3 | 2. Oxidation number of S in H2S2O7
C. 4 | 3. Oxidation number of Ca in CaO2
D. 6 | 4. Oxidation number of Al in NaAIH4
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Oxidation number calculation requires knowledge of each compound's formula; candidates may misapply rules for peroxides (CaO2) where oxygen has -1 oxidation state, giving Ca +2, not +4.
prelims.elimination_hint: CaO2 is calcium peroxide — O is -1 each, so Ca is +2. MnO2: O is -2×2=-4, so Mn is +4. Match A-3(Ca=+2), B-4(Al=-3 in NaAlH4), C-1(Mn=+4), D-2(S=+6 in H2S2O7). Answer: A.
factual_hooks: In peroxides (like CaO2), oxygen has -1 oxidation state (not -2)
In H2S2O7 (pyrosulfuric acid), S has +6 oxidation state

contrast_with: Peroxide bond (O-O, each O at -1) vs normal oxide (O at -2)

question: With reference to the Indian freedom struggle, which one of the following statements is not correct?
option_a: Hakim Ajmal Khan was one of the leaders to start a nations list and militant Ahrar movement
option_b: When the Indian National Congress was formed, Sayyid Ahmad Khan opposed it
option_c: The All-India Muslim League which was formed in 1906 vehemently opposed the partition of Bengal and separate electorates
option_d: Maulana Barkaullah and Maulana Obeidullah Sindhi were among those who formed a Provisional Government of India in Kabul
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Statement C is the incorrect one — the All India Muslim League (formed 1906) actually SUPPORTED the partition of Bengal and separate electorates, not opposed them; this is a commonly tested reversal.
prelims.elimination_hint: Muslim League was formed partly to protect Muslim interests and SUPPORTED separate electorates (Morley-Minto reforms 1909) and did NOT oppose Bengal's partition. Statement C is incorrect. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Muslim League (1906) supported partition of Bengal and demanded separate electorates
Khudai Khidmatgars (Red Shirts) under Khan Abdul Ghaffar Khan opposed British rule
Maulana Barkaullah established Provisional Government of India in Kabul (1915)

contrast_with: Muslim League (pro-partition, separate electorates) vs INC (anti-partition, joint electorates)

question: The term of the Lok Sabha
option_a: cannot be extended under any circumstances
option_b: can be extended by six months at a time
option_c: can be extended by one year at a time during the proclamation of emergency
option_d: can be extended-for two years at a time during the proclamation of emergency
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Candidates may remember that Lok Sabha term can be extended during emergency but forget the specific unit — it is one year at a time (not two years or six months at a time).
prelims.elimination_hint: Article 83(2): Lok Sabha can be extended by Parliament by law for one year at a time during National Emergency, for as long as the emergency is in force. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Lok Sabha term can be extended by one year at a time during National Emergency (Article 83)
Extension cannot exceed beyond the emergency period

contrast_with: Lok Sabha (can be dissolved/extended) vs Rajya Sabha (permanent, never dissolved)

question:
Consider the following:
1. Currency with the public
2. Demand deposits with banks
3. Time deposits with banks
Which of these are included in Broad Money (M3) in India?
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Candidates may exclude time deposits thinking they are not 'money'; M1 excludes time deposits but M3 (broad money) includes them as they can be converted to cash.
prelims.elimination_hint: M3 = M1 + time deposits with banks. M1 = currency with public + demand deposits + other deposits with RBI. All three components (1, 2, 3) are part of M3. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: M3 (Broad Money) = Currency with public + Demand deposits + Time deposits + Other deposits with RBI
M1 (Narrow Money) excludes time deposits; M3 includes them

mains.contemporary_relevance: RBI monitors M3 growth as an indicator of monetary expansion and inflation.

prerequisite_concepts: Money supply aggregates (M0, M1, M2, M3)
contrast_with: M1 (narrow money, excludes time deposits) vs M3 (broad money, includes time deposits)

question: During the Indian freedom struggle, the Khudai Khidmatgars, also known as Red Shirts, called for
option_a: the Union of Pashtun tribal areas in north-west with the Afghanistan
option_b: the adoption of terrorist tactics and methods for terrorising and finally ousting the colonial rulers
option_c: the adoption of communist revolutionary ideology for political and social reform
option_d: the Pathan regional nationalist unity and a struggle against colonialism
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Option A (union with Afghanistan) sounds plausible given Pashtun identity; option C (communist ideology) may attract candidates who know Khan was influenced by socialist ideas; the correct answer emphasizes Pashtun unity and anti-colonialism.
prelims.elimination_hint: Khudai Khidmatgars (Khan Abdul Ghaffar Khan's 'Red Shirts') advocated Pathan/Pashtun regional nationalism and non-violent resistance against British colonialism — not union with Afghanistan or communism. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Khudai Khidmatgars = 'Servants of God'; Khan Abdul Ghaffar Khan = 'Frontier Gandhi'
Red Shirts promoted non-violent Pashtun nationalism against British rule

contrast_with: Khudai Khidmatgars (non-violent) vs Chittagong Armory Raid (violent revolutionary movement)

question: Which one of the following statements is not correct ?
option_b: Round worm has no circulatory system
option_c: In bony fishes, swim bladder is usually present
option_d: In cartilaginous fishes, fertilisation is internal
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Option A states all echinoderms are viviparous — many echinoderms (like sea urchins) are oviparous (egg-laying), so this is clearly incorrect and is the 'not correct' answer.
prelims.elimination_hint: Echinoderms (sea stars, sea urchins) are predominantly oviparous (egg-laying), not viviparous (live birth). All other statements (round worm lacking circulatory system, bony fish swim bladder, cartilaginous fish internal fertilization) are correct. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Echinoderms are oviparous (lay eggs), not viviparous
Round worms (nematodes) have no circulatory system — nutrients diffuse directly

contrast_with: Echinoderms (oviparous) vs sharks/cartilaginous fish (some viviparous/ovoviviparous)

question:
Consider the following ecosystems:
1. Taiga
2. Tropical evergreen
3. Tropical deciduous
4. Tundra The correct sequence in decreasing order of the albedo values of these ecosystems is
subject: Environment

prelims.trap_factor: Albedo is the reflectivity of a surface; candidates may confuse the order assuming snow-covered tundra has highest albedo — but tundra snow cover makes it highest, with taiga second due to dark conifer trees absorbing light.
prelims.elimination_hint: Albedo order (decreasing): Tundra (snow-covered, high reflectivity) > Taiga (some snow + dark conifers) > Tropical deciduous (seasonal leaf cover) > Tropical evergreen (dense dark canopy, lowest albedo). Answer: C (4,1,3,2).
factual_hooks: Tundra has highest albedo due to snow and ice cover
Tropical evergreen forests have lowest albedo due to dense dark canopy absorbing sunlight

mains.contemporary_relevance: Albedo changes due to deforestation and ice melt are key factors in climate change.

contrast_with: High albedo (reflects sunlight, cooling effect) vs low albedo (absorbs sunlight, warming effect)

question: ‘Saddle Peak’, the highest peak of Andaman and Nicobar islands, is located in
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Great Nicobar being the southernmost island is well-known, causing candidates to incorrectly associate it with the highest peak; Saddle Peak is in the Andaman group (North Andaman), not Nicobar.
prelims.elimination_hint: Saddle Peak (732m) is the highest point in A&N Islands, located in North Andaman island, not Great Nicobar. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Saddle Peak (732m) is the highest point in Andaman & Nicobar Islands, located in North Andaman
Great Nicobar is the southernmost island of India, not the location of Saddle Peak

contrast_with: Saddle Peak (North Andaman) vs Indira Point (Great Nicobar, southernmost tip of India)

question: The sea coast of which one of the following states has become famous as a nesting place for the giant Olive Ridley turtles from South America?
subject: Environment

prelims.trap_factor: Goa's beaches are well-known, which may mislead candidates; Tamil Nadu also has turtle-nesting beaches. Odisha's Gahirmatha beach is the world's largest Olive Ridley nesting site.
prelims.elimination_hint: Odisha's (Orissa) Gahirmatha Marine Sanctuary and Rushikulya rookery are the largest Olive Ridley sea turtle nesting sites in the world. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Gahirmatha Beach, Odisha = world's largest mass nesting site for Olive Ridley turtles
Olive Ridley turtles travel from South America and elsewhere to nest at Odisha coast

mains.contemporary_relevance: Olive Ridley conservation is an ongoing concern due to fishing net entanglement and coastal development.

contrast_with: Olive Ridley (Odisha nesting) vs Leatherback (different species, nests at different locations)

question: Assertion (A) : Muhammad bin Tughlaq left Delhi, and, for two years lived in a camp called Swarga-dwari. Reason (R) : At that time, Delhi was ravaged by a form of plague and many people died.
option_a: Both A and R are individually true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_b: Both A and R are individually true but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_c: A is true but R is false
option_d: A is false but R is true
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Both A and R may appear correct; the camp 'Swarga-dwari' is an obscure fact, and both the desertion of Delhi and a plague connection are historically documented, making the assertion-reason link seem valid.
prelims.elimination_hint: Muhammad bin Tughlaq did leave Delhi for Swarga-dwari, and it was indeed due to a plague-like epidemic; both A and R are true and R explains A. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Muhammad bin Tughlaq left Delhi for Swarga-dwari camp due to an epidemic/plague
Muhammad bin Tughlaq is known for eccentric decisions including Delhi-to-Daulatabad capital shift

contrast_with: Muhammad bin Tughlaq (Tughluq dynasty, 1325-51) vs Alauddin Khilji (Khilji dynasty)

question:
Assertion (A)
: Mangroves are very specialised forest ecosystems of tropical and subtropical regions bordering certain sea coasts.
Reason (R)
: They stabilise the shoreline and act as bulwark against encroachments by sea. In the context of the above two statements, which one of the following is correct ?
option_a: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_b: Both A and R are true but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_c: A is true but R is false
option_d: A is false but R is true
subject: Environment

prelims.trap_factor: Both A and R are individually true, but R (shoreline stabilization) is the result of mangrove characteristics, not the reason why they are specialized ecosystems of tropical/subtropical coasts — the two are related but R doesn't explain why mangroves exist where they do.
prelims.elimination_hint: A is true: mangroves are indeed specialized ecosystems of tropical/subtropical coastal regions. R is also true: they do stabilize shorelines. But R does not explain why they are specialized coastal ecosystems — the reason is their adaptation to saline, tidal, anaerobic conditions. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Mangroves grow in tropical/subtropical intertidal zones with saline-tolerant adaptations (pneumatophores, prop roots)
They stabilize shorelines but this is an ecological service, not the reason for their coastal specialization

mains.contemporary_relevance: Mangrove conservation is critical for coastal resilience against cyclones and sea-level rise.

contrast_with: Mangroves (saline coastal) vs freshwater wetlands — different adaptations and locations

question: Q2. (b) Global warming and climate change are the outcomes of human greed in the name of development, indicating the direction in which extinction of organisms including human beings is heading towards loss of life on Earth. How do you put an end to this to protect life and bring equilibrium between the society and the environment? (Answer in 150 words)  10
subject: Ethics

factual_hooks: Climate change is anthropogenic — driven by industrial emissions, deforestation, overconsumption
Intergenerational equity demands present generations limit resource exploitation

answer_structure.intro_approach: Climate change reflects a fundamental ethical failure — the prioritization of short-term material gain over long-term planetary health and justice.
body_points: Root cause: industrial greed, consumerism, and political short-termism driving greenhouse gas emissions and ecological destruction
Ethical dimension: current generations are depleting resources at the cost of future generations — violation of intergenerational equity
Solutions: transition to renewable energy, circular economy, sustainable agriculture, and carbon pricing create equilibrium between society and environment
Role of governance: international cooperation (Paris Agreement), domestic policy (renewable targets), and behavioral change driven by ethical leadership
Individual-collective responsibility: lifestyle changes + systemic change both needed
answer_structure.conclusion_approach: Ending the exploitation-extinction spiral requires a paradigm shift from growth-at-any-cost to a values-driven, sustainable relationship between humanity and the Earth.

mains.contemporary_relevance: India's NDC targets and COP commitments make this directly relevant to current affairs.

contrast_with: Anthropocentric (human-centered) vs Ecocentric (ecosystem-centered) ethics on environment

question: When the Chief Justice of a High Court acts in an administrative capacity, he is subject to
option_a: the writ jurisdiction of any of the other judges of the High Court
option_b: special control exercised by the Chief Justice of India
option_c: discretionary powers of the Governor of the state
option_d: special powers provided to the Chief Minister in this regard
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Candidates may think the Chief Justice of India supervises all High Court CJs or that the Governor has administrative control; the correct answer involves the unique principle that CJ's administrative acts are subject to judicial review by fellow judges.
prelims.elimination_hint: When the Chief Justice of a High Court acts administratively, their actions are subject to the writ jurisdiction of other judges of the same High Court. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: CJ of HC in administrative capacity is subject to writ jurisdiction of other HC judges
This reflects the principle of judicial accountability even within the judiciary

contrast_with: CJ of India's supervisory role (Article 235) over subordinate courts vs HC CJ's administrative accountability

question: Hawala transactions relate to payments
option_a: received in rupees against overseas currencies and vice versa without going through the official channels
option_b: received for sale/transfer of shares without going through the established stock exchanges
option_c: received as commission for services rendered to overseas investors/buyers/ sellers in assisting them to get over the red tape and/or in getting preferential treatment
option_d: made to political parties or to individuals for meeting election expenses
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Candidates may confuse Hawala with stock market manipulation or commission payments; the defining feature of Hawala is informal cross-border money transfer bypassing official banking channels.
prelims.elimination_hint: Hawala = informal value transfer system where money is received in one country's currency and paid in another's without physical money crossing borders or going through official banking. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Hawala is an informal remittance system based on trust and a network of brokers (hawaladars)
Hawala transactions bypass official banking channels and currency controls — illegal in India

mains.contemporary_relevance: Hawala remains a key mechanism for terror financing and money laundering globally.

contrast_with: Hawala (informal, illegal transfer) vs SWIFT (formal, official international transfer)

question: Q11. Write a note on India's green energy corridor to alleviate the problem of conventional energy. [200 words] 10
subject: Economy

factual_hooks: India's Green Energy Corridor aims to evacuate renewable energy from renewable-rich states to demand centers
It involves upgrading transmission infrastructure to handle variable renewable generation

answer_structure.intro_approach: India's Green Energy Corridor is a transformative infrastructure initiative to integrate large-scale renewable energy into the national grid.
body_points: Objective: evacuate solar and wind energy from renewable-rich states (Rajasthan, Gujarat, Tamil Nadu, AP) to deficit regions
Components: inter-state and intra-state transmission lines, substations, and smart grid technologies to handle intermittent renewable supply
Implementing agencies: Power Grid Corporation of India (PGCIL) for inter-state; state transmission utilities for intra-state
Significance: reduces curtailment of renewable energy, improves grid stability, and reduces dependence on coal-based power
Challenges: land acquisition for transmission lines, financing, coordination among states
answer_structure.conclusion_approach: The Green Energy Corridor is central to India achieving its renewable energy targets and transitioning to a low-carbon economy.

mains.contemporary_relevance: Directly relevant given India's 500 GW renewable target by 2030 and energy transition goals.

contrast_with: Conventional energy grid (baseload, stable) vs smart/green grid (variable renewable integration)

question: Which one of the following sculptures invariably used green schist as the medium ?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Mathura sculptures are also known for their use of specific stone (spotted red sandstone); candidates may confuse the Gandhara-green schist link or assume Bharhut sculptures used schist.
prelims.elimination_hint: Gandhara sculptures, influenced by Greco-Roman traditions, characteristically used grey/green schist (phyllite) as medium, giving them their distinctive look. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Gandhara sculptures used grey/green schist — distinctive Greco-Roman influenced Buddhist art
Mathura school used spotted red sandstone; Amaravati used white marble

contrast_with: Gandhara (schist, Hellenistic style) vs Mathura (sandstone, indigenous style) — two major schools of Buddhist art

question: A complete cycle of a traffic light takes 60 seconds. During each cycle the light is green for 25 second, yellow for 5 second and red for 30 second. At a randomly chosen time, the probability that the light will not be green is
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Candidates may calculate P(green) = 25/60 and then subtract from 1, but may make arithmetic errors; the straightforward calculation is (30+5)/60 = 35/60 = 7/12.
prelims.elimination_hint: P(not green) = P(yellow) + P(red) = 5/60 + 30/60 = 35/60 = 7/12. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Complementary probability: P(A') = 1 - P(A)

contrast_with: Complementary probability vs conditional probability — different concepts

question: In the year 2001, NASA launched a spacecraft to Mars named
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Mars Climate Orbiter (1998) and Mars Global Surveyor (1996) are also NASA Mars missions launched around the same period, causing confusion with the 2001 launch.
prelims.elimination_hint: Mars Odyssey was launched in April 2001 and remains NASA's longest-operating Mars spacecraft. Mars Climate Orbiter was 1998 (failed). Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Mars Odyssey launched April 7, 2001 — NASA's 2001 Mars mission
Mars Odyssey detected hydrogen (possible water ice) near Mars south pole

contrast_with: Mars Odyssey (2001, orbiter) vs Mars Pathfinder (1996, lander/rover)

question: The last opportunity to avoid the partition of India was lost with the rejection of
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: The Rajagopalachari Formula and Wavell Plan also failed but the Cabinet Mission Plan (1946) is specifically considered the last real opportunity for a united India before partition became inevitable.
prelims.elimination_hint: The Cabinet Mission Plan (1946) proposed a united India with a three-tier federal structure; its rejection by both Congress and League made partition inevitable. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Cabinet Mission Plan (1946) was the last proposal for a united India with a federal structure
Both INC and Muslim League's rejection/acceptance disagreements led to its failure

contrast_with: Cabinet Mission (federal united India) vs Mountbatten Plan (partition) — sequential and contrasting outcomes

question: With reference to India, which one the following statements is not correct?
option_a: About one-third of the area of the country records more than 750 millimeters of annual rainfall
option_b: The dominant source of irrigation in the country is wells
option_c: Alluvial soil is the predominant type of soil in the northern plans of the country
option_d: The mountain areas accounts for about thirty percent of the surface area of the country
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Statement A (one-third area gets >750mm rainfall) may seem correct since India has diverse rainfall, but actually MORE than one-third gets >750mm; the dominant irrigation source being wells is also counterintuitive.
prelims.elimination_hint: Statement A is incorrect — more than one-third (roughly two-thirds) of India records >750mm rainfall annually. Groundwater/wells are indeed the dominant irrigation source. Answer: A (not correct statement).
factual_hooks: Wells/groundwater is the dominant irrigation source in India (~60-65% of irrigated area)
Mountain areas account for roughly 30% of India's surface area

contrast_with: Canal irrigation (dominant in north) vs well irrigation (dominant nationally overall)

question: A solid cube just gets completely immersed in water when a 0.2 kg mass is placed on it. If the mass is removed, the cube is 2 cm about the water level. What is the length of each side of the cube?
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Candidates must set up two equations: one for full immersion with 0.2kg mass, another for floating 2cm above water without mass. Confusing which condition applies to which equation is the main trap.
prelims.elimination_hint: Let side = a cm. Volume of cube = a³ cm³. When 2cm above water, submerged volume = a²(a-2). Buoyancy = a²(a-2)g = a³×ρ_cube×g. With 0.2kg: a³ρ_cube×g + 0.2×1000g = a³g → solving gives a = 10cm. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Archimedes principle: buoyancy = weight of fluid displaced
When cube floats 2cm above water: buoyancy equals weight of cube

contrast_with: Fully submerged (complete immersion) vs partially submerged (floating) — different buoyancy calculations

question: The historian Barani refused to consider the state in India under Delhi Sultans as truly Islamic because
option_a: the majority of the population did not follow Islam
option_b: the Muslims theologists were often disregarded
option_c: the Sultan supplemented the Muslim law by framing his own regulations
option_d: religious freedom was accorded to non-Muslims
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Options A and D seem plausible as reasons for a Muslim historian to question Islamic credentials; the actual reason (sultans supplemented Sharia with Zawabits — royal decrees) requires specific knowledge of Barani's critique.
prelims.elimination_hint: Barani's key objection was that Delhi Sultans (especially Alauddin Khilji) created Zawabits — regulations supplementary to Muslim law — undermining the primacy of Sharia. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Barani was the historian-author of Tarikh-i-Firuz Shahi and Fatawa-i-Jahandari
Delhi Sultans used Zawabits (royal regulations) alongside Sharia, which Barani considered un-Islamic

contrast_with: Theocratic ideal (Sharia supreme) vs sultanate practice (royal regulations alongside Sharia)

question:
Consider the following climatic and geographical phenomena:
1. Condensation
2. High temperature and humidity
3. Orography
4. Vertical wind
Thunder Cloud development is due to which of these phenomena?
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Candidates may select option B (2, 3, 4) thinking condensation is not needed for thunderclouds, or option A thinking only condensation and humidity matter without vertical wind.
prelims.elimination_hint: Thundercloud (cumulonimbus) formation requires all four: high temperature and humidity (energy source), vertical winds (lifting mechanism), orography (triggering uplift), and condensation (cloud formation). Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Cumulonimbus (thundercloud) formation requires strong vertical (convective) currents
All four factors — condensation, high T&H, orography, vertical wind — contribute to thundercloud development

mains.contemporary_relevance: Increasing thunderstorm frequency linked to climate change is a contemporary concern.

contrast_with: Orographic rainfall (stable uplift) vs convective thunderstorms (intense vertical motion)

question: A trader fixed the price of an article in such a way that by giving a rebate of 10% on the price fixed he made a profit of 15%. If the cost of the article is Rs 72, the price fixed on it is
subject: CSAT

prelims.trap_factor: Candidates may confuse the chain of percentage calculations — 10% rebate on marked price, 15% profit on cost price; working backwards: SP = Cost × 1.15, and SP = Marked Price × 0.90.
factual_hooks: If SP after 10% discount = Rs.82.8, marked price = 82.8 ÷ 0.9 = Rs.92
Profit % is always calculated on Cost Price, not Marked Price

contrast_with: Profit on CP vs markup on CP — both affect final selling price differently

question: Among living organisms, which one of the following is the most responsible factor for bringing about the origin of a new species?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Natural selection is often considered the primary driver of evolution; mutation provides genetic variation and sexual reproduction increases it, but isolation is the crucial mechanism that allows new species to form (speciation).
prelims.elimination_hint: Isolation (geographic/reproductive) prevents gene flow between populations, allowing natural selection and mutation to drive divergence into new species. Without isolation, gene flow prevents speciation. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Geographic isolation is the most important factor in allopatric speciation
Without isolation, gene flow between populations prevents divergence into new species

contrast_with: Isolation (prevents gene flow, enables speciation) vs mutation (provides variation but alone insufficient for speciation)

question: According to the Constitution of India, the term ‘district judge’ shall not include
option_d: chief judge of a small cause court
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Sessions judge and chief presidency magistrate are both judicial officers that might seem included in 'district judge'; the Constitution excludes tribunal judges from this definition.
prelims.elimination_hint: Article 236 defines 'district judge' to include sessions judge, chief presidency magistrate, chief judge of small cause court, etc., but NOT tribunal judges (who have a separate statutory basis). Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Article 236: 'district judge' includes sessions judge, additional district judge, chief presidency magistrate, etc.
Tribunal judges are appointed under separate Acts and are NOT covered by the term 'district judge' in the Constitution
paired_with: High Court jurisdiction over subordinate courts

related_concepts: High Court jurisdiction over subordinate courts
contrast_with: Courts (under Constitution's Chapter V) vs Tribunals (statutory, under separate Acts) — different appointment and oversight mechanisms

question: The ancient Indian play Mudrarakshasa of Visakhadutt has its subject on
option_a: a conflict between Gods and Demons of ancient Hindu lore
option_b: a romantic story of an Aryan prince and a tribal woman
option_c: the story of the power struggle between two Aryan tribes
option_d: the court intrigues at the time of Chandragupta Maurya
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: The name Mudrarakshasa contains 'rakshasa' which may suggest demonic or mythological content; the play is actually about political intrigue at Chandragupta's court, not mythology.
prelims.elimination_hint: Mudrarakshasa by Visakhadatta is a Sanskrit play about the political machinations of Chanakya (Kautilya) in installing and securing Chandragupta Maurya's throne. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Mudrarakshasa = 'The Signet Ring of the Rakshasa (minister)' — political drama about Chandragupta's court
Visakhadatta (Vishakhadatta) was the playwright; Chanakya is the protagonist

contrast_with: Mudrarakshasa (political drama) vs Mricchakatika (Shudraka) — social romantic drama of a different era

question: For short-term climate prediction, which one of the following events, detected in the last decade, is associated with occasional weak monsoon rains in the India subcontinent?
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: La Nina (opposite of El Nino) is associated with stronger monsoons, not weaker ones. Candidates may confuse La Nina with El Nino's effects on Indian monsoon.
prelims.elimination_hint: El Nino and Southern Oscillations (ENSO) warming the Pacific leads to weakened Indian monsoon. La Nina has opposite effect (stronger monsoon). Answer: C.
factual_hooks: El Nino warms the eastern Pacific, weakening the pressure differential that drives Indian monsoon
La Nina cools the eastern Pacific, typically strengthening the Indian monsoon

mains.contemporary_relevance: ENSO monitoring is critical for agricultural planning and disaster preparedness in India.

contrast_with: El Nino (warm, weak monsoon) vs La Nina (cool, strong monsoon) — opposite ENSO phases

question:
Match List I (Ancient site) with List II (Archaeological finding) and select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists
(Ancient site) | (Archaeological finding)
A. Lothal | 1. Ploughed field
B. Kalibangan | 2. Deckyard
C. Dholavira | 3. Terracotta replica of a plough
D. Banawali | 4. An inscription comprising ten large sized signs of the Harappan script
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Kalibangan and Banawali both have connections to ploughed fields and terracotta ploughs — candidates may swap these. Dholavira's large signboard inscription is a distinctive but less-known fact.
prelims.elimination_hint: Lothal = Dockyard (A-2), Kalibangan = Ploughed field (B-1), Dholavira = Large inscription signs (C-4), Banawali = Terracotta plough replica (D-3). Answer: B (A-2, B-1, C-4, D-3).
factual_hooks: Lothal had a dockyard — unique trade facility in Harappan civilization
Dholavira had a large inscription of 10 signs of Harappan script
Kalibangan had the earliest evidence of a ploughed field

contrast_with: Lothal (coastal trade, dockyard) vs Mohenjo-daro (inland urban center) — different Harappan site functions

question: The Eighth Five-Year Plan is different from the earlier ones. The critical difference lies in the fact that
option_a: it has a considerably larger outlay compared to the earlier plans
option_b: it has a major thrust on agricultural and rural development
option_c: considerable emphasis is placed on infrastructure growth
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Options A (larger outlay) and B (agricultural thrust) are plausible 'differences' of the 8th Plan; the critical distinguishing feature is the abolition of industrial licensing under LPG reforms, which began exactly with the 8th Plan.
prelims.elimination_hint: The 8th Five-Year Plan (1992-97) coincided with LPG (Liberalization, Privatization, Globalization) reforms; industrial licensing was abolished — a paradigm shift distinguishing it from earlier plans. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: 8th Five-Year Plan (1992-97) abolished industrial licensing as part of LPG reforms
New Industrial Policy 1991 dismantled the License Raj during 8th Plan period

contrast_with: 7th Plan (License Raj era) vs 8th Plan (post-liberalization) — fundamental policy shift

question: Which reference to the Indian economy, consider the following activities: 1. Agriculture, Forestry and Fishing 2. Manufacturing 3. Trade, Hotels Transport and Communication 4. Financing, Insurance, Real Estate and Business services The decreasing order of the contribution of these sectors to the Gross Domestic Product (GDP) at factor cost at constant prices (2000-01) is
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: Candidates may place Agriculture as largest contributor (as a common stereotype) or Manufacturing as second. In 2000-01, Trade/Hotels/Transport contributed more than Manufacturing to GDP.
prelims.elimination_hint: Decreasing order of GDP contribution (2000-01): Services (Trade, Hotels, Transport) > Financing/Insurance/Real Estate > Agriculture > Manufacturing gives 3, 4, 1, 2. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: India's GDP in 2000-01 was dominated by services sector
Trade, Hotels, Transport & Communication was the largest services component

contrast_with: India (services-led growth) vs China (manufacturing-led growth) — different development paths

question: Which one of the following statements is not correct?
option_a: The status of Gomateshwara at Sravanabelagola represents the last Tirthankara of Jains
option_b: India’s largest Buddhist monastery is in Arunachal Pradesh
option_c: The Khajuraho temples were built under Chandela Kings
option_d: The Hoysalesvara temple is dedicated to Siva
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Gomateshwara statue at Shravanabelagola is the tallest Jain statue — students may confuse which Tirthankara it represents. It represents Bahubali (son of first Tirthankara Rishabhanatha), not the last (24th) Tirthankara Mahavira.
prelims.elimination_hint: Statement A is incorrect — Gomateshwara (Bahubali) was not a Tirthankara at all; he was the son of Rishabhanatha (1st Tirthankara). The statue represents Bahubali's meditation. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Gomateshwara = Bahubali, son of Rishabhdev (1st Tirthankara) — NOT any Tirthankara
Mahavira was the 24th (last) Tirthankara of Jainism

contrast_with: Bahubali (Gomateshwara) vs Mahavira — both Jain figures but different roles

question: In ancient Indian Buddhist monasteries, a ceremony called Pavarana used to be held. It was the
option_a: occasion to elect the Sanghaparinayaka and two speakers one on Dhamma and the other on Vinaya
option_b: confession by monks of their offences committed during their stay in the monasteries during the rainy season
option_c: ceremony of initiation of new person into the Buddhist Sangha in which the head is shaved an when yellow robes are offered
option_d: gathering of Buddhist monks on the next day to the full moon day of Ashadha when they take up affixed abode for the next four months or rainy season
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Pavarana sounds like it could be about initiation (taking robes) due to 'pava' prefix; candidates may confuse it with Pabbajja (novice ordination) or Uposatha ceremony.
prelims.elimination_hint: Pavarana was held at the end of Vassa (rainy season retreat); monks confessed offences committed during the retreat period. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Pavarana = end-of-Vassa confession ceremony in Buddhist monastic tradition
Vassa is the rainy season retreat lasting ~3 months for monks

contrast_with: Pavarana (confession at end of Vassa) vs Uposatha (fortnightly recitation of Vinaya rules)

question:
Consider the following statements:
1. The World Intellectual Property Organisation (WIPO) is a specialised agency of United Nations System of Organisations
2. WIPO has its headquarters at Rome
3. The Trade Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights (TRIPS) Agreement is binding on all WTO members
4. Least developed country members of WTO are not required to apply the provisions of TRIPS Agreements for a period of 20 years from the general date of application of the Agreement
Which of these statements are correct?
subject: International Relations

prelims.trap_factor: WIPO's headquarters is Geneva (not Rome — that's FAO/WFP). Also, least developed countries had a 10-year transition period under TRIPS, not 20 years, making Statement 4 incorrect.
prelims.elimination_hint: Statement 2 (WIPO HQ at Rome) is false — WIPO is in Geneva. Statement 4 is false — LDCs had 10-year (extended further) transition under TRIPS. Only Statements 1 and 3 are correct. Answer: D (1 and 3).
factual_hooks: WIPO headquarters is in Geneva, Switzerland (not Rome)
TRIPS Agreement is binding on all WTO members; LDCs had 10-year (not 20-year) transition period

mains.contemporary_relevance: TRIPS flexibilities for LDCs and COVID-19 vaccine IP waivers remain highly relevant.

contrast_with: WIPO (UN body, Geneva) vs FAO/WFP (Rome) — different UN specialized agencies and locations

question: With reference to the blood in a normal person, which one of the following statements is correct?
option_a: Compared to arteries, veins are less numerous and hold less of the body’s blood at any given time
option_b: Blood cells constitute about 70 per cent of the total volume of the blood
option_c: White Blood Cells (WBC) are made by lymph nodes only
option_d: The blood has more platelets than WBC
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Statement B (blood cells = 70% of blood volume) is wrong — blood cells are ~45% (hematocrit). Statement C (WBC made only by lymph nodes) is wrong — bone marrow makes most WBCs. Statement D (more platelets than WBC) is the correct one.
prelims.elimination_hint: Platelets: 150,000-400,000 per µL; WBC: 4,000-11,000 per µL — platelets vastly outnumber WBCs. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Platelets (1.5-4 lakh/µL) greatly outnumber WBCs (4,000-11,000/µL)
Hematocrit (blood cell volume) is ~45%, not 70%

contrast_with: Platelets (thrombocytes, clotting) vs WBC (leukocytes, immunity) — different functions and counts

question: Which one of the following statements is correct with reference to our solar system?
option_a: The Earth is the densest of all the planets in our solar system
option_b: The predominant element in the composition of Earth is silicon
option_c: The Sun contains 75 per cent of the mass of the solar system
option_d: The diameter of the Sun is 190 times that of the Earth
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Candidates may think silicon is dominant in Earth's composition (it's second most abundant in crust), or that the Sun contains 99% of solar system mass. Earth IS the densest planet in the solar system.
prelims.elimination_hint: Earth is the densest planet (5.5 g/cm³). Iron is Earth's most abundant element by mass (not silicon). The Sun contains ~99.8% of solar system mass (not 75%). Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Earth is the densest planet in the solar system at ~5.5 g/cm³
Iron is Earth's most abundant element by mass (not silicon, which dominates the crust only)

contrast_with: Earth (densest) vs Saturn (least dense, would float on water)

question: With reference to Indian agriculture, which one of the following statements is correct?
option_a: About 90 per cent of the area under pulses in India is rainfed.
option_b: The share of pulses in the gross cropped area at the national level has double in the last two decades
option_c: India accounts for about 15 per cent of the total area under rice in the world
option_d: Rice occupies about 34 per cent of the gross cropped area of India
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Statement about pulses being 90% rainfed is the correct one but may seem too high; India does account for a large share of world pulse area but the rice percentage (15% of world area) and gross cropped area share are often misstated.
prelims.elimination_hint: About 90% of India's pulse area is indeed rainfed — pulses grow in dry, rainfed regions. India accounts for ~25-30% of world rice area (not 15%). Answer: A.
factual_hooks: ~90% of India's pulse cultivated area is rainfed
India is the world's largest producer and consumer of pulses

mains.contemporary_relevance: India's pulse production challenges continue to affect food security and farmer income.

contrast_with: Pulses (mostly rainfed, dry land) vs Rice (requires intensive irrigation in many regions)

question: The salaries and allowances of the Judges of the High Court are charged do the
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Candidates often confuse this with Supreme Court judges (charged to Consolidated Fund of India). High Court judges' salaries are charged to the Consolidated Fund of the STATE, not India.
prelims.elimination_hint: Article 202(3): High Court judges' salaries and allowances are charged to the Consolidated Fund of the respective State. Supreme Court judges are charged to Consolidated Fund of India. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: HC judges' salaries → Consolidated Fund of the State (Article 202)
SC judges' salaries → Consolidated Fund of India (Article 112)

prerequisite_concepts: Consolidated Fund of India vs State
contrast_with: SC judges (CFI) vs HC judges (CFS) — different funds despite both being 'charged' (not voted)

question:
Assertion (A)
: With the increase of temperature, the viscosity of glycerine increases.
Reason (R)
: Rise of temperature increases kinetic energy of molecules.
option_a: Both A and R are individually true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_b: Both A and R are individually true but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_c: A is true but R is false
option_d: A is false but R is true
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Both statements may seem true: glycerine viscosity does change with temperature and kinetic energy does increase with temperature. However, Assertion A is FALSE — glycerine's viscosity DECREASES with increasing temperature (like all liquids).
prelims.elimination_hint: Liquids (including glycerine) decrease in viscosity as temperature rises (molecules move faster, intermolecular forces weaken). Gases increase in viscosity with temperature. A is FALSE, R is TRUE. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Liquids: viscosity DECREASES with temperature (intermolecular forces weaken)
Gases: viscosity INCREASES with temperature (more molecular collisions)

contrast_with: Liquid viscosity (decreases with T) vs gas viscosity (increases with T) — opposite trends

question:
Assertion (A)
: The effect of labour participation in the Indian nationalist upsurge of the early 1930 s was weak.
Reason (R)
: The labour leaders considered the ideology of Indian National Congress as bourgeois and reactionary.
option_a: Both A and R are individually true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_b: Both A and R are individually true but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_c: A is true but R is false
option_d: A is false but R is true
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Both A and R seem plausible: labour participation was weak, and communist ideological differences existed. The trap is that R accurately explains A — communist/socialist unions kept distance from the 'bourgeois' Congress.
prelims.elimination_hint: Labour participation in the Civil Disobedience Movement (early 1930s) was indeed weak (A true); Communist leaders criticized INC as representing capitalist interests (R true and explains A). Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Communist Party of India viewed INC as bourgeois and did not support Civil Disobedience Movement
Labour unions under communist influence stayed away from Gandhi's movements in early 1930s

contrast_with: Nationalist labour (supporting INC movements) vs communist labour (ideologically opposed to INC)

question: The sufi saint who maintained that devotional music was one way of coming close to God was
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Baba Farid and Saiyid Muhammad Gesudaraz are also famous Chishti saints associated with devotion; the founder of the Chishti order in India (Muin-ud-din Chishti) specifically popularized devotional music (Sama/Qawwali).
prelims.elimination_hint: Khwaja Muin-ud-din Chishti of Ajmer is the founder of the Chishti Sufi order in India and specifically championed devotional music (Sama) as a path to God. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Muin-ud-din Chishti founded Chishti order in India; popularized Qawwali/Sama as devotional music
Chishti order is known for its emphasis on music (Sama) unlike the Naqshbandi order which opposed it

mains.contemporary_relevance: Qawwali tradition and Sufi shrines remain important elements of India's composite culture.

contrast_with: Chishti (music-accepting) vs Naqshbandi (music-rejecting) Sufi orders

question:
Assertion (A)
: Synthetic detergents can lather well in hard water.
Reason (R)
: Synthetic detergents form soluble calcium and magnesium salts with hard water.
option_a: Both A and R are individually true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_b: Both A and R are individually true but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_c: A is true but R is false
option_d: A is false but R is true
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Both A and R are true and causally linked, making this an assertion-reason question where both being true AND R explaining A is the correct combination, which candidates may overlook.
prelims.elimination_hint: Synthetic detergents do lather in hard water (A true) BECAUSE they form soluble calcium/magnesium salts (unlike soaps which form insoluble scum with hard water). R correctly explains A. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Synthetic detergents have sulfonate groups that form soluble Ca/Mg salts — work in hard water
Soaps form insoluble Ca/Mg salts (scum) in hard water — don't lather well

contrast_with: Soaps (natural, insoluble with hard water salts) vs detergents (synthetic, soluble with hard water salts)

question:
Assertion (A)
: 60°-65° latitudes in both the hemispheres have a low pressure belt instead of high pressure.
Reason (R)
: The low pressure areas are permanent over oceans rather than on land.
option_a: Both A and R are individually true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_b: Both A and R are individually true but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_c: A is true but R is false
option_d: A is false but R is true
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Both A and R may seem true — the sub-polar low pressure belt IS at 60-65° latitudes. However, the Reason given (low pressure permanent over oceans rather than land) misidentifies the cause; the real cause is polar front/convergence, not ocean vs land.
prelims.elimination_hint: A is TRUE: 60-65° latitudes have sub-polar low pressure. R is FALSE: the reason is polar front convergence of warm tropical and cold polar air masses, not the claim about oceans vs land. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Sub-polar low pressure at 60-65° is caused by polar front where warm and cold air masses converge
The low is NOT because low pressure is 'permanent over oceans rather than land'

contrast_with: Sub-polar low (dynamic, due to convergence) vs equatorial low (thermal, due to heating)

question: Which one of the following is part of the electoral college for the election of the President of India but does not .form part of the forum for his impeachment ?
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Candidates may think State Legislative Councils are excluded from both processes; actually they are excluded from BOTH (not part of electoral college and not part of impeachment). The question asks what is part of electoral college but NOT impeachment.
prelims.elimination_hint: State Legislative Assemblies are part of President's electoral college (Article 54) but NOT part of the impeachment process (Article 61 — only Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha participate). Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Presidential election electoral college: elected MPs + elected MLAs (State Legislative Assemblies)
Presidential impeachment: only Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha — State Assemblies excluded

contrast_with: Electoral college (includes MLAs) vs impeachment forum (only Parliament — LS and RS)

question: Which one of the following areas of India produces largest amount of cotton ?
option_a: North-western India and Gangetic West Bengal
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: Northern India (Punjab, Haryana) is associated with cotton historically but the majority of cotton production is in Western and Southern India (Maharashtra, Gujarat, Andhra Pradesh, Telangana).
prelims.elimination_hint: Western India (Gujarat, Maharashtra) and Southern India (AP, Telangana, Karnataka) produce the largest amount of cotton in India — black cotton soil (Regur) regions. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Black cotton soil (Regur) in Deccan and Western India is ideal for cotton cultivation
Maharashtra and Gujarat are among the largest cotton producing states

question:
Consider the following programmes :
I. Afforestation and development of wastelands.
II. Reforestation and replantation in existing forests.
III. Encouraging the wood substitutes and supplying other types of fuel.
IV. Promotion of wide use of insecticides and pesticides to restrict the loss of forest area from degradation caused by pests and insects. The National Forest Policy of 1988 includes
subject: Environment

prelims.trap_factor: Statement IV (promoting insecticides/pesticides) sounds like a government programme, but the National Forest Policy 1988 emphasizes natural regeneration and ecological balance — not chemical inputs.
prelims.elimination_hint: National Forest Policy 1988 includes afforestation (I), reforestation (II), and fuel substitutes (III) but NOT promotion of insecticides/pesticides (IV) — that contradicts its ecological philosophy. Answer: D (I, II and III).
factual_hooks: National Forest Policy 1988: goal to maintain 33% of land under forests
Policy emphasizes social forestry, JFM (Joint Forest Management), not chemical pest control

mains.contemporary_relevance: Forest conservation remains central to India's climate commitments and biodiversity goals.

contrast_with: National Forest Policy 1952 (industrial/commercial focus) vs 1988 (ecological/social focus)

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer by using the codes given below the lists
List I | List II
I. Visakhadatta | (A) Medicine
II. Varahamihira | (B) Drama
III. Charaka | (C) Astronomy
IV. Brahmagupta | (D) Mathematics
option_a: I - A, II - C, III - D, IV - B
option_b: I - B, II - A, III - C, IV - D
option_c: I - B, II - C, III - A, IV - D
option_d: I - C, II - B, III - A, IV - B
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Varahamihira is known for both astronomy AND astrology (Brihat Samhita); candidates may confuse him with mathematics. Brahmagupta is a mathematician, not an astronomer, though he did work in astronomy.
prelims.elimination_hint: Visakhadatta (Drama — Mudrarakshasa), Varahamihira (Astronomy — Panchasiddhantika, Brihat Samhita), Charaka (Medicine — Charaka Samhita), Brahmagupta (Mathematics — Brahmasphutasiddhanta). Answer: C (I-B, II-C, III-A, IV-D).
factual_hooks: Varahamihira: Panchasiddhantika (astronomy) and Brihat Samhita (encyclopedic)
Brahmagupta: Brahmasphutasiddhanta — introduced concept of zero and negative numbers

contrast_with: Varahamihira (astronomy/astrology) vs Brahmagupta (mathematics) — often confused Gupta scholars

question: Which one of the following statements is correct ? The Prime Minister of India
option_a: is free to choose his ministers only from among those who are members of either House of the Parliament
option_b: can choose his cabinet colleagues after due counselling by the President of India in this regard
option_c: has full discretion in the choice of persons who are to serve as ministers in his cabinet
option_d: has only limited powers in the choice of his cabinet colleagues because of the discretionary power vested with the President of India
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: Option A tempts candidates who confuse the convention with a constitutional requirement; option D misleads those who overestimate presidential discretion.
prelims.elimination_hint: Article 75 only requires ministers to be members of Parliament within six months; the PM has full discretion in choosing them, making C correct.
factual_hooks: Article 75: PM chooses ministers, President appoints on PM's advice
Ministers must become MPs within 6 months of appointment

mains.contemporary_relevance: Relevant during government formation debates about coalition politics and ministerial selection.

contrast_with: Presidential system where cabinet is independent of legislature

question: Local supply of coal is not available to
subject: Geography

prelims.elimination_hint: TISCO Jamshedpur and HSL Durgapur/Bhilai are near Jharkhand/Odisha coal fields; Bhadravati alone depends on imported/transported coal.
factual_hooks: VSL Bhadravati in Karnataka has no local coal — relies on coal from Singareni and imported sources
TISCO Jamshedpur uses coal from Jharia coalfields

question:

question: In medieval India, Mansabdari system was introduced mainly for
subject: History

Mansab determined salary (in cash or jagir) and status, not just military role

question:
Consider the following statements: Most international agencies which fund development programmes in India on intergovernmental bilateral agreements, mainly provide

option_c: I, II and IV are correct

prelims.elimination_hint: Most bilateral/multilateral agencies provide technical assistance (I), soft loans (II), and grants (III); food assistance is a minor specialized component, making B correct.
factual_hooks: Bilateral development agencies primarily provide: technical assistance, soft loans, and grants

question: Which one of the following is a modern tank?
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Akash is a missile system and Prithvi is a ballistic missile; Bhim is an artillery system — all could be confused for a tank.
prelims.elimination_hint: Arjun is India's indigenously developed Main Battle Tank by DRDO/CVRDE; the others are missile/artillery systems.
factual_hooks: Arjun is India's Main Battle Tank developed by DRDO
Akash = surface-to-air missile; Prithvi = ballistic missile; Bhim = self-propelled howitzer

mains.contemporary_relevance: India's defence indigenisation push under Make in India.

contrast_with: Arjun (tank) vs Akash/Prithvi (missiles)

question:
Assertion (A)
: Artificial satellites are always launched from the earth in the eastward direction.
Reason (R)
: The earth rotates from west to east and so the satellite attains the escape velocity.
option_a: Both A and R are individually true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_b: Both A and R are individually true but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_c: A is true but R is false
option_d: A is false but R is true
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Both A and R appear intuitively true — satellites are launched eastward and Earth rotates west to east — but the reason (escape velocity) is wrong; launching eastward helps gain extra velocity, not escape velocity.
prelims.elimination_hint: A is false because satellites CAN be launched westward (though inefficiently); R is also partly misleading. The correct answer is D.
factual_hooks: Satellites launched eastward to gain boost from Earth's rotational velocity
Earth's rotation gives an extra ~460 m/s boost, not escape velocity directly

mains.contemporary_relevance: Relevant for understanding ISRO launch mechanics.

contrast_with: Prograde (eastward) vs retrograde (westward) satellite launches

question: In which of the following countries will the no-confidence motion to bring down the government passed by the legislature be valid only when the legislature is able to find simultaneously a majority to elect a successor government ?
subject: International Relations

prelims.trap_factor: France or Italy may seem correct as they have parliamentary systems with frequent government changes; the concept of 'constructive vote of no-confidence' is specifically German.
prelims.elimination_hint: Germany's Basic Law (Article 67) requires a 'constructive vote of no-confidence' — the opposition must simultaneously elect a new Chancellor to bring down the government.
factual_hooks: Germany's constructive vote of no-confidence (Article 67 Basic Law)
Only Germany has this mechanism to prevent governmental instability

contrast_with: UK/India no-confidence (simple majority) vs Germany (must elect successor)

question:

II. Demand deposits with banks
III. Time deposits with banks
IV. Other deposits with RBI
Choose the correct answer using the codes given below :
subject: Economy

prelims.trap_factor: M1 (narrow money) includes only currency + demand deposits + other RBI deposits; M3 (broad money) additionally includes time deposits — candidates often stop at M1.
prelims.elimination_hint: Broad Money (M3) = Currency with public + Demand deposits + Time deposits + Other deposits with RBI = all four components, making C correct.
factual_hooks: M3 (Broad Money) = M1 + Time deposits with banks
M1 = Currency + Demand deposits + Other deposits with RBI

mains.contemporary_relevance: Relevant to RBI monetary policy and liquidity management.

prerequisite_concepts: Money supply measures M0, M1, M2, M3
contrast_with: Narrow money (M1) vs Broad money (M3)

question: Which one of the following was used as a chemical weapon in the First World War?
subject: History

prelims.trap_factor: Hydrogen cyanide was used but less famously; carbon monoxide is a poison but not a battlefield chemical weapon; candidates may confuse various toxic gases.

Mustard gas causes blistering — a vesicant/blister agent

question: Which one of the following factors is responsible for the change in the regular direction of the ocean currents in the Indian Ocean?

option_d: Indian Ocean has greater variation in salinity
subject: Geography

prelims.elimination_hint: The Indian Ocean has a unique reversal of currents due to monsoon winds (monsoon drift); unlike Pacific/Atlantic, seasonal wind reversal overrides Coriolis-driven currents.
factual_hooks: Indian Ocean currents reverse direction seasonally due to monsoon
During SW monsoon: currents flow eastward; NE monsoon: westward

mains.contemporary_relevance: Indian Ocean currents affect monsoon prediction and maritime navigation.

question: In the shaded area of the above map, the mean temperature for the month of July varies between

question:
Assertion (A)
: The reservation of thirty-three per cent of seats for women in Parliament and State Legislatures does not require Constitutional amendment.

option_c: A is true, but R is false
option_d: A is false, but R is true
subject: Polity

prelims.trap_factor: A (both true, R explains A) seems tempting if one thinks parties can internally reserve seats, but the Assertion itself is false — 33% reservation requires constitutional amendment.
prelims.elimination_hint: Reservation of seats for women in Parliament requires Constitutional amendment (as proven by the Women's Reservation Bill debate); political parties can voluntarily allocate seats but cannot guarantee reservation without amendment.
factual_hooks: Women's reservation in Parliament requires Constitutional amendment (106th Amendment 2023)
Voluntary allocation by parties ≠ constitutional reservation

mains.contemporary_relevance: The 106th Constitutional Amendment (2023) finally passed women's reservation in Parliament.

Alpha Centauri (Proxima Centauri) is the nearest star after Sun at 4.24 light years

question:
Assertion (A)
: The emergence of economic globalism does not imply the decline of socialist ideology.

option_b: Both A and R are true, but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_c: A is true, but R is false
option_d: A is false, but R is true
subject: Society

prelims.trap_factor: Candidates may pick B (both true, R not explaining A) without carefully analyzing whether socialism's universalism actually supports the assertion about globalism not declining socialism.
prelims.elimination_hint: Socialism is inherently internationalist/universalist; economic globalism doesn't automatically destroy socialist thought since socialism also advocates global solidarity — both A and R are true and R explains A.
factual_hooks: Socialist ideology is universalist — advocates international worker solidarity
Economic globalism and socialist globalism are distinct but not always contradictory

mains.contemporary_relevance: Rise of left-wing movements globally despite economic globalism.

question: The most reactive among the halogens is

Halogen reactivity: F > Cl > Br > I (decreases down group 17)

contrast_with: Fluorine (most reactive) vs Iodine (least reactive among common halogens)

question: A girl is swinging on a swing in sitting position. When the same girl stands up, the period of swing will

factual_hooks: Pendulum period T = 2π√(L/g); shorter L = shorter period
Standing raises center of mass → reduces effective pendulum length

contrast_with: Simple pendulum (point mass) vs physical pendulum (distributed mass)

question:
Assertion (A)

Reason (R)
: Essential amino acids cannot be synthesised in the human body.
option_a: Both A and R are individually true and R is the correct explanation of A

option_d: A is false but R is true

question:
Assertion (A)
: The surface winds spiral inwards upon the centre of the cyclone.
Reason (R)
: Air descends in the centre of the cyclone.
option_a: Both A and R are individually true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_b: Both A and R are individually true but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_c: A is true but R is false
option_d: A is false but R is true
subject: Geography

prelims.trap_factor: R (air descends in centre) sounds correct for anticyclone not cyclone; candidates may accept both statements without checking R carefully.
prelims.elimination_hint: In a cyclone, surface winds DO spiral inwards (A is true); but at the centre, air RISES (not descends — that's anticyclone), so R is false. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Cyclone: air converges at surface and rises at centre (low pressure)
Anticyclone: air diverges at surface and descends at centre (high pressure)

question:
Assertion (A)

: The specific gravity of iron is more than that of mercury.

option_d: A is false but R is true

Objects float when their density is less than the fluid; iron floats on mercury

contrast_with: Floating (density < fluid) vs sinking (density > fluid)

question: Q15. What do you understand by Umpire Decision Review System in Cricket ? Discuss its various components. Explain how silicone tape on the edge of a bat may fool the system? [200 words] 10
subject: Science & Technology

prelims.trap_factor: Candidates may not know the technical interplay between DRS technology (Hot Spot infrared, Snickometer, ball-tracking) and how silicone tape insulates sound/heat.
prelims.elimination_hint: Silicone tape is thermally and acoustically insulating — it prevents the Hot Spot infrared signature and Snickometer sound spike from registering, thus fooling both detection systems.
factual_hooks: DRS components: Ball-tracking (Hawk-Eye), Hot Spot (IR), Snickometer/Ultra-Edge, Real-Time Snickometer
Silicone tape insulates heat and sound, preventing Hot Spot and edge detection

answer_structure.intro_approach: The Umpire Decision Review System (DRS) represents the integration of cutting-edge technology into sporting adjudication.
body_points: DRS components: Hawk-Eye ball tracking, Hot Spot infrared imaging, Ultra-Edge/Snickometer for edge detection, and Real Ball Tracking
Hot Spot uses infrared to detect heat from ball-bat contact; silicone tape being thermally insulating prevents heat signature
Snickometer/Ultra-Edge detects sound vibrations; silicone tape's acoustic insulation dampens this signal
Limitations: human elements remain (umpire on-field call stands if inconclusive), technology can be exploited
answer_structure.conclusion_approach: While DRS has improved decision accuracy, understanding its technological basis reveals both its strengths and exploitable limitations.

interlinkages: Ethics: Fair play and technology misuse in sports
mains.contemporary_relevance: DRS continues to evolve with AI-assisted ball tracking and improved edge detection.

contrast_with: Hot Spot (heat-based) vs Snickometer (sound-based) detection
